topics.title: Fundamentals of Acoustics - General Types of Sound Wave Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Acoustics - General Types of Sound Wave Propagation ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acoustics** is the interdisciplinary branch of engineering and physics that studies **sound waves**, encompassing their mechanical generation, transmission through elastic media, reception by the human auditory system, and quantitative characterization using measurable physical quantities (pressure, intensity, power, frequency). A **sound wave** is formally defined as a *mechanical, longitudinal, pressure disturbance* that propagates through a deformable elastic medium (gas, liquid, or solid) by means of localized compression and rarefaction of constituent particles. The disturbance transports **acoustic energy**, not bulk mass, through the medium. > [!NOTE] > **Automotive Relevance (PEMUT863 Context):** > In an automobile, sound waves are generated by engine combustion, tire–road interaction, aerodynamic flow over the body, and transmission/driveline vibrations. These waves propagate through air (cabin noise), through the vehicle's sheet metal and glass (structure-borne path), and through fluids in the exhaust system before being perceived by passengers. Understanding propagation types is the foundation for effective NVH counter-measure design. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **"Dropping a Pebble on a Pond" Analogy** > Imagine tossing a small stone into a still pond. The water surface does not *travel* outward with the ripples, but the **pattern** of crests and troughs clearly propagates. Floating leaves only bob up-and-down (or in tiny circles) — they do not move across the pond. > Sound behaves identically in air: air molecules oscillate *back-and-forth* around their equilibrium positions, while the acoustic disturbance (compressions and rarefactions) travels outward at the **speed of sound (c ≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C in air)**. Now extend this to three dimensions. If you snap your fingers (a *point source*), the disturbance spreads as ever-expanding spheres — these are **spherical waves**. If you bang a long ruler flat against a wall (a *line source*), the disturbance spreads as expanding cylinders — these are **cylindrical waves**. Far away from any source, the curvature of the wavefronts becomes negligible and the wavefronts look like flat parallel planes — these are **plane waves**. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Reference Sound Pressure | $p_{\text{ref}}$ | **20 μPa** | Pascal (Pa) | | Reference Sound Intensity | $I_{\text{ref}}$ | **10⁻¹² W/m²** | Watt per sq. metre | | Reference Sound Power | $W_{\text{ref}}$ | **10⁻¹² W** | Watt | | Speed of Sound in Air (20 °C) | $c_{\text{air}}$ | **≈ 343 m/s** | m/s | | Density of Air (20 °C) | $\rho_{\text{air}}$ | **≈ 1.204 kg/m³** | kg/m³ | | Characteristic Impedance of Air | $Z_0$ | **≈ 413 Pa·s/m** | Rayls | | Ratio of Specific Heats (Air) | $\gamma$ | **1.40** | dimensionless | | Molar Mass of Air | $M$ | **0.02897 kg/mol** | kg/mol | | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | J/(mol·K) | --- ### 1.4 General Types of Sound Wave Propagation Sound waves are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (i) the *geometry/shape of the wavefront*, and (ii) the *particle-motion direction relative to the propagation direction*. #### A. Classification by Wavefront Geometry | Type | Wavefront Shape | Typical Source | Distance Behaviour | |---|---|---|---| | **Plane Wave** | Parallel flat planes | Far-field of any source, ducts | $p$ constant with distance | | **Spherical Wave** | Concentric spheres | Point source (loudspeaker in free field) | $p \propto 1/r$ | | **Cylindrical Wave** | Coaxial cylinders | Line source (highway traffic, long pipe) | $p \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$ | #### B. Classification by Particle-Motion Direction | Type | Particle Motion vs. Propagation Direction | Allowed Media | |---|---|---| | **Longitudinal (Compression) Wave** | Parallel (∥) | Solids, liquids, gases | | **Transverse (Shear) Wave** | Perpendicular (⊥) | Solids only | | **Surface Wave (Rayleigh)** | Elliptical orbit, decays with depth | Along free surfaces of solids | | **Surface Wave (Love)** | Horizontal shear, perpendicular to direction | Along layered solid interfaces | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Automotive NVH Insight:** In the air inside a vehicle cabin, only **longitudinal waves** exist. In sheet-metal body panels, **bending (flexural) waves** dominate — a hybrid of longitudinal and transverse motion. The exhaust system transmits waves through gas (longitudinal) and along pipe walls (structural), making it a *coupled* acoustic-structural problem. --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Wavefront Geometries — Operational Logic #### (a) Plane Wave - **Condition**: Valid in the *far field* of any source, or inside a uniform duct whose cross-section is constant and whose lateral dimensions are much smaller than the wavelength. - **Why it matters**: Plane waves give the **simplest mathematical description**, and most acoustic measurement standards (e.g., microphone free-field calibration) assume plane-wave incidence. - **How it behaves**: Acoustic pressure $p(x,t)$ and particle velocity $u(x,t)$ are in phase, and their ratio equals the **characteristic (specific) acoustic impedance** of the medium: $$Z_0 = \rho c \quad [\text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}]$$ #### (b) Spherical Wave - **Condition**: Generated by a *monopole point source* in a free, unbounded, lossless medium. - **Why it matters**: Real automotive sources (engine, exhaust tailpipe, individual tire contact patch) approximate monopoles in their low-frequency range. - **How it behaves**: Acoustic pressure decays as $1/r$ and intensity as $1/r^{2}$ — this is the acoustic analogue of the inverse-square law of light. #### (c) Cylindrical Wave - **Condition**: Generated by an infinitely long *line source* (e.g., a continuously moving line of traffic, or a vibrating pipe). - **How it behaves**: Acoustic pressure decays as $1/\sqrt{r}$ and intensity as $1/r$ — slower decay than spherical, because energy spreads over a cylindrical surface (area $\propto r$), not a spherical surface (area $\propto r^{2}$). ### 2.2 Particle-Motion Modes — Operational Logic #### (a) Longitudinal (Compression) Wave - Particle displacement $\xi$ is **parallel** to propagation direction $x$. - The wave consists of alternating **compressions** (high pressure, high density) and **rarefactions** (low pressure, low density). - **Only wave mode supported in fluids** (gases & liquids) because fluids cannot support static shear. - Wave equation in 1D: $$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$$ #### (b) Transverse (Shear) Wave - Particle displacement is **perpendicular** to propagation direction. - Cannot exist in fluids; appears in **solids only** because solids possess a non-zero shear modulus $G$. - Speed is lower than longitudinal: $$c_{s} = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}}$$ #### (c) Surface (Rayleigh) Wave - Particles trace **retrograde elliptical orbits** at the free surface, with amplitude decaying exponentially into the depth. - Speed is approximately $0.9\,c_{s}$ (slightly less than the bulk shear speed). - Important for ground-coupled vibration near roads and railways. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **exam-critical formula bank** for Module 1 problems. Memorize each row, its variables, and its SI unit. | # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $c = f\lambda$ | Wave speed–frequency–wavelength relation | General wave problems | | 2 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | Speed in a fluid (bulk modulus $K$) | Air, water, oil | | 3 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Speed of longitudinal wave in a slender solid bar | Engine mounts, shafts | | 4 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | Speed of sound in an ideal gas | Cabin air, exhaust gas | | 5 | $Z_0 = \rho c$ | Specific acoustic impedance | Reflection / transmission | | 6 | $I = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c} = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{Z_0}$ | Intensity from rms pressure | Energy calculations | | 7 | $W = I \cdot A$ | Acoustic power through area $A$ | Source power estimation | | 8 | $\nabla^{2} p = \dfrac{1}{c^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$ | 3-D wave equation | All propagation problems | | 9 | $L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) | Noise measurement | | 10 | $L_{I} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level (dB SIL) | Energy-based metrics | | 11 | $L_{W} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Sound Power Level (dB PWL) | Source rating | | 12 | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Logarithmic addition of incoherent sources | Multi-source vehicles | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Reflection & Transmission**: When a plane wave meets an impedance discontinuity (e.g., cabin air ↔ windshield glass), the **reflection coefficient** $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ and **transmission coefficient** $T = 2 Z_2/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ determine how much acoustic energy enters the cabin. This is the basis of every *Sound Transmission Loss (STL)* plot. - **Refraction & Snell's Law in Acoustics**: $\dfrac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2}$ — temperature gradients in a road or exhaust pipe bend sound rays. - **Diffraction**: Sound bends around obstacles whose size is comparable to $\lambda$. Low-frequency automotive rumble (e.g., 30 Hz boom from an engine, $\lambda \approx 11.4$ m) diffracts around the entire car, making it hard to block by body panels alone. - **NVH Counter-Measures**: Absorption materials (foam, fibrous batts), damping sheets, sealed seams, and Helmholtz resonators are all designed by manipulating the wave-propagation physics covered above. --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Every algebraic transition below is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. ### 3.1 Derivation of the One-Dimensional Acoustic Wave Equation **Start with a control volume of fluid inside a uniform duct of cross-sectional area $A$.** *Step 1 — Consider a thin slice of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$ at position $x$.* - Mass of slice: $\mathrm{d}m = \rho \, A \, \mathrm{d}x$ - Pressure on left face: $p(x,t)$ - Pressure on right face: $p(x+\mathrm{d}x, t) \approx p(x,t) + \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,\mathrm{d}x$ *Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law to the slice.* The net pressure force in the $+x$ direction is: $$F_x = p(x,t) \cdot A - p(x+\mathrm{d}x,t)\cdot A = -A\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,\mathrm{d}x$$ Equating $F_x = \mathrm{d}m \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}}$ (where $\xi$ is particle displacement): $$\rho A\,\mathrm{d}x \cdot \frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = -A\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,\mathrm{d}x$$ Cancel $A\,\mathrm{d}x$ from both sides: $$\rho\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \tag{3.1}$$ *Step 3 — Constitutive (acoustic) equation of state.* For an adiabatic (fast, no heat flow) acoustic disturbance in a fluid of bulk modulus $K$: $$\mathrm{d}p = -K\,\frac{\mathrm{d}V}{V} = -K\,\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$$ Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -K\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}} \tag{3.2}$$ *Step 4 — Combine (3.1) and (3.2).* Substitute (3.2) into (3.1): $$\rho\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = K\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}}$$ Divide through by $\rho$: $$\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = \frac{K}{\rho}\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}}$$ Define the **speed of sound** $c$: $$c^{2} = \frac{K}{\rho}$$ Therefore the **1-D acoustic wave equation** in displacement form is: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2}\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}}\;}$$ Taking $\partial/\partial x$ of the whole equation yields the same equation in acoustic pressure $p$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}\;}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas *Step 1 — Start from the fluid formula $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$.* *Step 2 — Use the adiabatic bulk modulus for an ideal gas.* For an adiabatic process, $pV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$. Differentiating and rearranging gives: $$K = \gamma\, p$$ *Step 3 — Use the ideal-gas equation of state $p = \rho R_{\text{sp}} T$, where $R_{\text{sp}} = R/M$.* Therefore: $$K = \gamma\, \rho\, R_{\text{sp}}\, T$$ *Step 4 — Substitute into the speed formula.* $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma\, \rho\, R_{\text{sp}}\, T}{\rho}}$$ The $\rho$ cancels, leaving: $$\boxed{\;c = \sqrt{\gamma\, R_{\text{sp}}\, T} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}\;}$$ **Numerical check** (air at $T = 293$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $M = 0.02897$ kg/mol): $$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 293}{0.02897}} = \sqrt{117\,800} \approx 343.2 \,\text{m/s} \;\checkmark$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of the Plane-Wave Acoustic Impedance For a harmonic plane wave travelling in the $+x$ direction: $$p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - k x)$$ The Euler equation (linearised, inviscid) gives particle velocity: $$u(x,t) = \frac{p_0}{\rho c}\,\cos(\omega t - k x) = \frac{p(x,t)}{\rho c}$$ Define the **specific acoustic impedance** $z(x,t)$ as the local ratio: $$z(x,t) = \frac{p(x,t)}{u(x,t)} = \rho c = Z_0$$ Hence the **characteristic (or specific) acoustic impedance** of the medium is a real, positive, frequency-independent constant — a remarkable property unique to plane progressive waves in lossless media. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decay Laws at a Glance | Wave Type | Pressure vs. $r$ | Intensity vs. $r$ | Energy Spreads Over | |---|---|---|---| | Plane | $p = \text{const}$ | $I = \text{const}$ | Cross-section $A$ (constant) | | Cylindrical | $p \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$ | $I \propto 1/r$ | Surface $\propto r$ | | Spherical | $p \propto 1/r$ | $I \propto 1/r^{2}$ | Surface $\propto r^{2}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory aid for the exam:** *Plane (0), Cylinder (−1/2 in $p$, −1 in $I$), Sphere (−1 in $p$, −2 in $I$).* The exponents double from $p$ to $I$ because intensity is proportional to $p^{2}$. ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — dB SPL Computation **Problem:** A microphone in a car cabin measures an rms sound pressure of $p_{\text{rms}} = 0.2$ Pa. Compute the Sound Pressure Level in dB. **Step 1 — Write the SPL formula:** $$L_{p} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right), \quad p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio:** $$\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10\,000 = 10^{4}$$ **Step 3 — Take the logarithm:** $$L_{p} = 20 \times \log_{10}(10^{4}) = 20 \times 4 = 80\,\text{dB}$$ **Result:** $L_{p} = \mathbf{80\ dB\ SPL}$ — a typical level for moderate highway cruising. --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Sound Wave Propagation ```mermaid graph TD A["SOUND WAVE PROPAGATION"] A --> B["BY WAVEFRONT GEOMETRY"] A --> C["BY PARTICLE MOTION"] B --> B1["PLANE WAVE"] B --> B2["SPHERICAL WAVE"] B --> B3["CYLINDRICAL WAVE"] B1 --> B1a["Wavefronts are parallel planes"] B1 --> B1b["Pressure constant with distance"] B1 --> B1c["Valid in far field or ducts"] B2 --> B2a["Wavefronts are concentric spheres"] B2 --> B2b["Pressure falls as 1 divided by r"] B2 --> B2c["Generated by point source"] B3 --> B3a["Wavefronts are coaxial cylinders"] B3 --> B3b["Pressure falls as 1 divided by square root of r"] B3 --> B3c["Generated by line source"] C --> C1["LONGITUDINAL COMPRESSION WAVE"] C --> C2["TRANSVERSE SHEAR WAVE"] C --> C3["SURFACE WAVE"] C1 --> C1a["Particle motion parallel to propagation"] C1 --> C1b["Allowed in solids liquids gases"] C2 --> C2a["Particle motion perpendicular to propagation"] C2 --> C2b["Allowed only in solids"] C2 --> C2c["Speed equals square root of G divided by rho"] C3 --> C3a["Elliptical particle orbits at free surface"] C3 --> C3b["Amplitude decays with depth"] C3 --> C3c["Example Rayleigh and Love waves"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NVH Sound Propagation Pathway in an Automobile ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["SOURCE LAYER
Engine Combustion
Tire Road Interaction
Exhaust Pulsation
Aerodynamic Turbulence"] SRC --> GEN["GENERATION
Mechanical to Acoustic
Power Conversion"] GEN --> MED["PROPAGATION MEDIUM"] MED --> AIR["AIR PATH
Longitudinal Spherical Waves
Cabin and External Field"] MED --> STR["STRUCTURE PATH
Bending and Shear Waves
Body Panels and Frame"] MED --> EXH["EXHAUST PATH
Plane and Cylindrical Waves
Inside Pipe and Tailpipe"] AIR --> RCVR STR --> RCVR EXH --> RCVR RCVR["RECEIVER LAYER
Driver Ear
Microphone
Passenger Cabin"] RCVR --> MET["MEASUREMENT
SPL dB
Intensity dB
Power Level dB"] MET --> CTR["COUNTER MEASURES
Absorption
Damping
Isolation
Active Cancellation"] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — How the Three Wave Types Arise from a Single Disturbance ```mermaid graph LR P["POINT DISTURBANCE
e.g. Engine Mount Pulse"] --> SW["SPHERICAL WAVE
Initial Stage
Close to Source"] SW -->|"Distance increases, curvature decreases"| PW["PLANE WAVE APPROXIMATION
Far Field Limit"] L["LINE DISTURBANCE
e.g. Continuous Traffic Stream"] --> CW["CYLINDRICAL WAVE
2D Spreading"] CW -->|"Distance increases"| PW2["PLANE WAVE APPROXIMATION
Very Far Field"] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Particle Motion in Longitudinal vs Transverse Wave ```mermaid graph TB subgraph LONG["LONGITUDINAL WAVE IN AIR"] direction LR A1["Particle 1"] --- A2["Particle 2"] --- A3["Particle 3"] --- A4["Particle 4"] --- A5["Particle 5"] A1 -. "vibration arrow parallel to propagation" .-> A5 end subgraph TRAN["TRANSVERSE WAVE IN SOLID ROD"] direction LR B1["Particle 1"] --- B2["Particle 2"] --- B3["Particle 3"] --- B4["Particle 4"] --- B5["Particle 5"] B3 -. "vibration arrow perpendicular to propagation" .-> B3 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** The dots between particles represent the equilibrium lattice. In a **longitudinal** wave (top), the particles oscillate left–right along the same axis as the wave travels. In a **transverse** wave (bottom), the particles oscillate up–down while the wave still travels left–right. --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]** *Define acoustic impedance. State its SI unit and write the expression for the characteristic impedance of air at 20 °C.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Acoustic impedance $Z$ at a surface is the complex ratio of acoustic pressure to the resultant volume velocity through that surface: $$Z = \frac{p}{U} \quad [\text{Pa·s/m}^{3}]$$ - **Characteristic (specific) impedance (1 mark):** For a plane progressive wave in a lossless medium, it reduces to the *real, frequency-independent* value: $$Z_0 = \rho\, c$$ - **Value for air at 20 °C (1 mark):** With $\rho \approx 1.204$ kg/m³ and $c \approx 343$ m/s, $$Z_0 \approx 1.204 \times 343 \approx 413\ \text{Pa·s/m} \equiv 413\ \text{Rayls}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Adaptation]** *List the three geometric types of sound wave propagation and write the distance-dependence law of acoustic pressure for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Wave Type | Pressure vs. Distance | |---|---| | Plane | $p(r) = p_0$ (constant) | | Cylindrical | $p(r) \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$ | | Spherical | $p(r) \propto 1/r$ | *(1 mark each — full table or equivalent statements)* --- ### PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Internal choice** between two questions within the same module. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- #### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Deep Theory + Numerical* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]** **(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the three general types of sound wave propagation based on wavefront geometry. Derive the relation between acoustic pressure and distance from the source for each case.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Plane Wave (2 marks):** Wavefronts are parallel planes perpendicular to the direction of propagation. Valid in ducts or in the far field. Acoustic pressure does not decay with distance because the wavefront area is constant. Sketch: parallel vertical lines representing wavefronts with an arrow indicating the propagation direction. 2. **Spherical Wave (2.5 marks):** A point (monopole) source emits waves whose wavefronts are concentric spheres of area $4\pi r^{2}$. Since the radiated acoustic power $W$ must be conserved across every wavefront, $$W = I \cdot 4\pi r^{2} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}\cdot 4\pi r^{2}$$ Solving for $p_{\text{rms}}$: $$p_{\text{rms}}(r) = \frac{1}{r}\,\sqrt{\frac{W \rho c}{4\pi}}$$ Hence $\,p \propto 1/r$. 3. **Cylindrical Wave (2.5 marks):** A line source emits waves with cylindrical wavefronts of area $2\pi r L$ per unit length $L$. Energy conservation per unit length gives: $$W' = I \cdot 2\pi r = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}\cdot 2\pi r$$ $$p_{\text{rms}}(r) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r}}\,\sqrt{\frac{W' \rho c}{2\pi}}$$ Hence $\,p \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$. --- **(b)** *The sound pressure level measured inside a car cabin at the driver's ear position is 78 dB SPL when the vehicle cruises at 60 km/h, and 82 dB SPL when it cruises at 100 km/h. Compute the ratio of acoustic intensities and the ratio of rms sound pressures at the two speeds.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Write the SPL formula:* $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ *Step 2 — Compute the pressure ratio directly from the dB difference:* $$\Delta L_p = 82 - 78 = 4\ \text{dB}$$ $$20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) = 4 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{p_2}{p_1} = 10^{4/20} = 10^{0.2} \approx 1.585$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{p_2}{p_1} \approx 1.59\;}$$ *Step 3 — Compute the intensity ratio:* $$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{2} = 10^{4/10} = 10^{0.4} \approx 2.51$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{I_2}{I_1} \approx 2.51\;}$$ *Step 4 — Optional check using absolute values:* $$p_1 = p_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{78/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{3.9} \approx 0.0158\ \text{Pa}$$ $$p_2 = p_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{82/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{4.1} \approx 0.0251\ \text{Pa}$$ $$p_2/p_1 = 0.0251/0.0158 \approx 1.59\ \checkmark$$ --- #### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Theory + Derivation + Numerical* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Alternative]** **(a)** *Derive the one-dimensional acoustic wave equation for a fluid medium, starting from Newton's second law and the adiabatic equation of state.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 — present step 1 through step 4.)* 1. Control volume: thin slice of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$, area $A$, density $\rho$, mass $\rho A\,\mathrm{d}x$. (1 mark) 2. Net force from pressure gradient: $$F_x = -A\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,\mathrm{d}x$$ Newton's law: $$\rho A\,\mathrm{d}x \cdot \frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = -A\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,\mathrm{d}x$$ ⇒ $\rho\,\partial^{2}\xi/\partial t^{2} = -\partial p/\partial x$ (2 marks) 3. Adiabatic equation of state: $\mathrm{d}p = -K\,\partial\xi/\partial x$ ⇒ $\partial p/\partial x = -K\,\partial^{2}\xi/\partial x^{2}$ (2 marks) 4. Substitution yields the wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = \frac{K}{\rho}\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}} = c^{2}\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}}$$ with $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$. (2 marks) --- **(b)** *Compute the speed of sound in (i) air at 35 °C, and (ii) a solid steel rod, given the following data:* - *Air: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $M = 0.029$ kg/mol, $T = 308$ K.* - *Steel: Young's modulus $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, density $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.* *Also compute the wavelength of a 1 kHz tone in each medium.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Air (3.5 marks):** $$c_{\text{air}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 308}{0.029}}$$ Numerator: $1.4 \times 8.314 \times 308 = 3585.5$ Divide by $0.029$: $123\,638$ Square root: $c_{\text{air}} \approx 351.6\ \text{m/s}$ Wavelength at 1 kHz: $$\lambda_{\text{air}} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{351.6}{1000} = 0.352\ \text{m}$$ **(ii) Steel rod (3.5 marks):** $$c_{\text{steel}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.0 \times 10^{11}}{7850}} = \sqrt{2.548 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5048\ \text{m/s}$$ Wavelength at 1 kHz: $$\lambda_{\text{steel}} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{5048}{1000} = 5.048\ \text{m}$$ **Remark:** Sound travels ~14× faster in steel than in air, and the wavelength of a 1 kHz tone is correspondingly ~14× larger in steel. --- ### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Using 10 instead of 20 (and vice versa) in dB formulas.** > Use $20\log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ for pressure and $10\log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$ for intensity. Mixing these up costs the *full* 3–4 marks allocated to numerical answers. > > 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in the ideal-gas speed-of-sound formula. A 273 K error nearly doubles the answer and yields zero marks for the numerical part. > > 3. **Writing "speed of sound in vacuum" or "in space".** Sound is a *mechanical* wave — it requires a medium. Saying it propagates in vacuum is an immediate 1-mark cut. > > 4. **Confusing longitudinal with transverse in solids.** Students often state that "transverse waves cannot exist in solids" — the truth is the opposite: transverse (shear) waves *cannot exist in fluids*. This distinction is a favourite 2-mark trap. > > 5. **Dropping units in final answers.** KTU examiners are instructed to deduct up to ½ mark per numerical question for missing or wrong units (e.g., writing "343" without "m/s"). > > 6. **In spherical-wave derivations**, failing to explicitly write the area of a sphere ($4\pi r^{2}$) or cylinder ($2\pi r L$). The energy-conservation argument must be **shown**, not just the result. > > 7. **Sketch quality.** Module 1 questions frequently ask "with a neat sketch". A missing or unlabelled diagram forfeits 1–2 marks even if the written explanation is perfect. --- ### 5.2 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important things to remember before stepping into the exam hall:** - [ ] **Acoustics** studies *mechanical* waves — needs a medium, never a vacuum. - [ ] **Sound** is, in air, a *longitudinal compression* wave; in solids it can be longitudinal, transverse, or surface (Rayleigh/Love). - [ ] **Master wavefront-geometry classification:** Plane (constant $p$), Cylindrical ($p \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$), Spherical ($p \propto 1/r$). - [ ] **Intensity** for each geometry: Plane (const), Cylinder ($1/r$), Sphere ($1/r^{2}$). - [ ] **Speed of sound in fluids:** $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$. **In ideal gas:** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$. - [ ] **Speed of sound in slender solid bar:** $c = \sqrt{E/\rho}$. **Transverse (shear):** $c_{s} = \sqrt{G/\rho}$. - [ ] **Reference values:** $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$, $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^{2}$, $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$. - [ ] **SPL uses 20 log**, **SIL/PWL use 10 log**. Use $\log_{10}$, never natural log. - [ ] **Characteristic impedance** $Z_0 = \rho c$ is *real and frequency-independent* only for plane progressive waves in lossless media. - [ ] **1-D wave equation:** $\partial^{2} p/\partial x^{2} = (1/c^{2})\,\partial^{2} p/\partial t^{2}$. - [ ] **3-D wave equation:** $\nabla^{2} p = (1/c^{2})\,\partial^{2} p/\partial t^{2}$. - [ ] **Always convert °C → K** by adding 273 before any gas-law calculation. - [ ] **Sketch every geometry** in wavefront questions — it is mandatory in KTU valuation. - [ ] **$c_{\text{air}}$ at 20 °C = 343 m/s** — a "magic number" to remember for sanity-checking numerical answers.

topics.title: Linearized Wave Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linearized Wave Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Linearized Wave Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that governs the propagation of small-amplitude acoustic disturbances (sound waves) through a fluid medium. In the context of **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)**, it forms the fundamental mathematical backbone for predicting and analyzing acoustic phenomena such as **engine intake noise**, **exhaust system pulsations**, **tire cavity resonance**, and **cabin acoustic response**.

It is derived by applying a **first-order perturbation analysis** to the fundamental governing equations of fluid dynamics — namely, the **Continuity Equation**, the **Momentum (Euler) Equation**, and the **Isentropic Equation of State** — after assuming a quiescent (or uniform) mean flow and neglecting all higher-order (quadratic and above) acoustic perturbation products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The linearized wave equation is the cornerstone of *Linear Acoustics*. It is valid only when the acoustic pressure perturbation $p'$ is much smaller than the ambient (mean) pressure $p_0$ (typically $p' / p_0 \ll 1$, i.e., $\leq 0.01$ or 1% of ambient).

### 1.2 The Governing Master Equation

In its most general three-dimensional form, the linearized homogeneous wave equation is expressed as:

$$\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

where:
- $p' = p(\mathbf{r}, t) - p_0$ is the **acoustic (fluctuating) pressure** in Pascals (Pa).
- $c$ is the **adiabatic speed of sound** in the medium, measured in m/s.
- $\nabla^2$ is the **Laplacian operator** ($\partial^2/\partial x^2 + \partial^2/\partial y^2 + \partial^2/\partial z^2$).
- $t$ is time in seconds (s).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stone in a Pond"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for B.Tech Students:**
> Imagine throwing a small stone into a perfectly still pond. The water surface is the "mean state" of the fluid. The tiny ripples spreading outward are "acoustic perturbations" — small deviations from the calm surface. Now imagine doing this in slow motion: the ripples are so small that they do not disturb each other (no turbulence, no wave breaking). This is exactly what the *linear* wave equation models — tiny disturbances that **propagate independently** without nonlinear interaction. In an automobile, the cylinder's exhaust valve opening creates a small pressure pulse (the "stone"), and this pulse travels down the exhaust pipe (the "pond") as a sound wave.

### 1.4 Key Assumptions Embedded in the Linearization

The derivation strictly hinges on five classical assumptions standard in **linear acoustics**:

1. **Inviscid Fluid** — Viscous (frictional) effects are neglected (no boundary layer damping in the bulk).
2. **Homogeneous Medium** — The mean density $\rho_0$ is uniform in space.
3. **Quiescent Mean Flow** — The fluid is at rest in the mean ($v_0 = 0$) OR the mean flow is uniform and the convective derivative linearization applies.
4. **Isentropic Process** — Acoustic processes are adiabatic and reversible ($\Delta s = 0$), no heat conduction.
5. **Small Perturbations** — Products of two or more fluctuating quantities (e.g., $p' \cdot v'$, $\rho' \cdot v'$) are negligible compared to first-order terms.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants (KTU Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these constants — they appear repeatedly in KTU numerical problems.**

| Constant | Symbol | Value (Air, 20°C) | Units |
|----------|--------|-------------------|-------|
| Speed of Sound (Air) | $c$ | **343** | m/s |
| Ratio of Specific Heats | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | dimensionless |
| Gas Constant (Air) | $R$ | **287** | J/(kg·K) |
| Reference Density | $\rho_0$ | **1.204** | kg/m³ |
| Reference Pressure | $p_0$ | **101,325** | Pa |

The speed of sound for an ideal gas is given by the fundamental thermodynamic identity:

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$$

where $T_0$ is the absolute mean temperature in Kelvin. At $T_0 = 293\,\text{K}$ ($20^\circ\text{C}$), $c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 293} \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Parent Equations of Fluid Dynamics

To derive the linearized wave equation, we begin with three fundamental governing laws for a compressible, inviscid fluid. Each is **nonlinear** in its full form; linearization is what simplifies them.

#### (a) Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

This expresses that mass is neither created nor destroyed within a fluid element.

#### (b) Momentum Equation (Euler's Equation — Inviscid Navier-Stokes)

$$\rho \frac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = \rho\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}\right) = -\nabla p$$

This is Newton's second law applied to a fluid parcel. The **convective acceleration term** $(\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}$ is the source of nonlinearity.

#### (c) Equation of State (Isentropic Relation)

$$p = p(\rho, s) \quad \text{and for isentropic flow:} \quad p = c^2 \rho$$

For an isentropic process, $dp = c^2 d\rho$, where $c$ is the local speed of sound.

### 2.2 The Perturbation (Linearization) Recipe

Each instantaneous variable is decomposed into a **mean (time-averaged) component** and a **small fluctuating acoustic component**:

$$\rho(\mathbf{r}, t) = \rho_0 + \rho'(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

$$p(\mathbf{r}, t) = p_0 + p'(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

$$\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \mathbf{v}_0 + \mathbf{v}'(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

Substituting these into the parent equations and retaining **only first-order terms** (i.e., dropping products like $\rho' \mathbf{v}'$, $\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v}'$) yields the **linearized governing equations**.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Linearization

- **Step 1 — Substitute the perturbation expansions** into continuity, momentum, and state equations.
- **Step 2 — Expand products** like $\rho \mathbf{v} = (\rho_0 + \rho')(\mathbf{v}_0 + \mathbf{v}') = \rho_0 \mathbf{v}_0 + \rho_0 \mathbf{v}' + \rho' \mathbf{v}_0 + \rho' \mathbf{v}'$.
- **Step 3 — Drop the mean-flow contributions** (since the mean state is steady and satisfies the equations trivially).
- **Step 4 — Neglect the second-order products** ($\rho' \mathbf{v}'$, $\mathbf{v}' \nabla \mathbf{v}'$, etc.) by the small-perturbation assumption.
- **Step 5 — Combine the linearized equations** by eliminating $\mathbf{v}'$ and $\rho'$ to obtain a single equation in $p'$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the bread-and-butter of KTU NVH paper derivations.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Linearized Continuity | $\dfrac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = 0$ | Mass conservation for small disturbances |
| 2 | Linearized Momentum (Euler) | $\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla p'$ | Newton's law for acoustic particle motion |
| 3 | Linearized Isentropic State | $p' = c^2 \rho'$ | Acoustic Hooke's law for fluids |
| 4 | 3D Linearized Wave Eq. | $\nabla^2 p' - \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$ | **MASTER EQUATION** — pressure wave propagation |
| 5 | 1D Linearized Wave Eq. | $\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$ | For plane waves in pipes/exhausts |
| 6 | Speed of Sound (Ideal Gas) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ | Thermodynamic wave speed |
| 7 | Acoustic Particle Velocity | $v' = \dfrac{p'}{\rho_0 c}$ | Relation between $p'$ and $v'$ in a plane wave |
| 8 | Acoustic Specific Impedance | $z = \rho_0 c$ | $\approx 413\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m}$ in air at 20°C |
| 9 | Acoustic Energy Density | $E = \dfrac{p'^2}{2\rho_0 c^2} + \dfrac{\rho_0 v'^2}{2}$ | Sum of potential + kinetic energy densities |
| 10 | Sound Intensity | $I = \dfrac{p'^2}{\rho_0 c} = p' v'$ | Acoustic power per unit area (W/m²) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Automotive NVH

- **Exhaust System Design:** The 1D wave equation governs the propagation of pressure pulses in the exhaust pipe. Standing waves formed by reflections at the muffler tailpipe and catalytic converter create the characteristic "drone" at specific RPMs. Engineers solve the wave equation with appropriate boundary conditions to design reactive mufflers.
- **Intake Manifold Acoustics:** The "Helmholtz resonance" of the intake plenum is a direct consequence of the wave equation's modal solutions.
- **Cabin Acoustic Modeling:** Finite Element Method (FEM) and Boundary Element Method (BEM) solvers in tools like **LMS Virtual.Lab** and **Actran** solve discretized forms of the linearized wave equation to predict cabin SPL (Sound Pressure Level).
- **Tire Cavity Resonance:** The trapped air volume inside a pneumatic tire behaves as a 1D acoustic cavity governed by the linearized wave equation, producing a resonance typically in the **200–250 Hz** range.
- **EV Motor Whining Noise:** High-frequency electric motor noise is analyzed using the same wave equation framework in the surrounding air medium.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Nonlinear Conservation Laws to the Linearized Wave Equation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **board-favorite derivation** in KTU Module 1. Practice writing it out in full at least three times before the exam.

#### Given (Full Nonlinear Governing Equations):

**Continuity:**
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Momentum (Euler):**
$$\rho \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{v} = -\nabla p \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Isentropic State:**
$$p = p(\rho) \implies \nabla p = c^2 \nabla \rho \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

#### Step 1 — Apply the Perturbation Decomposition

Substitute $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$, $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_0 + \mathbf{v}'$, $p = p_0 + p'$ into (i):

$$\frac{\partial (\rho_0 + \rho')}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot [(\rho_0 + \rho')(\mathbf{v}_0 + \mathbf{v}')] = 0$$

The mean values $\rho_0$, $p_0$ are constants in time and space, and we assume a quiescent mean flow ($\mathbf{v}_0 = 0$). Expanding:

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot [\rho_0 \mathbf{v}' + \rho' \mathbf{v}' + \rho' \mathbf{v}_0 + \rho_0 \mathbf{v}_0] = 0$$

#### Step 2 — Drop Mean-Flow and Second-Order Terms

The terms $\rho_0 \mathbf{v}_0$ and $\rho' \mathbf{v}_0$ vanish since $\mathbf{v}_0 = 0$. The product $\rho' \mathbf{v}'$ is **second order** (linearization hypothesis). We are left with:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = 0} \quad \text{...(1) Linearized Continuity}$$

This is the **first-order continuity equation** in terms of fluctuating quantities.

#### Step 3 — Linearize the Momentum Equation

Substitute into (ii) with $\mathbf{v}_0 = 0$:

$$(\rho_0 + \rho') \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} + (\rho_0 + \rho')(\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}' = -\nabla(p_0 + p')$$

The gradient of $p_0$ (a constant) is zero. Dropping the second-order products $\rho' \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t}$ and $(\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}'$:

$$\boxed{\rho_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla p'} \quad \text{...(2) Linearized Euler}$$

This is the **acoustic Newton's second law** — the pressure gradient is the only force accelerating the fluid.

#### Step 4 — Linearize the Equation of State

From $p = p_0 + p' = c^2 (\rho_0 + \rho')$ and $p_0 = c^2 \rho_0$, we get:

$$\boxed{p' = c^2 \rho'} \quad \text{...(3) Acoustic State Relation}$$

This is the **acoustic constitutive law** — analogous to Hooke's law for a spring, but for fluid compressibility.

#### Step 5 — Eliminate $\rho'$ and $\mathbf{v}'$ to Obtain a Single Equation in $p'$

**Sub-step 5a — Differentiate (1) with respect to time:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Sub-step 5b — Take the divergence of (2):**

$$\rho_0 \nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot (\nabla p') = -\nabla^2 p'$$

**Sub-step 5c — Combine:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = 0$$

**Sub-step 5d — Substitute $\rho' = p'/c^2$ from (3):**

$$\frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2}\left(\frac{p'}{c^2}\right) - \nabla^2 p' = 0$$

Assuming $c$ is spatially uniform (homogeneous medium):

$$\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = 0$$

**Sub-step 5e — Rearrange into the canonical form:**

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0} \quad \text{...(4) The Linearized Wave Equation}$$

This is the **master equation of linear acoustics**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The d'Alembert (General) Solution

The 1D form of equation (4) is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

Its general solution (via the **method of characteristics**) is the d'Alembert solution:

$$p'(x, t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$$

where $f$ and $g$ are arbitrary twice-differentiable functions determined by initial and boundary conditions.

**Physical Interpretation:**

- The term $f(x - ct)$ represents a wave traveling in the **+x direction** (forward-propagating).
- The term $g(x + ct)$ represents a wave traveling in the **-x direction** (backward-propagating).
- The argument $(x - ct) = \text{constant}$ defines a **characteristic line** in the $(x, t)$ plane along which the wave shape is preserved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** A standing wave is formed when $f = g$, e.g., $p' = 2A\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$. This is the basis for **organ-pipe resonance** in exhaust tailpipes.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Speed of Sound in the Intake Manifold

**Problem (KTU-style):** At wide-open throttle, air enters an engine's intake manifold. The mean temperature is $T_0 = 350\,\text{K}$ and the gas behaves as ideal diatomic air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287\,\text{J/kg·K}$). Calculate the speed of sound and the wavelength of the 1st-order pressure pulsation at engine firing frequency $f = 100\,\text{Hz}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound:**

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 350}$$

$$c = \sqrt{140,630} \approx 375.0\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{375.0}{100} = 3.75\,\text{m}$$

**Interpretation:** The intake manifold must be analyzed as a 1D acoustic system because the wavelength (3.75 m) is comparable to the manifold's physical length (~0.3–0.5 m), and standing waves can form. This is why intake tuning is critical for naturally aspirated engines.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Plane Wave Acoustic Impedance

**Problem:** A plane acoustic wave with $p' = 10\,\text{Pa}$ amplitude propagates in air at 20°C. Calculate the associated acoustic particle velocity $v'$ and the acoustic intensity $I$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the medium properties:** $\rho_0 = 1.204\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 343\,\text{m/s}$, so $z_0 = \rho_0 c = 1.204 \times 343 \approx 413\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m}$.

**Step 2 — Particle velocity:**

$$v' = \frac{p'}{z_0} = \frac{10}{413} \approx 0.0242\,\text{m/s} = 24.2\,\text{mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Acoustic intensity:**

$$I = \frac{p'^2}{z_0} = \frac{10^2}{413} \approx 0.242\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Sound Pressure Level (SPL):**

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p'}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \approx 114\,\text{dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Derivation Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical flow** of the linearization procedure, from the nonlinear parent equations to the master wave equation. It is engineered to avoid Mermaid parsing errors (no reserved keywords used as node IDs; all labels are pure alphanumeric uppercase).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: NONLINEAR PARENT EQUATIONS] --> B[CONTINUITY EQUATION]
    A --> C[EULER MOMENTUM EQUATION]
    A --> D[ISENTROPIC STATE EQUATION]

    B --> E[APPLY PERTURBATION EXPANSION]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    E --> F[DROP SECOND ORDER PRODUCTS]
    F --> G[LINEARIZED CONTINUITY: drho/dt plus rho0 div v equals zero]
    F --> H[LINEARIZED EULER: rho0 dv/dt equals grad p]
    F --> I[LINEARIZED STATE: p prime equals c squared rho prime]

    G --> J[DIFFERENTIATE WRT TIME]
    H --> K[TAKE DIVERGENCE]
    J --> L[COMBINE TO ELIMINATE v PRIME]
    K --> L

    I --> M[SUBSTITUTE rho prime equals p prime over c squared]
    L --> M
    M --> N[OUTPUT: 3D LINEARIZED WAVE EQUATION]
    N --> O[MASTER FORM: laplacian p minus one over c squared times d2p/dt2 equals zero]
    O --> P[1D REDUCTION FOR PLANE WAVES]
    P --> Q[DALEMBERT SOLUTION: f OF x minus ct plus g OF x plus ct]
```

### 4.2 Physical Schematic — Acoustic Perturbation Concept

This second diagram illustrates the **physical meaning** of a fluctuating pressure field $p'(x,t)$ superimposed on a uniform mean state $p_0$, as it propagates through an automotive exhaust pipe.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[MEAN STATE: p0, rho0, v0 equals zero] --> B0[PERTURBATION INTRODUCED AT SOURCE]
    B0 --> C0[ACOUSTIC FIELD: p prime OF x comma t]
    C0 --> D0[PROPAGATES AT SPEED c]
    D0 --> E0[REFLECTS AT BOUNDARY]
    E0 --> F0[STANDING WAVE OR TRANSMISSION]
    F0 --> G0[RADIATION OR DAMPING AT OUTLET]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Application in Automotive NVH

The following shows how the linearized wave equation is integrated into the broader NVH engineering workflow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VEHICLE NOISE SOURCE: ENGINE, TIRE, WIND] --> B[ACOUSTIC MEDIUM: AIR OR EXHAUST GAS]
    B --> C[GOVERNING PDE: LINEARIZED WAVE EQUATION]
    C --> D[SOLVER DOMAIN]

    D --> D1[1D PLANAR PIPE: EXHAUST AND INTAKE]
    D --> D2[3D FEM: CABIN INTERIOR]
    D --> D3[BOUNDARY ELEMENT: EXTERIOR RADIATION]

    D1 --> E[TRANSFER FUNCTION AND SPL PREDICTION]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    E --> F[NVH TARGET VALIDATION: PASS OR FAIL]
    F --> G[DESIGN ITERATION: MUFFLER, LINER, SEAL]
```

### 4.4 Concept Map: Relationship Between Acoustic Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LINEARIZED WAVE EQUATION MASTER PDE] --> B[ACOUSTIC PRESSURE p prime]
    A --> C[PARTICLE VELOCITY v prime]
    A --> D[DENSITY PERTURBATION rho prime]

    B --> E[RELATION 1: p prime equals c squared rho prime]
    D --> E
    C --> F[RELATION 2: v prime equals p prime over rho0 c]
    B --> F
    F --> G[ACOUSTIC INTENSITY I equals p prime squared over rho0 c]
    B --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Define the linearized wave equation. State the key assumptions used in its derivation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **linearized wave equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that describes the propagation of small-amplitude acoustic pressure disturbances through a fluid medium.

**Mathematical form:**
$$\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

**Key assumptions (1 mark each):**
1. The fluid is **inviscid** and the process is **isentropic** (no heat conduction, no viscosity).
2. The mean flow is **quiescent** ($v_0 = 0$) and the medium is **homogeneous** ($\rho_0$ = constant).
3. Acoustic perturbations are **small** ($p' \ll p_0$), so all second-order products like $\rho' \mathbf{v}'$ and $(\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}'$ are neglected.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
What is the speed of sound? Derive the expression $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ for an ideal gas.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **speed of sound** $c$ is the rate at which a small pressure disturbance propagates through a fluid medium. It is the conversion factor between the spatial and temporal derivatives in the linearized wave equation.

**Derivation (2 marks):** For an ideal gas undergoing an isentropic process, $pV^\gamma = \text{constant}$. Differentiating and applying the definitions of density $\rho = m/V$ and the bulk modulus $K = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}$:

$$c^2 = \frac{dp}{d\rho}\bigg|_s = \frac{\gamma p_0}{\rho_0}$$

Using the ideal gas law $p_0 = \rho_0 R T_0$:

$$c^2 = \gamma R T_0 \implies \boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}}$$

**Numerical value in air at 20°C:** $c \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from the continuity equation, the momentum (Euler) equation, and the isentropic equation of state for a compressible inviscid fluid, derive the **linearized wave equation** in terms of acoustic pressure $p'$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plane acoustic wave of frequency $f = 500\,\text{Hz}$ propagates in air at $25^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate (i) the speed of sound, (ii) the wavelength, and (iii) the acoustic particle velocity amplitude if the pressure amplitude is $p' = 5\,\text{Pa}$. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287\,\text{J/kg·K}$, $\rho_0 = 1.184\,\text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** [Follow the derivation given in **Section 3.1** of these notes verbatim. Marks are awarded as follows:]
- Statement of parent equations: **1 mark**
- Perturbation substitution: **1 mark**
- Linearized continuity derivation: **1 mark**
- Linearized momentum derivation: **1 mark**
- Linearized state relation: **1 mark**
- Elimination steps leading to master equation: **2 marks**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Speed of sound:** $T_0 = 25 + 273 = 298\,\text{K}$.

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 298} = \sqrt{119,748} \approx 346.05\,\text{m/s} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**(ii) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{346.05}{500} = 0.6921\,\text{m} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**(iii) Particle velocity amplitude:** Acoustic impedance $z_0 = \rho_0 c = 1.184 \times 346.05 \approx 409.7\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m}$.

$$v' = \frac{p'}{z_0} = \frac{5}{409.7} \approx 0.0122\,\text{m/s} = 12.2\,\text{mm/s} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Tabular result summary (2 marks for presentation):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $c$ | $346.05\,\text{m/s}$ |
| $\lambda$ | $0.6921\,\text{m}$ |
| $v'$ | $0.0122\,\text{m/s}$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the linearized wave equation in the context of **automotive NVH**. Discuss its application in **exhaust noise analysis** and **cabin acoustics**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A car's exhaust tailpipe behaves as a closed-open (closed at engine, open at tail) organ pipe of length $L = 1.2\,\text{m}$. If exhaust gas temperature is $T_0 = 600\,\text{K}$, determine the **first three natural frequencies** of the standing wave. Take $\gamma = 1.3$ (for hot exhaust gases), $R = 287\,\text{J/kg·K}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical Significance & Applications (7 marks):**

The linearized wave equation mathematically captures how small pressure disturbances (sound) propagate through a fluid — the dominant mode of energy transfer in most automotive noise problems. **(1 mark)**

**Exhaust Noise Analysis (3 marks):** The exhaust system acts as a 1D acoustic waveguide. Pressure pulses generated at the exhaust valve propagate as waves described by the 1D form of the linearized wave equation. These waves reflect at the open tailpipe (pressure-release boundary) and the closed engine end (rigid boundary), creating **standing waves**. The resonant frequencies are given by $f_n = (2n-1)c/(4L)$ for a closed-open pipe. Reactive mufflers exploit destructive interference of these waves.

**Cabin Acoustics (3 marks):** The cabin is a complex 3D enclosure. The linearized wave equation is solved numerically using **FEM** (Finite Element Method) or **BEM** (Boundary Element Method) over the cabin volume. Eigenmode analysis yields the **acoustic modal frequencies** of the cavity (e.g., the "booming" mode near 80–100 Hz in a sedan). The frequency-dependent SPL response is then computed by convolving the boundary excitation with the cabin's acoustic transfer function.

**(b) Standing Wave Frequencies (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound in hot exhaust gas (1 mark):**

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} = \sqrt{1.3 \times 287 \times 600} = \sqrt{223,860} \approx 473.1\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the closed-open pipe formula (2 marks):** For a closed-open organ pipe, only **odd harmonics** are supported:

$$f_n = \frac{(2n - 1)c}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 3 — Compute the first three frequencies (4 marks):**

- **Mode 1** ($n = 1$): $f_1 = \dfrac{1 \times 473.1}{4 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{473.1}{4.8} \approx 98.6\,\text{Hz}$
- **Mode 2** ($n = 2$): $f_2 = \dfrac{3 \times 473.1}{4.8} \approx 295.7\,\text{Hz}$
- **Mode 3** ($n = 3$): $f_3 = \dfrac{5 \times 473.1}{4.8} \approx 492.8\,\text{Hz}$

**Interpretation (included for context):** These resonances contribute to the "drone" heard in the cabin at specific engine RPMs (the engine's firing frequency must match these modes for maximum excitation).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where B.Tech students most commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the linearization step** — writing the full nonlinear equations and saying "we assume small perturbations" without showing the algebraic product-expansion and term-dropping explicitly. The examiner awards marks for the *act* of linearization, not just the final equation. **Always write out** the expansion $\rho \mathbf{v} = (\rho_0 + \rho')(\mathbf{v}_0 + \mathbf{v}') = \ldots$ and **then strike through** the second-order terms.
> 2. **Using the wrong speed of sound** — confusing $c$ in air ($343\,\text{m/s}$) with $c$ in the exhaust gas (where $T_0$ is much higher, often $500$–$800\,\text{K}$, giving $c \approx 470$–$570\,\text{m/s}$). **Always re-compute** $c$ from $T_0$ given in the problem.
> 3. **Sign errors in the master equation** — writing $\frac{1}{c^2}\nabla^2 p' = \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$ instead of the correct $\nabla^2 p' = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$. Memorize the canonical form.
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions** — the linearization is only valid under the five assumptions listed in Section 1.4. A complete answer must list them.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners actively deduct marks for missing units. Always append Pa, m/s, Hz, kg/m³, etc.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this list before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ The **linearized wave equation** is the master PDE of linear acoustics: $\nabla^2 p' = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$.
- ✅ It is derived from **continuity + Euler + isentropic state** under **small-perturbation, inviscid, isentropic, homogeneous, quiescent** assumptions.
- ✅ The **speed of sound** in an ideal gas: $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$. For air at 20°C, $c \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$.
- ✅ The **d'Alembert solution** in 1D: $p'(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct)$ — forward + backward traveling waves.
- ✅ **Acoustic impedance of air:** $z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m}$ at 20°C.
- ✅ **Particle velocity in a plane wave:** $v' = p' / (\rho_0 c)$.
- ✅ **Acoustic intensity:** $I = p'^2 / (\rho_0 c) = p' v'$.
- ✅ **Sound Pressure Level:** $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}(p' / p_{\text{ref}})$, with $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
- ✅ **Automotive applications:** exhaust noise, intake pulsation, cabin acoustics, tire cavity resonance, EV motor whine.
- ✅ **Closed-open pipe resonances:** $f_n = (2n-1)c / (4L)$ — only odd harmonics.
- ✅ **Open-open pipe resonances:** $f_n = n c / (2L)$ — all harmonics.
- ✅ **Closed-closed pipe resonances:** $f_n = n c / (2L)$ — all harmonics.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions** before any derivation — the examiner's checklist includes this explicitly.
- ✅ Always **re-compute** $c$ from the problem-specific $T_0$ — never hardcode $343\,\text{m/s}$.
- ✅ Always include **units** in the final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Velocity Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Acoustic Velocity Potential # MODULE 1 — ACOUSTIC VELOCITY POTENTIAL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acoustic Velocity Potential (φ):** A scalar function of position and time, φ(x, y, z, t), defined in an acoustic medium, whose spatial gradient yields the instantaneous acoustic particle velocity vector of the sound wave. Mathematically, the particle velocity **v** is given by **v** = ∇φ. The velocity potential is a fundamental construct in linear acoustics because it reduces the vector problem of sound propagation to a single scalar wave equation. For this definition to be physically valid, the acoustic flow must be **irrotational** (i.e., ∇ × **v** = 0), which is satisfied when the medium is an inviscid, barotropic fluid undergoing small-amplitude perturbations (the linear acoustic assumption used throughout NVH analysis). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Ripple in a Pond:** > Imagine dropping a small pebble into a still pond. The water surface ripples outward. At any point in the water, the small back-and-forth motion of the water particle (the acoustic velocity) can be described by a single number — its instantaneous up/down displacement. That single scalar field (the height of the water surface) is analogous to the velocity potential. Just as you can obtain the sloshing velocity of the water by taking the slope of the water surface, the acoustic particle velocity is obtained by taking the gradient of φ. In **automotive NVH**, this concept is directly applied when modelling cabin noise, exhaust radiation, and tyre/road noise propagation. The pressure field inside a car cabin, the sound radiated from an engine block, or the noise field around a vibrating body panel can all be reconstructed from a single scalar φ — a massive simplification for numerical and analytical work. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference) | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude / Unit | |---|---|---| | **ρ₀** | Mean (equilibrium) air density | ≈ **1.21 kg/m³** at 20 °C | | **c** | Speed of sound in air | ≈ **343 m/s** at 20 °C | | **p₀** | Ambient static pressure | ≈ **101 325 Pa** | | **γ** | Ratio of specific heats (air) | **1.4** (adiabatic) | | **φ** | Acoustic velocity potential | m²/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In every derivation that follows, ρ₀ and c are treated as **constants** (linear acoustic assumption). This is valid only for small Mach numbers (M ≪ 1) — typically true for automotive NVH applications. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Can We Define a Velocity Potential? The existence of a scalar potential φ such that **v** = ∇φ requires that the flow be **irrotational**: $$\nabla \times \mathbf{v} = 0$$ For an inviscid (frictionless), barotropic fluid with conservative body forces, Kelvin's circulation theorem guarantees that if a fluid particle is irrotational at any instant, it remains irrotational forever. Since sound is initiated from rest, ∇ × **v** = 0 holds for all time. Hence, φ exists. ### 2.2 Foundational Acoustic Equations (Linearized) The starting point for the velocity potential is the triad of linear acoustic equations: **1. Linearized Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation):** $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$ **2. Linearized Euler's Equation (Momentum Conservation):** $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \nabla p' = 0$$ **3. Linearized Equation of State (Adiabatic Process):** $$p' = c^2 \rho'$$ where the primed quantities (ρ′, p′) denote small acoustic perturbations about the mean state. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Outline 1. Assume irrotational flow → **v** = ∇φ. 2. Substitute into Euler's equation → obtain a relationship between φ and pressure p′. 3. Substitute into the continuity equation. 4. Eliminate ρ′ using the equation of state. 5. Obtain the **scalar wave equation** for φ. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize the following table — these are the equations that appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 question.** | # | Relation | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Velocity from potential | $\mathbf{v} = \nabla \phi$ | Particle velocity is gradient of φ | | 2 | Pressure from potential | $p' = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$ | Acoustic pressure is the time-derivative of φ | | 3 | Density from potential | $\rho' = -\dfrac{\rho_0}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$ | Density perturbation from φ | | 4 | Scalar wave equation | $\nabla^2 \phi = \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$ | Governing PDE of φ | | 5 | Helmholtz equation (harmonic) | $\nabla^2 \Phi + k^2 \Phi = 0$ | Frequency-domain form, k = ω/c | | 6 | Harmonic time dependence | $\phi(\mathbf{r},t) = \text{Re}\{\Phi(\mathbf{r}) e^{j\omega t}\}$ | Steady-state complex amplitude | | 7 | Plane-wave solution | $\phi = f(t - x/c) + g(t + x/c)$ | d'Alembert solution in 1-D | | 8 | Spherical-wave solution | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{r}\left[f\left(t - \dfrac{r}{c}\right) + g\left(t + \dfrac{r}{c}\right)\right]$ | Outgoing / incoming spherical waves | | 9 | Acoustic intensity vector | $\mathbf{I} = p'\mathbf{v} = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \nabla \phi$ | Sound energy flux | | 10 | Particle displacement | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = \nabla\left(\int \phi \, dt\right)$ | Displacement field via time-integrated potential | > **Note on notation:** In the table, the absolute value / magnitude of a quantity (e.g., magnitude of **v**) is written as \vert \mathbf{v} \vert in equations and $\lvert \mathbf{v} \rvert$ inline to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Automotive NVH - **Cabin acoustics:** Pressure inside the cabin reconstructed from φ on the boundary using Kirchhoff–Helmholtz integral. - **Engine noise radiation:** The vibrating engine block surface velocity is integrated to give far-field φ. - **Tyre noise:** A quadrupole-like source is described by second spatial derivatives of φ. - **Muffler / silencer design:** 1-D plane-wave solution of φ is the basis of the transfer-matrix method. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure in Terms of Velocity Potential **Start with the linearized Euler equation:** $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p'$$ **Substitute v = ∇φ:** $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\nabla \phi) = -\nabla p'$$ **Swap the order of differentiation (∇ and ∂/∂t commute for smooth fields):** $$\nabla \left( \rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = -\nabla p'$$ **Integrate spatially:** $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -p' + f(t)$$ The function f(t) is an arbitrary function of time only (a "constant" of spatial integration). Physically, f(t) corresponds to a spatially uniform pressure fluctuation, which is excluded by the standard acoustic boundary conditions. We therefore set f(t) = 0: $$\boxed{\, p' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \,}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Density in Terms of Velocity Potential **Linearized equation of state:** $$p' = c^2 \rho' \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho' = \frac{p'}{c^2}$$ **Substitute the result from Derivation 1:** $$\rho' = \frac{1}{c^2}\left(-\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\, \rho' = -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \,}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Acoustic Wave Equation for φ **Linearized continuity equation:** $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$ **Substitute v = ∇φ:** $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$ **Differentiate the result of Derivation 2 with respect to time:** $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} = -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$ **Substitute back:** $$-\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$ **Divide by ρ₀ (a non-zero positive constant):** $$\nabla^2 \phi - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ **Or, equivalently:** $$\boxed{\, \nabla^2 \phi = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} \,}$$ This is the **scalar acoustic wave equation** for the velocity potential. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Helmholtz Equation (Time-Harmonic Form) For **time-harmonic (steady-state sinusoidal) fields**, the velocity potential is written as: $$\phi(\mathbf{r}, t) = \text{Re}\left\{ \Phi(\mathbf{r}) e^{j\omega t} \right\}$$ **Substitute into the wave equation:** $$\nabla^2 \left[ \Phi(\mathbf{r}) e^{j\omega t} \right] = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2} \left[ \Phi(\mathbf{r}) e^{j\omega t} \right]$$ **Compute the derivatives:** $$\nabla^2 \Phi \cdot e^{j\omega t} = \frac{1}{c^2} \Phi(\mathbf{r}) \cdot (j\omega)^2 e^{j\omega t}$$ **Use j² = −1:** $$\nabla^2 \Phi = -\frac{\omega^2}{c^2} \Phi$$ **Rearrange, with k = ω/c (acoustic wave number):** $$\boxed{\, \nabla^2 \Phi + k^2 \Phi = 0 \,}$$ ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — 1-D Plane-Wave Solution (d'Alembert) In **one dimension** (say, x-direction), the wave equation reduces to: $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$ **Introduce the characteristic variables:** $$\xi = t - \frac{x}{c}, \quad \eta = t + \frac{x}{c}$$ **Apply the chain rule:** $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \eta}\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{c}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \xi} + \frac{1}{c}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \eta}$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \xi^2} - 2\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \xi \partial \eta} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \eta^2}\right)$$ $$\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \xi^2} + 2\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \xi \partial \eta} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \eta^2}\right)$$ **Subtract the two:** $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{4}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \xi \partial \eta} = 0$$ This gives the **d'Alembert general solution**: $$\boxed{\, \phi(x,t) = f\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right) + g\left(t + \frac{x}{c}\right) \,}$$ The first term represents a wave travelling in the +x direction; the second term represents a wave travelling in the −x direction. ### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Particle Velocity & Pressure for a Plane Wave **For a forward-travelling wave** φ = f(t − x/c): $$\mathbf{v} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = -\frac{1}{c} f'\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right) \hat{\mathbf{x}}$$ $$p' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -\rho_0 f'\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right)$$ **Ratio (specific acoustic impedance of the medium):** $$\boxed{\, \frac{p'}{v} = \rho_0 c = Z_0 \,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key result:** The characteristic impedance of air Z₀ = ρ₀c ≈ **413 Pa·s/m** (N·s/m³) is the ratio of acoustic pressure to particle velocity for a plane wave. This is one of the most-tested KTU facts. ### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Spherical Wave Solution For a spherically symmetric source, the Laplacian in spherical coordinates reduces to: $$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{1}{r^2} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\right)$$ The wave equation becomes: $$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$ **Substitute the Ansatz φ = (1/r) ψ(r, t):** $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = -\frac{1}{r^2}\psi + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$ $$r^2 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = -\psi + r \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$ $$\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\right) = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} + r\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = r \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2}$$ **Substitute back:** $$\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}$$ This is the **one-dimensional wave equation in r**: $$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}$$ **Therefore ψ(r, t) = f(t − r/c) + g(t + r/c), and the spherical solution is:** $$\boxed{\, \phi(r,t) = \frac{1}{r}\left[f\left(t - \frac{r}{c}\right) + g\left(t + \frac{r}{c}\right)\right] \,}$$ The (1/r) factor is the well-known **geometric spreading** of spherical waves — a critical concept for far-field vehicle noise assessment. ### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Acoustic Variables to φ | Acoustic Variable | Expression in terms of φ | Differential Operator on φ | |---|---|---| | Particle velocity **v** | $\mathbf{v} = \nabla \phi$ | First spatial derivative (∇) | | Acoustic pressure p′ | $p' = -\rho_0 \partial_t \phi$ | First time derivative (∂/∂t) | | Density perturbation ρ′ | $\rho' = -(\rho_0 / c^2) \partial_t \phi$ | First time derivative | | Particle displacement ξ | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = \nabla\!\left(\int \phi \, dt\right)$ | Time integral + gradient | | Acoustic intensity **I** | $\mathbf{I} = p'\mathbf{v}$ | Product of time and spatial derivatives | | Volume velocity Q | $Q = \oint_S \mathbf{v}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = \oint_S \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n}\, dS$ | Normal derivative on closed surface | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Concept Map — Velocity Potential in the NVH Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Excitation
Engine Vibration / Panel Motion] --> B[Boundary Velocity Field
v on Surface S] B --> C[Define Velocity Potential
v = grad phi on S] C --> D[Solve Wave Equation
grad squared phi = 1 by c squared times d2 phi by dt2] D --> E{Pick Solution Form} E --> F[Plane Wave phi x t
1 D Duct and Muffler] E --> G[Spherical Wave phi r t
Far Field Vehicle Noise] E --> H[Helmholtz Phi r
Steady State Harmonic] F --> I[Recover Pressure p prime
p prime = minus rho0 d phi by dt] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Compute SPL and Intensity
NVH Output Metrics] J --> K[Cabin Noise / Pass By / Refinement] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the φ-Method ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Physical Acoustic Source
Vibrating Surface] --> B1[Boundary Condition
Normal velocity v sub n] B1 --> C1[Formulate phi Problem
Wave Equation plus BCs] C1 --> D1[Analytical or Numerical Solver
Green Function or FEM or BEM] D1 --> E1[Recover phi field] E1 --> F1[Derive v and p prime
via gradient and time derivative] F1 --> G1[Compute Acoustic Quantities
SPL Intensity Power] G1 --> H1[NVH Design Decisions
Damping Layout Muffler Geometry] ``` ### 4.3 Relationship Diagram — φ vs. v vs. p′ ```mermaid flowchart LR A2[Velocity Potential phi] -->|grad| B2[Particle Velocity v] A2 -->|minus rho0 d by dt| C2[Pressure p prime] A2 -->|minus rho0 by c squared d by dt| D2[Density rho prime] B2 -->|cross product identity| E2[Irrotationality
curl v = 0] C2 -->|chain rule with continuity| F2[Continuity Equation] D2 -->|chain rule with Euler| G2[Euler Equation] ``` ### 4.4 Spherical vs. Plane Wave — Geometric Spreading Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Plane Wave] --> P2[Amplitude Constant
with distance] P3[Spherical Wave] --> P4[Amplitude Falls as 1 by r
Inverse distance law] P2 --> P5[Impedance Z0 = rho0 c
Real and Constant] P4 --> P6[Impedance Z r = rho0 c times kr over 1 plus jkr
Complex and Distance Dependent] P5 --> P7[1 D Muffler Model] P6 --> P8[3 D Far Field Vehicle Pass By Noise] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define acoustic velocity potential. Why is it useful in linear acoustics?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The acoustic velocity potential φ(x, y, z, t) is a scalar function whose spatial gradient equals the acoustic particle velocity: **v** = ∇φ. (1 mark) > It exists when the flow is irrotational (∇ × **v** = 0), which holds for small-amplitude sound in an inviscid fluid starting from rest. (1 mark) > Utility: It converts the vector wave-propagation problem into a single scalar PDE (the wave equation), simplifying the determination of pressure, density, and intensity. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Derive the relation between acoustic pressure and velocity potential.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Starting from the linearized Euler equation ρ₀(∂**v**/∂t) + ∇p′ = 0 and substituting **v** = ∇φ: > ρ₀(∂/∂t)(∇φ) + ∇p′ = 0 → ∇(ρ₀ ∂φ/∂t) + ∇p′ = 0 → p′ = −ρ₀ ∂φ/∂t + f(t). (2 marks) > Setting the arbitrary spatial-constant f(t) = 0 (no uniform pressure jump) gives **p′ = −ρ₀ ∂φ/∂t**. (1 mark) --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks on Module 1 by **(a)** forgetting to justify irrotationality before defining φ, **(b)** dropping the constant f(t) without explanation, and **(c)** confusing the harmonic Ansatz (e^{jωt} vs. e^{−jωt}). Write each step clearly; partial credit is awarded liberally in KTU for correct method even if arithmetic slips. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* **(a)** Derive the scalar wave equation for the acoustic velocity potential in a homogeneous, inviscid fluid starting from the linearized continuity, Euler, and equation-of-state equations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a one-dimensional plane acoustic wave travelling in the +x direction, show that the specific acoustic impedance equals ρ₀c. Compute its value for air at 20 °C. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q. A(a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — State the three governing linear equations** (1 mark) - Continuity: $\dfrac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ - Euler: $\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \nabla p' = 0$ - Equation of state: $p' = c^2 \rho'$ **Step 2 — Invoke irrotationality and define φ** (1 mark) For an inviscid, barotropic fluid with sound starting from rest, ∇ × **v** = 0, hence: $$\mathbf{v} = \nabla \phi$$ **Step 3 — Substitute v = ∇φ into Euler and integrate** (1 mark) $$\nabla\!\left( \rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = -\nabla p' \;\Rightarrow\; p' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$ (choosing f(t) = 0 since the acoustic field is purely perturbation) **Step 4 — Eliminate ρ′ using the equation of state and substitute into continuity** (2 marks) $$\rho' = -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$ $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla^2 \phi = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$ **Step 5 — Final wave equation** (1 mark) $$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$ This is the acoustic wave equation in terms of the velocity potential. **Step 6 — Physical interpretation** (1 mark) The equation shows that acoustic disturbances propagate at the speed c, and the pressure/density/velocity fields can all be obtained from φ by simple differential operations. --- **Model Solution to Q. A(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Take the 1-D plane wave potential** (1 mark) For a wave travelling in +x direction: $$\phi(x,t) = f\!\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Compute particle velocity** (1 mark) $$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{c} f'\!\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Compute acoustic pressure** (1 mark) $$p' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -\rho_0 f'\!\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Form the ratio p′/v** (1 mark) $$\frac{p'}{v} = \frac{-\rho_0 f'}{-(1/c) f'} = \rho_0 c$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation for air at 20 °C** (2 marks) $$\rho_0 \approx 1.21 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad c \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Z_0 = \rho_0 c = 1.21 \times 343 \approx 415 \text{ Pa·s/m}$$ **Step 6 — Concluding statement** (1 mark) The specific acoustic impedance of a plane wave is purely real and independent of frequency, equal to ρ₀c. This quantity governs reflection, transmission, and absorption at acoustic interfaces. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** Starting from the general d'Alembert solution, obtain the acoustic pressure and particle velocity expressions for a plane harmonic wave. Show that the pressure and velocity are in phase for a plane wave. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A point source radiates spherical sound waves with a velocity potential given by: $\phi(r,t) = \dfrac{A}{r}\cos(\omega t - k r)$ where A, ω, k are constants and k = ω/c. Find (i) the radial particle velocity, (ii) the acoustic pressure, and (iii) the acoustic intensity at radius r. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q. B(a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Take the d'Alembert solution for a forward-travelling wave** (1 mark) $$\phi(x,t) = f\!\left(t - \frac{x}{c}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Apply harmonic Ansatz** (1 mark) $$f(\xi) = \Phi_0 \cos(\omega \xi) = \Phi_0 \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{\omega x}{c}\right) = \Phi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ where k = ω/c. **Step 3 — Compute pressure** (1 mark) $$p' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \rho_0 \omega \Phi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 4 — Compute velocity** (1 mark) $$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = k \Phi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 5 — Phase comparison** (2 marks) Both p′ and v contain $\sin(\omega t - kx)$ — the same phase. Therefore they are **in phase** for a plane wave.
