The specific acoustic impedance Z₀ = p′/v = ρ₀ω/k = ρ₀c is a real positive constant, confirming in-phase behaviour. **Step 6 — Physical remark** (1 mark) In-phase pressure and velocity mean that energy is continuously transported in the direction of propagation with no reactive (stored) component, a unique feature of plane waves in unbounded media. --- **Model Solution to Q. B(b) — 7 marks** **Given:** $$\phi(r,t) = \frac{A}{r}\cos(\omega t - k r)$$ **(i) Radial particle velocity** (2 marks) In spherical coordinates, vᵣ = ∂φ/∂r: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = -\frac{A}{r^2}\cos(\omega t - kr) + \frac{A k}{r}\sin(\omega t - kr)$$ $$\boxed{\, v_r = \frac{A}{r}\!\left[k \sin(\omega t - kr) - \frac{1}{r}\cos(\omega t - kr)\right] \,}$$ **(ii) Acoustic pressure** (2 marks) $$p' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega A}{r}\sin(\omega t - kr)$$ **(iii) Acoustic intensity** (3 marks) For a spherical wave the intensity is the time-averaged product: $$I(r) = \langle p' v_r \rangle_{\text{time}}$$ Substituting: $$I(r) = \frac{\rho_0 \omega A^2}{r^2}\left[\frac{k}{2}\sin^2(\omega t - kr) - \frac{1}{2r}\sin(\omega t - kr)\cos(\omega t - kr)\right]_{\text{avg}}$$ Using $\langle \sin^2 \rangle = 1/2$ and $\langle \sin \cos \rangle = 0$: $$I(r) = \frac{\rho_0 \omega k A^2}{2 r^2} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 A^2}{2 c\, r^2}$$ $$\boxed{\, I(r) = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 A^2}{2 c\, r^2} \,}$$ This shows **inverse-square spreading** (∝ 1/r²), the hallmark of point-source radiation. This is a key concept for vehicle pass-by noise assessment. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Definition:** φ is a scalar such that **v** = ∇φ. Valid only when ∇ × **v** = 0 (irrotational). - **Pressure from φ:** $p' = -\rho_0 \, \partial_t \phi$ — this is the most-tested relation. - **Density from φ:** $\rho' = -(\rho_0/c^2) \, \partial_t \phi$. - **Wave equation (time-domain):** $\nabla^2 \phi = (1/c^2) \, \partial_t^2 \phi$. - **Helmholtz equation (frequency-domain):** $\nabla^2 \Phi + k^2 \Phi = 0$ where k = ω/c. - **1-D d'Alembert solution:** $\phi = f(t - x/c) + g(t + x/c)$. - **3-D spherical solution:** $\phi = (1/r)[f(t - r/c) + g(t + r/c)]$ — includes 1/r geometric spreading. - **Specific acoustic impedance of plane wave:** $Z_0 = p'/v = \rho_0 c \approx 413 \text{ N·s/m}^3$ (air, 20 °C). - **Acoustic intensity:** $\mathbf{I} = p' \mathbf{v} = -\rho_0 (\partial_t \phi)(\nabla \phi)$. - **In-phase behaviour:** For plane waves, p′ and v are in phase; for spherical waves, they are not (complex, distance-dependent impedance). - **Time-averaged intensity of a spherical source:** $I(r) \propto 1/r^2$ — critical for pass-by noise regulations. - **Harmonic substitution:** When shifting to the frequency domain, always use the convention $\phi = \text{Re}\{\Phi e^{j\omega t}\}$ and remember that $\partial_t \to j\omega$ and $\partial_t^2 \to -\omega^2$. - **Irrotationality justification:** Always state that ∇ × **v** = 0 (Kelvin's theorem + flow starting from rest) **before** defining φ — examiners award a mark for this justification. - **Common pitfall:** Never use **v** = ∇φ inside a **viscous** or **rotational** flow — the potential does not exist there. - **KTU exam shorthand:** When asked "express pressure in terms of φ", write $p' = -\rho_0 \partial \phi/\partial t$ and mention "ignoring the spatially-uniform constant f(t)". - **Units of φ:** m²/s (since ∇φ has units of m/s). - **For 1-D problems (mufflers, ducts):** Plane-wave assumption is valid when duct cross-section ≪ wavelength. - **For far-field vehicle noise:** Spherical wave approximation is the standard first model.

topics.title: Propagation of Sound Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Propagation of Sound Waves # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sound** is a mechanical disturbance that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) as a **longitudinal pressure wave**, transporting energy from a source to a receiver without the net transport of matter. In the context of **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)**, sound propagation refers to the mechanism by which acoustic pressure fluctuations—generated by engine combustion, tire-road interaction, exhaust pulsations, or aerodynamic flow—travel through the vehicle structure, the cabin air volume, and the surrounding atmosphere. Mathematically, a propagating sound wave is described by the **acoustic wave equation**: $$\nabla^2 p - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ where $p$ is the acoustic pressure fluctuation (Pa), $c$ is the **speed of sound** (m/s), and $t$ is time (s). The standard speed of sound in air at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ and standard atmospheric pressure is **343 m/s**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight — PEMUT863 Module 1** > Sound propagation is the foundational concept for all subsequent NVH topics including **sound absorption, transmission loss, sound intensity measurement, and active noise control**. Mastering the wave equation is mandatory before tackling muffler design, cabin acoustic treatment, and structural-borne noise analysis. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine dropping a **pebble into a still pond**. The water surface does not travel outward to the edges—instead, the *disturbance* (the ripple) travels outward while each individual water molecule only oscillates up and down in place. **Sound behaves identically in air**: - **The medium (air)**: analogous to the pond's water. - **The disturbance (sound wave)**: analogous to the ripple. - **Air molecules**: they only oscillate back and forth around their mean position—they do not physically travel from the engine to your ear. - **The energy**: it is *the energy* that propagates, not the particles. In an **automotive context**, the engine combustion chamber acts as the "pebble dropper," the engine block and air mass act as the "pond," and your ear (or the cabin microphone) acts as the observer at the edge of the pond detecting the ripple. ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $c_0$ | Speed of sound in air ($20\,^\circ\text{C}$) | **343 m/s** | | $\rho_0$ | Density of air (STP) | **1.204 kg/m³** | | $P_0$ | Atmospheric pressure | **101 325 Pa** | | $\gamma$ | Ratio of specific heats for air | **1.4 (dimensionless)** | | $Z_0$ | Characteristic acoustic impedance of air | **$\rho_0 c_0 \approx 413$ Pa·s/m** | | $p_{\text{ref}}$ | Reference sound pressure | **$20\,\mu\text{Pa}$** (threshold of hearing) | | $I_{\text{ref}}$ | Reference sound intensity | **$10^{-12}$ W/m²** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In SI units, sound pressure level (SPL) is referenced to $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ because this corresponds approximately to the faintest sound a healthy human ear can detect at **1 kHz**. Any NVH measurement (engine noise, tire roar, wind whistle) is expressed in decibels referenced to this value. ## 1.4 Classification of Sound Waves Relevant to NVH - **Longitudinal waves**: particle motion is *parallel* to wave propagation direction. These dominate in air and are the primary mechanism for **airborne noise** in a vehicle cabin. - **Transverse waves**: particle motion is *perpendicular* to propagation. They occur in solid media and govern **structure-borne noise** transmitted through the chassis, body panels, and powertrain mounts. - **Plane waves**: wavefronts are flat and parallel; this is the *idealized* model used for **exhaust pipe acoustics and muffler design**. - **Spherical waves**: wavefronts expand radially from a point source; this approximates **engine noise radiation** in the far field. - **Standing waves**: formed by superposition of two counter-propagating waves in a confined space; this is the cause of **"booming" or "drumming"** in vehicle cabins at specific resonant frequencies. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Sound Propagation — Step-by-Step Logic A sound wave propagates through a medium via **three coupled physical processes**: 1. **Compression (Compression Phase)**: A source (e.g., a vibrating engine valve) pushes adjacent air molecules closer together, creating a region of *slightly elevated pressure* (compression). 2. **Restoring Force**: The compressed air molecules, being elastic, push back on their neighbors, transferring the disturbance forward. The local pressure begins to *recover* to ambient. 3. **Rarefaction**: The original source retreats (the valve closes or the piston moves down), leaving behind a momentary region of *lower-than-ambient pressure* (rarefaction). Neighboring molecules rush in to fill this void. > **Why does this matter for NVH?** Every automotive noise source—combustion knock, exhaust valve clatter, brake squeal—is fundamentally a *periodic compression-rarefaction generator*. The cabin door, firewall, floor pan, and glass must attenuate these pressure fluctuations before they reach the occupant's ear. ## 2.2 Derivation of the One-Dimensional Wave Equation (Conceptual) The 1-D acoustic wave equation is obtained by combining three physical laws: - **Continuity equation (mass conservation)**: links particle velocity $\partial u/\partial x$ to density change $\partial \rho'/\partial t$. - **Equation of state (adiabatic process)**: links pressure change $\partial p/\partial t$ to density change $\partial \rho'/\partial t$ via the bulk modulus $K$. - **Newton's second law (momentum)**: links pressure gradient $\partial p/\partial x$ to particle acceleration $\partial u/\partial t$ via the medium density $\rho_0$. Combining and eliminating $u$ and $\rho'$ yields the canonical acoustic wave equation. ## 2.3 Speed of Sound in Different Media The speed of sound is governed by the **stiffness** and **inertia** of the medium: | Medium | Governing Formula | Approximate Speed | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Ideal gas** (air) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | **343 m/s** ($20\,^\circ\text{C}$) | | **Newton's (isothermal) formula** | $c = \sqrt{P_0 / \rho_0}$ | 280 m/s (incorrect) | | **Laplace corrected (adiabatic)** | $c = \sqrt{\gamma P_0 / \rho_0}$ | 331 m/s (close) | | **Liquid** (water) | $c = \sqrt{K / \rho}$ | **1 480 m/s** | | **Solid — long bar** | $c = \sqrt{E / \rho}$ | **5 000 m/s** (steel) | | **Solid — bulk (3-D)** | $c = \sqrt{K + \frac{4}{3}G} / \rho$ | 5 960 m/s (steel) | where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats, $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature (K), $M$ is molar mass, $E$ is Young's modulus, $K$ is bulk modulus, and $G$ is shear modulus. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation | Mathematical Form | Variables | NVH Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Wave equation** | $\nabla^2 p = \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$ | $p$, $c$, $t$ | Foundation of all acoustic analysis | | **1-D wave equation** | $\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$ | $p(x,t)$ | Exhaust duct, simple enclosures | | **General solution** | $p(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ | right- and left-traveling waves | Directional energy flow | | **Harmonic solution** | $p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$ | $\omega = 2\pi f$, $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | Steady-state tonal noise (engine orders) | | **Dispersion relation** | $c = \lambda f = \omega / k$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength | Frequency-wavelength conversion | | **Speed in gas (Laplace)** | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K | Cabin & environmental acoustics | | **Speed in solid (bar)** | $c = \sqrt{E / \rho}$ | $E$ (Pa), $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Chassis vibration, panel noise | | **Acoustic impedance** | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ | Pa·s/m | Muffler transmission loss | | **Sound intensity** | $I = p^2 / (\rho_0 c) = p \, u$ | W/m² | Sound power mapping | | **Sound Pressure Level** | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_{\text{ref}})$ | dB | Standard NVH metric | | **Sound Intensity Level** | $\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{\text{ref}})$ | dB | Source identification | | **Particle displacement** | $\xi(x,t) = \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$ | m | Vibration sensor interpretation | | **Particle velocity** | $u = \partial \xi / \partial t$ | m/s | Microphone calibration | | **Specific acoustic impedance** | $z(x) = p(x) / u(x)$ | Pa·s/m | Reflection/absorption analysis | | **Reflection coefficient** | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | dimensionless | Cabin glass, firewall lining | | **Transmission coefficient** | $\tau = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | dimensionless | NVH material selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip — Memorize the Laplace speed-of-sound formula $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$**. Questions worth 3–7 marks frequently ask to derive or apply this. Also remember the **Rayleigh's formula** $c^2 = (\partial P / \partial \rho)_s$ which is the generalized form valid for any medium. ## 2.5 Real-World NVH Engineering Utility - **Exhaust System Design**: Plane-wave propagation in tailpipes determines the placement of baffles, resonators, and Helmholtz chambers. - **Cabin Acoustics**: Standing wave analysis reveals "booming" frequencies that require targeted absorption or damping. - **Powertrain Mounting**: Knowledge of structure-borne wave speeds in cast iron, aluminum, and steel allows prediction of vibration transmission paths. - **Wind Noise Prediction**: Spherical wave models from A-pillar vortex shedding help engineers design seals and side mirrors. - **Active Noise Control (ANC)**: Headphones and cabin ANC systems use destructive interference of counter-propagating waves generated in real time. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas (Laplace's Correction) **Statement**: Derive an expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas, taking into account the *adiabatic* nature of the process. **Step 1 — Newton's hypothesis (isothermal, for contrast)**: Newton assumed the sound process is isothermal. From Boyle's law, $P V = \text{constant}$, differentiating: $$P\,dV + V\,dP = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; dP = -\frac{P}{V}\,dV$$ The bulk modulus is defined as $K = -V\,dP/dV$, giving $K = P$. The speed of sound is then: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\rho}}$$ This yields $c \approx 280$ m/s for air, which is **incorrect** when compared with experiments ($\approx 332$ m/s). **Step 2 — Laplace's correction (adiabatic process)**: Sound propagates too rapidly for the heat to escape; the process is **adiabatic**, governed by $P V^\gamma = \text{constant}$. Differentiating: $$\gamma P V^{\gamma - 1} dV + V^\gamma dP = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; dP = -\frac{\gamma P}{V}\,dV$$ The adiabatic bulk modulus is therefore: $$K_s = -V\frac{dP}{dV} = \gamma P$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the speed-of-sound formula**: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of gas constants**: For an ideal gas, $P V = n R T$ and $\rho = M P / (R T)$, where $M$ is the molar mass. Substituting: $$\frac{\gamma P}{\rho} = \frac{\gamma P \cdot R T}{M P} = \frac{\gamma R T}{M}$$ Hence the **canonical Laplace formula**: $$\boxed{\,c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical verification at $T = 293$ K**: For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $M = 0.02897$ kg/mol: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 293}{0.02897}} = \sqrt{117\,800} \approx 343.2\ \text{m/s}$$ This matches the experimental value within 0.1%, confirming the derivation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 1-D Acoustic Wave Equation from First Principles **Step 1 — Continuity equation** (mass conservation in a 1-D control volume of cross-section $S$): $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Equation of state (adiabatic linearization)**: For small perturbations $\rho' \ll \rho_0$ and $p' \ll P_0$: $$p' = c^2 \rho' \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\partial p'}{\partial t} = c^2 \frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t}$$ **Step 3 — Euler's equation (Newton's second law for fluid element)**: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x}$$ **Step 4 — Eliminate $\rho'$**: Differentiate the continuity equation with respect to $t$: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Eliminate $u$**: Differentiate Euler's equation with respect to $x$: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = -\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2}$$ Substituting this into the time-differentiated continuity equation and using $p' = c^2 \rho'$: $$\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} = 0$$ **Final canonical form**: $$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}\,}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Plane Harmonic Wave Solution Assume a sinusoidal pressure disturbance of the form $p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)$. Substituting into the 1-D wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = -k^2 p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)$$ $$\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\omega^2}{c^2} p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)$$ Equating the two: $$k^2 = \frac{\omega^2}{c^2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\,\omega = c k\,}$$ This **dispersion relation** is the heart of acoustics. It tells us that for a non-dispersive medium (like air at low frequencies), the phase speed is independent of frequency—so high- and low-frequency components travel at the same speed and the waveform shape is preserved. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Concept-to-Formula Mapping | Physical Concept | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Wave speed | $c = \lambda f$ | Distance traveled per second by a wavefront | | Acoustic wave equation | $\nabla^2 p - \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$ | Local pressure obeys this PDE in lossless media | | d'Alembert general solution | $p = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ | Any waveform propagating right/left | | Harmonic solution | $p = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$ | Steady-state sinusoidal wave | | Acoustic intensity | $I = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c}$ | Energy flux per unit area | | Intensity-pressure link | $I = p_{\text{rms}} \cdot u_{\text{rms}}$ | Pressure × velocity product | | Plane wave impedance | $Z_0 = \dfrac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c$ | Real and frequency-independent | | Spherical wave decay | $p \propto 1/r$ | 6 dB drop per doubling of distance | | Boundary condition: rigid wall | $u = 0$ at wall | Particle velocity vanishes | | Boundary condition: open end | $p = 0$ at opening | Pressure node, velocity antinode | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sound Propagation in a Vehicle The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete **NVH signal path** for a typical sound wave originating at the engine, propagating through the vehicle structure, and being perceived by the occupant. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NVH Source
Engine Combustion or Tire Road] --> B[Initial Disturbance
Pressure Fluctuation] B --> C{Propagation Medium} C -->|Airborne Path| D[Acoustic Wave in Air
Longitudinal Compression] C -->|Structure Borne| E[Mechanical Wave in Body Panel
Transverse Bending] D --> F[Firewall and Floor Damping] E --> F F --> G[Cabin Air Volume
Modal Resonance] G --> H[Interior Sound Field
Standing Waves and Modes] H --> I[Occupant Ear
Subjective Response] I --> J[NVH Metric
Sound Pressure Level dB] style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Compression–Rarefaction Mechanism (Schematic Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Source Oscillates Forward] --> A2[Region of Compression
Pressure Above Ambient] A2 --> A3[Adjacent Air Pushed Forward] A3 --> A4[Source Retreats Backward] A4 --> A5[Region of Rarefaction
Pressure Below Ambient] A5 --> A6[Neighboring Air Rushes In] A6 --> A7[Wavefront Advances by One Wavelength] A7 --> A1 style A1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A4 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A7 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Acoustic Wave Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD W[Sound Waves] --> A[By Particle Motion] W --> B[By Wavefront Geometry] W --> C[By Medium State] A --> A1[Longitudinal
Airborne Noise in Cabin] A --> A2[Transverse
Body Panel Vibration] B --> B1[Plane Wave
Exhaust Duct Propagation] B --> B2[Spherical Wave
Engine Far Field] B --> B3[Cylindrical Wave
Radiating from Long Sources] C --> C1[Traveling Wave
Open Road Wind Noise] C --> C2[Standing Wave
Cabin Booming at Resonance] C --> C3[Evanescent Wave
Localized Structural Damping] style W fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.4 Acoustic Impedance Matching Block (NVH Material Insight) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source Medium
Engine Bay Air] -->|Z1| IF[Acoustic Interface
Firewall and Liner] IF -->|Z2| REC[Receiver Medium
Cabin Air] IF --> M1[Matched Impedance
Z1 equals Z2
Maximum Transmission] IF --> M2[Mismatched Impedance
Z2 much greater than Z1
Maximum Reflection] M1 --> OUT1[Loud Cabin Noise] M2 --> OUT2[Quiet Cabin Noise] style SRC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style IF fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style REC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style OUT1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style OUT2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > The four diagrams together cover: (i) the **system-level NVH signal path**, (ii) the **physical mechanism** of compression-rarefaction, (iii) the **classification taxonomy**, and (iv) the **material insight** of impedance mismatch. These visual anchors reinforce every concept discussed in Sections 1–3. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT863]** **Define the speed of sound in air. Why is the isothermal formula $c = \sqrt{P/\rho}$ incorrect, and what is the corrected form?** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: The speed of sound in air is the distance traveled by an acoustic wavefront per unit time, typically $343$ m/s at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$. Newton's isothermal formula $c = \sqrt{P/\rho}$ assumes the compression-rarefaction process is isothermal, yielding a value of $\approx 280$ m/s, which is **lower than the experimental value**. Laplace corrected this by recognizing that sound oscillations are too rapid for heat exchange; the process is **adiabatic**, giving $P V^\gamma = \text{constant}$ and the corrected formula: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$$ > **Mark distribution**: (i) Definition — 1 mark; (ii) Explanation of isothermal flaw — 1 mark; (iii) Corrected formula — 1 mark. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PEMUT863]** **Distinguish between longitudinal and transverse waves. Give one example of each in an automotive context.** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: In a **longitudinal wave**, particle oscillation is *parallel* to the direction of wave propagation. In a **transverse wave**, particle oscillation is *perpendicular* to the direction of propagation. | Wave Type | Automotive Example | | :--- | :--- | | Longitudinal | Cabin airborne noise from HVAC blower pulsations | | Transverse | Roof panel flexing due to engine vibration at idle | > **Mark distribution**: (i) Longitudinal definition + example — 1.5 marks; (ii) Transverse definition + example — 1.5 marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the one-dimensional acoustic wave equation starting from the continuity equation, the adiabatic equation of state, and Euler's equation of motion. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For air at standard conditions ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $M = 0.029$ kg/mol, $T = 300$ K), calculate: 1. The speed of sound. **(2 marks)** 2. The wavelength of a 1 kHz tonal noise from a four-cylinder engine's second-order vibration. **(2 marks)** 3. The acoustic impedance of air. **(2 marks)** 4. The transmission coefficient when sound passes from air into a porous liner of impedance $Z_2 = 4 \rho_0 c$. **(1 mark)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions** (1 mark): 1. Medium is **homogeneous, isotropic, and at rest** initially. 2. Sound is a **small-amplitude disturbance**: $p' \ll P_0$, $\rho' \ll \rho_0$, $|u| \ll c$. 3. The medium is **inviscid** (no viscous losses). 4. The process is **adiabatic and lossless**. 5. No mean flow; only **linearized perturbations** are considered. **Continuity equation** (1.5 marks): For a 1-D control volume of cross-section $S$, the net mass flux out equals the rate of mass decrease: $$-\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} S = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} S$$ Linearizing $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$ and retaining first-order small quantities: $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0}$$ **Adiabatic equation of state** (1 mark): For an adiabatic process, $p = c^2 \rho$. Linearizing: $$p' = c^2 \rho' \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\partial p'}{\partial t} = c^2 \frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t}$$ **Euler's equation** (1.5 marks): Newton's second law applied to a fluid element: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x}$$ **Combining the three** (2 marks): Differentiate the continuity equation with respect to $t$ and Euler's equation with respect to $x$: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = 0$$ $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = -\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2}$$ Substituting and using $p' = c^2 \rho'$: $$\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} = 0$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Speed of sound at $T = 300$ K** (2 marks): $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 300}{0.029}} = \sqrt{120\,442} \approx 347.1\ \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Wavelength at $f = 1$ kHz** (2 marks): $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{347.1}{1000} = 0.347\ \text{m} = 34.7\ \text{cm}$$ **(iii) Acoustic impedance of air** (2 marks): First compute $\rho_0$ from the ideal gas law: $\rho_0 = P_0 M / (R T) = 101325 \times 0.029 / (8.314 \times 300) \approx 1.178$ kg/m³. $$Z_0 = \rho_0 c = 1.178 \times 347.1 \approx 408.8\ \text{Pa·s/m}$$ **(iv) Transmission coefficient** (1 mark): With $Z_1 = \rho_0 c$ and $Z_2 = 4 \rho_0 c$: $$\tau = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{2 \times 4 \rho_0 c}{4 \rho_0 c + \rho_0 c} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a)** > - Students frequently **forget to linearize** the continuity equation and lose 1 mark. > - Many skip the **assumptions section** entirely; KTU awards 1 mark explicitly for it. > - The adiabatic equation $p' = c^2 \rho'$ must be stated separately, *not* mixed with Newton's isothermal form. > - For Part (b), use $T$ in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. $20\,^\circ\text{C} = 293$ K, not $20$ K. Loss of 1 mark per numerical error is common. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** With a clear schematic, explain the **compression and rarefaction** mechanism of sound wave propagation. Derive an expression for the **acoustic intensity** in a plane progressive wave. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A diesel engine radiates tonal noise at 800 Hz with an RMS sound pressure of $2$ Pa at a distance of $5$ m from the source. Calculate: **(7 marks)** 1. The sound pressure level (SPL) in dB referenced to $20\,\mu\text{Pa}$. **(2 marks)** 2. The acoustic intensity at that point (use $Z_0 = 413$ Pa·s/m). **(2 marks)** 3. The total sound power radiated, assuming a hemispherical spreading pattern over a hard ground plane. **(3 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Compression–rarefaction mechanism** (3 marks): A sound source (e.g., a vibrating engine valve) oscillates sinusoidally. During the *forward stroke*, it compresses the adjacent air layer, creating a region of pressure slightly above ambient—this is **compression**. This high-pressure region pushes neighboring air molecules forward, transmitting the disturbance. During the *return stroke*, the source retreats, leaving a momentary low-pressure region—this is **rarefaction**. Neighboring molecules rush in, propagating the rarefaction forward. > **Schematic (in words)**: …… | ▶ | ▶ | ▶ | → wavefront travels right > Regions: ___C___R___C___R___ (C = compression, R = rarefaction) **Derivation of acoustic intensity** (4 marks): For a plane harmonic wave $p = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$, the particle velocity is: $$u = \frac{p_0}{\rho_0 c}\cos(\omega t - kx) = \frac{p_0}{Z_0}\cos(\omega t - kx)$$ The instantaneous intensity is the product of instantaneous pressure and instantaneous velocity: $$I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t) = \frac{p_0^2}{Z_0}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$ Averaging over one full cycle ($\langle \cos^2 \rangle = 1/2$) and using $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0/\sqrt{2}$: $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{Z_0} = \frac{p_0^2}{2 \rho_0 c}$$ $$\boxed{I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) SPL at 5 m** (2 marks): $$\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(10^5) = 100\ \text{dB}$$ **(ii) Acoustic intensity** (2 marks): $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{Z_0} = \frac{2^2}{413} = \frac{4}{413} \approx 9.69 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **(iii) Sound power (hemispherical)** (3 marks): The surface area of a hemisphere of radius $r = 5$ m is $A = 2\pi r^2 = 2\pi(5)^2 = 50\pi$ m². The total radiated power: $$W = I \times A = 9.69 \times 10^{-3} \times 50\pi \approx 9.69 \times 10^{-3} \times 157.08 \approx 1.52\ \text{W}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B** > - In Part (a), students often **forget to take the time average** and present only the instantaneous intensity. The RMS form is mandatory for "intensity" in NVH. > - For Part (b)(i), the reference pressure $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ must be stated explicitly. Omitting it costs 0.5 marks. > - For Part (b)(iii), students frequently use the full sphere area $4\pi r^2$ instead of hemisphere $2\pi r^2$ — ground reflection effectively doubles the power, but the formula must reflect the *geometric* hemisphere, not be confused with doubling the SPL. > - Always express the final SPL in **dB with no units**; writing "100 dB SPL" or "100 dB" both acceptable, but never "100 dB Pa". --- ## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Propagation of Sound Waves** - **Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave** requiring a material medium; it cannot travel through a vacuum. - The **acoustic wave equation** $\nabla^2 p = (1/c^2) \partial^2 p/\partial t^2$ is the cornerstone of all NVH theory. - **Laplace's formula** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ is the correct form for gases; Newton's isothermal formula is wrong by a factor of $\sqrt{\gamma}$. - The **speed of sound in air at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$** is **343 m/s**; it increases with temperature and decreases with molar mass. - **Speed in solids** is governed by $c = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ (long bars) — steel propagates sound ~17× faster than air. - The **dispersion relation** $\omega = c k$ implies non-dispersive behavior in air: all frequencies travel at the same speed. - **Acoustic impedance** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ is real and frequency-independent for plane waves in air (≈ 413 Pa·s/m). - **Sound intensity** $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho_0 c)$ is the *time-averaged* energy flux. - **dB scales**: SPL uses a factor of 20 (pressure ratio); SIL uses 10 (power/intensity ratio). The reference values are $20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ and $10^{-12}$ W/m² respectively. - **Spherical spreading** causes a 6 dB drop per doubling of distance from a point source. - **Impedance mismatch** governs reflection and transmission at boundaries — the foundation of muffler, firewall, and glass design. - **Three equations** you must always be ready to write on demand: (i) wave equation, (ii) speed of sound, (iii) intensity-pressure relation. - In **automotive applications**, three regimes matter: (i) **airborne** (cabin, intake, exhaust tailpipe), (ii) **structure-borne** (chassis, panels, mounts), and (iii) **coupled** (sound-structure interaction at the firewall and glass). > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip**: When a question asks "explain with reference to an automotive application," always close your answer with a concrete example — e.g., "In a four-cylinder engine at 3000 rpm, the second-order firing frequency is 100 Hz, giving a wavelength of 3.4 m in air, comparable to cabin length, which explains why standing waves form inside the cabin at this RPM." This sentence alone can fetch 1–2 extra marks for application-level thinking, which is what KTU 2024 Scheme rewards.

topics.title: Sound Intensity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sound Intensity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sound Intensity ($I$)** is formally defined as the **time-averaged rate of sound energy flow** transmitted in a specified direction through a unit area normal to that direction at a given point in a sound field. It is a **vector quantity** (possessing both magnitude and direction), expressed in **watts per square metre (W/m²)**.

Mathematically, for a harmonic (sinusoidal) acoustic field, the **instantaneous sound intensity** is the product of the instantaneous acoustic pressure $p(t)$ and the instantaneous particle velocity $u(t)$:

$$\vec{I}(t) = p(t) \cdot \vec{u}(t)$$

The **time-averaged (mean) sound intensity** for a periodic signal is:

$$\vec{I} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} p(t) \cdot \vec{u}(t) \, dt$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standard (ISO 3744 / ISO 9614):** The standard reference sound intensity is $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$, corresponding to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine standing in a river. The **current of water** flowing past you carries energy. If you hold a square wire mesh (1 m × 1 m) perpendicular to the flow, the *volume* of water passing through the mesh every second tells you how strong the current is. The **total energy** carried by that water per second, divided by the mesh area, is exactly analogous to **sound intensity**.

Key parallel mappings:
- **River current speed** $\leftrightarrow$ Acoustic **particle velocity** ($u$)
- **Mesh area (1 m²)** $\leftrightarrow$ Unit **transmission area**
- **Water energy per second** $\leftrightarrow$ **Sound power (W)** flowing through the area
- **Direction of flow** $\leftrightarrow$ **Vector direction** of acoustic energy propagation

> [!TIP]
> If you only stand in the river and feel *how hard* the water pushes on your body, that "push" is like **sound pressure** ($p$) — a **scalar** quantity. Sound pressure alone cannot tell you *which direction* the energy is flowing. **Intensity can**.

### 1.3 Distinction — Sound Pressure vs. Sound Intensity

| Property | Sound Pressure ($p$) | Sound Intensity ($I$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Scalar (magnitude only) | Vector (magnitude + direction) |
| **Unit** | Pascal (Pa = N/m²) | Watt per square metre (W/m²) |
| **Measured by** | Single microphone | Two-microphone (P-U) probe |
| **Information** | Local pressure fluctuation | Net energy flow direction |
| **Use in NVH** | SPL mapping, dB levels | Source identification, sound power |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Acoustic Field Decomposition**
A real sound field is decomposed into two fundamental microscopic quantities:
1. Acoustic pressure $p(t)$ — the local deviation from ambient atmospheric pressure.
2. Particle velocity $\vec{u}(t)$ — the small oscillatory velocity of the air molecules about their equilibrium position.

**Step 2 — Instantaneous Power Flux**
The instantaneous power crossing a unit area is the mechanical work rate: force × velocity, which yields $p(t)\cdot u(t)$. This is the **Poynting-like vector** of acoustics.

**Step 3 — Time Averaging (Active vs. Reactive Intensity)**
For harmonic signals $p(t)=p_0\cos(\omega t)$ and $u(t)=u_0\cos(\omega t-\phi)$:
- The **active (real) intensity** represents energy actually *transported away* from the source.
- The **reactive (imaginary) intensity** represents energy that *oscillates back and forth* locally without net transport — it stores and releases energy but does not radiate.

**Step 4 — The "Why" Behind the 10·log₁₀ for Intensity vs. 20·log₁₀ for Pressure**
Sound intensity is proportional to the **square** of sound pressure ($I \propto p^2$) and **square** of particle velocity. Because a logarithmic scale compresses multiplicative ranges, taking $\log_{10}$ of a squared quantity gives a factor of 2:

$$10 \log_{10}(p^2) = 20 \log_{10}(p)$$

This is why **intensity level** uses 10 and **sound pressure level** uses 20.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations examiners expect to see in derivations and numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{I}(t) = p(t)\cdot\vec{u}(t)$ | Instantaneous intensity vector | W/m² |
| 2 | $\vec{I} = \overline{p(t)\cdot\vec{u}(t)}$ | Time-averaged intensity | W/m² |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$ | Plane-wave intensity from pressure | W/m² |
| 4 | $\rho c = Z_0$ | **Characteristic acoustic impedance** of air ($\approx 413 \, \text{Pa·s/m}$) | Rayl |
| 5 | $L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)** | dB |
| 6 | $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ | Reference intensity (ISO standard) | W/m² |
| 7 | $L_I = L_p + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)}{p_{\text{ref}}^2/Z_0}\right)$ | Intensity–pressure level relation | dB |
| 8 | $L_p = L_I$ (plane wave, free field) | Equivalence under free-field plane-wave conditions | dB |
| 9 | $\vec{I} = \vec{I}_{\text{active}} + j\,\vec{I}_{\text{reactive}}$ | Complex intensity decomposition | W/m² |
| 10 | $W = \oint_S \vec{I}\cdot d\vec{S}$ | Total radiated sound power via intensity integration | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical board-exam trap:** The relation $L_I = L_p$ holds **ONLY** for a plane progressive wave in a free field. In a reverberant field or near a source, $L_p \neq L_I$ — students often lose marks by not stating this condition.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Automotive NVH

In vehicle noise engineering, sound intensity is the **gold standard for sound power measurement** because:
- It inherently **rejects background noise** from other sources outside the measurement surface.
- It provides **directional information**, enabling engineers to pinpoint which component (engine block, transmission casing, tyre cavity, exhaust manifold) is the dominant radiator.
- Standards such as **ISO 9614-1/2** (sound intensity for sound power) and **SAE J1074** govern automotive source ranking using intensity probes.
- Modern Brüel & Kjær, HEAD acoustics, and Siemens LMS intensity probes are routinely deployed in NVH test cells to rank-order contribution of subassemblies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Instantaneous and Time-Averaged Intensity from First Principles

**Starting point:** The acoustic wave equation in 1-D, with pressure $p(x,t)$ and particle velocity $u(x,t)$ related by Euler's linearised momentum equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1 — Express instantaneous intensity.**
By definition, instantaneous acoustic power per unit area transmitted in the $+x$ direction is:

$$I(x,t) = p(x,t)\cdot u(x,t)$$

*Logic:* Pressure is force/area (N/m²), particle velocity is m/s. Their product has units (N·m/s)/m² = W/m² — dimensionally consistent with power flux.

**Step 2 — Assume a harmonic plane wave travelling in $+x$.**
The acoustic pressure and particle velocity solutions of the wave equation are:

$$p(x,t) = \hat{p}\cos(\omega t - kx)$$

$$u(x,t) = \hat{u}\cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply and simplify.**
For a plane progressive wave, the pressure and particle velocity are **in phase** ($\phi = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I(x,t) &= \hat{p}\hat{u}\cos^2(\omega t - kx) \\
&= \frac{\hat{p}\hat{u}}{2}\bigl[1 + \cos\bigl(2(\omega t - kx)\bigr)\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

The term inside the brackets contains a constant (1) and a rapidly oscillating part at $2\omega$.

**Step 4 — Time average over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.**
The average of $\cos(2\omega t)$ over a full period is zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle I \rangle &= \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{\hat{p}\hat{u}}{2}\bigl[1+\cos(2\omega t)\bigr]\,dt \\
&= \frac{\hat{p}\hat{u}}{2}\cdot 1 + 0 \\
\therefore \quad I_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\hat{p}\,\hat{u}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak to RMS.**
For sinusoidal quantities, $\hat{p} = \sqrt{2}\,p_{\text{rms}}$ and $\hat{u} = \sqrt{2}\,u_{\text{rms}}$, giving:

$$I_{\text{avg}} = p_{\text{rms}}\cdot u_{\text{rms}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — The Plane-Wave Intensity–Pressure Relation $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$

**Step 1 — Plane-wave relation between pressure and particle velocity.**
For a plane wave in a lossless medium, $p = \rho_0 c\, u$, where $c$ is the speed of sound.

**Step 2 — Substitute into $I_{\text{avg}} = p_{\text{rms}}\cdot u_{\text{rms}}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= p_{\text{rms}} \cdot \left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{\rho_0 c}\right) \\
&= \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c}
\end{aligned}$$

Defining the **characteristic acoustic impedance** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$, the master relation is:

$$\boxed{\,I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{Z_0} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Sound Intensity Level (SIL) from Sound Pressure Level

**Given:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_{\text{ref}})$ with $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2\times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa}$.

**Step 1 — Express $I$ in terms of $p_{\text{rms}}^2$.**
From §3.2, $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $I/I_{\text{ref}}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}} &= \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)}{10^{-12}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the plane-wave free-field condition** $I_{\text{ref}} = p_{\text{ref}}^2/(\rho c)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_I &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)}{p_{\text{ref}}^2/(\rho c)}\right) \\
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_{\text{ref}}^2}\right) \\
&= 10\cdot 2\cdot\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \\
&= L_p
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,L_I = L_p \quad \text{(free-field plane wave only)}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A compressor in a hybrid vehicle powertrain generates a sound intensity of $I = 5\times 10^{-6}\,\text{W/m}^2$ at the operator's ear position. Calculate (a) the intensity level $L_I$ in dB, and (b) the equivalent RMS sound pressure assuming free-field plane-wave propagation in air ($\rho_0 c = 413\,\text{Pa·s/m}$).

**Solution (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_I &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5\times 10^{-6}}{10^{-12}}\right) \\
&= 10\log_{10}(5\times 10^{6}) \\
&= 10\,[\log_{10}(5) + 6] \\
&= 10\,[0.6990 + 6] \\
&= 66.99 \approx 67.0\,\text{dB re 1 pW/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{rms}} &= \sqrt{I\cdot \rho_0 c} \\
&= \sqrt{(5\times 10^{-6})(413)} \\
&= \sqrt{2.065\times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 0.04544\,\text{Pa} \\
&\approx 45.4\,\text{mPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via SPL:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(0.04544/2\times 10^{-5}) = 20\log_{10}(2272) \approx 67.1\,\text{dB}$. Matches $L_I$ within rounding. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sound Intensity Measurement Chain

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal-flow architecture of a two-microphone (P-U) intensity probe system, which is the standard hardware used for automotive NVH source ranking.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sound Source\nEngine Block] -->|Acoustic Waves| B[Microphone 1\nMeasures p1 t]
    C[Sound Source\nExhaust Manifold] -->|Acoustic Waves| B
    D[Background Noise\nTest Cell HVAC] -.->|Rejection by Vector Math| B
    A --> E[Microphone 2\nMeasures p2 t]
    C --> E
    D -.->|Rejection by Vector Math| E
    B --> F[Signal Conditioner\nBandpass 20 Hz to 10 kHz]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Cross-Spectral Processor\nFFT Analysis]
    G --> H[Particle Velocity Calculator\nu = Integral of dp dx over spacer]
    B --> I[Pressure p t Channel]
    E --> J[Pressure p t Channel]
    I --> K[Intensity Vector Calculator\nI = p times u time averaged]
    H --> K
    K --> L[Display 1\nIntensity Magnitude W per m2]
    K --> M[Display 2\nIntensity Level dB re 1 pW]
    K --> N[Display 3\nDirectional Map 2D or 3D]
    K --> O[Output\nSound Power W via Surface Integration]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The dashed lines from `D (Background Noise)` indicate that incoherent ambient noise is mathematically rejected by the cross-spectral averaging process — this is the key advantage of intensity over pressure-based measurement.
- `I` and `J` retain the raw pressure signals; `H` derives particle velocity via the finite-difference approximation $u \approx -\frac{1}{\rho_0}\int\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\Delta s}\,dt$, where $\Delta s$ is the microphone spacer distance.

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy — Sound Field Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SF[Sound Field Energy Domain] --> SC[Scalar Quantities]
    SF --> VEC[Vector Quantities]
    SF --> INT[Derived Level Quantities]
    SC --> SP[Sound Pressure p in Pa]
    SC --> PV[Sound Pressure Level Lp in dB]
    VEC --> SI[Sound Intensity I in W per m2]
    VEC --> SV[Particle Velocity u in m per s]
    INT --> SIL[Sound Intensity Level LI in dB]
    INT --> SWL[Sound Power Level Lw in dB]
    SP --> SI
    SV --> SI
    SI --> SIL
    SI --> SWL
    SWL --> SP_OUT[Total Sound Power W\nvia Surface Integration]
```

---
