<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> *Define Sound Intensity. How is it distinguished from Sound Pressure in terms of physical nature?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sound intensity is the time-averaged acoustic power transmitted per unit area in a specified direction through a sound field, with SI unit **W/m²**. It is a **vector quantity** carrying directional information about net energy flow. In contrast, sound pressure is a **scalar quantity** (unit: Pa) that describes only the local magnitude of pressure fluctuation at a point, without indicating energy flow direction. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *State the standard reference value of sound intensity used for computing Sound Intensity Level.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ISO standard reference sound intensity is $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$, corresponding to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. The sound intensity level is then $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$ in decibels. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Sample Paper]*

**(a)** Define sound intensity. Derive the relationship between sound intensity and RMS sound pressure for a plane progressive wave in a free field. State clearly the role of the characteristic acoustic impedance of the medium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC-machined automotive gearbox housing is tested in a semi-anechoic chamber. The time-averaged sound intensity measured normal to a 2 m² surface enclosing the source is $I = 8\times 10^{-5}\,\text{W/m}^2$. Calculate:
(i) The total sound power radiated.
(ii) The sound intensity level in dB re 1 pW/m².
(iii) The equivalent free-field SPL, stating the assumption under which this equivalence holds. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Sound intensity $\vec{I}$ is the time-averaged rate of sound energy flow per unit area in a specified direction:

$$\vec{I} = \overline{p(t)\cdot\vec{u}(t)}$$

It is a vector quantity measured in W/m².

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For a plane progressive wave, $p$ and $u$ are in phase, so the time average of their product yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= p_{\text{rms}}\cdot u_{\text{rms}}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the linearised Euler equation for a plane wave, the pressure–velocity relation is:

$$p = \rho_0 c\, u \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \frac{p}{\rho_0 c}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= p_{\text{rms}}\cdot \frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{\rho_0 c} \\
&= \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Role of characteristic impedance (1 mark):**
The quantity $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ (Rayl) is the characteristic acoustic impedance; it couples pressure and particle velocity in the medium. For air at 20°C, $Z_0 \approx 413\,\text{Pa·s/m}$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Sound power (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= I\cdot S \\
&= (8\times 10^{-5})\cdot 2 \\
&= 1.6\times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} = 160\,\mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Intensity level (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_I &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8\times 10^{-5}}{10^{-12}}\right) \\
&= 10\log_{10}(8\times 10^{7}) \\
&= 10\,[\log_{10}(8) + 7] \\
&= 10\,[0.9031 + 7] \\
&= 79.03\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Equivalent SPL (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{rms}} &= \sqrt{I\cdot\rho c} = \sqrt{(8\times 10^{-5})(413)} \\
&= \sqrt{0.03304} = 0.1818\,\text{Pa} \\
L_p &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1818}{2\times 10^{-5}}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(9090) = 20(3.9586) \\
&= 79.17 \approx 79.2\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Assumption (stated):** Equivalence $L_p = L_I$ holds only under **free-field plane-wave** propagation; reverberant or near-field conditions invalidate it.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a two-microphone (P-U) sound intensity probe. Highlight its advantages over a single-microphone sound pressure measurement for sound power determination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Sound Intensity Level. The sound power of an automotive HVAC blower is measured to be $W = 4\,\text{mW}$. The measurement surface is hemispherical with radius $r = 1.5\,\text{m}$.
(i) Compute the average intensity on this surface.
(ii) Compute the SIL in dB.
(iii) Comment on the directional information that intensity measurement provides which pressure-only measurement cannot. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
A P-U probe uses **two phase-matched microphones** separated by a fixed spacer $\Delta s$. The cross-spectral density $S_{12}(f)$ of the two pressure signals $p_1(t)$ and $p_2(t)$ is used to estimate the pressure gradient $\partial p/\partial x \approx (p_2 - p_1)/\Delta s$, from which particle velocity is recovered via Euler's equation:

$$u(x,t) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0}\int \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,dt$$

Sound intensity follows as $I = \overline{p(t)\cdot u(t)}$ computed in the frequency domain via cross-spectral multiplication.

**Block diagram (refer to SECTION_4 diagram):** Two microphones → conditioning → FFT cross-spectrum → velocity estimator → intensity computer → display.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Background noise rejection:** Incoherent noise from outside the measurement surface cancels out via time averaging; only coherent energy *flowing through* the surface contributes.
2. **Directional information:** Sign of intensity reveals whether a source is *radiating into* or *out of* the surface — vital for separating two adjacent components (e.g., engine vs. transmission).
3. **In-situ sound power:** Sound power $W = \oint_S \vec{I}\cdot d\vec{S}$ is measured directly without needing a fully anechoic chamber.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Sound intensity level $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$, with $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$.

**(i) Average intensity (2 marks):**
Surface area of hemisphere:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 2\pi r^2 = 2\pi(1.5)^2 = 4.5\pi \approx 14.137\,\text{m}^2 \\
I_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{W}{S} = \frac{4\times 10^{-3}}{14.137} \\
&\approx 2.83\times 10^{-4}\,\text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) SIL (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_I &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.83\times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) \\
&= 10\log_{10}(2.83\times 10^{8}) \\
&= 10\,[0.4518 + 8] = 84.52\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Directional comment (2 marks):**
Intensity measurement provides a **vector** with sign and direction, enabling the engineer to determine:
- Whether the blower is acting as a **source** (positive outward intensity) or a **sink/absorber** (negative inward intensity).
- The **angular distribution** of radiated energy, allowing location of dominant lobes (e.g., blower inlet vs. outlet duct ends).
Pressure-only measurements give a scalar SPL that *cannot* distinguish direction, so a quiet zone between two noisy sources looks identical to a quiet zone adjacent to a single source.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in plane-wave intensity:** Many students write $I = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$ by mistakenly leaving in the peak-to-RMS factor. The correct form uses $p_{\text{rms}}$ *directly*, with the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ already absorbed during the $\cos^2$ time-averaging step. **Lose 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Using 20·log for intensity level:** Intensity $\propto p^2$, so the *intensity level* uses **10·log**. Confusing this with the SPL formula costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Stating $L_I = L_p$ without the free-field plane-wave qualifier:** Examiners deduct **1 mark** for omitting the condition.
> 4. **Mixing up the hemisphere area:** A full sphere is $4\pi r^2$; the hemispherical measurement surface is **$2\pi r^2$**, not $\pi r^2$. Errors here cascade through (i), (ii), and (iii).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this page before entering the exam hall.**

- **Definition:** Sound intensity $I = \overline{p(t)\cdot u(t)}$ — vector quantity, units **W/m²**.
- **Reference:** $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$.
- **SIL formula:** $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$ in **dB**.
- **Master relation (plane wave):** $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho_0 c)$.
- **Acoustic impedance of air:** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413\,\text{Pa·s/m}$ at 20°C.
- **Equivalence rule:** $L_I = L_p$ **only** under free-field plane-wave conditions.
- **Why 10 vs. 20?** Intensity uses 10·log because $I \propto p^2$; the squared exponent multiplies the log by 2.
- **Vector advantage:** Intensity gives both magnitude and direction of net energy flow; pressure does not.
- **Active vs. Reactive intensity:** Active (real part) = radiated/transported energy; Reactive (imaginary part) = locally oscillating stored energy.
- **Sound power via intensity:** $W = \oint_S \vec{I}\cdot d\vec{S}$ — direct in-situ measurement, no anechoic chamber needed.
- **Hardware:** Two-microphone P-U probe with cross-spectral FFT processing; spacer $\Delta s$ typically 6 mm, 12 mm, or 50 mm depending on frequency range.
- **Automotive applications:** Engine block, transmission casing, exhaust manifold, tyre-cavity noise source ranking; standards ISO 9614, SAE J1074.
- **Background rejection:** Incoherent noise outside the measurement surface cancels in the cross-spectral average — the killer advantage over SPL-based sound power.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** (1) Wrong factor of 2 in plane-wave intensity; (2) 10 vs. 20 confusion; (3) omitting free-field qualifier; (4) hemisphere area = $2\pi r^2$, not $4\pi r^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sound Power ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sound Power ($W$)** is defined as the total acoustic energy radiated by a sound source per unit time, in all directions, irrespective of the surrounding environment. It is an intrinsic property of the source — meaning it is independent of the distance from the source, the room acoustics, or the listener's position. The SI unit of sound power is the **Watt (W)**, and it is the most objective descriptor of how "noisy" a machine or component truly is. Mathematically, sound power is expressed as: $$ W = \frac{dE_{acoustic}}{dt} $$ where $dE_{acoustic}$ is the acoustic energy radiated in time $dt$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams** > Sound Power is a property of the **SOURCE**, whereas Sound Pressure and Sound Intensity are properties of the **SOUND FIELD** at a given location. This distinction is the single most commonly tested concept in NVH theory papers. ### 1.2 The Sound Power Hierarchy In acoustics, three measurable quantities form a hierarchy: | Quantity | Symbol | Depends on | Unit | |----------|--------|------------|------| | Sound Pressure | $p$ | Field & distance | Pascal (Pa) | | Sound Intensity | $I$ | Field & distance | W/m² | | **Sound Power** | $W$ | **Source only** | **Watt (W)** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Light Bulb Imagine a light bulb in a dark room: - The **wattage rating** of the bulb (e.g., 60 W) is an intrinsic property — it tells you how much energy the bulb radiates regardless of where you stand. - The **illumination at your eye** (in lux) depends on how far you are from the bulb, the room's reflectiveness, etc. **Sound Power is to a sound source exactly what wattage is to a light bulb.** A turbocharger, engine, or exhaust system has a fixed sound power — but the sound pressure you measure with a microphone depends on where you place it. This is why automotive OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) specifications are quoted in **sound power level (dB re 1 pW)**, not sound pressure level. ### 1.4 Reference Value The standard reference sound power is defined as: $$ W_0 = 10^{-12} \text{ W} = 1 \text{ picowatt} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination), always remember: the reference value for **sound power level** is $10^{-12}$ W, NOT $10^{-12}$ Pa (which is for pressure) and NOT $10^{-12}$ W/m² (which is for intensity). Mixing these up is one of the top scoring-loss mistakes. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Origin of Sound Power When a structure (engine block, gearbox housing, tire, exhaust muffler) vibrates, it does work on the surrounding air particles. This work is transmitted outward as pressure fluctuations — i.e., sound waves. The **time-averaged rate at which this work is done** is the sound power. For a vibrating surface of area $S$ with a normal surface velocity $u_n$ and a surface sound pressure $p_s$: $$ W = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \oint_S p_s(t) \, u_n(t) \, dS \, dt $$ For a **monopole (point) source** in a free field, this collapses to a beautifully simple relation involving sound intensity. ### 2.2 Relationship Between Sound Power, Intensity, and Pressure #### (a) Sound Power ↔ Intensity For a **closed surface $S$** completely enclosing the source in a free field: $$ W = \oint_S I \, dS $$ For a spherical wave radiating uniformly (ideal point source): $$ W = 4\pi r^2 \cdot I $$ This tells us: **intensity decreases with the square of distance ($1/r^2$)**, while power remains constant. #### (b) Sound Power ↔ Pressure (Free Field) In a free progressive (far) field, sound intensity and mean-square pressure are related by the characteristic acoustic impedance of air $\rho c$: $$ I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} $$ For air at 20 °C: $\rho c \approx 413 \text{ N·s/m³}$ (often rounded to **400 N·s/m³** in KTU problems). Combining: $$ W = \frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} $$ ### 2.3 Sound Power Level ($L_W$) Since sound power in Watts spans an enormous range (from $10^{-12}$ W for a quiet watch to $10^{3}$ W for a jet engine on takeoff), it is almost always expressed in **decibels** as the **Sound Power Level**: $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \text{ dB re } 10^{-12} \text{ W} $$ where $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W is the reference sound power. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > This is the consolidated reference table — memorize it before the ESE. Question banks typically expect direct application of these. | # | Formula | Description | Common Use | |---|---------|-------------|------------| | 1 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level in dB | Universal conversion | | 2 | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | Reference sound power | Always quoted | | 3 | $W = 4\pi r^2 I$ | Power from intensity (point source) | Free-field problems | | 4 | $W = 4\pi r^2 p_{rms}^2 / \rho c$ | Power from pressure (point source) | Free-field problems | | 5 | $\rho c \approx 413$ N·s/m³ | Specific acoustic impedance of air | At 20 °C | | 6 | $I = p_{rms}^2 / \rho c$ | Intensity from pressure (free field) | Derivation step | | 7 | $L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(4\pi r^2)$ | Convert intensity level to power level | Measurement link | | 8 | $L_I = L_p - 10 \log_{10}(\rho c / I_0)$ | Intensity level from pressure level | Field calculations | | 9 | $W = \bar{I} \cdot S$ | Power = avg. intensity × enclosing surface area | General enclosure | | 10 | $L_{W,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\sum 10^{L_{W,i}/10}$ | Total power of incoherent sources | Multi-source addition | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Automotive NVH - **Homologation (Type-Approval)**: European regulation UN/ECE R51 and ISO 362-1 require vehicle pass-by noise to be expressed as **Sound Power Level**, not pressure level. This ensures fair comparison between vehicles tested in different environments. - **Component Benchmarking**: A turbocharger's sound power can be compared on a bench across manufacturers without worrying about test-cell acoustics. - **Source Ranking**: NVH engineers rank subsystems (engine, exhaust, tires, transmission) by their A-weighted sound power contribution to set development priorities. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Acoustic Pressure to Sound Power of a Point Source **Given:** A point (monopole) source in a free field radiating spherical waves. At distance $r$, the time-averaged sound intensity is $I$ and the rms sound pressure is $p_{rms}$. **To derive:** The expression for total radiated sound power $W$. --- **Step 1 — Set up the integrating surface.** For a point source, choose a sphere of radius $r$ centered on the source. The total power crossing this closed spherical surface equals the total radiated power. $$ W = \oint_S I \, dS $$ **Step 2 — Use spherical symmetry.** Because of symmetry, the intensity $I$ is constant over the sphere. Therefore: $$ W = I \cdot \oint_S dS = I \cdot (4\pi r^2) $$ This yields the key relation: $$ W = 4\pi r^2 \cdot I \quad \text{...(Eq. A)} $$ **Step 3 — Introduce the free-field relation between intensity and pressure.** For a plane or spherical progressive wave in air: $$ I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} $$ This comes from the definition of acoustic intensity $I = \overline{p \cdot u}$ and using the plane-wave relation $p = \rho c \, u$. **Step 4 — Substitute into Eq. A.** $$ W = \frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to Sound Power Level (dB).** Take both sides over the reference $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W: $$ \frac{W}{W_0} = \frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot p_{rms}^2}{\rho c \, W_0} $$ Take the base-10 logarithm and multiply by 10: $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot p_{rms}^2}{\rho c \, W_0}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Split the logarithm (high-yield step).** $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}^2}{p_{ref}^2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi r^2 \, p_{ref}^2}{\rho c \, W_0}\right) $$ where $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$. The first term is just the **Sound Pressure Level** $L_p$: $$ L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi r^2}{\rho c} \cdot p_{ref}^2 \cdot \frac{1}{W_0}\right) $$ Plugging the standard values gives the compact working equation: $$ L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(r) + 10.9 \quad \text{(dB, for } r \text{ in meters)} $$ The constant 10.9 dB absorbs $\rho c$, $p_{ref}^2$, and $W_0$ for a hemispherical radiator — often rounded to **11 dB** in KTU problems. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example > **Problem:** A small electric motor, treated as a point source on an acoustically hard floor, produces a sound pressure level of $L_p = 74$ dB re 20 μPa at a distance of $r = 1$ m. Compute the radiated sound power level. Assume $\rho c = 413$ N·s/m³. **Solution:** Using the free-field point-source relation: $$ W = \frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} $$ **Step A:** Convert $L_p$ to $p_{rms}$. $$ p_{rms} = p_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_p/20} = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 10^{74/20} $$ $$ p_{rms} = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 10^{3.7} = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 5011.87 $$ $$ p_{rms} \approx 0.1002 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step B:** Compute $W$. $$ W = \frac{4\pi (1)^2 (0.1002)^2}{413} $$ $$ W = \frac{4\pi \times 0.01005}{413} $$ $$ W = \frac{0.1262}{413} \approx 3.057 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} $$ **Step C:** Compute $L_W$. $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3.057 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) $$ $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}(3.057 \times 10^{8}) $$ $$ L_W = 10 \,[\log_{10}(3.057) + 8] = 10 \,[0.4854 + 8] $$ $$ \boxed{L_W \approx 84.85 \text{ dB re } 10^{-12} \text{ W}} $$ **Interpretation:** The motor radiates acoustic power equivalent to ~85 dB sound power level, which classifies it as a moderately noisy component in the automotive auxiliaries category. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Aspect | Sound Power ($W$) | Sound Intensity ($I$) | Sound Pressure ($p$) | |--------|-------------------|------------------------|-----------------------| | Definition | Energy/time | Power/area | Pressure fluctuation | | Depends on | Source only | Field & direction | Field & position | | Unit | W | W/m² | Pa | | Reference | $10^{-12}$ W | $10^{-12}$ W/m² | $20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa | | Doubling of source | $W \rightarrow 2W$ (Lw + 3 dB) | Doubles at all points | $\sqrt{2} \times p$ (Lp + 3 dB) | | Distance law | Constant | $\propto 1/r^2$ | $\propto 1/r$ | | Best for | Source rating | Energy flow direction | Perceived loudness | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Sound Power Measurement Methodology Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Acoustic Source
(Engine, Tyre, Motor)"] --> B["Sound Power
Emission W (W)"] B --> C["Free-Field
Method
ISO 3745"] B --> D["Reverberation
Room Method
ISO 3741"] B --> E["Semi-Reverberant
Method
ISO 3743"] B --> F["Substitution
Method
ISO 3747"] C --> G["Surface SPL
Scan + Integrate"] D --> H["Sabine Formula
Computation"] E --> I["Standard
Test Room"] F --> J["Reference Source
Calibration"] G --> K["Sound Power
Level Lw in dB"] H --> K I --> K J --> K K --> L["NVH Reports
and OEM Specs"] ``` ### 4.2 Sound Power Hierarchy and Field Relationships ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["Source
(Intrinsic Property)"] -->|Radiates| PWR["Sound Power W (W)
Constant with distance"] PWR -->|Spread over sphere| INT["Sound Intensity I (W/m2)
Drops as 1 / r squared"] INT -->|Measured as| PRS["Sound Pressure p (Pa)
Drops as 1 / r"] PWR -->|"Lw = 10 log10 W / W0"| LWP["Lw in dB"] INT -->|"Li = 10 log10 I / I0"| LIP["Li in dB"] PRS -->|"Lp = 20 log10 p / p_ref"| LPP["Lp in dB"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: From Source to Reported Sound Power Level ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph IN["INPUT DOMAIN"] I1["Mechanical Vibration of Source"] I2["Reference Acoustic Impedance rho c"] end subgraph PROC["COMPUTATION DOMAIN"] P1["Calculate rms Pressure p_rms"] P2["Apply Free Field Relation p_rms squared / rho c"] P3["Multiply by Enclosing Area S = 4 pi r squared"] P4["Form Ratio W / W zero"] P5["Apply 10 log10"] end subgraph OUT["OUTPUT DOMAIN"] O1["Sound Power W in Watts"] O2["Sound Power Level Lw in dB re 10 to power minus 12 W"] O3["Automotive Pass-By Noise Report"] end I1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 I2 --> P2 P3 --> O1 P5 --> O2 O1 --> O3 O2 --> O3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **"Define Sound Power. Why is it considered an intrinsic property of the source?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sound Power ($W$) is the total acoustic energy radiated by a source per unit time, measured in **Watts (W)**. Mathematically, $$ W = \frac{dE_{acoustic}}{dt} $$ It is considered an **intrinsic property** of the source because it depends only on the source's physical vibration characteristics (force, velocity, surface area) and **not** on the distance from the source, the acoustic environment, or the listener's position. This makes it the most objective descriptor of a source's noise output, ideal for OEM specifications and homologation. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for explanation of "intrinsic")* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **"State the reference value used for Sound Power Level and write its formula. Mention the SI unit."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Sound Power Level ($L_W$) is given by: $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \text{ dB} $$ The reference sound power is $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W (1 picowatt), and $L_W$ is expressed in **decibels (dB) re 10⁻¹² W**. *(3 marks: 1 each for formula, reference value, and unit)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between Sound Power and Sound Intensity for a point source in a free field. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A point source placed on a rigid floor (hemispherical radiation) gives an intensity level of $L_I = 80$ dB re 10⁻¹² W/m² at 2 m. Calculate the Sound Power Level radiated. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** For a point source in a free field, the sound radiates spherically. At any distance $r$, the intensity $I$ is constant over the spherical surface of area $4\pi r^2$. Total power crossing the sphere: $$ W = \oint_S I \, dS = I \cdot 4\pi r^2 $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{W = 4\pi r^2 \cdot I} $$ Taking logarithms (base-10) and multiplying by 10: $$ L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(4\pi r^2) $$ For a point source on a rigid floor, the lower hemisphere is reflected, effectively doubling the intensity at the measurement surface. The integrating surface becomes a hemisphere of area $2\pi r^2$: $$ W = 2\pi r^2 \cdot I \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(2\pi r^2) $$ *(Mark split: 2 marks setup, 2 marks spherical integration, 2 marks final relation, 1 mark for logarithmic form)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $L_I = 80$ dB re 10⁻¹² W/m², $r = 2$ m, hemispherical radiation. **Step 1:** Convert intensity level to intensity: $$ I = I_0 \cdot 10^{L_I/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{80/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{8} = 10^{-4} \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 2:** Apply hemispherical formula: $$ W = 2\pi r^2 \cdot I = 2\pi (2)^2 \cdot (10^{-4}) $$ $$ W = 2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot 10^{-4} = 8\pi \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ W \approx 25.13 \times 10^{-4} = 2.513 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} $$ **Step 3:** Compute $L_W$: $$ L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.513 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.513 \times 10^{9}) $$ $$ L_W = 10 [\log_{10}(2.513) + 9] = 10 [0.4005 + 9] = 94.0 \text{ dB} $$ **Alternative quick path using the decibel form:** $$ L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(2\pi r^2) = 80 + 10 \log_{10}(2\pi \cdot 4) $$ $$ L_W = 80 + 10 \log_{10}(25.13) = 80 + 10(1.4003) = 94.0 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{L_W \approx 94.0 \text{ dB re } 10^{-12} \text{ W}} $$ *(Mark split: 2 marks for unit conversion, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for final answer + units)* --- **Q3(b). [Alternative Question – OR Choice]** **(a)** Explain the difference between Sound Power, Sound Intensity, and Sound Pressure. State their units and reference values. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The Sound Power Level of an IC engine at rated speed is 102 dB re 10⁻¹² W. If two identical engines run simultaneously and their sound fields are incoherent, what is the combined Sound Power Level? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Explanation (7 marks):** | Quantity | Definition | Unit | Reference | |----------|------------|------|-----------| | Sound Power ($W$) | Total acoustic energy radiated per unit time, intrinsic to source | **Watt (W)** | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | | Sound Intensity ($I$) | Sound power per unit area transmitted in a specified direction | **W/m²** | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | | Sound Pressure ($p$) | Fluctuating pressure superimposed on atmospheric pressure | **Pascal (Pa)** | $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu$Pa | - **Sound Power** is a scalar, source-only property — independent of position. - **Sound Intensity** is a vector quantity — it has direction (energy flow). - **Sound Pressure** is a scalar field quantity — what microphones actually measure. In automotive NVH, sound power is preferred for source ranking, intensity for directional energy mapping (e.g., beamforming), and pressure for SPL-based drive-by noise. *(Mark split: 3 marks for definitions + table, 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for automotive context)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $L_W = 102$ dB for each engine, two incoherent sources. **Step 1:** Convert to absolute power: $$ W = W_0 \cdot 10^{L_W/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{102/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{10.2} $$ $$ W = 10^{-12} \cdot 1.585 \times 10^{10} = 1.585 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} $$ **Step 2:** Sum powers (incoherent sources add as powers): $$ W_{total} = 2 \times 1.585 \times 10^{-2} = 3.170 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} $$ **Step 3:** Compute combined $L_W$: $$ L_{W,total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3.170 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3.170 \times 10^{10}) $$ $$ L_{W,total} = 10 [\log_{10}(3.170) + 10] = 10[0.5011 + 10] $$ $$ \boxed{L_{W,total} \approx 105.0 \text{ dB re } 10^{-12} \text{ W}} $$ **Quick-check:** For $N$ identical incoherent sources, $L_{W,total} = L_W + 10\log_{10}(N)$. With $N = 2$: $102 + 10 \log_{10}(2) = 102 + 3.01 \approx 105$ dB. ✓ *(Mark split: 2 marks conversion, 2 marks summation logic, 2 marks final decibel calc, 1 mark verification)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hotspots** > 1. **Reference confusion:** Writing $L_W = 20 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ instead of 10 log — that's the **power vs. field-quantity** trap. **Power** uses 10, **pressure/intensity-via-pressure** uses 20. > 2. **Spherical vs. hemispherical:** A source on a rigid floor uses **half-sphere** ($2\pi r^2$); a free-standing source uses **full sphere** ($4\pi r^2$). Missing this costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Incoherent vs. coherent addition:** Sound powers (or intensities) of **uncorrelated** sources add in power domain (10 log). Correlated phases need phasor addition — usually not asked at KTU level, but be aware. > 4. **Forgetting units:** Always quote the answer as "dB re 10⁻¹² W" — examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sound Power ($W$)**: Total acoustic energy per unit time, in **Watts**, intrinsic to the source. - **Sound Power Level ($L_W$)**: Logarithmic form, $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$, with $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W. - **Free-field spherical relation**: $W = 4\pi r^2 I$; hemispherical: $W = 2\pi r^2 I$. - **Pressure-power link (free field)**: $W = 4\pi r^2 p_{rms}^2 / \rho c$, with $\rho c \approx 413$ N·s/m³ for air at 20 °C. - **Key hierarchy**: Power (source) → Intensity (field) → Pressure (field measurement). - **Inverse-square law**: Intensity decays as $1/r^2$, pressure as $1/r$, but **power is invariant**. - **Multi-source addition**: Total $L_W = 10\log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_{W,i}/10})$ for incoherent sources. - **Standards reference**: ISO 3740-series (sound power measurement standards); UN/ECE R51 (vehicle pass-by noise uses $L_W$). - **Automotive application**: OEM component-level noise targets are specified as **Sound Power Level** because they are environment-independent. - **Rule of thumb**: For a hemispherical point source, $L_W \approx L_p + 10\log_{10}(r) + 11$ (in dB, with $r$ in metres). - **Trap to avoid**: Do **not** confuse the reference value $10^{-12}$ W (power) with $20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa (pressure) — both small numbers, very different physical meanings.

topics.title: Inhomogeneous Wave Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARMONESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inhomogeneous Wave Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Inhomogeneous Wave Equation** (also called the **Forced Wave Equation** or **Wave Equation with Source Term**) is a second-order partial differential equation that describes the propagation of waves through a medium when an **external excitation**, **source distribution**, or **forcing function** is present in the domain.

In its canonical form (3-D acoustic pressure version, used extensively in automotive NVH for cabin acoustic analysis, road noise modeling, and powertrain radiation):

$$\nabla^{2} p(\mathbf{r}, t) - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p(\mathbf{r}, t)}{\partial t^{2}} = -S(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

where the right-hand side **S(r, t)** is the **source term** that makes the equation *inhomogeneous* (non-zero RHS). For the homogeneous case, **S(r, t) = 0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Whenever a vehicle component is *driven* by a mechanical force, *excited* by a sound source, or *energized* by a turbulent flow, the governing PDE becomes the **Inhomogeneous Wave Equation**. The source term mathematically captures the physical injection of energy into the system.

## 1.2 General Forms of the Inhomogeneous Wave Equation

| Variant | Equation | Physical Context |
|---|---|---|
| **1-D String with forcing** | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial t^{2}} - c^{2}\dfrac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{F(x,t)}{\mu}$ | Transverse string vibration under external force |
| **3-D Acoustic (pressure)** | $\nabla^{2} p - \dfrac{1}{c^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = -\rho_{0}\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}$ | Sound radiation from a mass source $q$ |
| **3-D Acoustic (Helmholtz)** | $\nabla^{2} P + k^{2} P = -j\omega \rho_{0} Q$ | Time-harmonic acoustic radiation |
| **Elastic solid** | $\rho\dfrac{\partial^{2} \mathbf{u}}{\partial t^{2}} - \mu\nabla^{2}\mathbf{u} - (\lambda+\mu)\nabla(\nabla\cdot\mathbf{u}) = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r},t)$ | Structural body under body-force distribution |
| **Membrane/plate** | $\nabla^{4} w + \dfrac{\rho h}{D}\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial t^{2}} = \dfrac{q(x,y,t)}{D}$ | Plate vibration under distributed load |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Drum with a Drummer":** Imagine a still pond (homogeneous case: the wave equation has zero RHS, so waves only form if the pond is already disturbed). Now drop a stone (source term!) — ripples spread outward, and the **forcing function** continuously feeds energy into the medium. The *inhomogeneous* term tells us "**where** and **how strongly** the disturbance is being injected." In an automobile, the **engine** is the "drummer," the **vehicle body** is the "pond," and the cabin acoustic pressure is the "ripple pattern" we measure with microphones.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **c** = speed of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** at 20 °C (used in cabin acoustics)
- **ρ₀** = mean air density ≈ **1.204 kg/m³** at 20 °C
- **k = ω/c** = acoustic wavenumber (rad/m)
- **λ, μ** = Lamé constants for elastodynamic problems
- **D** = flexural rigidity of a plate (Nm)
- **μ** = linear mass density of a string (kg/m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why "Inhomogeneous"? — The Mathematical Origin

A PDE is called **homogeneous** if every term contains the dependent variable or its derivatives. The moment an **independent, non-derivative source term** appears on the right-hand side, the PDE becomes **inhomogeneous**.

Homogeneous wave equation:

$$\nabla^{2} p - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = 0$$

Inhomogeneous wave equation (same LHS, with a *forcing function*):

$$\nabla^{2} p - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = f(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

The **superposition principle** no longer applies directly to the solution; instead, we must solve the **complete** equation, typically by:

1. **Particular integral method** (find any $p_p$ satisfying the equation).
2. **Complementary function** (solve the homogeneous version and add $p_h$).
3. **Green's function method** (impulse response → convolution with source).
4. **Separation of variables with non-homogeneous BCs.**

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In automotive NVH, the inhomogeneous wave equation is the *governing PDE* for BEM (Boundary Element Method) cabin acoustic simulations, where the source is the vibrating body panels and the field point is inside the cabin. Every commercial NVH tool (LMS Virtual.Lab, Actran, COMSOL Acoustics) solves this equation routinely.

## 2.2 Structure of the Solution

The total acoustic field can be decomposed as:

$$p(\mathbf{r}, t) = p_{\text{hom}}(\mathbf{r}, t) + p_{\text{part}}(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

- $p_{\text{hom}}$: solution of $\nabla^{2} p_{\text{hom}} - \dfrac{1}{c^{2}} \dfrac{\partial^{2} p_{\text{hom}}}{\partial t^{2}} = 0$ (free wave, depends on initial/BC).
- $p_{\text{part}}$: any particular solution that satisfies the inhomogeneous equation.

## 2.3 Time-Harmonic Specialization (Helmholtz Inhomogeneous Equation)

For sinusoidal sources $S(\mathbf{r},t) = S(\mathbf{r})\,e^{j\omega t}$, substitute $p(\mathbf{r},t) = P(\mathbf{r})e^{j\omega t}$:

$$\nabla^{2} P(\mathbf{r}) + k^{2} P(\mathbf{r}) = -S(\mathbf{r})$$

with $k = \omega/c$. This is the **Inhomogeneous Helmholtz Equation** — the workhorse of steady-state acoustic and structural-acoustic analysis in KTU Module 1.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla^{2} p - \dfrac{1}{c^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = -S(\mathbf{r},t)$ | 3-D inhomogeneous acoustic wave equation | Pa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial t^{2}} - c^{2}\dfrac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{F(x,t)}{\mu}$ | 1-D forced string equation | m |
| 3 | $\nabla^{2} P + k^{2} P = -S(\mathbf{r})$ | Inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation (frequency domain) | Pa |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c}$ | Acoustic wavenumber | rad/m |
| 5 | $\lambda = c\,T = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Acoustic wavelength | m |
| 6 | $P(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{V} G(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}^{\prime})\,S(\mathbf{r}^{\prime})\,dV^{\prime}$ | Green's function integral solution | Pa |
| 7 | $G_{3D}(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}^{\prime}) = \dfrac{e^{-j k R}}{4\pi R}$ | Free-space 3-D Green's function | 1/m |
| 8 | $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^{\prime} \vert$ | Source-to-field-point distance | m |
| 9 | $\nabla^{2} G + k^{2} G = -\delta(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}^{\prime})$ | Green's function governing equation | 1/m³ |
| 10 | $p(\mathbf{r},t) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi}\int_{V}\dfrac{S(\mathbf{r}^{\prime},t - R/c)}{R}\,dV^{\prime}$ | Retarded-potential (3-D) solution | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> In all entries above, the notation $\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^{\prime} \vert$ has been written *without* the vertical bar inside markdown tables. For exams, students are advised to write it as $|\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}'|$ on paper.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Automotive NVH

| Application | Source Term |
|---|---|
| Engine NVH | Combustion pressure pulse exciting cylinder block |
| Road noise | Tire-pavement interaction force on suspension |
| Wind noise | Turbulent pressure fluctuations on A-pillar/mirrors |
| Electric vehicle whine | Electromagnetic force from motor on stator |
| HVAC noise | Fan-induced pressure field in duct |
| Brake squeal | Friction-induced vibration at rotor-pad interface |

All of these are modeled mathematically by **forcing functions** that drive the inhomogeneous wave equation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the 3-D Inhomogeneous Acoustic Wave Equation

The acoustic wave equation with sources is derived from the **continuity equation** and the **linearized momentum (Euler) equation** in fluid mechanics, augmented with a **mass source term**.

### Step 1 — Continuity Equation (with mass injection rate $q_m$, kg/(m³·s))

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = \rho_{0}\, q_{m}(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

**Conversion logic:** Mass per unit volume changes due to net outflow plus injected mass from sources (e.g., a pulsating sphere, an air-injection valve, a vibrating surface).

### Step 2 — Linearize about a quiescent mean state

Let $\rho = \rho_{0} + \rho^{\prime}$, $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} + \mathbf{v}^{\prime}$, $p = p_{0} + p^{\prime}$. For small perturbations and dropping second-order products:

$$\frac{\partial \rho^{\prime}}{\partial t} + \rho_{0}\,\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}^{\prime} = \rho_{0}\, q_{m}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute adiabatic relation $\rho^{\prime} = \dfrac{p^{\prime}}{c^{2}}$

(Valid for acoustic perturbations in air.)

$$\frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial p^{\prime}}{\partial t} + \rho_{0}\,\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}^{\prime} = \rho_{0}\, q_{m}$$

### Step 4 — Linearized Euler's momentum equation

$$\rho_{0}\,\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}^{\prime}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p^{\prime} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}^{\prime}}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{\rho_{0}}\nabla p^{\prime}$$

**Conversion logic:** Newton's second law for fluid parcels; pressure gradient is the only restoring force in linear acoustics.

### Step 5 — Take divergence of the momentum equation

$$\nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}^{\prime}}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \nabla^{2} p^{\prime}$$

Interchanging $\nabla$ and $\partial/\partial t$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}^{\prime}\bigr) = -\frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \nabla^{2} p^{\prime}$$

### Step 6 — Differentiate Step-3 equation with respect to time

$$\frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p^{\prime}}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}^{\prime}\bigr) = \rho_{0} \frac{\partial q_{m}}{\partial t}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute Step-5 result into Step-6

$$\frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p^{\prime}}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \left( -\frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \nabla^{2} p^{\prime} \right) = \rho_{0} \frac{\partial q_{m}}{\partial t}$$

### Step 8 — Rearrange (multiply by $-1$ to standard form)

$$\nabla^{2} p^{\prime} - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p^{\prime}}{\partial t^{2}} = -\rho_{0} \frac{\partial q_{m}}{\partial t}$$

Dropping the prime and identifying the source term:

$$\boxed{\;\nabla^{2} p - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = -\rho_{0} \frac{\partial q_{m}}{\partial t}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The forcing function on the RHS is **$\rho_{0} \partial q_{m}/\partial t$**. The minus sign is a notational convention; some textbooks absorb the negative sign into the source definition.

## 3.2 Derivation of Green's Function Solution (3-D Free Space)

### Step 1 — Define the Green's function $G(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}^{\prime})$ as the field produced by a **unit point source**:

$$\nabla^{2} G + k^{2} G = -\delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^{\prime})$$

(Here we have used the time-harmonic form, so $G$ depends on $e^{j\omega t}$ implicitly.)

### Step 2 — Use spherical symmetry by placing the source at the origin. Let $R = \vert \mathbf{r} \vert$ (distance from source to field point). Then $G = G(R)$ and:

$$\nabla^{2} G = \frac{1}{R^{2}} \frac{d}{dR}\!\left( R^{2}\, \frac{dG}{dR} \right)$$

### Step 3 — For $R \neq 0$, $\delta = 0$, so:

$$\frac{1}{R^{2}} \frac{d}{dR}\!\left( R^{2}\, \frac{dG}{dR} \right) + k^{2} G = 0$$

Multiply by $R^{2}$:

$$\frac{d^{2}(RG)}{dR^{2}} + k^{2}(RG) = 0$$

### Step 4 — This is a simple harmonic ODE in $R$ with general solution:

$$RG(R) = A\,e^{-j k R} + B\,e^{+j k R}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the **Sommerfeld radiation condition** (outgoing wave only, since energy must radiate away from the source), forcing $B = 0$:

$$G(R) = \frac{A\,e^{-j k R}}{R}$$

### Step 6 — Determine $A$ by integrating both sides of the Green's equation over a small sphere of radius $\varepsilon$ around the source:

$$\int_{V_{\varepsilon}} \nabla^{2} G\, dV + k^{2} \int_{V_{\varepsilon}} G\, dV = -\int_{V_{\varepsilon}} \delta(\mathbf{r})\, dV = -1$$

As $\varepsilon \to 0$, the second integral vanishes (the integrand $\sim 1/\varepsilon$ over a volume $\sim \varepsilon^{3}$), and by the divergence theorem:

$$\oint_{S_{\varepsilon}} \nabla G \cdot d\mathbf{S} = -1$$

Computing $\nabla G$ on the sphere $\partial R/\partial n = dG/dR$:

$$\oint_{S_{\varepsilon}} \frac{dG}{dR}\, dS = 4\pi \varepsilon^{2} \cdot \frac{dG}{dR}\bigg|_{R=\varepsilon} = -1$$

### Step 7 — Differentiate $G(R) = A e^{-j k R}/R$:

$$\frac{dG}{dR} = A\left( -\frac{e^{-j k R}}{R^{2}} - \frac{j k\, e^{-j k R}}{R} \right)$$

At $R = \varepsilon \to 0$, only the first term dominates:

$$\frac{dG}{dR}\bigg|_{R=\varepsilon} \approx -\frac{A}{\varepsilon^{2}}$$

### Step 8 — Substitute back:

$$4\pi \varepsilon^{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{A}{\varepsilon^{2}} \right) = -1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -4\pi A = -1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

### Step 9 — Final result — the **3-D free-space Green's function**:

$$\boxed{\;G_{3D}(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}^{\prime}) = \frac{e^{-j k \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^{\prime} \vert}}{4\pi \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^{\prime} \vert}\;}$$

### Step 10 — Acoustic pressure field via superposition

For a distributed source $S(\mathbf{r}^{\prime})$ in volume $V$:

$$P(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{V} G(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}^{\prime})\, S(\mathbf{r}^{\prime})\, dV^{\prime} = \int_{V} \frac{e^{-j k R}}{4\pi R}\, S(\mathbf{r}^{\prime})\, dV^{\prime}, \quad R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^{\prime} \vert$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Pulsating Sphere in Air (Automotive Analogue)

A sphere of radius $a$ pulsates with radial velocity $v_{s} = U_{0} e^{j\omega t}$. Find the radiated pressure field.

### Step 1 — Equivalent volume velocity source
The source strength (volume flux) is $Q = 4\pi a^{2} U_{0} e^{j\omega t}$. For an omnidirectional monopole, the source distribution can be replaced by an equivalent point source at the center with $Q$ replaced by its volume-velocity equivalent.

### Step 2 — Apply the Green's function result with the field point at distance $r$ from the sphere center:

$$P(r) = j\omega \rho_{0}\, \frac{Q}{4\pi r} \, e^{-j k r} = j\omega \rho_{0}\, \frac{4\pi a^{2} U_{0}}{4\pi r} \, e^{-j k r} = \frac{j\omega \rho_{0} a^{2} U_{0}}{r} e^{-j k r}$$

### Step 3 — Acoustic intensity (used in cabin-noise dashboards):

$$I(r) = \frac{\vert P(r) \vert^{2}}{2\rho_{0} c} = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} a^{4} U_{0}^{2}}{2 r^{2} c}$$

> [!TIP]
> This result — pressure $\propto 1/r$ and intensity $\propto 1/r^{2}$ — is the **monopole radiation law**, fundamental for tire-cavity resonance, HVAC blower noise, and subwoofer characterization in automotive audio.

## 3.4 Boundary Conditions Catalogue (for KTU problem-solving)

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet (essential)** | $p = 0$ on $\Gamma$ | Rigid (sound-soft) wall — typical for sealed cabin walls |
| **Neumann (natural)** | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} = 0$ on $\Gamma$ | Sound-hard (rigid, massless) wall |
| **Impedance (Robin)** | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} + jk \beta\, p = 0$ | Locally reacting absorbing surface (headliner, glass wool) |
| **Mixed/Continuity** | $p_{1} = p_{2}$, $\;\dfrac{1}{\rho_{1}}\dfrac{\partial p_{1}}{\partial n} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{2}}\dfrac{\partial p_{2}}{\partial n}$ | Fluid–fluid or fluid–structure interface |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solving the Inhomogeneous Wave Equation in NVH Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the engineering workflow used in commercial NVH solvers when handling the inhomogeneous wave equation. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter (per Mermaid safeguard rules); labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[NVH SOURCE INPUT] --> A2[COMBUSTION FORCE]
    A1 --> A3[TIRE PATCH EXCITATION]
    A1 --> A4[TURBULENT WALL PRESSURE]
    A2 --> B1[FORM FORCING FUNCTION S R T]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[WRITE INHOMOGENEOUS WAVE EQUATION]
    C1 --> D1[3D TIME DOMAIN LAPLACIAN OF P]
    C1 --> D2[SECOND TIME DERIVATIVE TERM]
    C1 --> D3[SOURCE TERM RHO NAUGHT DQ BY DT]
    D1 --> E1[CHOOSE SOLVER STRATEGY]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[FEM STRUCTURAL SOLVER]
    E1 --> F2[BEM ACOUSTIC SOLVER]
    E1 --> F3[SEA STATISTICAL METHOD]
    F1 --> G1[DISPLACEMENT FIELD U]
    F2 --> G2[GREEN FUNCTION CONVOLUTION]
    F3 --> G3[POWER BALANCE]
    G1 --> H1[APPLY BC RIGID WALL OR IMPEDANCE]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[SUM HOMOGENEOUS AND PARTICULAR PARTS]
    I1 --> J1[POST PROCESSING SPL AND FREQ RESPONSE]
    J1 --> K1[VALIDATION WITH MEASUREMENT]
    K1 --> L1[DESIGN ITERATION]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Interpretation Table

| Block | Meaning |
|---|---|
| NVH SOURCE INPUT | Engine, road, wind, brake, etc. |
| FORM FORCING FUNCTION S R T | Mathematical $S(\mathbf{r},t)$ assembled from sources |
| INHOMOGENEOUS WAVE EQUATION | $\nabla^{2} p - \dfrac{1}{c^{2}} \dfrac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = -S$ |
| CHOOSE SOLVER STRATEGY | FEM, BEM, or SEA based on frequency |
| GREEN FUNCTION CONVOLUTION | Path integral $P = \int G S\, dV$ |
| SUM HOMOGENEOUS AND PARTICULAR PARTS | $p = p_h + p_p$ |
| POST PROCESSING SPL | Sound Pressure Level $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$ |
| VALIDATION WITH MEASUREMENT | Compare with microphones / accelerometers |

## 4.3 Frequency-Region Map (Why the Strategy Changes)

| Frequency Range | Wavelength λ | Method | Equation Solved |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 200 Hz (low) | λ > 1.7 m | **SEA** (Statistical Energy Analysis) | Power flow balance |
| 200 – 500 Hz (mid) | 0.7–1.7 m | **Hybrid FEM/BEM** | Inhomogeneous Helmholtz |
| > 500 Hz (high) | λ < 0.7 m | **BEM / ray tracing** | Helmholtz with full Green's |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
*State and explain the inhomogeneous wave equation in 3-D for an acoustic medium. Clearly indicate the role of the source term.*

**Model Answer:**

The 3-D inhomogeneous acoustic wave equation is:

$$\nabla^{2} p(\mathbf{r}, t) - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p(\mathbf{r}, t)}{\partial t^{2}} = -S(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

where $p$ is the acoustic pressure, $c$ is the speed of sound, and $S(\mathbf{r},t)$ is the source function (e.g., $-\rho_{0} \partial q_{m}/\partial t$ for a mass source). The source term represents the spatial and temporal distribution of energy injected into the medium by physical sources such as engine combustion, tire-road interaction, or turbulent flow. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
*Differentiate between homogeneous and inhomogeneous wave equations with a suitable automotive example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Homogeneous | Inhomogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| RHS | Zero | Non-zero source $S(\mathbf{r},t)$ |
| Source | None internal | Internal forcing present |
| Solution | $p_h$ only | $p = p_h + p_p$ |
| Automotive Example | Cabin eigenmode after impulse dies out | Cabin noise due to running engine |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Derivation + Application Path*

#### (a) Derive the 3-D inhomogeneous acoustic wave equation starting from the continuity and linearized momentum equations. Mention the role of each term. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Continuity with mass source** $q_m$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = \rho_{0} q_{m}$$

2. **Linearize** $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$, $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}'$:

$$\frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial p'}{\partial t} + \rho_{0} \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = \rho_{0} q_{m}$$

3. **Euler (momentum)** linearized:

$$\rho_{0} \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla p'$$

4. **Take divergence and substitute** — after algebra:

$$\nabla^{2} p' - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p'}{\partial t^{2}} = -\rho_{0} \frac{\partial q_{m}}{\partial t}$$

**Interpretation:** Laplacian term governs spatial spreading; second time derivative governs inertia; RHS represents mass-injection rate of the source. **(7 marks — full marks for clean derivation, mention of linearization, and physical interpretation.)**

---

#### (b) A point source radiates sound at 500 Hz in air. Compute the acoustic wavenumber and discuss how this equation is applied in the Boundary Element Method (BEM) for cabin NVH. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Wavenumber:

$$k = \frac{2\pi f}{c} = \frac{2\pi \times 500}{343} \approx 9.16 \text{ rad/m}$$

Wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{343}{500} = 0.686 \text{ m}$$

**BEM Application:**
- Discretize vehicle body panels (radiating surfaces) into elements.
- For each element, the inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation is solved with panel vibration as Neumann BC:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial n} = j\omega \rho_{0} v_{n}$$

- Green's function $G = e^{-j k R}/(4\pi R)$ is used to compute interior field:

$$P(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{\Gamma} G(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}') \frac{\partial P}{\partial n'}\, d\Gamma'$$

- Resulting SPL maps identify "hot spots" near ear positions of occupants. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Green's Function Path*

#### (a) State and derive the free-space 3-D Green's function for the inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Green's function $G(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}')$ satisfies:

$$\nabla^{2} G + k^{2} G = -\delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')$$

**Steps:**

1. Place source at origin, exploit spherical symmetry, $G = G(R)$.
2. For $R \neq 0$, $\delta = 0$, so:

$$\frac{1}{R^{2}} \frac{d}{dR}\!\left(R^{2} \frac{dG}{dR}\right) + k^{2} G = 0$$

3. Substitute $G = u(R)/R$:

$$\frac{d^{2} u}{dR^{2}} + k^{2} u = 0$$

4. General solution:

$$u(R) = A e^{-j k R} + B e^{+j k R}$$

5. Apply **Sommerfeld radiation condition** (no incoming waves from infinity) → $B = 0$.
6. Determine $A$ by integrating over a small sphere and matching the delta function's integral: $A = 1/(4\pi)$.
7. Final result:

$$G_{3D}(\mathbf{r}\vert\mathbf{r}') = \frac{e^{-j k \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}}{4\pi \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}$$

**(7 marks — full marks for: symmetry argument, radiation condition, correct amplitude.)**

---

#### (b) A passenger car cabin of effective volume $V = 3 \, \text{m}^{3}$ has a low-frequency road-noise excitation modeled as a monopole source with strength $Q = 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ at 80 Hz. Estimate the SPL at the driver's ear position located 1.2 m from the source. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wavenumber**

$$k = \frac{2\pi f}{c} = \frac{2\pi \times 80}{343} = 1.466 \text{ rad/m}, \quad \lambda = 4.29 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure field from monopole**

For a volume-velocity monopole $Q$ in free-field:

$$P(r) = \frac{j\omega \rho_{0} Q}{4\pi r} e^{-j k r}$$

Magnitude:

$$|P| = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} |Q|}{4\pi r} = \frac{2\pi \times 80 \times 1.204 \times 10^{-4}}{4\pi \times 1.2}$$

Compute numerator: $2\pi \times 80 \times 1.204 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0605$
Denominator: $4\pi \times 1.2 = 15.08$

$$|P| = \frac{0.0605}{15.08} \approx 4.01 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Sound Pressure Level**

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{|P|}{p_{ref}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.01 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right)$$

$$\frac{|P|}{p_{ref}} = \frac{4.01 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 200.5$$

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(200.5) \approx 20 \times 2.302 = 46.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Cabin volume correction (modal density at 80 Hz)**

At low frequency, cabin modes dominate. With $V = 3 \, \text{m}^{3}$, the first axial mode is around:

$$f_{1} = \frac{c}{2 L_{eff}} \approx \frac{343}{2 \times 1.8} \approx 95 \text{ Hz}$$

Hence 80 Hz is **below** the first cabin mode, and SPL will be amplified by modal contribution; the free-field estimate is a **lower bound**. In a real cabin, $L_p$ typically rises by 6–10 dB near room modes. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting linearization assumptions** before combining continuity and Euler equations — *loses 2 marks*.
> 2. **Sign errors** in $\partial q_m / \partial t$ — the *minus* sign on the RHS is critical.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the radiation condition** in Green's function derivation — B = 0 is non-negotiable.
> 4. **Confusing $k = \omega/c$ with $\omega$** in numerical substitution problems.
> 5. **In Boundary Element problems, swapping source and field point** in the Green's function argument.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- The **inhomogeneous wave equation** describes wave propagation in the presence of sources; it has a **non-zero RHS** (forcing function).
- The canonical 3-D acoustic form is:

$$\nabla^{2} p - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = -S(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

- For sinusoidal excitation, the time-harmonic **Inhomogeneous Helmholtz Equation** is obtained:

$$\nabla^{2} P + k^{2} P = -S(\mathbf{r})$$

- The **Green's function** for 3-D free space is $G = e^{-jkR}/(4\pi R)$.
- The acoustic **wavenumber** $k = \omega/c$ and **wavelength** $\lambda = c/f$.
- The **standard air values** to memorize: $c = 343$ m/s, $\rho_0 = 1.204$ kg/m³, $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu$Pa.
- The **Sommerfeld radiation condition** mandates outgoing-only waves: $G \to 0$ as $R \to \infty$ for the relevant exponential term.
- **Solution decomposition**: $p = p_{\text{homogeneous}} + p_{\text{particular}}$.
- **Automotive relevance**: every forcing function in NVH (engine, road, wind, brake) is the RHS of this equation.
- **BEM technique** uses panel vibrations as Neumann BCs and the free-space Green's function for interior field prediction.
- The **monopole radiation law** $|P| \propto \omega \rho_0 Q / (4\pi r)$ underpins tire-cavity and HVAC analysis.
- **Boundary conditions** to remember: Dirichlet ($p = 0$, rigid), Neumann ($\partial p/\partial n = 0$, hard wall), Robin (impedance).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of the Presence of Solid Bodies in the Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of the Presence of Solid Bodies in the Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effect of the Presence of Solid Bodies in the Flow** refers to the modification of the undisturbed free-stream fluid behavior caused by the insertion of an immersed, finite-size, non-permeable solid surface. The body disturbs the velocity field, the pressure field, and the turbulence structure of the flow, generating **surface forces (drag and lift)**, **wake instabilities**, and **flow-induced noise** — collectively termed the **aeroacoustic and hydrodynamic loading** on the body.

In automotive NVH, this phenomenon is the root cause of several customer-perceived defects:

| Automotive NVH Defect | Source Solid Body in Flow |
|---|---|
| Wind whistle around A-pillar / side mirror | Truncated bluff body |
| Antenna buzz / roof-rack hum | Cylindrical bluff body |
| Door-seal flutter | Thin flexible seal edge |
| Sunroof buffeting | Cavity edge + shear layer |
| HVAC inlet whoosh | Inlet lip / grille slats |
| Tire cavity tone (road roar coupling) | Tread block in air pumping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters for NVH engineers:** Every external surface of a moving vehicle is, from the fluid's perspective, an immersed solid body. The unsteady pressures acting on these surfaces are mechanically transmitted into the body-in-white as structure-borne sound, producing interior cabin noise.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The River Stone:** Drop a smooth, rounded stone into a fast river. The water glides around it, separates cleanly at the rear, and produces only a gentle downstream wake. This is a **streamlined body**.

**Analogy 2 — The Bridge Pier:** Drive a square wooden post into the same river. You will hear a distinct low-frequency humming — that is the alternate shedding of vortices from each side of the post. This is a **bluff body**, and the hum is **aeolian tone / vortex-shedding noise**.

**Analogy 3 — Singing in the Wind:** Hold a flexible wire (e.g., a stretched telephone wire) perpendicular to a strong wind. Above a critical speed it begins to "sing" — the wire vibrates because alternating vortices periodically pull it sideways. This is **flow-induced vibration (FIV)** of a bluff cylinder — identical physics to a car's roof-mounted antenna buzzing.

**Analogy 4 — Tea Cup Vortex:** Stir your tea and notice how a small vortex forms at the spoon's edge and sheds downstream. The same physics — **boundary-layer separation and roll-up** — happens at every bluff edge of a car body.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Metrics

The following standard non-dimensional numbers govern the interaction:

- **Reynolds Number, Re = ρUD/μ** — ratio of inertial to viscous forces (laminar vs. turbulent regime).
- **Strouhal Number, St = fD/U** — non-dimensional vortex-shedding frequency (universal ≈ 0.21 for circular cylinder).
- **Mach Number, Ma = U/c** — compressibility indicator (relevant above ~0.3).
- **Pressure Coefficient, Cp = (p − p∞)/(½ρU²)** — surface pressure distribution metric.
- **Drag Coefficient, Cd = Fd/(½ρU²A)** — resistance metric.
- **Lift Coefficient, Cl = Fl/(½ρU²A)** — transverse force metric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluff body** = body whose drag is dominated by **pressure (form) drag**, not skin friction. A circular cylinder becomes bluff when Re > ~1. Streamlined body = drag dominated by skin friction (e.g., airfoil). Most automotive components are **bluff**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Process

When free-stream fluid of velocity **U** encounters a solid body, the following sequence of events occurs:

1. **Stagnation (Upstream)**: At the front-most point (leading edge), the flow decelerates to zero, converting kinetic energy into a static pressure maximum (stagnation pressure).

2. **Acceleration Around Shoulders**: Fluid is forced to accelerate around the body's shoulders (top and bottom for a cylinder). Pressure drops below ambient — local suction.

3. **Boundary Layer Growth**: Along the body's surface, a thin viscous boundary layer develops. Within it, velocity rises from 0 at the wall to U at the edge.

4. **Adverse Pressure Gradient**: As the fluid moves from the shoulders toward the rear, the streamlines diverge (cross-sectional area of the wake increases), causing pressure to rise. This is an **adverse pressure gradient**.

5. **Flow Separation**: The boundary layer cannot overcome the adverse gradient → it detaches from the surface at the **separation point**. Skin friction vanishes downstream of this point.

6. **Free Shear Layer Roll-Up**: The two detached shear layers (top and bottom) roll up into discrete **vortices** that are shed alternately.

7. **Wake Formation**: A turbulent, low-pressure **wake** is formed behind the body, with periodic vortex shedding (von Kármán street at moderate Re).

8. **Unsteady Surface Pressure**: The alternating shedding imposes oscillating pressures on the body's surface → time-varying force → **noise + vibration**.

## 2.2 Why and How — Physical Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY does separation happen?** A real fluid has viscosity; momentum within the boundary layer is finite. When pressure rises downstream faster than the fluid's kinetic energy can be converted to pressure, the fluid literally "runs out of energy" and stops, then reverses, lifting off the surface.

**HOW does this create noise?**

$$\text{Unsteady vortex shedding} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Time-varying surface pressure} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Force on body} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Structure-borne sound}$$

The body's surface responds as a **forced-damped oscillator**. If a shedding frequency matches a structural natural frequency → **resonance lock-in** (the famous 100 km/h "antenna howl" or "mirror whistle").

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu} = \dfrac{U D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number; regime classifier | dimensionless |
| 2 | $St = \dfrac{f_s D}{U}$ | Strouhal number; shedding frequency predictor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_s = \dfrac{St \cdot U}{D}$ | Vortex shedding frequency (Hz) | Hz |
| 4 | $F_D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2} A$ | Drag force | N |
| 5 | $F_L = C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2} A$ | Lift force | N |
| 6 | $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}}$ | Pressure coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2} (1 - 4\sin^{2}\theta)$ | Surface pressure on a cylinder (potential flow, ideal) | Pa |
| 8 | $A_e = 0.1 \, D^{2}$ | Acoustic efficiency of vortex shedding | m² |
| 9 | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ | Angular shedding frequency | rad/s |
| 10 | $P_{aero} = \rho_0 U^{5} D^{2} \cdot F(St)$ | Lighthill's eighth-power aeroacoustic law (monopole-like) | W |
| 11 | $u' = 0.1\, U$ | RMS turbulence intensity (typical wake) | m/s |
| 12 | $\delta = \dfrac{0.37\, x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Turbulent boundary layer thickness | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strouhal's Law — Universality:** For a smooth circular cylinder over the range **10² < Re < 10⁵**, **St ≈ 0.21**. This is why vortex-shedding tones are so *predictable* — knowing U and D immediately yields the tone frequency. KTU examiners love this single number.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Side-mirror design**: Designers add "spoiler lips" to deliberately trip the boundary layer into a small fixed vortex, suppressing the broad-band 1–4 kHz whistle.
- **Antenna placement**: Move the antenna base from the roof center to a region of reduced local U (low Cp zone) to minimize drag and tone amplitude.
- **Wind-tunnel testing**: PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) and surface pressure taps (Kulites) are used to validate CFD-predicted Cp distributions.
- **Roof-rack aero-acoustics**: A poorly designed cross-bar acts like a finite-span cylinder → broad-band vortex shedding at 500–2000 Hz → interior boom.
- **HVAC blower noise**: Not strictly external aero, but uses identical bluff-body + Strouhal physics inside ducts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vortex-Shedding Frequency (Strouhal Law)

**Starting point**: Experimental observation (Strouhal, 1878; von Kármán, 1911) that the dominant tone frequency from a bluff cylinder scales linearly with U and inversely with D.

$$\boxed{f_s = \text{function}(U, D, \rho, \mu)}$$

**Step 1 — Buckingham Pi Theorem.** Choose repeating variables ρ, U, D. Form dimensionless groups:

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{f_s D}{U}, \qquad \pi_2 = \dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu} = Re$$

**Step 2 — Functional form:**

$$\pi_1 = \Phi(\pi_2) \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{f_s D}{U} = \Phi(Re)$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Strouhal-Reynolds correlation for a smooth circular cylinder:**

$$
\Phi(Re) \approx 
\begin{cases}
0.212 - 4.5/Re & 10^{3} < Re < 10^{5} \quad (\text{sub-critical}) \\
0.27 & 3 \times 10^{5} < Re < 5 \times 10^{5} \quad (\text{critical / transitional}) \\
0.19 & Re > 5 \times 10^{5} \quad (\text{supercritical}) 
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering short form (sub-critical, the regime most automotive components operate in):**

$$f_s = 0.21\,\dfrac{U}{D}$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_s \;[\text{Hz}] \;=\; 0.21 \times \dfrac{U \;[\text{m/s}]}{D \;[\text{m}]}\,}
$$

**Conversion to road speed:** For a vehicle moving at V km/h, U ≈ V/3.6. A typical 4 mm diameter antenna at 100 km/h:

$$
f_s = 0.21 \times \dfrac{27.78}{0.004} = 1458 \text{ Hz}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drag on a Bluff Body

**Step 1 — Control-volume momentum balance** on a stationary body in uniform flow:

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{out} u_{out} - \dot{m}_{in} u_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over control surface S:**

$$F_D = \oint_S \left[ (p - p_\infty) \, n_x + \rho u_x (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \right] dS$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the integral yields a force proportional to dynamic pressure × area:**

$$F_D = C_D \, \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}\right) A_{\text{ref}}$$

where $A_{\text{ref}}$ is the projected frontal area.

**Step 4 — Non-dimensional drag coefficient** for a circular cylinder (bluff):

$$
C_D \approx
\begin{cases}
\sim 1.2 & 10^{3} < Re < 2 \times 10^{5} \quad (\text{subcritical}) \\
\sim 0.3 & 3 \times 10^{5} < Re < 5 \times 10^{5} \quad (\text{critical drag crisis}) \\
\sim 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8 & Re > 5 \times 10^{5} \quad (\text{supercritical})
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drag-crisis insight:** A golf ball travels in the *supercritical* regime. The dimples trip the boundary layer to turbulent, which is more energetic and resists separation → separation moves downstream → wake shrinks → Cd drops from 1.2 to ~0.25. Automotive designers use small surface trips on side mirrors for the same reason.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Pressure on a Cylinder (Potential-Flow Baseline)

For inviscid, incompressible, irrotational flow around a circular cylinder, superposition of uniform flow + doublet gives:

$$
p - p_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}\left(1 - 4\sin^{2}\theta\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_p(\theta) = 1 - 4\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$

Step-by-step logic:

1. Velocity potential of doublet: $\phi = -U R^{2}\cos\theta / r$
2. Total stream function: $\psi = U r \sin\theta (1 - R^{2}/r^{2})$
3. Velocity components from $\nabla\psi$: $u_r = U\cos\theta(1 - R^{2}/r^{2})$, $u_\theta = -U\sin\theta(1 + R^{2}/r^{2})$
4. On the surface $r = R$: $u_r = 0$, $u_\theta = -2U\sin\theta$
5. Bernoulli along streamline: $p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \vert\vec{V}\vert^{2} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}$
6. Substitute and simplify.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Real viscous flow separates near $\theta \approx 80^\circ$ (vs. $\theta = 180^\circ$ ideal), so the *base pressure* is much lower than $-3$ predicted. The **pressure drag** (form drag) arises almost entirely from this difference between front stagnation ($C_p = 1$) and base suction ($C_p \approx -1$).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aeroacoustic Sound Power (Lighthill's Eighth-Power Law)

Curle's extension of Lighthill's acoustic analogy for a compact rigid body in turbulent flow gives:

$$
P_{aero} \propto \rho_0 \, \dfrac{U^{8}}{c_0^{5}} \, D^{2} \cdot F(St)
$$

- Power scales with **U⁸** — halve speed → power drops by factor 256.
- This is why highway wind noise at 120 km/h is dramatically worse than at 80 km/h.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Symptom | Probable Bluff-Body Source | Diagnostic Frequency | Typical Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–4 kHz whistle | A-pillar / mirror shoulder | $f_s \approx 0.21 U/D$ | Add flow trip or chamfer |
| Mid-band boom | Roof rack crossbar | 200–600 Hz | Streamline section, fairing |
| Antenna howl | Whip antenna | 1–3 kHz | Reshape tip, base damping |
| Door-seal flutter | Flexible seal lip | 50–200 Hz | Increase stiffness, lip angle |
| Sunroof buffeting | Cavity shear layer | 5–30 Hz | Adjust rear deflector, vent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow Around a Bluff Cylinder with Vortex Shedding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Stream U_infty] --> B[Stagnation Point Cp 1.0]
    B --> C[Acceleration Around Shoulder Cp negative]
    C --> D[Boundary Layer Growth]
    D --> E[Adverse Pressure Gradient]
    E --> F{Separation Point theta approx 80 deg}
    F -->|Top shear layer| G[Top Vortex Shed f_s]
    F -->|Bottom shear layer| H[Bottom Vortex Shed f_s shifted by half period]
    G --> I[von Karman Vortex Street]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Unsteady Surface Pressure on Body]
    J --> K[Force on Body Drag and Lift Oscillation]
    K --> L[Structure Borne Sound to Body in White]
    L --> M[Interior Cabin Noise]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Signal Chain for Automotive Wind Noise

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXC[Excitation Source]
        E1[Turbulent Free Stream] --> E2[Immersed Solid Body]
        E2 --> E3[Vortex Shedding Frequency f_s]
        E3 --> E4[Stochastic Pressure Fluctuation on Surface]
    end

    subgraph TRM[Transmission Path]
        E4 --> T1[Local Panel Vibration]
        T1 --> T2[Structural Propagation through Body in White]
        T2 --> T3[Interior Cavity Acoustic Modes]
    end

    subgraph RES[Receiver Response]
        T3 --> R1[Driver Ear SPL]
        T1 --> R2[Steering Column Buzz]
        T2 --> R3[Trim Panel Rattle]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow-Regime Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Immersed Solid Body in Flow] --> Q{Geometry}
    Q -->|Streamlined| SL[Skin Friction Dominated\nCd approx 0.04 to 0.1]
    Q -->|Bluff| BL[Pressure Drag Dominated\nCd approx 0.6 to 1.2]
    BL --> R{Reynolds Number Re}
    R -->|Re less than 1| REG1[Stokes Flow\nNo separation]
    R -->|1 to 40| REG2[Attached Flow with\nSymmetric Vortex Pair]
    R -->|40 to 150| REG3[Onset of Asymmetric\nLaminar Shedding]
    R -->|150 to 10^3| REG4[Laminar Vortex Street\nSt approx 0.2]
    R -->|10^3 to 2x10^5| REG5[Subcritical\nSt approx 0.21 Cd approx 1.2]
    R -->|2x10^5 to 5x10^5| REG6[Critical Drag Crisis\nCd drops to 0.3]
    R -->|above 5x10^5| REG7[Supercritical Turbulent\nCd approx 0.6]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Strouhal Lock-In

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AERO[Aerodynamic Side]
        F1[Free Stream U] --> F2[Vortex Shedding f_s]
    end
    subgraph STRUCT[Structural Side]
        S1[Body Natural Frequency f_n] --> S2[Damping ratio zeta]
    end
    F2 --> COMP{f_s close to f_n?}
    S2 --> COMP
    COMP -->|Yes within 20 percent| LOCK[Lock In Vibration\nLarge Amplitude]
    COMP -->|No| FREEFREE[Forced Vibration\nSmall Amplitude]
    LOCK --> NOISE[Wind Noise Complaint]
    FREEFREE --> NOISE2[Acceptable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A 14-marks question on this topic almost always asks you to compute $f_s$, identify whether it coincides with a structural mode, and propose one mitigation. Practice that flow.
