# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Explain the **Strouhal number** and its significance in characterizing flow-induced noise from a cylindrical antenna.

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Strouhal number is a dimensionless frequency defined as $St = f_s D / U$, where $f_s$ is vortex shedding frequency, $D$ is the characteristic dimension (diameter for a cylinder), and $U$ is the free-stream velocity.

- **Significance for circular cylinder (1 mark):** For a smooth circular cylinder in the sub-critical Reynolds-number range $10^3 < Re < 10^5$, $St \approx 0.21$. This universality allows engineers to predict the dominant tone simply from geometry and speed.

- **Predictive use (1 mark):** At 100 km/h, U = 27.78 m/s; a 4 mm antenna gives $f_s = 0.21 \times 27.78 / 0.004 \approx 1458$ Hz. This tone may excite interior cavity modes → "antenna howl." Mitigation: re-shape tip, change diameter, or relocate to lower local velocity.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> What is **flow separation**? Why does it occur, and what is its consequence for the drag on a bluff body?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Flow separation is the detachment of the boundary layer from a solid surface, occurring when the streamwise pressure gradient becomes adverse (rising in the flow direction) faster than the boundary-layer fluid's residual momentum can overcome.

- **Reason for occurrence (1 mark):** Viscosity dissipates the boundary layer's kinetic energy. When the adverse pressure gradient exceeds the fluid's local energy, the near-wall flow stagnates and reverses, lifting off the surface.

- **Consequence for bluff-body drag (1 mark):** Separation occurs early on bluff bodies (e.g., circular cylinder at $\theta \approx 80^\circ$), producing a large low-pressure wake. Pressure difference between front stagnation ($C_p = 1$) and base ($C_p \approx -1$) yields high **pressure (form) drag**, $C_D \approx 1.2$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

A car is travelling at **120 km/h**. A roof-mounted whip antenna of diameter **6 mm** is observed to generate a strong tonal complaint at approximately **1130 Hz** when the driver is alone in the cabin.

**(a)** Derive the relationship between vortex-shedding frequency, Strouhal number, free-stream velocity, and antenna diameter. Hence, compute the predicted shedding frequency and verify it matches the complaint. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the physical mechanism by which this aerodynamic excitation reaches the driver's ear as interior cabin noise. With a labeled sketch in words, propose **two** design modifications to suppress the antenna tone. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Derivation (3 marks):** By Buckingham Pi analysis of governing variables ($f_s$, U, D, ρ, μ):

$$\dfrac{f_s D}{U} = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu}\right) = \Phi(Re)$$

$$\boxed{\,f_s = St \cdot \dfrac{U}{D}\,} \quad \text{with } St = 0.21 \text{ for a smooth cylinder at subcritical } Re.$$

**Step 2 — Numerical verification (3 marks):**

$$
U = \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{3600} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{1.225 \times 33.33 \times 0.006}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{4}
$$

Since $10^3 < Re < 10^5$, $St \approx 0.21$ is valid.

$$
f_s = 0.21 \times \dfrac{33.33}{0.006} = 1166 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (1 mark):** The predicted 1166 Hz matches the observed 1130 Hz complaint within ±3% (the slight offset is explained by antenna-tip Reynolds-number effects, non-uniform inflow, and small structural lock-in shift). Diagnosis confirmed: **vortex-shedding-induced aeroacoustic tone**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Mechanism (3 marks):** The boundary layer separates alternately from each side of the cylindrical antenna. Shed vortices roll into a Kármán street, imposing unsteady surface pressure. The fluctuating pressure excites antenna bending modes at $f_s$. Vibration transmits through the antenna base into the roof panel, into the body-in-white, and radiates as cabin sound. Simultaneously, the shed vortices themselves act as compact quadrupole/dipole sources of aerodynamic sound. If $f_s$ coincides with an interior cavity acoustic mode, **resonance amplification** occurs.

**Modification 1 — Reshape the antenna (2 marks):** Replace the uniform 6 mm cylinder with a **tapered (conical) tip** or a "slim-tip" profile. The diameter varies along the length, so shedding frequency becomes a **broadband smear** instead of a pure tone — psychoacoustically far less objectionable.

**Modification 2 — Add base damping or relocate (2 marks):** Either (i) fit a **viscoelastic base mount** to interrupt the structural transmission path, or (ii) move the antenna base from the roof center (high local U ≈ 1.05 × vehicle speed) to a lower-velocity region such as behind the rear spoiler or near the C-pillar base. Reducing local U lowers $f_s$ below the most sensitive 1–3 kHz ear range and reduces the acoustic source strength by the **U⁸ law**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Alternative]`

A wind-tunnel study of an automotive side mirror at **80 km/h** shows a strong tonal peak at **1750 Hz** with an SPL of 78 dB(A) inside the cabin.

**(a)** Treating the mirror's bluff shoulder as a circular cylinder of effective diameter 7 mm in the oncoming flow, determine (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the predicted Strouhal shedding frequency, and (iii) whether the operating regime is sub-critical, critical, or super-critical. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the role of the **boundary layer** and **adverse pressure gradient** in generating this tone. Show, by deriving the pressure coefficient for potential flow over a cylinder, why real viscous flow deviates strongly from the ideal case at the rear of the body. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Reynolds number (2 marks):**

$$
U = \dfrac{80}{3.6} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.225 \times 22.22 \times 0.007}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{4}
$$

**(ii) Strouhal shedding frequency (3 marks):**

Since $10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$, the flow is **subcritical** with $St = 0.21$:

$$
f_s = St \cdot \dfrac{U}{D} = 0.21 \times \dfrac{22.22}{0.007} = 666 \text{ Hz}
$$

The measured 1750 Hz is **higher** than the 666 Hz Strouhal prediction — this implies the **effective** bluff-body diameter is smaller (a sharp edge), and/or that higher harmonics of the shedding ($3 f_s$) are dominating, and/or that lock-in to a structural mirror-mount mode is occurring. Diagnosis requires PIV or microphone-array testing.

**(iii) Regime classification (2 marks):** $Re \approx 1.05 \times 10^4$ falls within **sub-critical** ($10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$) — laminar boundary layer separates on the forward shoulder; broad Kármán shedding; $C_D \approx 1.2$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Boundary-layer and adverse-gradient role (3 marks):** As the free-stream approaches the mirror shoulder, the **boundary layer** grows from the leading stagnation point. Past the shoulder, the streamlines diverge to fill the wake, so static pressure rises downstream. This rising-pressure region constitutes an **adverse pressure gradient**. The boundary-layer fluid, having lost kinetic energy to viscous shear, cannot overcome the gradient → **separation occurs near $\theta \approx 80^\circ$**. The detached free shear layers roll up into alternating vortices that shed at $f_s$, producing the tonal pressure fluctuation.

**Potential-flow derivation (3 marks):** Superposing uniform flow + doublet on a cylinder of radius $R$ gives surface velocity:

$$
u_\theta(\theta) = -2U\sin\theta
$$

Bernoulli's equation between freestream and surface point:

$$
p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_\theta^{2} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}
$$

$$
p - p_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}(1 - 4\sin^{2}\theta)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_p(\theta) = 1 - 4\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$

**Deviation of real flow (1 mark):** Ideal prediction gives $C_p(\theta = 180^\circ) = -3$ at the rear — but real viscous flow *separates* before reaching the rear, the wake-base pressure is approximately $C_p \approx -1$, not $-3$. The shortfall $(−1) - (−3) = +2$ is the *pressure drag deficit* removed from the ideal rear suction. This large discrepancy is the origin of the high bluff-body drag coefficient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors in Reynolds number** (using dynamic viscosity μ instead of kinematic ν — wrong by factor 10⁶). Use SI throughout.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s** in $U$. KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 3. **Citing Strouhal = 0.21 without stating the Re range** — without this qualification, the answer is technically incomplete (no marks awarded in Part B).
> 4. **Confusing form drag with skin-friction drag** — for a bluff body in automobile context, it is *always* pressure (form) drag dominant.
> 5. **Skipping the free-body diagram or boundary-layer sketch** in 7-mark derivations — at least one labeled schematic is required for full marks.
> 6. **Writing "vortex shedding causes noise" without quantifying f_s** — always compute the tone and compare to a structural or acoustic mode.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Strouhal number $St = f_s D / U \approx 0.21$ for smooth circular cylinder in sub-critical Re ($10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$).
- [ ] **Bluff body** = form-drag dominated; **Streamlined body** = skin-friction dominated.
- [ ] Flow **separation** occurs where the boundary layer cannot overcome the **adverse pressure gradient**.
- [ ] Bluff-body drag is dominated by the **front-to-base pressure difference**, not viscous shear.
- [ ] **Vortex shedding** produces tonal excitation; **turbulent wake** produces broad-band excitation.
- [ ] **Curle / Lighthill** acoustic analogy → aeroacoustic power scales as **U⁸** (eighth-power law).
- [ ] **Lock-in** occurs when shedding frequency lies within ~±20% of a structural natural frequency → resonance.
- [ ] Practical automotive fixes: **streamline, trip the boundary layer, chamfer edges, dampen base, relocate source**.
- [ ] Reynolds number regime dictates $St$ and $C_D$ — always classify first.
- [ ] Potential flow gives $C_p = 1 - 4\sin^2\theta$ on a cylinder — a textbook idealization that real viscous flow violates at the rear due to separation.
- [ ] In KTU Part B answers, always show: **numerical substitution with units, comparison to a structural mode, and one design fix**.
- [ ] Three "magic numbers" to memorize: $St = 0.21$, $C_D = 1.2$ (subcritical cylinder), $U^8$ acoustic-power law.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powell Howe Theory of Vortex Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Powell Howe Theory of Vortex Sound ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Powell-Howe Theory of Vortex Sound** is the rigorous aeroacoustic framework that describes the generation of sound by **unsteady vortical flow** in compressible media. It was developed independently by **Alan Powell (1964)** and **M.S. Howe (1975)**, extending Sir James Lighthill's Acoustic Analogy specifically to identify **vorticity (rotational motion)** as the primary source of sound in the absence of solid boundaries, mass sources, or thermal fluctuations. Mathematically, the theory isolates the **vorticity-stress term** within Lighthill's stress tensor $T_{ij}$ and demonstrates that the acoustic source is fundamentally related to the **time-rate of change of vorticity** as it is convected through the fluid medium. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT863):** This theory forms the foundational basis for analyzing **aerodynamic noise** in road vehicles, including side-mirror whistle, antenna tone, HVAC fan noise, sunroof buffeting, and intake/exhaust system tonalities. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistling Kettle" of Automotive NVH Imagine blowing across the mouth of a glass bottle: the air at the lip **separates** and **rolls up into a tiny spinning vortex** that detaches periodically (a *Kármán vortex street*). Each time that little tornado of air is shed, it nudges the surrounding still air, creating a tiny **pressure pulse** — a sound wave. The faster you blow, the faster the vortexes shed, and the higher the tone. **The Powell-Howe theory formalises exactly this phenomenon.** In a car: - Air flowing past the **A-pillar mirror** sheds vortices → that annoying **whistle at 80 km/h**. - Air over the **roof rails** generates vortices → the **"thrum"** heard inside the cabin. - Air entering the **HVAC blower** housing → **whirring tone** in the cabin. In each case, **vorticity** (the local "spin" of the air) is the **source of sound**, and the Powell-Howe theory provides the mathematical machinery to *predict*, *locate*, and *mitigate* this noise. ### 1.3 Why Vortices Generate Sound — The Physical Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** A steady, rigidly translating vortex in a uniform flow is **silent**. Sound is generated only when the vorticity distribution is **unsteady** (e.g., the vortex is growing, convecting, interacting with a surface, or being strained). The acoustic source in the Powell-Howe formulation is the **vorticity flux**: $$\mathbf{S} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$$ where $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$ is the vorticity vector and $\mathbf{v}$ is the flow velocity. The term $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ represents the **"vorticity wave"** — a non-linear, convected disturbance that radiates acoustic energy. ### 1.4 Constants and Reference Metrics - **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** $c_0 = \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$ - **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_0 = \mathbf{1.204 \; kg/m^3}$ - **Characteristic acoustic Mach number:** $M = v/c_0$, with vortex sound typically a **low-Mach-number** phenomenon ($M < 0.3$). - **Specific acoustic impedance:** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0 \approx \mathbf{413 \; kg/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Building From First Principles — Lighthill's Acoustic Analogy The Powell-Howe theory is built on Lighthill's rearrangement of the **Navier-Stokes equations** and the **continuity equation** into a single inhomogeneous wave equation. The right-hand side (the "source") of this wave equation is the **divergence of Lighthill's stress tensor**. For an **isentropic, viscous, compressible flow**, the source term is: $$S_{ij} = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$ Lighthill's stress tensor $T_{ij}$ has three physically distinct contributions: 1. **Reynolds stress (convective / vortical):** $\rho u_i u_j$ — Powell-Howe focus. 2. **Pressure / enthalpy excess:** $(p - c_0^2 \rho)\delta_{ij}$ — negligible for isentropic low-Mach flow. 3. **Viscous stress:** $-\sigma_{ij}$ — usually small for automotive aerodynamic flows. ### 2.2 Powell's Key Insight (1964) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Powell's Central Result:** For an **inviscid, isentropic** low-Mach-number flow with no heat addition, the dominant term in $T_{ij}$ is the Reynolds-stress term. Acoustic radiation is therefore generated by the **unsteady advection of vorticity**. Powell introduced the **convected source concept**: vorticity acts as a "tracer" that generates sound as it is convected through the fluid, with the source strength proportional to the rate of change of the local vorticity field. ### 2.3 Howe's Generalisation (1975) — The Crocco Formulation Howe re-derived vortex sound theory using **Crocco's form of the momentum equation**, which retains the **total enthalpy** as a thermodynamic variable. This is more general than Powell's and is valid for **non-isentropic**, **non-uniform** mean flows, making it ideal for automotive applications where temperature gradients (e.g., engine bay) and mean shear are significant. Howe's key result is the **vortex-sound wave equation**: $$\frac{1}{c_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}\right)$$ where $p'$ is the acoustic pressure fluctuation, and the right-hand side is the **Howe source term** — the time derivative of the dot product of vorticity and velocity. ### 2.4 Howe's Energy Corollary > [!NOTE] > **The most powerful tool in the Powell-Howe toolkit** is the acoustic energy corollary, which quantifies how vortical kinetic energy is converted into acoustic energy. $$\frac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS + \int_V p' \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \frac{1}{\rho} \, dV$$ - **Surface integral** → acoustic power radiated through surfaces. - **Volume integral** → work done by the acoustic field on the fluid (zero for incompressible mean flow). ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation / Result | Physical Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \dfrac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Lighthill's wave equation | Foundation | | 2 | $T_{ij} = \rho u_i u_j + \left[(p - p_0) - c_0^2(\rho - \rho_0)\right]\delta_{ij} - \sigma_{ij}$ | Lighthill's stress tensor | Source identification | | 3 | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$ | Vorticity definition | Vortex strength | | 4 | $\dfrac{\partial \boldsymbol{\omega}}{\partial t} = \nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) + \nu \nabla^2 \boldsymbol{\omega}$ | Vorticity transport eqn. (inviscid limit) | Source convection | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}\right)$ | **Howe's vortex-sound equation** | Core Powell-Howe result | | 6 | $\dfrac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS$ | Howe's energy corollary (incompressible limit) | Acoustic power calc. | | 7 | $f_s = \dfrac{St \cdot U}{D}$ | Strouhal shedding frequency | Tone prediction | | 8 | $p_{rms}' \sim \dfrac{\rho_0 U^2 L^2 \omega}{r c_0^2}$ | Far-field pressure scaling | Order-of-magnitude estimate | | 9 | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0 \approx 413$ rayl | Acoustic impedance of air | Intensity calculations | | 10 | $I = \dfrac{\langle p'^2 \rangle}{Z_0}$ | Acoustic intensity | Power flux | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorise equations **(5)** and **(6)** — they are the most frequently asked core results in any 14-mark question on this topic. ### 2.6 Real-World Automotive Utility The theory is the **engineering backbone** for predicting and controlling: - **Side-mirror whistle** (Kármán vortex shedding at $St \approx 0.18$–$0.22$) - **Antenna and trim whistling** - **HVAC blower tonal noise** (vortex shedding at fan-blade trailing edge) - **Intake manifold buzz** (vortex pairing at valve opening) - **Exhaust tailpipe drone** (vortex shedding at the tailpipe lip) - **Sunroof buffeting** (vortex pair formation at leading edge) - **Wiper blade noise** (vortex shedding at the blade) By quantifying how each source couples to the acoustic far field, NVH engineers design **vortex spoilers, splitter edges, and fairings** that *weaken* the $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ source term — for example, by trip-strip-induced transition that moves the vortex shedding point outboard. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — From Navier-Stokes to Lighthill's Wave Equation **Starting Point:** The continuity equation and isentropic momentum equation for a compressible viscous fluid. **Step 1 — Continuity Equation:** $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_i)}{\partial x_i} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Momentum Equation (Navier-Stokes):** $$\frac{\partial (\rho u_i)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u_i u_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j}$$ **Step 3 — Eliminate Momentum via Material Derivative.** Multiply continuity by $u_i$ and add to momentum: $$\rho \frac{D u_i}{D t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Isentropic Pressure Relation** $p - p_0 = c_0^2 (\rho - \rho_0) + \text{non-linear terms}$: $$p = c_0^2 \rho + \left[p - c_0^2 \rho - p_0\right]$$ Define excess pressure $p' = p - p_0$ and excess density $\rho' = \rho - \rho_0$. **Step 5 — Form the Lighthill Stress Tensor.** Subtract the acoustic wave operator from the momentum equation. The non-linear residual is collected into $T_{ij}$: $$\begin{aligned} T_{ij} &= \rho u_i u_j + \left[(p - p_0) - c_0^2 (\rho - \rho_0)\right]\delta_{ij} - \sigma_{ij} \\ &= \underbrace{\rho u_i u_j}_{\text{Reynolds}} + \underbrace{\left[p - c_0^2 \rho - p_0\right]\delta_{ij}}_{\text{Enthalpy excess}} + \underbrace{(-\sigma_{ij})}_{\text{Viscous}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Final Lighthill Wave Equation:** Substituting back yields: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$ **Interpretation:** The acoustic density $\rho'$ obeys a **wave equation with source $\partial^2 T_{ij}/\partial x_i \partial x_j$**. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Powell's Reduction to Vorticity **Step 1 — Take the Curl of the Momentum Equation.** For an inviscid flow with no body forces: $$\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\omega}}{\partial t} = \nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})$$ This is the **Helmholtz vorticity equation** — vorticity is *frozen* into the fluid and convected with it. **Step 2 — Decompose Velocity** into irrotational (acoustic) and rotational (vortical) parts: $$\mathbf{v} = \nabla \phi + \mathbf{v}_{\omega}$$ where $\nabla \times (\nabla \phi) = 0$ and $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\omega} = 0$ (incompressible vortex). **Step 3 — Insert into Lighthill's Tensor.** Powell showed that for isentropic, low-Mach flow, the only surviving source term is: $$T_{ij} \approx \rho_0 u_i u_j$$ **Step 4 — Powell's Vortex-Sound Source:** The acoustic source density reduces to: $$q(\mathbf{x}, t) = \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}(\rho_0 u_i u_j) = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}\right)$$ This is the **Powell source term**, which depends *only* on the interaction between vorticity and velocity. ### 3.3 Derivation III — Howe's Wave Equation for Vortex Sound **Step 1 — Start from Crocco's Form of the Momentum Equation:** $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \nabla B = T \nabla s - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v} + \frac{1}{\rho}\nabla \cdot \sigma$$ where $B$ is the total enthalpy, $s$ the entropy, and $T$ the temperature. **Step 2 — For Isentropic Mean Flow** ($\nabla s = 0$ and $\sigma = 0$): $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \nabla B = -\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$$ **Step 3 — Linearise around Mean Flow** $(\mathbf{v}_0, B_0, \rho_0)$. Perturbations $p'$, $B'$, $\mathbf{v}'$, $\boldsymbol{\omega}'$ obey: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} + \nabla B' = -\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}'$$ **Step 4 — Take the Divergence** and use the linearised continuity equation $\partial \rho'/\partial t + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = 0$: $$\nabla \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t}\right) + \nabla^2 B' = -\nabla \cdot (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}')$$ **Step 5 — Use the Speed-of-Sound Relation** $c_0^2 = (\partial p / \partial \rho)_s$ and $B' = p'/\rho_0$: $$\frac{1}{c_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result (Howe's Vortex-Sound Equation):** This is the **master equation** for the Powell-Howe theory — a wave equation whose right-hand-side source is the time derivative of $\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}$, the **unsteady vorticity flux**. ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Howe's Energy Corollary **Step 1 — Multiply the Linearised Momentum Equation by $p'$** and integrate over the fluid volume $V$: $$\int_V p' \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_V p' \nabla B' \, dV = -\int_V p' (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}) \, dV$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Divergence Theorem** to the second term and use $B' = p'/\rho_0$: $$\int_V p' \nabla B' \, dV = \oint_S p' B' \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS - \int_V B' \nabla p' \, dV$$ **Step 3 — Recognise** $\rho_0 \partial B'/\partial t = \partial p'/\partial t$. After manipulation, the time-rate of change of total acoustic energy is: $$\frac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS - \int_V p' \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \frac{1}{\rho} \, dV$$ **Step 4 — For Incompressible Mean Flow** the volume integral vanishes: $$\frac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** Acoustic power is radiated **only through the surface enclosing the flow** — vortex sound is a **bulk-source, surface-radiated** phenomenon, and any **vortex-surface interaction** is the dominant energy-transfer mechanism. This is why A-pillar vortex generators, mirror stays, and tailpipe lip designs are so acoustically significant. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Order-of-Magnitude Estimates For a typical side-mirror vortex shedding at $U = 30 \; \text{m/s}$ (≈ 108 km/h), with characteristic length $D = 0.10 \; \text{m}$ and Strouhal number $St = 0.20$: $$\begin{aligned} f_s &= \frac{St \cdot U}{D} = \frac{0.20 \times 30}{0.10} = 60 \;\text{Hz} \\ M &= \frac{U}{c_0} = \frac{30}{343} \approx 0.087 \\ p_{rms}' &\sim \frac{\rho_0 U^2 D}{r} \cdot M \sim \frac{1.2 \times 900 \times 0.10}{1.0} \times 0.087 \approx 9.4 \;\text{Pa} \\ \text{SPL} &\approx 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{9.4}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) \approx 113 \;\text{dB} \end{aligned}$$ This explains why even a small exterior mirror becomes a **dominant interior-noise source** at cruising speed. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Block Architecture — The Powell-Howe Theoretical Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["A. UNSTEADY VORTICAL FLOW
Rotational motion in fluid"] node2["B. NAVIER STOKES EQUATIONS
Momentum + Continuity"] node3["C. LIGHTHILLS STRESS TENSOR T_ij
Reynolds + Enthalpy + Viscous"] node4["D. POWELL 1964
Vorticity identified as source"] node5["E. HOWE 1975
Crocco formulation generalisation"] node6["F. HOWE WAVE EQUATION
Laplacian of p prime equals d/dt of omega dot v"] node7["G. HOWE ENERGY COROLLARY
Acoustic power via surface integral"] node8["H. AUTOMOTIVE NVH SOURCES
Mirror whistle, HVAC, intake, exhaust"] node9["I. NVH MITIGATION DESIGN
Vortex spoilers, fairings, edge treatments"] node10["J. VALIDATION
CFD, wind tunnel, in-cabin measurements"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node5 --> node7 node6 --> node8 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10 node10 -. feedback .-> node9 ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Schematic — Vortex Generation and Acoustic Radiation in an Automotive Mirror ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["FREESTREAM U_infty
Oncoming air flow"] nodeB["MIRROR HOUSING
Bluff body geometry"] nodeC["SHEAR LAYER SEPARATION
Leading edge"] nodeD["KÁRMÁN VORTEX STREET
Alternating shedding"] nodeE["VORTICITY FIELD omega x v
Powell source term"] nodeF["ACOUSTIC WAVEFRONT
Radiated pressure field"] nodeG["DRIVER EAR POSITION
Interior noise SPL"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeH["MITIGATION
Splitter plate, vortex spoiler, edge fillet"] nodeH -.-> nodeB ``` ### 4.3 Source-Term Decomposition Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeT["LIGHTHILL STRESS TENSOR T_ij"] nodeR["Reynolds Term
rho times u_i times u_j"] nodeH["Enthalpy Term
p minus c0 squared rho times delta_ij"] nodeV["Viscous Term
minus sigma_ij"] nodeP["POWELL HOWE SOURCE
Time derivative of omega dot v"] nodeS["SILENT OR NEGLIGIBLE
For low Mach isentropic flow"] nodeT --> nodeR nodeT --> nodeH nodeT --> nodeV nodeR --> nodeP nodeH --> nodeS nodeV --> nodeS nodeP --> nodeA["Acoustic radiation"] nodeS --> nodeB["Negligible contribution"] ``` ### 4.4 Energy-Flow Schematic — Howe's Energy Corollary Applied to a Tailpipe ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["EXHAUST PULSE
Pulsating mass flow at tailpipe lip"] node2["TOROIDAL VORTEX RING
Formed at lip"] node3["VORTEX CONVECTION
Helmholtz frozen flow"] node4["HOWE SURFACE INTEGRAL
p prime times v dot n dS"] node5["RADIATED ACOUSTIC POWER
dE_a by dt"] node6["TAILPIPE DRONE / BUZZ
Audible in cabin"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node7["CONTROL: CHAMFERED LIP, DUCT LINER, RESONATOR"] node7 -.-> node2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]** > *Define vortex sound and state the fundamental source term in the Powell-Howe theory.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Vortex sound is the acoustic radiation produced by unsteady vortical (rotational) motion in a compressible fluid, in the absence of solid boundaries, mass injection, or thermal sources. - **Source term (1 mark):** The acoustic source is the time-rate-of-change of the dot product of vorticity and velocity, i.e., $\rho_0 \, \partial(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})/\partial t$. - **Howe's wave equation (1 mark):** It appears as the right-hand side of Howe's vortex-sound wave equation, $\dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})$. --- > **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Style]** > *State Howe's energy corollary and explain its physical significance for automotive NVH.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (1 mark):** Howe's energy corollary states that the time-rate of change of acoustic energy in a fluid volume is given by $\dfrac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS - \int_V p'(\partial \rho / \partial t)/\rho \, dV$, which for incompressible mean flow reduces to the surface integral only. - **Physical significance (2 marks):** It shows that *acoustic power is radiated through any surface enclosing the vortical source* and that **vortex–surface interactions** are the dominant energy-transfer mechanism. In automotive applications, this means the **interior noise** of a vehicle is strongly influenced by how vortices generated at the A-pillar mirror, sunroof, antenna, or tailpipe lip interact with the surrounding sheet metal and glass — the radiation is controlled at the surface, not the bulk. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice) > **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** > **(a) Starting from the Navier-Stokes equations, derive Lighthill's acoustic analogy and clearly identify the three physical contributions to Lighthill's stress tensor. State which term is the focus of the Powell-Howe vortex-sound theory. (7 marks)** > **(b) Derive Howe's vortex-sound wave equation and explain how it differs from Powell's original formulation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** - Start with the continuity equation and the Navier-Stokes momentum equation for a compressible, viscous fluid. - Combine them and eliminate the momentum density using the material derivative to obtain: $$\rho \frac{D u_i}{D t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j}$$ - Introduce the speed-of-sound relation $c_0^2 = (\partial p / \partial \rho)_s$ and linearise about the ambient state $(p_0, \rho_0)$ to obtain the perturbation form. - Collect all non-linear terms into Lighthill's stress tensor: $$T_{ij} = \rho u_i u_j + \left[(p - p_0) - c_0^2(\rho - \rho_0)\right]\delta_{ij} - \sigma_{ij}$$ - Take the second divergence to obtain the inhomogeneous wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$ - Identify the three contributions: - **Reynolds-stress term** $\rho u_i u_j$ — the *vortical/convective* source. - **Enthalpy-excess term** $(p - c_0^2 \rho - p_0)\delta_{ij}$ — the *thermodynamic* source. - **Viscous-stress term** $-\sigma_{ij}$ — the *viscous* source. - **Powell-Howe focus (1 mark):** For isentropic, low-Mach automotive flows, the Reynolds-stress term dominates and represents the **vorticity contribution** — the source of vortex sound. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** - Begin with **Crocco's form** of the momentum equation for an inviscid, isentropic flow: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \nabla B = -\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$$ where $B$ is the total enthalpy and $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$ is the vorticity. - Linearise about the mean flow $(\mathbf{v}_0, B_0, \rho_0)$, with perturbations $(p', B', \mathbf{v}', \boldsymbol{\omega}')$: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} + \nabla B' = -\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}'$$ - Take the divergence of this equation and use the linearised continuity equation $\partial \rho'/\partial t + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = 0$: $$\frac{1}{\rho_0 c_0^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})$$ - Hence the **Howe vortex-sound wave equation**: $$\frac{1}{c_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})$$ - **Difference from Powell's original (2 marks):** Powell's 1964 formulation was restricted to *isentropic, uniform mean flow* and identified the source as $\rho_0 \, \partial(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})/\partial t$ via the Lighthill tensor. Howe's 1975 generalisation uses **Crocco's form** and is valid for *non-uniform mean flow* and *non-isentropic* conditions (e.g., engine-bay thermal gradients), and explicitly provides the **energy corollary** for power calculation. --- > **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** > **(a) State and derive Howe's energy corollary for vortex sound. Explain its physical significance with reference to the design of an automotive side-mirror. (7 marks)** > **(b) A side mirror of characteristic width $D = 0.12 \; \text{m}$ is exposed to oncoming airflow at $U = 33 \; \text{m/s}$. If the Strouhal number is $St = 0.21$ and the mean density is $\rho_0 = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m^3}$, estimate (i) the vortex-shedding frequency, (ii) the acoustic Mach number, and (iii) the approximate far-field SPL at $r = 1.0 \; \text{m}$ from the mirror. Take $c_0 = 340 \; \text{m/s}$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** - **Statement (1 mark):** Howe's energy corollary states that the rate of change of acoustic energy within a fluid volume equals the negative of the surface integral of $p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ (for incompressible mean flow): $$\frac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS$$ - **Derivation (4 marks):** - Start from the linearised Crocco equation $\partial \mathbf{v}'/\partial t + \nabla B' = -\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}'$. - Multiply by $\rho_0 \mathbf{v}'$ and integrate over the fluid volume $V$. - Use the product-rule identity $\rho_0 \mathbf{v}' \cdot \partial \mathbf{v}'/\partial t = \partial(\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_0 v'^2)/\partial t$. - Convert the $\rho_0 \mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla B'$ term using the divergence theorem: $\int_V \rho_0 \mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla B' \, dV = \oint_S \rho_0 B' \mathbf{v}' \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS - \int_V \rho_0 B' \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' \, dV$. - Apply the linearised continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = -(1/\rho_0 c_0^2) \partial p'/\partial t$ and recognise $\rho_0 B' = p'$. - Combine to obtain: $$\frac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS - \int_V p' \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \frac{1}{\rho} \, dV$$ - For incompressible mean flow the volume integral vanishes, giving Howe's corollary. - **Physical significance for side-mirror design (2 marks):** The corollary shows that **vortex-sound power is radiated through the mirror surface**. This explains why a small exterior mirror becomes a significant cabin-noise source: Kármán vortices shed from the mirror edges drive a strong surface-integral acoustic source. **Design strategies** such as *fillet edges*, *splitter plates*, *vortex spoilers*, and *tapered trailing edges* are chosen specifically to *weaken* $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ at the mirror surface, thereby reducing the radiated power. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** - **(i) Vortex-shedding frequency (2 marks):** $$f_s = \frac{St \cdot U}{D} = \frac{0.21 \times 33}{0.12} = \frac{6.93}{0.12} = 57.75 \;\text{Hz} \approx 57.8 \;\text{Hz}$$ - **(ii) Acoustic Mach number (2 marks):** $$M = \frac{U}{c_0} = \frac{33}{340} \approx 0.0971$$ - **(iii) Far-field SPL estimate (3 marks):** The Lighthill–Powell-Howe scaling for vortex-sound pressure is: $$p_{rms}' \sim \frac{\rho_0 U^2 M D}{r} = \frac{\rho_0 U^3 D}{r c_0}$$ Substituting values: $$p_{rms}' \approx \frac{1.2 \times 33^3 \times 0.12}{1.0 \times 340} = \frac{1.2 \times 35937 \times 0.12}{340} = \frac{5174.9}{340} \approx 15.22 \;\text{Pa}$$ SPL in decibels: $$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}'}{p_{ref}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{15.22}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(7.61 \times 10^{5}) \approx 117.6 \;\text{dB}$$ - **Interpretation (optional discussion):** A SPL of ≈ 118 dB at 1 m from the mirror is *very loud* — this confirms that even small exterior appendages can be dominant NVH sources in the cabin at highway speeds, validating the design of vortex-control devices. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the linearisation step:** Marks are awarded for explicitly linearising the Navier-Stokes / Crocco equation about the mean flow. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing Powell's and Howe's formulations:** Powell uses the Lighthill tensor (isentropic, uniform flow); Howe uses the Crocco form (general mean flow, includes non-isentropic effects). Confusing them loses up to 2 marks. > 3. **Missing the units in numerical problems:** Always include units in intermediate steps and the final answer. Marks are reserved for a clean, dimensionally consistent solution. > 4. **Not citing the Strouhal number relation $f_s = StU/D$:** This is a *must-mention* in any tonal-noise problem. > 5. **Mis-stating the source term:** The source is $\partial(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})/\partial t$ — not $\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ itself, and not just $\boldsymbol{\omega}$. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Lighthill's wave equation** is the parent framework; $T_{ij}$ contains Reynolds + enthalpy + viscous contributions. - **Powell's key insight:** The Reynolds-stress part of $T_{ij}$ is the source of **vortex sound**; the source is the time derivative of $\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v}$. - **Howe's wave equation:** $\dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = \rho_0 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{v})$. - **Howe's energy corollary:** $\dfrac{dE_a}{dt} = -\oint_S p' \mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS$ (incompressible limit). This is the single most important engineering result of the theory. - **Vortex sound requires unsteadiness:** A steady, rigidly translating vortex is silent. - **Vorticity is frozen into the fluid** (Helmholtz theorem, inviscid limit) — vortices are convected with the mean flow. - **Strouhal scaling:** $f_s = StU/D$ governs tonal aerodynamic noise from bluff bodies. - **Low-Mach-number assumption** is central — $M \ll 1$ justifies neglecting enthalpy-excess and viscous terms in the source. - **Automotive applications:** Side mirrors, A-pillars, antennas, sunroofs, HVAC blowers, intake/exhaust systems. - **Mitigation strategies:** Edge fillets, splitter plates, vortex spoilers, fairings, duct liners, Helmholtz resonators — all designed to weaken $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ at the source surface. - **Reference values to memorise:** $c_0 = 343 \; \text{m/s}$, $\rho_0 = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m^3}$, $Z_0 = 413$ rayl, $p_{ref} = 20 \; \mu\text{Pa}$. - **Master equations to write from memory in the exam:** (1) Lighthill's wave equation, (2) Howe's vortex-sound equation, (3) Howe's energy corollary.

topics.title: Acoustic Source Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Source Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acoustic Source Models** are mathematical idealizations used to characterize how sound is generated, radiated, and propagated by physical mechanisms. In the context of **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)**, these models reduce complex real-world sources — such as engine combustion, tire-road interaction, exhaust pulsations, and HVAC fans — into canonical mathematical equivalents that allow prediction of radiated sound fields, directivity patterns, and sound power levels.

The four fundamental canonical acoustic sources, ordered by increasing complexity and decreasing acoustic efficiency at low frequencies, are:

1. **Monopole Source** (Order 0) — a simple volume-velocity source.
2. **Dipole Source** (Order 1) — a force source (two out-of-phase monopoles).
3. **Lateral (Transverse) Quadrupole Source** (Order 2) — a shear-stress source.
4. **Longitudinal (Axial) Quadrupole Source** (Order 2) — a normal-stress source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Acoustic source models are the *foundation* for understanding directivity in noise measurement. Every automotive noise source (engine, exhaust, tire) can be approximated as a combination of these four basic source types.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at the surface of a pond and you tap the water periodically with your finger:
- If you push water **in and out** with a single finger (a small sphere expanding and contracting), ripples spread equally in all directions → this is a **Monopole**. It is the most efficient radiator of low-frequency sound.
- If you oscillate a **paddle** back and forth in water (like a tiny oar), the water is pushed one way as the paddle moves forward and the opposite way as it returns. The radiation pattern has a *figure-eight* shape → this is a **Dipole**.
- If two paddles oscillate perpendicular to the line connecting them → **Lateral Quadrupole**.
- If two paddles oscillate along the line connecting them → **Longitudinal Quadrupole**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In automotive applications, a vibrating engine block behaves like a **dipole** (force radiating), a tire cavity resonance acts like a **monopole** (volume pulsation), and turbulent flow in an exhaust pipe produces **quadrupole** radiation. The choice of model dramatically affects the predicted SPL (Sound Pressure Level) at low frequencies.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of sound in air at 20 °C:** $c_0 = \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$
- **Density of air at 20 °C:** $\rho_0 = \mathbf{1.204 \; kg/m^3}$
- **Characteristic acoustic impedance of air:** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0 = \mathbf{413 \; N\cdot s/m^3}$ (or **rayl** in CGS)
- **Reference sound pressure:** $p_{ref} = \mathbf{20 \; \mu Pa}$
- **Reference sound power:** $W_{ref} = \mathbf{10^{-12} \; W}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Sources — Structured Logic

### A. Monopole (Simple Source / Pulsating Sphere)

A monopole is a point source with spherically symmetric radiation. Physically realized as a small sphere whose radius pulsates uniformly, injecting volume velocity $Q$ into the surrounding medium.

**Why it matters:** Monopoles radiate sound omnidirectionally with maximum efficiency. In automotive NVH, **reflux valves**, **leaking gaskets**, and **cavity resonances** exhibit monopole behavior.

**Radiated Pressure Field:**

$$p(r,t) = \frac{j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4 \pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

where:
- $Q_0$ = peak volume velocity amplitude $[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$
- $k = \omega / c_0$ = acoustic wavenumber $[\text{rad/m}]$
- $r$ = radial distance from source $[\text{m}]$

**Mean Square Pressure:**

$$\overline{p^2} = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{32 \pi^2 r^2}$$

**Time-Average Radiated Sound Power:**

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{8 \pi c_0} = \frac{\rho_0 k^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{8 \pi}$$

### B. Dipole (Two Out-of-Phase Monopoles)

A dipole consists of two monopoles of equal magnitude but opposite phase, separated by an infinitesimal distance $d$.

**Why it matters:** Dipole sources model **force-excited surfaces** such as a vibrating engine block, a speaker cone without baffle, or aerodynamic forces on a mirror.

**Far-Field Pressure (for $kr \gg 1$):**

$$p(r,\theta,t) \approx \frac{j k \rho_0 c_0 Q_0 d \cos\theta}{4 \pi r^2} (jkr + 1) e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

For $kr \gg 1$, the dominant term simplifies to:

$$p(r,\theta,t) \approx \frac{-k^2 \rho_0 c_0 Q_0 d \cos\theta}{4 \pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

**Mean Square Pressure (far-field):**

$$\overline{p^2} = \frac{k^4 \rho_0^2 c_0^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2 \cos^2\theta}{32 \pi^2 r^2}$$

**Time-Average Radiated Sound Power (dipole):**

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^4 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2}{24 \pi c_0^3}$$

### C. Lateral (Transverse) Quadrupole

Formed by two dipoles oriented perpendicular to the axis joining them. Represents a **shear-stress source** with characteristic dimension $d$.

**Time-Average Radiated Sound Power:**

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4}{80 \pi c_0^5}$$

### D. Longitudinal (Axial) Quadrupole

Formed by two dipoles aligned along the axis joining them. Represents a **normal-stress source**.

**Time-Average Radiated Sound Power:**

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4}{40 \pi c_0^5}$$

## 2.2 Frequency Scaling — A Critical KTU Concept

Observe the **frequency-power exponents**:

| Source Type | Power ∝ $\omega^n$ | Low-Frequency Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Monopole | $\omega^2$ | Most efficient |
| Dipole | $\omega^4$ | Less efficient |
| Quadrupole (both) | $\omega^6$ | Least efficient |

This explains **Lighthill's acoustic analogy** for jet noise — turbulence produces quadrupole-type radiation, which is weak at low frequencies but dominant at high frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** At low engine RPMs (low frequencies), a vibrating panel (dipole) is *much quieter* than a hole/leak (monopole) producing the same volume displacement. This is why sealing leaks on a vehicle can yield 10–20 dB reductions at low frequencies.

## 2.3 Directivity and Source Strength

**Directivity Factor $Q_d$:** Ratio of the sound pressure squared at a specific point to the average over a sphere of the same radius.

**Directivity Index $DI$:**

$$DI = 10 \log_{10} Q_d \quad [\text{dB}]$$

| Source | Directivity Factor $Q_d$ | DI [dB] |
|---|---|---|
| Monopole | 1 | 0 |
| Dipole | 3 | 4.8 |
| Lateral Quadrupole | 5 | 7.0 |
| Longitudinal Quadrupole | 5 | 7.0 |
| Piston in infinite baffle (on-axis) | 2 (at low $ka$) | 3.0 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic wavenumber | $k = \omega / c_0 = 2\pi f / c_0$ | rad/m |
| 2 | Monopole radiated power | $\overline{W} = \rho_0 \omega^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 / (8\pi c_0)$ | W |
| 3 | Dipole radiated power | $\overline{W} = \rho_0 \omega^4 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2 / (24\pi c_0^3)$ | W |
| 4 | Lateral Quadrupole power | $\overline{W} = \rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4 / (80\pi c_0^5)$ | W |
| 5 | Longitudinal Quadrupole power | $\overline{W} = \rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4 / (40\pi c_0^5)$ | W |
| 6 | Monopole directivity | $Q_d = 1$ | — |
| 7 | Dipole directivity | $Q_d = 3$ | — |
| 8 | Sound power level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(\overline{W} / W_{ref})$ | dB |
| 9 | Sound pressure level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms} / p_{ref})$ | dB |
| 10 | Acoustic impedance | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0$ | Pa·s/m |
| 11 | Sound intensity (far field) | $I = \overline{p^2} / (\rho_0 c_0)$ | W/m² |
| 12 | Piston in baffle, on-axis | $p = j \omega \rho_0 U_0 A \cdot (e^{-jkr}) / (2\pi r) \cdot [J_1(ka\sin\theta)/(ka\sin\theta)]$ | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat on Notation:** In the formulae above, $\vert Q_0 \vert$ denotes the *modulus* (magnitude) of the complex volume velocity amplitude. In KTU valuation, students must NOT confuse this with absolute-value signs in a markdown table — use the modulus brackets $\vert \cdot \vert$ or modulus bars $mod$ as needed.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Automotive NVH

- **Monopole → Volume Velocity Sources:** Intake manifold pulsations, exhaust tailpipe radiation, HVAC duct leaks.
- **Dipole → Force-Driven Surfaces:** Engine block vibration, body panel excitation from road noise, side mirror aerodynamic forces.
- **Quadrupole → Turbulent Flows:** Tire cavity resonances at high frequencies, exhaust jet mixing, brake duct turbulence.
- **Higher-Order Multipoles:** Used in boundary element method (BEM) modeling to capture complex radiation from finite structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Radiated Sound Power from a Monopole

We begin from the **wave equation in spherical coordinates** for a pulsating sphere of radius $a$, surface velocity $u_0 = U_0 e^{j\omega t}$.

### Step 1: Governing Differential Equation

The acoustic pressure $p$ must satisfy the **Helmholtz equation**:

$$\nabla^2 p + k^2 p = 0$$

For spherical symmetry, the Laplacian reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right) + k^2 p = 0$$

### Step 2: General Solution

The general outgoing-wave solution (Sommerfeld radiation condition) is:

$$p(r) = \frac{A e^{-jkr}}{r}$$

The constant $A$ is determined from the **boundary condition at the sphere surface** ($r = a$):

$$u_r(a) = \left. \frac{1}{j\omega \rho_0} \frac{\partial p}{\partial r} \right|_{r=a} = U_0$$

### Step 3: Apply the Boundary Condition

Compute the radial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = A \left( \frac{-j k e^{-jkr}}{r} - \frac{e^{-jkr}}{r^2} \right) = A e^{-jkr} \left( \frac{-j k}{r} - \frac{1}{r^2} \right)$$

Substitute at $r = a$ and equate to $j\omega \rho_0 U_0$:

$$\frac{A e^{-jka}}{j\omega \rho_0} \left( \frac{-j k}{a} - \frac{1}{a^2} \right) = U_0$$

### Step 4: Solve for A (assuming $ka \ll 1$ — small source)

When $ka \ll 1$, the term $1/a^2$ dominates the parenthesis:

$$\frac{A e^{-jka}}{j\omega \rho_0} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{a^2} \right) \approx U_0$$

$$A \approx -j \omega \rho_0 U_0 a^2 e^{jka} \approx -j \omega \rho_0 U_0 a^2 \quad (\text{for } ka \to 0)$$

Recognize that the **volume velocity** $Q_0 = 4\pi a^2 U_0$:

$$A = -j \omega \rho_0 \frac{Q_0}{4\pi}$$

### Step 5: Final Pressure Field

Substituting back:

$$p(r) = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

Including the time dependence $e^{j\omega t}$:

$$p(r,t) = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4 \pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

(The negative sign on $j$ is conventional; many texts absorb it into the phase reference.)

### Step 6: Mean Square Pressure in Far Field

The complex pressure amplitude is $\hat{p} = \omega \rho_0 Q_0 / (4\pi r)$. Hence:

$$\overline{p^2} = \frac{\vert \hat{p} \vert^2}{2} = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{32 \pi^2 r^2}$$

### Step 7: Time-Average Radiated Power

The intensity on a sphere of radius $r$ is $I = \overline{p^2} / (\rho_0 c_0)$. Integrating over the sphere $4\pi r^2$:

$$\overline{W} = I \cdot 4 \pi r^2 = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{32 \pi^2 r^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_0 c_0} \cdot 4 \pi r^2$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{8 \pi c_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Note that the power is **independent of $r$** — energy is conserved, as expected for a lossless medium. The factor $\omega^2$ reveals that low-frequency monopole sources are very inefficient radiators.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dipole as a Derivative of a Monopole

A dipole is constructed from two monopoles of strength $+Q_0$ at $\vec{r}_+$ and $-Q_0$ at $\vec{r}_-$, separated by vector $\vec{d}$.

### Step 1: Superposed Pressure

$$p_{\text{dip}} = p_{\text{mono}}(\vec{r} - \vec{r}_+) - p_{\text{mono}}(\vec{r} - \vec{r}_-)$$

### Step 2: Taylor Expansion for Small d

For $d \ll r$ and observation angle $\theta$ from the dipole axis:

$$p_{\text{dip}} \approx d \cos\theta \cdot \frac{\partial p_{\text{mono}}}{\partial r}$$

### Step 3: Differentiate the Monopole Field

Starting from:

$$p_{\text{mono}}(r) = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

$$\frac{\partial p_{\text{mono}}}{\partial r} = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( \frac{e^{-jkr}}{r} \right) = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi} e^{-jkr} \left( \frac{-j k}{r} - \frac{1}{r^2} \right)$$

### Step 4: Far-Field Approximation (kr ≫ 1)

In the far field, the $1/r$ term dominates over the $1/r^2$ term:

$$\frac{\partial p_{\text{mono}}}{\partial r} \approx \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{-j k}{r} e^{-jkr} = \frac{-\omega \rho_0 k Q_0}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

### Step 5: Dipole Pressure Field

$$p_{\text{dip}}(r,\theta) \approx \frac{-\omega \rho_0 k Q_0 d \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} = \frac{-k^2 \rho_0 c_0 Q_0 d \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

(Using $\omega = k c_0$.)

### Step 6: Time-Average Power

The mean square pressure is:

$$\overline{p^2} = \frac{k^4 \rho_0^2 c_0^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2 \cos^2\theta}{32 \pi^2 r^2}$$

The intensity is $I = \overline{p^2} / (\rho_0 c_0)$. Power involves integrating $I \cos\alpha$ over a sphere, but the far-field direction is radial, so:

$$\overline{W} = \oint I \, dS = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \frac{k^4 \rho_0 c_0 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2 \cos^2\theta}{32 \pi^2 r^2} \cdot r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

### Step 7: Evaluate the Integral

$$\overline{W} = \frac{k^4 \rho_0 c_0 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2}{32 \pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \int_0^{\pi} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

$$\int_0^{\pi} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \left[ -\frac{\cos^3\theta}{3} \right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{1}{3} - \left(-\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{k^4 \rho_0 c_0 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2}{32 \pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{\rho_0 c_0 k^4 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2}{24 \pi}$$

Substituting $k = \omega / c_0$:

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^4 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2}{24 \pi c_0^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The integral $\int_0^{\pi} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = 2/3$ is a standard result. Students are expected to show the substitution $u = \cos\theta$ explicitly to earn full marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Monopole | Dipole | Lat. Quad. | Long. Quad. |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $kr$ fall-off (near) | $1/r$ | $1/r^2$ | $1/r^3$ | $1/r^3$ |
| $kr$ fall-off (far) | $1/r$ | $1/r$ | $1/r$ | $1/r$ |
| Frequency exponent | $\omega^2$ | $\omega^4$ | $\omega^6$ | $\omega^6$ |
| Directivity | Omni | $\cos^2\theta$ | $\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta$ | $\cos^4\theta$ |
| Source mechanism | Volume velocity | Force | Shear stress | Normal stress |
| Automotive example | Intake leak | Engine block | Tire cavity | Exhaust jet |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Source Family — Block Architecture

The four fundamental acoustic sources can be visualized as a hierarchical *family tree* based on construction from out-of-phase monopoles:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ACOUSTIC SOURCES] --> B[Order 0 Monopole]
    A --> C[Order 1 Dipole]
    A --> D[Order 2 Quadrupole]
    B --> B1[Pulsating Sphere]
    B --> B2[Reflux Valve Leak]
    B --> B3[Exhaust Tailpipe End]
    C --> C1[Two Antiphase Monopoles]
    C --> C2[Oscillating Paddle]
    C --> C3[Engine Block Vibration]
    C --> C4[Side Mirror Aero Force]
    D --> E[Lateral Quadrupole]
    D --> F[Longitudinal Quadrupole]
    E --> E1[Two Antiphase Dipoles]
    E --> E2[Shear Stress Source]
    E --> E3[Tire Cavity Resonance]
    F --> F1[Two Antiphase Dipoles Aligned]
    F --> F2[Normal Stress Source]
    F --> F3[Exhaust Jet Turbulence]
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Scaling Behavior of Power Output

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Radiated Power vs Frequency] --> M[Monopole omega squared]
    P --> D[Dipole omega to the 4]
    P --> Q[Quadrupole omega to the 6]
    M --> MI[Low frequency dominant]
    D --> DI[Mid frequency]
    Q --> QI[High frequency dominant]
    MI --> APP1[Engine intake leak]
    DI --> APP2[Vibrating panels]
    QI --> APP3[Turbulent jets]
```

## 4.3 Source-to-Noise Application Mapping (Automotive Context)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SOURCE[Acoustic Source Model] --> PICK[Selection Logic]
    PICK --> PICK1[Identify mechanism]
    PICK --> PICK2[Determine frequency range]
    PICK --> PICK3[Measure directivity pattern]
    PICK1 --> MECH[Volume, Force, Stress]
    PICK2 --> FRQ[Low, Mid, High]
    PICK3 --> DIR[Omni, Cosine, Higher]
    MECH --> APP{Match to application}
    FRQ --> APP
    DIR --> APP
    APP --> APP1[Intake Manifold - Monopole]
    APP --> APP2[Engine Block - Dipole]
    APP --> APP3[Exhaust Jet - Quadrupole]
    APP --> APP4[Tire Cavity - Quadrupole]
```

    RP --> DIPO[Dipole Pattern]

The **piston in an infinite rigid baffle** is a critical source model for loudspeakers, vibrating panels, and engine covers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define an acoustic monopole source. Give one automotive example of a source that behaves approximately as a monopole.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An acoustic monopole is the simplest source model: a point that injects a time-varying volume velocity $Q(t)$ into the surrounding fluid, producing spherically symmetric outgoing waves. The radiated pressure field is $p(r,t) = \frac{j\omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$.

*Automotive example (1 mark):* A **leaking intake manifold gasket** or **reflux valve cavity** behaves as a monopole, since fluid is being injected/withdrawn isotropically into the air.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain why a dipole source radiates less efficiently at low frequencies than a monopole source.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The radiated sound power scales as $\omega^2$ for a monopole and $\omega^4$ for a dipole, both for the same volume velocity $Q_0$. Hence at low frequencies, the dipole power is smaller by a factor of $\omega^2$ (i.e., **−40 dB/decade** for dipole vs **−20 dB/decade** for monopole). Physically, the two out-of-phase monopoles in a dipole partially cancel in the far field, suppressing low-frequency radiation. **(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for comparison, 1 for physical reason).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Starting from the Helmholtz equation in spherical coordinates, derive the far-field pressure radiated by a pulsating sphere of radius $a$ oscillating with surface velocity $U_0 e^{j\omega t}$. Hence obtain the time-average radiated sound power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small engine component (modeled as a monopole) oscillates at $f = 200 \; \text{Hz}$ with peak volume velocity $Q_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate: (i) the acoustic wavenumber, (ii) the time-average radiated power, and (iii) the sound power level. Take $\rho_0 = 1.204 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ and $c_0 = 343 \; \text{m/s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Write the Helmholtz equation in spherical coordinates assuming spherical symmetry:

$$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r^2 \frac{dp}{dr} \right) + k^2 p = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the substitution $p(r) = \hat{p}(r) / r$ to convert to a 1D Helmholtz equation. The general outgoing-wave solution is:

$$p(r) = \frac{A e^{-jkr}}{r}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the boundary condition at $r = a$ (radial particle velocity equals $U_0$):

$$u_r(a) = \frac{1}{j\omega \rho_0} \left. \frac{dp}{dr} \right|_{a} = U_0$$

Computing $\frac{dp}{dr} = -\frac{A}{r^2} e^{-jkr} - \frac{jkA}{r} e^{-jkr}$ and assuming $ka \ll 1$ (compact source), the $1/r^2$ term dominates. Solving gives:

$$A = -j \omega \rho_0 U_0 a^2 = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi}$$

where $Q_0 = 4\pi a^2 U_0$.

**Step 4:** The far-field pressure:

$$p(r,t) = \frac{-j \omega \rho_0 Q_0}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

**Step 5:** The mean-square pressure magnitude is $\overline{p^2} = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{32 \pi^2 r^2}$.

**Step 6:** Intensity $I = \overline{p^2}/(\rho_0 c_0)$. Power $W = I \cdot 4\pi r^2$:

$$\overline{W} = \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{8\pi c_0}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Wavenumber:**

$$k = \frac{2\pi f}{c_0} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 200}{343} = 3.664 \;\text{rad/m}$$

**(ii) Radiated power:**

$$\overline{W} = \frac{(2\pi \cdot 200)^2 \cdot 1.204 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{8\pi \cdot 343}$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{(1.5708 \times 10^6) \cdot 1.204 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{8613.5}$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{0.4725}{8613.5} = 5.486 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{W}$$

**(iii) Sound power level:**

$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{5.486 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(5.486 \times 10^{7})$$

$$L_W = 10 \cdot (7 + \log_{10} 5.486) = 10 \cdot (7 + 0.739) = 77.4 \;\text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:** Common errors include (1) forgetting the factor of $1/2$ in converting peak amplitude to RMS (or vice versa) — both $Q_0$ as peak or RMS must be consistent; (2) using $\omega = 2\pi f$ incorrectly; (3) forgetting the $\rho_0 c_0$ in the intensity formula. Also, students often write $L_W = 10 \log(\overline{W})$ forgetting the $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W reference.

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Pattern — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With neat mathematical expressions, explain the construction of a dipole source from two monopoles. Derive the far-field pressure for a dipole oriented along the z-axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the radiated sound power of a monopole, dipole, and quadrupole. For an automotive engine compartment source, comment on the dominant source type at (i) idle (≈ 800 rpm, fundamental ≈ 13 Hz) and (ii) cruising (≈ 3000 rpm, fundamental ≈ 50 Hz), assuming a characteristic source dimension of $d = 0.1 \; \text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** A dipole is formed by placing a monopole of strength $+Q_0$ at $(0,0,+d/2)$ and a monopole of strength $-Q_0$ at $(0,0,-d/2)$.

**Step 2:** The total pressure is:

$$p = p_{\text{mono}}(\vec{r} - \vec{r}_+) - p_{\text{mono}}(\vec{r} - \vec{r}_-)$$

**Step 3:** For $d \ll r$, Taylor expansion gives:

$$p \approx d \cos\theta \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial r} p_{\text{mono}}(r)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the monopole field and using the far-field approximation ($kr \gg 1$):

$$p_{\text{dip}}(r,\theta,t) = \frac{-k^2 \rho_0 c_0 Q_0 d \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

**Step 5:** The cosine factor creates a figure-eight directivity — zero on the equatorial plane ($\theta = 90°$) and maximum on the dipole axis.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Write the three power formulae for a unit $Q_0$ and source dimension $d$:

- Monopole: $W_M = \dfrac{\rho_0 \omega^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2}{8\pi c_0}$
- Dipole: $W_D = \dfrac{\rho_0 \omega^4 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2}{24\pi c_0^3}$
- Quadrupole: $W_Q = \dfrac{\rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4}{80\pi c_0^5}$ (lateral)

**Step 2:** Compute the dipole-to-monopole power ratio at idle ($f = 13$ Hz, $\omega = 81.68$ rad/s, $d = 0.1$ m):

$$\frac{W_D}{W_M} = \frac{\omega^2 d^2}{3 c_0^2} = \frac{(81.68)^2 (0.1)^2}{3 (343)^2} = \frac{66.72}{3 \cdot 117649} = 1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$

In dB: $10 \log_{10}(1.89 \times 10^{-4}) = -37.2$ dB. **Dipole is 37 dB weaker than monopole at idle.**

**Step 3:** At cruising ($f = 50$ Hz, $\omega = 314.16$ rad/s):

$$\frac{W_D}{W_M} = \frac{(314.16)^2 (0.1)^2}{3 (343)^2} = \frac{986.96}{353068} = 2.80 \times 10^{-3}$$

In dB: $-25.5$ dB. **Dipole is 25.5 dB weaker than monopole at cruising.**

**Step 4:** Quadrupole-to-monopole ratio at cruising:

$$\frac{W_Q}{W_M} = \frac{\omega^4 d^4}{10 c_0^4} = \frac{(314.16)^4 (0.1)^4}{10 (343)^4} = \frac{9.74 \times 10^8 \cdot 10^{-4}}{1.385 \times 10^{11}} = 7.04 \times 10^{-7}$$

In dB: $-61.5$ dB. **Quadrupole is 61.5 dB weaker than monopole at cruising.**

**Step 5:** Conclusion: For these low frequencies, the **monopole mechanism dominates** for any equivalent $Q_0$. The engine's surface vibration (dipole) becomes comparable in importance only when the structure actually moves significant volume of air (e.g., via leak paths or cavity resonances). Therefore, the engineer should **focus on sealing potential monopole sources** (gaskets, breather tubes) for low-frequency noise reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** Two common errors: (1) Students sometimes write the dipole field as $p \propto 1/r^2$ without specifying it is the **near-field** form — in the far field, both monopole and dipole fall off as $1/r$. (2) In part (b), students may forget to convert engine RPM to frequency via $f = (\text{RPM}/60) \times (\text{number of cylinders}/2)$ for a 4-stroke engine — for a 4-cylinder engine, the firing frequency is $\text{RPM}/30$, not $\text{RPM}/60$. Always state your assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Acoustic Source Models**

- [x] **Monopole** = a point source of volume velocity $Q$; simplest acoustic radiator; omnidirectional; $W \propto \omega^2$.
- [x] **Dipole** = two antiphase monopoles infinitesimally close; figure-eight directivity ($\cos^2\theta$); $W \propto \omega^4$.
- [x] **Lateral Quadrupole** = two antiphase dipoles perpendicular to their connecting axis; $W \propto \omega^6$.
- [x] **Longitudinal Quadrupole** = two antiphase dipoles along their connecting axis; $W \propto \omega^6$ (larger constant).
- [x] **Sound Power Formulae (memorize):**
  - Monopole: $W = \rho_0 \omega^2 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 / (8\pi c_0)$
  - Dipole: $W = \rho_0 \omega^4 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^2 / (24\pi c_0^3)$
  - Lateral Quadrupole: $W = \rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4 / (80\pi c_0^5)$
  - Longitudinal Quadrupole: $W = \rho_0 \omega^6 \vert Q_0 \vert^2 d^4 / (40\pi c_0^5)$
- [x] **Directivity Indices:** Monopole = 0 dB, Dipole ≈ 4.8 dB, Quadrupole ≈ 7.0 dB.
- [x] **Lighthill's Law (frequency scaling):** $W \propto \omega^{2(n+1)}$ where $n$ = source order (0, 1, 2).
- [x] **Reference values:** $p_{ref} = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa}$, $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W, $Z_0 = 413$ N·s/m³ (air).
- [x] **Automotive mapping:** Volume leak → Monopole; Vibrating surface → Dipole; Turbulent jet → Quadrupole.
- [x] **Piston in infinite baffle** directivity: $D(\theta) = 2 J_1(ka\sin\theta) / (ka\sin\theta)$.
- [x] **Boundary condition trick:** at a compact source ($ka \ll 1$), the $1/r^2$ term in the velocity dominates over the $1/r$ term — this is how monopole strength $A$ is extracted.
- [x] **Integral to remember:** $\int_0^{\pi} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = 2/3$ (for dipole power).
- [x] **Conversion:** $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / 10^{-12})$; $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms} / 20 \times 10^{-6})$.

---
