topics.title: Monopoles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Monopoles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH)**, a **Monopole** (also called a *simple spherical source* or *point source*) is the most fundamental class of acoustic radiator. Mathematically, it is defined as a point in a fluid medium that injects a uniform, time-varying volume velocity $Q(t)$ into the surrounding space, producing perfectly **spherical** pressure waves that radiate omnidirectionally.

Formally, a monopole satisfies the inhomogeneous wave equation:

$$\nabla^{2} p - \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = -\rho_{0} \frac{\partial Q}{\partial t} \delta(\mathbf{r})$$

where $p$ is the acoustic pressure, $c$ is the **speed of sound in air (≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C)**, $\rho_{0}$ is the **mean density of air (≈ 1.21 kg/m³)**, $Q$ is the volume velocity, and $\delta(\mathbf{r})$ is the Dirac delta function locating the source at the origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Monopoles form the **fundamental building block** of all acoustic source theory. The KTU board frequently tests the relationship between a monopole's pressure field, intensity field, and radiated sound power. Mastery of the *1/r* pressure decay and *1/r²* intensity decay is mandatory.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine dropping a small pebble into the still surface of a pond. The ripples spread outward as perfect concentric circles. Now lift that picture into **three dimensions** and imagine a tiny pulsating balloon underwater that rhythmically expands and contracts.

- **The balloon** is the *monopole source*.
- **The pulsation rate** is the *frequency* of sound.
- **The water (or air)** is the *transmission medium*.
- **The 3D ripples** are *spherical sound waves*.

Because the balloon pushes air equally in every direction, a microphone placed 1 m away will measure the *same loudness* as a microphone placed 1 m away on the opposite side. This is what we mean by **omnidirectional radiation** — the defining hallmark of an ideal monopole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world automotive monopole examples:**
> - The **pulsating end of an open exhaust pipe** (especially at low engine RPMs, where the tailpipe behaves like a Helmholtz-like aperture).
> - **Cavity resonance** of a tire on a smooth road (the trapped air volume pulses as the tread contacts the surface).
> - **HVAC blower inlets** in the passenger cabin.
> - **Refrigerant pulsations** in compressor discharge lines.

## Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (Air, 20 °C) |
|--------|----------|------------------------------|
| $c$ | Speed of sound | **343 m/s** |
| $\rho_{0}$ | Density of air | **1.21 kg/m³** |
| $Z_{0} = \rho_{0} c$ | Characteristic acoustic impedance | **≈ 415 Pa·s/m** |
| $k = \omega/c$ | Acoustic wave number | rad/m |
| $p_{0}$ | Reference sound pressure | **20 µPa** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Mechanics of an Acoustic Monopole

A monopole is the mathematical idealization of any source that is **small compared to the wavelength** of the sound it radiates ($d \ll \lambda$). Under this *compact source* assumption, we can treat the entire source as a single point at the origin.

### Step-by-Step Physical Logic

1. **Source Volume Velocity Q(t)**
   - The monopole alternately *injects* and *withdraws* a small volume of fluid per unit time.
   - For a harmonic source: $Q(t) = \hat{Q} \, e^{j \omega t}$.
   - Units: **m³/s**.

2. **Continuity of Mass**
   - The injected fluid displaces the surrounding air, creating a compression.
   - The compression propagates outward as a longitudinal pressure wave.

3. **Spherical Symmetry**
   - Because the source is point-like and isotropic, the pressure field depends *only* on the radial distance $r$ from the origin.
   - No $\theta$ or $\phi$ dependence exists.

4. **Near-Field vs. Far-Field Behavior**
   - **Near field** ($kr \ll 1$, i.e., $r \ll \lambda / 2\pi$): pressure and particle velocity are **90° out of phase**; little net acoustic energy is transported outward (energy oscillates back and forth).
   - **Far field** ($kr \gg 1$, i.e., $r \gg \lambda / 2\pi$): pressure and particle velocity are **in phase**; energy propagates steadily outward — this is the region relevant to NVH measurements.

5. **Inverse-Distance Decay**
   - Because the same total acoustic power must pass through ever-larger spherical surfaces ($A = 4\pi r^{2}$), the intensity must fall as $1/r^{2}$.
   - Pressure, being the square root of intensity, falls as $1/r$.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Decay with $r$ |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Acoustic pressure (complex) | $p(r) = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} \, e^{-j k r}$ | Pa | $1/r$ |
| 2 | RMS pressure (far field) | $p_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi r}$ | Pa | $1/r$ |
| 3 | Particle velocity (far field) | $u(r) = \dfrac{j k \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} \, e^{-j k r}$ | m/s | $1/r$ |
| 4 | Acoustic intensity | $I(r) = \dfrac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2} c}$ | W/m² | $1/r^{2}$ |
| 5 | Radiated sound power | $W = \dfrac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} c k^{2} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi}$ | W | **constant** |
| 6 | Sound Power Level | $L_{W} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{0}}\right)$, $W_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W | dB | — |
| 7 | Sound Pressure Level (far field) | $L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{0}}\right)$, $p_{0} = 20 \, \mu$Pa | dB | decreases **6 dB per doubling of distance** |
| 8 | Source strength (monopole strength) | $Q = S \, u_{n}$ (aperture area $\times$ normal velocity) | m³/s | — |
| 9 | Compactness criterion | $k a \ll 1$, or $a \ll \lambda / 6$ | — | — |
| 10 | Directivity factor (monopole) | $Q_{\text{dir}} = 1$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The "**6 dB per doubling of distance**" rule is a *hallmark* of monopole radiation. In automotive pass-by-noise testing (ISO 362), an engineer must use a microphone at the standardized 7.5 m distance; if they mistakenly halve the distance, the measured SPL rises by 6 dB — an enormous, and often non-physical, increase.

## Why the $1/r$ Decay? — The Spherical Spreading Law

Consider a sphere of radius $r$ centered on the monopole. The total radiated acoustic power $W$ must pass through the spherical surface of area $A = 4 \pi r^{2}$. Therefore:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^{2}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}$$

Since acoustic intensity is proportional to the square of RMS pressure, $I \propto p_{\text{rms}}^{2}$, we get $p_{\text{rms}} \propto 1/r$, and the Sound Pressure Level decreases as $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ dB per doubling of distance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Acoustic Pressure Field of a Monopole

We start from the **inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation** (the wave equation in the frequency domain) for a point source located at the origin:

$$\nabla^{2} p + k^{2} p = -j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q} \, \delta(\mathbf{r})$$

Because the source is point-like, we work in **spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$, and the solution must depend only on $r$. The Laplacian simplifies to:

$$\nabla^{2} p(r) = \frac{1}{r^{2}} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right)$$

Substituting into the Helmholtz equation (away from the source, $\delta(\mathbf{r}) = 0$):

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right) + k^{2} p = 0$$

**Multiplying through by $r^{2}$** to clear the denominator:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right) + k^{2} r^{2} p = 0$$

We attempt a **trial solution** of the form $p(r) = \dfrac{A \, e^{-j k r}}{r}$. Differentiating once:

$$\frac{dp}{dr} = A \left( \frac{-j k \, e^{-j k r}}{r} - \frac{e^{-j k r}}{r^{2}} \right) = A \, e^{-j k r} \left( \frac{-j k r - 1}{r^{2}} \right)$$

Multiplying by $r^{2}$:

$$r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} = A \, e^{-j k r} (-j k r - 1)$$

Differentiating once more:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right) = A \left[ -j k \, e^{-j k r}(-j k r - 1) + e^{-j k r}(-j k) \right]$$

Simplifying the bracket:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right) = A \, e^{-j k r} \left[ k^{2} r + j k - j k \right] = A \, e^{-j k r} \, k^{2} r$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right) + k^{2} r^{2} p = A \, e^{-j k r} k^{2} r + k^{2} r^{2} \cdot \frac{A \, e^{-j k r}}{r} = A k^{2} r \, e^{-j k r} + A k^{2} r \, e^{-j k r} = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

The trial solution **satisfies** the Helmholtz equation. The constant $A$ is determined by enforcing the source-strength boundary condition: integrating the wave equation over a small sphere of radius $r_{0} \to 0$ around the source gives:

$$A = \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi}$$

**Final result — the monopole pressure field:**

$$\boxed{\, p(r) = \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} \, e^{-j k r} \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $e^{-jkr}$ is the **outward-traveling wave term**; we have discarded the $e^{+jkr}$ (inward-traveling) solution on physical grounds, since energy must flow *away* from the source.

## Derivation 2 — Acoustic Intensity and Radiated Power

The **acoustic particle velocity** in the far field is obtained from Euler's linearized equation:

$$-j \omega \rho_{0} u = \frac{dp}{dr} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u(r) = \frac{j}{\omega \rho_{0}} \frac{dp}{dr}$$

Differentiating $p(r)$:

$$\frac{dp}{dr} = \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( \frac{e^{-j k r}}{r} \right) = \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi} \, e^{-j k r} \left( \frac{-j k r - 1}{r^{2}} \right)$$

In the **far field** ($kr \gg 1$), the $-1$ term is negligible compared to $-j k r$:

$$\frac{dp}{dr} \approx \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{-j k \, e^{-j k r}}{r} = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q} k}{4 \pi r} e^{-j k r}$$

Substituting back into Euler's equation:

$$u(r) = \frac{j}{\omega \rho_{0}} \cdot \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q} k}{4 \pi r} e^{-j k r} = \frac{j k \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} e^{-j k r}$$

**Acoustic intensity** is the time-averaged product of pressure and velocity (real part of $\tfrac{1}{2} p u^{*}$):

$$I(r) = \frac{1}{2} \Re\!\left( p \, u^{*} \right)$$

Computing $u^{*}$ (complex conjugate):

$$u^{*} = -\frac{j k \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} e^{+j k r}$$

Therefore:

$$p \, u^{*} = \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} e^{-j k r} \cdot \left( -\frac{j k \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} e^{+j k r} \right) = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2} k}{16 \pi^{2} r^{2}}$$

Note that $j \cdot (-j) = +1$ and $e^{-jkr} \cdot e^{+jkr} = 1$. Taking the real part (the expression is already real) and halving:

$$\boxed{\, I(r) = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2} k}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2}} = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2} c} \,}$$

**Total radiated power** is the intensity integrated over any closed surface, conveniently a sphere of radius $r$:

$$W = \oint I \, dA = I(r) \cdot 4 \pi r^{2} = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2} c} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\, W = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c} = \frac{\rho_{0} c k^{2} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical observation:** $W$ is **independent of $r$** — energy is conserved! The intensity may fall as $1/r^{2}$, but the *total* power passing through each spherical shell is identical. This is the acoustic analog of Gauss's law for electric fields.

## Derivation 3 — The 6 dB per Doubling of Distance Rule

The Sound Pressure Level at distance $r_{1}$ is:

$$L_{p}(r_{1}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r_{1})}{p_{0}} \right)$$

At a doubled distance $r_{2} = 2 r_{1}$, the RMS pressure is halved: $p_{\text{rms}}(r_{2}) = p_{\text{rms}}(r_{1}) / 2$. Therefore:

$$L_{p}(r_{2}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r_{1})}{2 p_{0}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r_{1})}{p_{0}} \right) - 20 \log_{10}(2)$$

$$L_{p}(r_{2}) = L_{p}(r_{1}) - 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

Equivalently, **halving the distance** to a monopole increases SPL by 6 dB. This rule is the *first* thing an automotive NVH engineer checks when interpreting microphone traverses around a vehicle.

## Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A tire cavity behaves as a monopole of strength $\hat{Q} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, oscillating at $f = 250$ Hz. Find the radiated sound power and the SPL at $r = 10$ m. Take $\rho_{0} = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi (250) = 1570.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Radiated power:**

$$W = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c} = \frac{(1570.8)^{2} (1.21) (5 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{8 \pi (343)}$$

Evaluating numerator:

$$(1570.8)^{2} = 2.4674 \times 10^{6}$$

$$2.4674 \times 10^{6} \times 1.21 = 2.9856 \times 10^{6}$$

$$2.9856 \times 10^{6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 0.7464$$

Denominator:

$$8 \pi \times 343 = 8620.6$$

$$W = \frac{0.7464}{8620.6} = 8.66 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 86.6 \; \mu\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — SPL at 10 m:**

First, RMS pressure:

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi r} = \frac{(1570.8)(1.21)(5 \times 10^{-4})}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, (10)}$$

Numerator:

$$1570.8 \times 1.21 = 1900.7, \quad 1900.7 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9503$$

Denominator:

$$4 \sqrt{2} \pi \times 10 = 177.7$$

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{0.9503}{177.7} = 5.348 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} = 5.348 \text{ mPa}$$

**Step 4 — SPL:**

$$L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{5.348 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(267.4) = 20 \times 2.427 = 48.5 \text{ dB}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Doubling the distance to 20 m should give $L_{p} \approx 48.5 - 6 = 42.5$ dB. The 6-dB rule holds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Monopole Source

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Excitation Input] --> B[Source Region d less than lambda over 6]
    B --> C[Time Varying Volume Velocity Q of t]
    C --> D[Inhomogeneous Wave Equation Solver]
    D --> E[Spherical Pressure Field p of r = j omega rho Q over 4 pi r times e to power negative j k r]
    E --> F[Near Field Region kr less than 1 Reactive Energy Storage]
    E --> G[Far Field Region kr greater than 1 Propagating Waves]
    F --> H[Intensity I of r proportional to 1 over r squared]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Total Radiated Power W constant with r]
    I --> J[Sound Power Level L subscript W in dB]
    H --> K[SPL at Microphone L subscript P in dB minus 6 dB per doubling of r]
```

## Figure 2 — Concept Map of Monopole Physics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Compact Source d much less than lambda] --> A2[Volume Velocity Q]
    A2 --> A3[Wave Equation Solution]
    A3 --> A4[Pressure p proportional to 1 over r]
    A3 --> A5[Velocity u proportional to 1 over r]
    A4 --> A6[Intensity I proportional to 1 over r squared]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Power W is constant]
    A7 --> A8[Spherical Spreading Law]
    A8 --> A9[6 dB drop per doubling of distance]
    A2 --> B1[Automotive Example Exhaust Tailpipe]
    A2 --> B2[Automotive Example Tire Cavity]
    A2 --> B3[Automotive Example HVAC Inlet]
```

## Figure 3 — Far-Field Spherical Wave Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Monopole at Origin] -->|distance r| S1[Spherical Surface A1 equals 4 pi r1 squared]
    P -->|distance 2r| S2[Spherical Surface A2 equals 4 pi 2r squared = 16 pi r squared]
    S1 --> I1[Intensity I1 proportional to W over 4 pi r squared]
    S2 --> I2[Intensity I2 proportional to W over 16 pi r squared equals I1 over 4]
    I1 --> SPL1[SPL1 in dB]
    I2 --> SPL2[SPL2 equals SPL1 minus 6 dB]
```

## Figure 4 — Near-Field vs. Far-Field Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Monopole Source] -->|kr less than 1| NF[Near Field Region]
    R -->|kr approximately equal to 1| TR[Transition Region]
    R -->|kr greater than 1| FF[Far Field Region]
    NF --> NF1[Pressure and Velocity 90 degrees out of phase]
    NF --> NF2[Reactive Energy Oscillates]
    TR --> TR1[Mixed Behavior]
    FF --> FF1[Pressure and Velocity In Phase]
    FF --> FF2[Active Energy Propagates Outward]
    FF --> FF3[Standard 6 dB per Doubling Rule Applies]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram interpretation tip:** For KTU board answers, sketching a **sphere of radius $r$** around the monopole with a labeled outward arrow $p(r)$ and explicitly writing the area $A = 4\pi r^{2}$ instantly earns you full marks on directivity-related sub-parts.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an acoustic monopole. State two practical examples encountered in an automobile.**

**Model Answer:**
An acoustic monopole is the simplest idealized sound source that radiates sound waves uniformly in all directions, characterized by a time-varying volume velocity $Q(t)$ injected at a point. It obeys spherical symmetry and produces a pressure field that decays as $1/r$ with radial distance $r$ in the far field.

**Automotive examples:**
1. **Pulsating end of an exhaust tailpipe** at low engine RPMs, where the exit aperture acts as a compact monopole.
2. **Tire air-cavity resonance** during road contact, where trapped air pulses as a compact monopole at low frequencies.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula or $1/r$ behavior, 1 mark for two examples.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**For a monopole source, show that the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) decreases by 6 dB for every doubling of distance in the far field.**

**Model Answer:**
In the far field, the RMS pressure of a monopole is $p_{\text{rms}} \propto 1/r$. When distance doubles from $r$ to $2r$, the pressure halves:

$$\Delta L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r)}{p_{\text{rms}}(2r)} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6 \text{ dB}$$

Hence the SPL **decreases by 6 dB** per doubling of distance, known as the **spherical spreading law**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the acoustic pressure field $p(r)$ of a simple monopole source radiating into free air, starting from the inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation. Clearly state the compactness condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small electric coolant pump operating at $f = 800$ Hz behaves approximately as a monopole of strength $\hat{Q} = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Compute: (i) the total radiated sound power, and (ii) the Sound Pressure Level at 3 m, given $\rho_{0} = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s, $p_{0} = 20\;\mu$Pa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Compactness condition:** The source dimension $a$ must satisfy $a \ll \lambda$, equivalently $k a \ll 1$. This ensures the source radiates as a *point* with spherical symmetry.

**Inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation:**

$$\nabla^{2} p + k^{2} p = -j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q} \, \delta(\mathbf{r})$$

In spherical coordinates and away from the source ($\delta = 0$):

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^{2} \frac{dp}{dr} \right) + k^{2} p = 0$$

We try a solution $p(r) = \dfrac{A \, e^{-j k r}}{r}$, representing an outward-traveling spherical wave. Substituting and simplifying (as derived earlier in Section 3):

$$p(r) = \frac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} \, e^{-j k r}$$

The constant $A = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi}$ is found by integrating the wave equation over an infinitesimal sphere around the origin and applying the source-strength boundary condition.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Angular frequency and wave number:**

$$\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi (800) = 5026.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$k = \frac{\omega}{c} = \frac{5026.5}{343} = 14.65 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Step 2 — Radiated power:**

$$W = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c} = \frac{(5026.5)^{2} (1.21) (8 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}{8 \pi (343)}$$

Numerator: $(5026.5)^{2} = 2.527 \times 10^{7}$, multiplied by $1.21 = 3.057 \times 10^{7}$, multiplied by $6.4 \times 10^{-9} = 0.1957$.

Denominator: $8 \pi \times 343 = 8620.6$.

$$W = \frac{0.1957}{8620.6} = 2.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 22.7 \; \mu\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — RMS pressure at 3 m:**

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi r} = \frac{(5026.5)(1.21)(8 \times 10^{-5})}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, (3)}$$

Numerator: $5026.5 \times 1.21 = 6082.1$, $\times 8 \times 10^{-5} = 0.4866$.

Denominator: $4 \sqrt{2} \pi \times 3 = 53.31$.

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{0.4866}{53.31} = 9.13 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} = 9.13 \text{ mPa}$$

**Step 4 — SPL:**

$$L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{9.13 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(456.4) = 20 \times 2.659 = 53.2 \text{ dB}$$

**Final answers:**
- (i) $W = 22.7 \; \mu$W
- (ii) $L_{p}(3\;\text{m}) = 53.2$ dB

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the spherical spreading law for a monopole. Prove that the total radiated acoustic power is independent of distance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car's HVAC inlet is modeled as a monopole of strength $\hat{Q} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s at $f = 400$ Hz. Calculate the Sound Power Level $L_{W}$ and the SPL at 1 m and 4 m. Take $\rho_{0} = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s, $W_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W, $p_{0} = 20\;\mu$Pa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Diagram description (sphere of radius $r$ around monopole with arrows of intensity $I$):**

A spherical surface of area $A = 4\pi r^{2}$ encloses the monopole. The acoustic intensity $I(r)$ is uniform over this surface due to spherical symmetry. The total radiated power is:

$$W = I(r) \cdot 4\pi r^{2}$$

Substituting the monopole far-field intensity:

$$I(r) = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2} c}$$

Therefore:

$$W = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2} c} \times 4 \pi r^{2} = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c}$$

**The $r^{2}$ in the denominator and the $r^{2}$ from the spherical area cancel exactly**, leaving a result that is **independent of $r$**. This proves that acoustic power is conserved: the same total power passes through every concentric sphere, even though the local intensity weakens with distance.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Radiated power:**

$$\omega = 2\pi(400) = 2513.3 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$W = \frac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c} = \frac{(2513.3)^{2} (1.21) (2 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{8 \pi (343)}$$

Numerator: $(2513.3)^{2} = 6.317 \times 10^{6}$, $\times 1.21 = 7.643 \times 10^{6}$, $\times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 0.3057$.

Denominator: $8620.6$.

$$W = \frac{0.3057}{8620.6} = 3.547 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Sound Power Level:**

$$L_{W} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{3.547 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-12}} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(3.547 \times 10^{7}) = 10 \times 7.550 = 75.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — SPL at 1 m:**

$$p_{\text{rms}}(1) = \frac{\omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi (1)} = \frac{(2513.3)(1.21)(2 \times 10^{-4})}{4 \sqrt{2} \, \pi}$$

Numerator: $2513.3 \times 1.21 = 3041.1$, $\times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.6082$.

Denominator: $4 \sqrt{2} \pi = 17.77$.

$$p_{\text{rms}}(1) = \frac{0.6082}{17.77} = 0.0342 \text{ Pa} = 34.2 \text{ mPa}$$

$$L_{p}(1) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{0.0342}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(1710) = 20 \times 3.233 = 64.7 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — SPL at 4 m (using 6-dB rule):**

Distance increased by factor of 4 (= $2^{2}$), so SPL drops by $2 \times 6 = 12$ dB:

$$L_{p}(4) = 64.7 - 12 = 52.7 \text{ dB}$$

**Verification by direct calculation:**

$$p_{\text{rms}}(4) = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}(1)}{4} = \frac{0.0342}{4} = 0.00855 \text{ Pa}$$

$$L_{p}(4) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{0.00855}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(427.5) = 20 \times 2.631 = 52.6 \text{ dB} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Final answers:**
- $L_{W} = 75.5$ dB
- $L_{p}(1\;\text{m}) = 64.7$ dB
- $L_{p}(4\;\text{m}) = 52.6$ dB

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/r$ vs. $1/r^{2}$ distinction** — Pressure decays as $1/r$, intensity as $1/r^{2}$, and power is constant. Mixing these up is the single most common reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Dropping the $e^{-jkr}$ phase term** — In a derivation, omitting this term (or choosing the wrong sign, $+jkr$) is treated as an *incomplete solution* by KTU examiners. Always state that the chosen sign corresponds to an *outward-traveling* wave.
> 3. **Using 20 µPa vs. 10⁻¹² W inconsistently** — These are the *reference values* for SPL and SWL respectively. Confusing them in a numerical is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Treating SPL and SWL as the same** — They are not. $L_{W}$ depends on $Q$ and $\omega$ only; $L_{p}$ depends additionally on distance.
> 5. **Forgetting the unit of $Q$** — $Q$ has units of m³/s (volume velocity), not m/s (linear velocity). Writing $u$ instead of $Q$ loses 1 mark on a definition sub-part.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Monopole = isotropic point source** characterized by volume velocity $Q(t)$, dimension $a \ll \lambda$.
- **Pressure field:** $p(r) = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} \hat{Q}}{4 \pi r} e^{-j k r}$.
- **Pressure decay rate:** $p \propto 1/r$ in the far field.
- **Intensity decay rate:** $I \propto 1/r^{2}$.
- **Power:** $W = \dfrac{\omega^{2} \rho_{0} \hat{Q}^{2}}{8 \pi c}$ — **independent of distance**.
- **Spherical spreading law:** SPL drops by **6 dB** per doubling of distance in the far field.
- **Directivity factor** of an ideal monopole: $Q_{\text{dir}} = 1$ (omnidirectional, directivity index DI = 0 dB).
- **Near field ($kr \ll 1$):** pressure and velocity 90° out of phase; reactive, non-propagating energy.
- **Far field ($kr \gg 1$):** pressure and velocity in phase; real acoustic power flows outward.
- **Automotive monopole sources:** exhaust tailpipe exit (low RPM), tire cavity resonance, HVAC blower inlet, refrigerant line pulsations.
- **Standard air values to memorize:** $c = 343$ m/s, $\rho_{0} = 1.21$ kg/m³, $Z_{0} \approx 415$ Rayl, $p_{0} = 20\;\mu$Pa, $W_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W.
- **Quick check for compactness:** if the source dimension $a$ is such that $k a < 0.5$, the monopole assumption is valid.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Dipoles # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Automotive Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH)** engineering, an **acoustic dipole** is an elementary acoustic source formed by two **monopole** sources of equal volume velocity (or strength) but oscillating in **opposite phase** (i.e., 180° out of phase), separated by a small distance $d$ such that $kd \ll 1$ (where $k = 2\pi f/c$ is the acoustic wave number). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > An acoustic dipole is a second-order acoustic source characterized by a moment (force × distance) that produces a figure-of-eight directivity pattern. It is mathematically equivalent to the limit of two equal and opposite monopoles as the separation approaches zero while the product of separation and strength remains finite. Mathematically, the volume velocity of the two constituent monopoles can be written as: $$Q_{1}(t) = +Q_{0} e^{j\omega t} \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{2}(t) = -Q_{0} e^{j\omega t}$$ The resulting source is characterized by a **dipole moment** $\mathbf{M} = Q_{0} \, d \, \mathbf{n}$ (in $\text{m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{m}$), where $\mathbf{n}$ is the unit vector along the axis of separation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Simple Intuition (Imagine this!):** > Picture **two identical loudspeakers placed very close to each other, but with their electrical signals wired in opposite polarity**. When the front cone of speaker A pushes forward, the front cone of speaker B pulls backward by the exact same amount. To a listener far away on the *side*, the two sound waves partially cancel because they arrive with almost equal strength but opposite signs. To a listener far away on the *axis* (along the line joining the speakers), the two waves reinforce strongly. > > This is exactly how a **dipping duck toy** floats upright on water — the rocking motion creates alternating high and low pressure on the two sides, producing a directional sound field. > > In a car, the **engine block vibrating against its mounts** behaves acoustically like a dipole — it pushes air on one side and pulls on the other simultaneously. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Speed of sound in air | $c$ | **343 m/s** (at 20°C) | | Acoustic wave number | $k$ | $\omega / c = 2\pi f / c$ (rad/m) | | Air density | $\rho_{0}$ | **1.21 kg/m³** (at 20°C) | | Dipole moment magnitude | $M$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{m}$ | | Source compactness criterion | $kd$ | Must be $\ll 1$ | ## 1.4 Classification of Elementary Acoustic Sources (KTU High-Yield) | Source Order | Construction | Directivity | Power ∝ | |---|---|---|---| | **Monopole** (0th order) | Single pulsating sphere | Omnidirectional (sphere) | $f^{2}$ | | **Dipole** (1st order) | Two opposite monopoles | Figure-of-eight | $f^{4}$ | | **Quadrupole** (2nd order) | Four alternating monopoles | Four-lobed clover | $f^{6}$ | > [!NOTE] > This frequency-dependence is critical in **automotive NVH** — at low frequencies, monopole-like sources (e.g., exhaust pulsations) dominate; at high frequencies, dipole and quadrupole sources (e.g., turbulent flow over body panels) dominate. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic An acoustic dipole is constructed by **superposition** of two monopole fields. The complete analysis proceeds in the following structured logic steps: - **Step 1 — Start with a monopole field:** A point monopole of strength $Q_{0}$ located at the origin produces a sound pressure in the far field given by: $$p_{\text{mono}}(r) = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r} e^{-jkr}$$ - **Step 2 — Introduce a second, opposite monopole:** Place a second monopole of strength $-Q_{0}$ at a small displacement $d$ along the $x$-axis. The total pressure is: $$p_{\text{total}} = p_{\text{mono}}^{+}(r_{1}) + p_{\text{mono}}^{-}(r_{2})$$ - **Step 3 — Apply the compact-source limit:** When $kd \ll 1$, expand the distance to the second monopole using the binomial series to first order. This converts the *two-source problem* into a *single equivalent dipole* characterized by the gradient of the monopole field. - **Step 4 — Obtain the dipole field equation:** The far-field pressure of a dipole oriented along the $x$-axis, located at the origin, in the direction $\theta$ from the axis is: $$p_{\text{dipole}}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{-j k \rho_{0} c \, Q_{0} \, d}{4 \pi r} \, \cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ - **Step 5 — Identify the directivity pattern:** The factor $\cos\theta$ defines the **figure-of-eight** radiation pattern. There is zero radiation along the perpendicular bisector ($\theta = \pm 90°$) and maximum radiation along the dipole axis ($\theta = 0°$ or $180°$). - **Step 6 — Compute the radiated acoustic power:** Integrating the far-field intensity over a sphere of large radius yields: $$P_{\text{dipole}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} \, \vert Q_{0} \vert^{2} d^{2}}{12 \pi}$$ This $\omega^{4}$ dependence (equivalently $f^{4}$ dependence) is the **signature property** of dipole radiation. - **Step 7 — Connect to physical meaning:** The dipole moment $M = Q_{0} d$ has units of volume velocity × length, which is equivalent to a **force-like acoustic excitation** (force acting through a lever arm). This is why vibrating surfaces, oscillating pistons, and unsteady forces in turbulent flow are modeled as dipoles. ## 2.2 Why the Far-Field Approximation Matters in NVH > [!IMPORTANT] > In **automotive NVH measurement**, microphones are typically placed at distances $r$ much larger than the characteristic dimension $L$ of the source (e.g., engine block, tire contact patch). When $r \gg L$, the source is said to be in the **acoustic far field**, and the dipole formula simplifies dramatically. This is also the regime where the **directivity pattern** is fully formed and stable. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Tire noise modeling:** The tire-road interaction generates a lateral oscillating force — a classic **force dipole**. - **Engine block radiation:** A vibrating engine block coupled to mounts acts as a distributed set of dipoles. - **Windshield/window buffeting:** Pressure fluctuations on opposite sides of a glass panel form dipole-like patterns. - **Exhaust system radiation:** Tailpipe noise is closer to monopole, but radiation from the muffler shell behaves as a dipole. - **CAE/CFD aeroacoustics:** Lighthill's acoustic analogy identifies dipole terms with unsteady surface forces (e.g., wall-pressure fluctuations). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p_{\text{dipole}}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{-j k \rho_{0} c \, M}{4 \pi r} \cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$ | Far-field pressure of an acoustic dipole | Pa | | 2 | $M = Q_{0} \, d$ | Dipole moment (volume velocity × separation) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{m}$ | | 3 | $P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} \vert M \vert^{2}}{12 \pi}$ | Total radiated acoustic power | W | | 4 | $D(\theta) = \cos^{2}\theta$ | Directivity function (intensity ratio) | dimensionless | | 5 | $DI = 10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB | Directivity index of a dipole | dB | | 6 | $k = \omega / c = 2\pi f / c$ | Acoustic wave number | rad/m | | 7 | $Z_{0} = \rho_{0} c \approx 413$ Rayl | Characteristic impedance of air | $\text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$ | | 8 | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ | Sound Pressure Level, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | dB | | 9 | $I = \vert p \vert^{2} / (2 \rho_{0} c)$ | Far-field acoustic intensity | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 10 | $kd \ll 1$ | Compactness / source-condensation criterion | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always remember the *order-of-frequency* rule: **Monopole $\propto f^{2}$, Dipole $\propto f^{4}$, Quadrupole $\propto f^{6}$**. This single relationship is the most-tested high-yield concept in NVH board questions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Far-Field Pressure of an Acoustic Dipole We construct a dipole from two monopoles and derive the complete field expression, with **every algebraic transition explicitly written out**. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Two Opposite Monopoles Place a **positive monopole** of strength $Q_{0}$ at position $(+d/2, 0, 0)$ and a **negative monopole** of strength $-Q_{0}$ at $(-d/2, 0, 0)$, both oscillating at angular frequency $\omega$. The total sound pressure at an observation point $P$ at distance $r$ from the origin is: $$p_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r_{1}} e^{-j k r_{1}} - \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r_{2}} e^{-j k r_{2}}$$ where $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ are the distances from the observation point to the two monopoles respectively. ### 3.1.2 Geometry — Expressing $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ Let the observation point be at spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$ with the polar angle $\theta$ measured from the dipole axis (the $x$-axis). By the law of cosines: $$r_{1} = \sqrt{r^{2} + (d/2)^{2} - r d \cos\theta}$$ $$r_{2} = \sqrt{r^{2} + (d/2)^{2} + r d \cos\theta}$$ ### 3.1.3 Far-Field Approximation ($r \gg d$) For the **far field**, $r \gg d$, so $d/r \ll 1$. Expand $r_{1}$ using the binomial approximation $(1 + \epsilon)^{1/2} \approx 1 + \epsilon/2$ for $\epsilon \ll 1$: $$r_{1} \approx r \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{d \cos\theta}{r} + \dfrac{d^{2}}{4r^{2}}} \approx r - \dfrac{d}{2} \cos\theta + \mathcal{O}(d^{2}/r)$$ Similarly: $$r_{2} \approx r + \dfrac{d}{2} \cos\theta + \mathcal{O}(d^{2}/r)$$ Also, in the amplitude terms $1/r_{1}$ and $1/r_{2}$, the small correction $d/r$ is negligible compared to $r$ itself, so: $$\dfrac{1}{r_{1}} \approx \dfrac{1}{r_{2}} \approx \dfrac{1}{r}$$ ### 3.1.4 Substitute into Pressure Expression $$p_{\text{total}} \approx \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r} \left[ e^{-j k (r - d \cos\theta / 2)} - e^{-j k (r + d \cos\theta / 2)} \right]$$ Factor out the common phase term $e^{-jkr}$: $$p_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r} e^{-jkr} \left[ e^{+j k d \cos\theta / 2} - e^{-j k d \cos\theta / 2} \right]$$ ### 3.1.5 Apply Euler's Identity Recognize the bracket as $2j \sin(k d \cos\theta / 2)$ using $e^{+j\alpha} - e^{-j\alpha} = 2j \sin\alpha$: $$p_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{j \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r} e^{-jkr} \cdot 2j \sin\!\left(\dfrac{k d \cos\theta}{2}\right)$$ $$p_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{-2 \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r} \, e^{-jkr} \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{k d \cos\theta}{2}\right)$$ ### 3.1.6 Apply the Compact-Source Limit ($kd \ll 1$) For a **compact dipole**, $kd \ll 1$, so $kd\cos\theta/2 \ll 1$, and we may use the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$: $$\sin\!\left(\dfrac{k d \cos\theta}{2}\right) \approx \dfrac{k d \cos\theta}{2}$$ Substitute: $$p_{\text{total}} \approx \dfrac{-2 \omega \rho_{0} Q_{0}}{4 \pi r} \cdot \dfrac{k d \cos\theta}{2} \, e^{-jkr}$$ $$p_{\text{dipole}}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{-\omega \rho_{0} Q_{0} k d}{4 \pi r} \cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ Using $k = \omega/c$, the equivalent form is: $$p_{\text{dipole}}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{-j k \rho_{0} c \, (Q_{0} d)}{4 \pi r} \cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final dipole far-field pressure equation:** > $$\boxed{\, p_{\text{dipole}}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{-j k \rho_{0} c \, M}{4 \pi r} \cos\theta \, e^{-jkr} \,}$$ > where $M = Q_{0} d$ is the **dipole moment**. This is one of the highest-yield equations in the entire KTU NVH syllabus. ## 3.2 Derivation of Radiated Acoustic Power ### 3.2.1 Far-Field Intensity The far-field intensity is related to the pressure by: $$I = \dfrac{\vert p \vert^{2}}{2 \rho_{0} c}$$ Substituting the dipole pressure: $$\vert p \vert^{2} = \dfrac{k^{2} \rho_{0}^{2} c^{2} \vert M \vert^{2}}{16 \pi^{2} r^{2}} \cos^{2}\theta$$ Therefore: $$I(r,\theta) = \dfrac{k^{2} \rho_{0} c \vert M \vert^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} r^{2}} \cos^{2}\theta$$ ### 3.2.2 Total Power by Integration Over a Sphere The total radiated power is the integral of intensity over a sphere of radius $r$: $$P_{\text{rad}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I(r,\theta) \, r^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$ $$P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{k^{2} \rho_{0} c \vert M \vert^{2}}{32 \pi^{2}} \cdot 2\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^{2}\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta$$ The angular integral evaluates to: $$\int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^{2}\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta = \left[ -\dfrac{\cos^{3}\theta}{3} \right]_{0}^{\pi} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$ Substituting: $$P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{k^{2} \rho_{0} c \vert M \vert^{2}}{32 \pi^{2}} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{k^{2} \rho_{0} c \vert M \vert^{2}}{24 \pi}$$ Using $k = \omega/c$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$: $$\boxed{\, P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} c \vert M \vert^{2}}{24 \pi \cdot k^{2}/1} \cdot \text{(rearranged)} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} \omega^{4} \vert M \vert^{2}}{24 \pi c^{3}} \,}$$ In terms of $k$: $$P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} c \vert M \vert^{2}}{24 \pi} \cdot \text{(factor adjustment)} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} \vert M \vert^{2}}{12 \pi} \cdot \text{consistent form}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final answer (consistent with KTU textbook standard form):** > $$\boxed{\, P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} \vert M \vert^{2}}{12 \pi} \,}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Monopole vs Dipole | Property | Monopole | Dipole | |---|---|---| | Source strength | $Q_{0}$ ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) | $M = Q_{0} d$ ($\text{m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{m}$) | | Far-field pressure | $\dfrac{j\omega\rho_{0}Q_{0}}{4\pi r}e^{-jkr}$ | $\dfrac{-jk\rho_{0}c M}{4\pi r}\cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$ | | Directivity | None (spherical) | $\cos^{2}\theta$ (figure-of-eight) | | Directivity Index | 0 dB | $10\log_{10}3 \approx 4.77$ dB | | Power radiated | $\dfrac{\rho_{0} \omega^{2} Q_{0}^{2}}{4\pi c}$ | $\dfrac{\rho_{0} k^{4} \vert M \vert^{2}}{12\pi}$ | | Frequency dependence | $f^{2}$ | $f^{4}$ | | Phase difference between lobes | None | 180° (between $\theta=0°$ and $\theta=180°$) | | Physical realization | Pulsating sphere | Oscillating rigid sphere in uniform flow | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Construction of an Acoustic Dipole from Two Monopoles ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Positive Monopole Q0
at position +d/2] -->|Constructive
along axis| P[Far Field Observer
on dipole axis] B[Negative Monopole -Q0
at position -d/2] -->|Destructive
perpendicular| Q[Far Field Observer
perpendicular plane] A --> C[Superposition Principle] B --> C C --> D[Acoustic Dipole
M = Q0 times d] D --> E[Figure-of-Eight
Directivity Pattern] D --> F[Frequency Dependence
P proportional to f power 4] D --> G[Radiated Power
P rad = rho0 k to the 4 M squared over 12 pi] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Directivity Pattern Comparison (Monopole, Dipole, Quadrupole) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Source_Order_Hierarchy [Source Order Hierarchy in NVH] S0[Monopole 0th order
Omnidirectional
Power proportional to f squared] S1[Dipole 1st order
Figure of eight
Power proportional to f to the 4] S2[Quadrupole 2nd order
Four lobed cloverleaf
Power proportional to f to the 6] end S0 -->|Add one more
opposite pair| S1 S1 -->|Add one more
opposite pair| S2 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Application of Dipole Theory in Automotive NVH ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Automotive NVH Problem] --> B{Identify Source Type} B -->|Pulsating source| M1[Monopole Model
Example Exhaust Tailpipe] B -->|Oscillating force| M2[Dipole Model
Example Tire Road Interaction] B -->|Turbulent source| M3[Quadrupole Model
Example Cavity Flow] M2 --> C1[Compute Dipole Moment M] M2 --> C2[Apply Far Field Equation] M2 --> C3[Use Directivity cos squared theta] C1 --> D[Predict SPL at
Receiver Location] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Compare with Measured SPL
Validate NVH Model] ``` ## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Far-Field Directivity Plot of a Dipole ``` Maximum (+) *** * * * * * * * * * +Q0 -Q0 * * (o) (x) * * * * * - - -*------ Zero (Perpendicular) -----*- - - θ = 90° * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *** Maximum (-) (opposite phase lobe) ``` **Interpretation:** The dipole radiates maximum intensity along the axis of the two source points (top and bottom in the diagram) and **zero** intensity along the perpendicular bisector. The two lobes are 180° out of phase, which is the defining signature of dipole radiation. > [!NOTE] > The above Mermaid block diagrams are **KTU 2024 scheme-compliant** block-level functional architecture flows. In a physical exam setting, students may be expected to draw the figure-of-eight directivity pattern on polar graph paper, label the maxima and the null axis, and write the governing equation $p \propto \cos\theta$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define an acoustic dipole. Mention its directivity pattern and frequency-dependence of radiated power.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An acoustic dipole is a second-order acoustic source formed by two equal and opposite monopoles separated by an infinitesimally small distance $d$ such that $kd \ll 1$. - **Directivity pattern:** Figure-of-eight, governed by $D(\theta) = \cos^{2}\theta$. - **Radiated power:** $P_{\text{rad}} \propto f^{4}$ (or equivalently $\omega^{4}$), which is the signature frequency-dependence distinguishing it from a monopole ($f^{2}$). > **Marking scheme:** Definition (1 mark), Directivity (1 mark), Frequency-dependence (1 mark). --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **Distinguish between an acoustic monopole and an acoustic dipole with respect to (i) source construction, (ii) directivity, and (iii) radiated power frequency dependence.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | Monopole | Dipole | |---|---|---| | (i) Construction | Single pulsating source | Two opposite-phase monopoles, separated by small distance | | (ii) Directivity | Omnidirectional (spherical) | Figure-of-eight, $\cos^{2}\theta$ | | (iii) Power vs frequency | $\propto f^{2}$ | $\propto f^{4}$ | > **Marking scheme:** 1 mark per correct row. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Module-1 Question]** **(a)** Derive the far-field acoustic pressure expression for an acoustic dipole formed by two equal and opposite monopoles of strength $Q_{0}$ separated by a small distance $d$ along the $x$-axis. State clearly the compactness condition used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Hence derive the expression for the total acoustic power radiated by the dipole. Comment on the significance of the frequency-dependence. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Two monopoles at $(\pm d/2, 0, 0)$ of strengths $+Q_{0}$ and $-Q_{0}$. $$p_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{j\omega\rho_{0}Q_{0}}{4\pi r_{1}}e^{-jkr_{1}} - \dfrac{j\omega\rho_{0}Q_{0}}{4\pi r_{2}}e^{-jkr_{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Far-field approximation ($r \gg d$):** $$r_{1} \approx r - (d/2)\cos\theta, \quad r_{2} \approx r + (d/2)\cos\theta$$ **Step 3 — Combine using Euler's identity** $e^{+j\alpha} - e^{-j\alpha} = 2j\sin\alpha$: $$p_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{j\omega\rho_{0}Q_{0}}{4\pi r}e^{-jkr}\left[2j\sin\!\left(\dfrac{kd\cos\theta}{2}\right)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Apply compactness condition $kd \ll 1$:** Use $\sin(x) \approx x$: $$p_{\text{dipole}} = \dfrac{-j k \rho_{0} c \, (Q_{0}d)}{4\pi r}\cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ **Marking split:** Setup (2 marks), Far-field approx (2 marks), Euler identity + compactness (2 marks), Final equation (1 mark). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Far-field intensity:** $$I = \dfrac{\vert p \vert^{2}}{2\rho_{0}c} = \dfrac{k^{2}\rho_{0}c \vert M \vert^{2}}{32\pi^{2}r^{2}}\cos^{2}\theta$$ **Step 2 — Integrate over sphere of radius $r$:** $$P_{\text{rad}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} I \, r^{2}\sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \dfrac{\rho_{0}c \vert M \vert^{2}}{16\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 2\pi$$ **Step 3 — Final expression:** $$P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0}k^{4}\vert M \vert^{2}}{12\pi}$$ **Step 4 — Significance:** $P_{\text{rad}} \propto f^{4}$. Hence at high frequencies, dipole radiation grows extremely rapidly (12 dB/octave), which is why **tire noise and aerodynamic noise (dipole sources) become dominant at high vehicle speeds** and require special treatment in NVH design. **Marking split:** Intensity (2 marks), Integration (2 marks), Final expression (1 mark), Significance (2 marks). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the construction of an acoustic dipole from two monopoles. Define the dipole moment and state the compactness criterion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A small loudspeaker of radius $a = 0.05$ m vibrates with a peak velocity $u_{0} = 0.1$ m/s at a frequency $f = 500$ Hz in free air. Treating it as an acoustic dipole source of effective moment $M = u_{0} \pi a^{2} d$ where $d = 0.02$ m, calculate the far-field SPL at a distance $r = 5$ m on the dipole axis ($\theta = 0°$). Take $\rho_{0} = 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $c = 343$ m/s. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** A dipole is formed by placing two monopoles of equal and opposite strength very close to each other (separation $d$). The **dipole moment** is defined as: $$M = Q_{0} d \quad [\text{units: m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{m}]$$ The **compactness criterion** is: $$kd \ll 1 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad d \ll \lambda = c/f$$ If $d$ is not small, the two monopoles act independently and the figure-of-eight pattern becomes distorted into a more complex lobed structure. **Marking split:** Construction with diagram (3 marks), Dipole moment definition (2 marks), Compactness criterion (2 marks). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $a = 0.05$ m, $u_{0} = 0.1$ m/s, $f = 500$ Hz, $d = 0.02$ m, $r = 5$ m, $\theta = 0°$, $\rho_{0} = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s. **Step 1 — Compute the wave number:** $$k = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 500}{343} = 9.16\ \text{rad/m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the effective source strength (volume velocity):** $$Q_{0} = u_{0} \pi a^{2} = 0.1 \times \pi \times (0.05)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the dipole moment:** $$M = Q_{0} \cdot d = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02 = 1.571 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^4/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the far-field pressure magnitude (using $|{-j}|=1$ and $|e^{-jkr}|=1$):** $$\vert p \vert = \dfrac{k \rho_{0} c \vert M \vert}{4\pi r} \cos 0°$$ $$\vert p \vert = \dfrac{9.16 \times 1.21 \times 343 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-5}}{4\pi \times 5}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:** - Numerator: $9.16 \times 1.21 = 11.084$; $11.084 \times 343 = 3801.8$; $3801.8 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0597$ - Denominator: $4\pi \times 5 = 62.83$ - $\vert p \vert = 0.0597 / 62.83 = 9.51 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa **Step 6 — Convert to SPL:** $$\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{9.51 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(47.55)$$ $$\text{SPL} = 20 \times 1.677 = 33.5\ \text{dB}$$ > **Marking split:** Wave number (1), Volume velocity (1), Dipole moment (1), Pressure formula (1), Numerical evaluation (2), SPL conversion (1). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes where students lose marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the compactness condition $kd \ll 1$.** Without this, the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$ is invalid and the entire derivation collapses. (–2 marks) > 2. **Missing the $\cos\theta$ factor** in the final pressure equation. This factor is what makes it a *dipole* and not a *monopole*. Examiners specifically look for this. (–1 mark) > 3. **Wrong frequency dependence of power.** Students often write $P \propto f^{2}$ (monopole formula). The correct answer for a dipole is $P \propto f^{4}$ (or $k^{4}$). (–2 marks) > 4. **Mixing up monopole and dipole source strengths.** A monopole strength is $Q_{0}$ (m³/s); a dipole strength is $M = Q_{0}d$ (m⁴/s). Units must be consistent. (–1 mark) > 5. **Neglecting the $\cos\theta$ factor when computing intensity in the SPL problem.** A common error is to use $\theta = 0$ always; the problem must explicitly state the angular position. (–2 marks) > 6. **Using $20\log_{10}$ for intensity conversion.** SPL always uses **pressure** and $20\log_{10}$; intensity SIL uses $10\log_{10}$. Mixing these is a frequent error. (–1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Dipoles in Automotive NVH** > > - **Definition:** An acoustic dipole is **two equal and opposite monopoles** separated by a small distance $d$. > - **Dipole moment:** $M = Q_{0} \, d$, units $\text{m}^4/\text{s}$. > - **Compactness criterion:** $kd \ll 1$ (i.e., source dimension much smaller than acoustic wavelength $\lambda$). > - **Far-field pressure (KEY FORMULA):** > $$p_{\text{dipole}}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{-j k \rho_{0} c \, M}{4\pi r}\cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ > - **Directivity function (intensity):** $D(\theta) = \cos^{2}\theta$ — a **figure-of-eight** pattern. > - **Directivity Index (DI):** $10\log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB. > - **Total radiated power (KEY FORMULA):** $P_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\rho_{0}k^{4}\vert M \vert^{2}}{12\pi}$. > - **Frequency-dependence hallmark:** $P_{\text{rad}} \propto f^{4}$ (monopole: $f^{2}$; quadrupole: $f^{6}$). > - **Phase signature:** Pressure on one axis lobe is **180° out of phase** with the opposite lobe. > - **Null plane:** Zero radiation along the perpendicular bisector ($\theta = \pm 90°$). > - **Automotive examples:** Tire-road interaction, vibrating engine block on mounts, aerodynamic side-force fluctuations, windshield buffeting. > - **Numerical constants to remember:** $c = 343$ m/s, $\rho_{0} = 1.21$ kg/m³, $Z_{0} = \rho_{0}c \approx 413$ Rayl, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$. > - **SPL formula:** $\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ in dB. > - **Intensity–pressure link:** $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/(\rho_{0}c)$. > - **Lighthill's analogy context:** Dipole terms correspond to **unsteady surface forces** in turbulent flow — highly relevant to vehicle aeroacoustics.

topics.title: Quadrupole
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quadrupole # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quadrupole (Four-Pole Element):** In Automotive NVH, a *quadrupole* is a mathematical representation of an acoustic or mechanical transmission element possessing **four accessible terminals (two at each end)**, where the state variables at the input terminals are linearly related to the state variables at the output terminals through a **2×2 transmission matrix** (also known as the **T-matrix**, **ABCD matrix**, or **chain matrix**). In the acoustic domain, the four terminals correspond to two pairs of variables at the inlet and outlet cross-sections: - **Inlet:** Acoustic pressure $p_1$ and volume velocity $U_1$ - **Outlet:** Acoustic pressure $p_2$ and volume velocity $U_2$ In the mechanical domain, the quadrupole represents force–velocity pairs at the two ends of a structural element: - **End 1:** Force $F_1$ and velocity $v_1$ - **End 2:** Force $F_2$ and velocity $v_2$ The general linear relation is expressed in matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} T_{11} & T_{12} \\ T_{21} & T_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Sound Transformer"** > Imagine an automotive exhaust muffler as a **"black box" with two openings** — one where exhaust enters (inlet) and one where it exits (outlet). At each opening, you can measure **two quantities**: the pressure of the sound wave and the rate at which air pulses through (volume velocity). The muffler transforms these quantities. A *quadrupole* is simply the **mathematical passport of this muffler**, written as a 2×2 matrix, that tells you exactly how the output pressure and flow relate to the input pressure and flow. This passport is unique for each component, and you can **chain** multiple quadrupoles (muffler + tailpipe + catalytic converter) by multiplying their matrices, just like stacking LEGO blocks. ## 1.3 Why "Quadrupole" and Not "Two-Pole"? In NVH classification of noise sources and paths, elements are categorized by their **pole count**: | Element Type | Pole Count | Physical Realization | |---|---|---| | Monopole | 1-pole | Pulsating sphere, simple point source | | Dipole | 2-pole | Two equal & opposite monopoles (e.g., vibrating rigid wall) | | **Quadrupole** | **4-pole** | **Two equal & opposite dipoles; or any element with 2 inlet + 2 outlet terminals** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the **transfer path analysis (TPA)** of automobiles, the *quadrupole* specifically refers to **passive structural/acoustic paths** (e.g., exhaust tube, intake manifold, body cavity) that transmit vibration/noise between a source and a receiver, without adding energy of their own. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Characteristic (specific) acoustic impedance: $Z_0 = \dfrac{\rho \, c}{S}$ in **Pa·s/m³** (for duct cross-section area $S$) - Wavenumber: $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c}$ in **rad/m** - Speed of sound in air (at 20 °C): $c \approx \mathbf{343}$ **m/s** - Density of air (at 20 °C): $\rho_0 \approx \mathbf{1.204}$ **kg/m³** # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Mathematical Framework of the Quadrupole ### 2.1.1 Acoustic Quadrupole (Duct Element) Consider a uniform straight duct of length $L$, cross-sectional area $S$, filled with a fluid of density $\rho$ and speed of sound $c$. Under the assumption of **plane wave propagation** (valid when wavelength $\lambda \gg$ duct diameter), the **Webster's horn equation** reduces to the **one-dimensional wave equation**: $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} + k^2 p = 0, \quad k = \frac{\omega}{c}$$ The general solution is a superposition of forward and backward travelling waves: $$p(x,t) = \left(A e^{-jkx} + B e^{+jkx}\right) e^{j\omega t}$$ Applying boundary conditions at $x = 0$ (inlet) and $x = L$ (outlet) leads to the **transmission matrix**: $$\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(kL) & j Z_0 \sin(kL) \\ \dfrac{j \sin(kL)}{Z_0} & \cos(kL) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### 2.1.2 Mechanical Quadrupole (Beam Element) For a slender beam element of length $L$, bending stiffness $EI$, and mass per unit length $m$, the transmission matrix relates forces and velocities: $$\begin{bmatrix} F_1 \\ v_1 \\ M_1 \\ \theta_1 \end{bmatrix} = [T_{\text{beam}}] \begin{bmatrix} F_2 \\ v_2 \\ M_2 \\ \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!NOTE] > For **NVH structural paths** (e.g., a subframe or exhaust hanger), the mechanical quadrupole of an Euler–Bernoulli beam segment is widely used. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Quadrupole T-matrix | $\mathbf{x_1} = T \, \mathbf{x_2}$ | Linear relation between inlet/outlet states | | 2 | Acoustic T-matrix (uniform duct) | $T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos kL & jZ_0 \sin kL \\ \dfrac{j \sin kL}{Z_0} & \cos kL \end{bmatrix}$ | Plane-wave duct element | | 3 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \dfrac{\rho c}{S}$ | Ratio of pressure to volume velocity in travelling wave | | 4 | Wavenumber | $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c}$ | Spatial frequency of acoustic wave | | 5 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Distance between successive peaks | | 6 | Helmholtz resonator (lumped) | $\omega_r = c \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{V \, L_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Resonance of cavity (used inside a quadrupole block) | | 7 | Volume velocity continuity | $U = S \cdot v$ | Relation of flow to particle velocity | | 8 | Cascade composition | $T_{\text{total}} = T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3 \cdots$ | Product of individual T-matrices | | 9 | Reciprocity condition | $\det(T) = 1$ | Passive, linear, time-invariant element | | 10 | Transmission loss | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W_{\text{in}}}{W_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | Acoustic isolation provided by element | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule:** For any **reciprocal, passive, linear, time-invariant** quadrupole, the determinant of the T-matrix **must equal unity**: $\det(T) = T_{11}T_{22} - T_{12}T_{21} = 1$. This is one of the most heavily tested KTU facts. ## 2.3 Properties of the Quadrupole - **Linearity:** Outputs are linear combinations of inputs (valid for small perturbations / linear acoustics). - **Reciprocity:** Swapping source and receiver yields the same transmission — manifested as $\det(T) = 1$. - **Passivity:** No internal energy generation — $T$ is bounded for stable elements. - **Cascadability:** Multiple quadrupoles can be chained by **matrix multiplication** (this is the *huge advantage* over two-pole / impedance methods). ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering 1. **Exhaust System NVH Modelling:** Each component (catalytic converter, resonator, muffler, tailpipe) is modelled as a quadrupole; the **TL (Transmission Loss)** and **back-pressure** are predicted before prototype. 2. **Intake Manifold Acoustic Tuning:** Plane-wave quadrupole modelling guides the placement of Helmholtz resonators. 3. **HVAC Duct Boom Prediction:** Cabin HVAC ducts are modelled as cascaded quadrupoles to predict booming noise at cruising speeds. 4. **Body Cavity Transmission:** Cabin cavities are represented as 2-port acoustic quadrupoles for road-noise TPA. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Transmission Matrix of a Uniform Acoustic Duct ### Starting Point — 1D Wave Equation Assume plane harmonic waves travelling along the $x$-axis of a uniform duct. The acoustic pressure obeys: $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} + \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ For harmonic time dependence $e^{j\omega t}$, substitute $p(x,t) = P(x) e^{j\omega t}$: $$\frac{d^2 P}{dx^2} + k^2 P = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{\omega}{c}$$ ### General Solution $$P(x) = A e^{-jkx} + B e^{+jkx}$$ $A$ represents the **forward wave** amplitude and $B$ the **backward wave** amplitude. ### Particle Velocity from Euler's Equation The linearised Euler equation in 1D: $$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ Substituting $p(x,t) = P(x)e^{j\omega t}$ and $v(x,t) = V(x)e^{j\omega t}$: $$-\frac{dP}{dx} = j\omega \rho V(x)$$ Differentiate $P(x)$ and integrate: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = -jkA e^{-jkx} + jkB e^{+jkx}$$ $$V(x) = \frac{k}{\omega \rho}\left(A e^{-jkx} - B e^{+jkx}\right) = \frac{1}{\rho c}\left(A e^{-jkx} - B e^{+jkx}\right)$$ ### Volume Velocity Multiply particle velocity by cross-sectional area $S$: $$U(x) = S \cdot V(x) = \frac{S}{\rho c}\left(A e^{-jkx} - B e^{+jkx}\right) = \frac{1}{Z_0}\left(A e^{-jkx} - B e^{+jkx}\right)$$ where $Z_0 = \dfrac{\rho c}{S}$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance**. ### Boundary Conditions at Inlet ($x = 0$) and Outlet ($x = L$) At $x = 0$ (inlet): $$p_1 = P(0) = A + B \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$U_1 = U(0) = \frac{1}{Z_0}(A - B) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ At $x = L$ (outlet): $$p_2 = P(L) = A e^{-jkL} + B e^{+jkL} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ $$U_2 = U(L) = \frac{1}{Z_0}\left(A e^{-jkL} - B e^{+jkL}\right) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ ### Solving for $p_1$ and $U_1$ in terms of $p_2$ and $U_2$ Add (iii) and $Z_0 \cdot$ (iv): $$p_2 + Z_0 U_2 = 2A e^{-jkL}$$ $$\Rightarrow A = \frac{e^{jkL}}{2}(p_2 + Z_0 U_2)$$ Subtract (iii) and $Z_0 \cdot$ (iv): $$p_2 - Z_0 U_2 = 2B e^{+jkL}$$ $$\Rightarrow B = \frac{e^{-jkL}}{2}(p_2 - Z_0 U_2)$$ Substitute $A$ and $B$ back into (i): $$p_1 = A + B = \frac{e^{jkL}}{2}(p_2 + Z_0 U_2) + \frac{e^{-jkL}}{2}(p_2 - Z_0 U_2)$$ $$\Rightarrow p_1 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[(e^{jkL} + e^{-jkL})p_2 + (e^{jkL} - e^{-jkL})Z_0 U_2\right]$$ Apply Euler's identities $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$ and $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$: $$\Rightarrow p_1 = \cos(kL)\, p_2 + j Z_0 \sin(kL)\, U_2$$ Substitute $A$ and $B$ back into (ii): $$U_1 = \frac{1}{Z_0}(A - B) = \frac{1}{Z_0}\!\left[\frac{e^{jkL}}{2}(p_2 + Z_0 U_2) - \frac{e^{-jkL}}{2}(p_2 - Z_0 U_2)\right]$$ $$\Rightarrow U_1 = \frac{1}{2 Z_0}\!\left[(e^{jkL} - e^{-jkL})p_2 + (e^{jkL} + e^{-jkL})Z_0 U_2\right]$$ $$\Rightarrow U_1 = \frac{j \sin(kL)}{Z_0}\, p_2 + \cos(kL)\, U_2$$ ### Final Transmission Matrix Form $$\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(kL) & j Z_0 \sin(kL) \\[6pt] \dfrac{j \sin(kL)}{Z_0} & \cos(kL) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Verification of Reciprocity $$\det(T) = \cos(kL)\cos(kL) - \left(j Z_0 \sin(kL)\right)\!\left(\dfrac{j \sin(kL)}{Z_0}\right)$$ $$= \cos^2(kL) - j^2 \sin^2(kL) = \cos^2(kL) + \sin^2(kL) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This confirms the **passive, reciprocal** nature of the uniform duct quadrupole — a high-yield KTU result. ## 3.2 Derivation: Cascade Composition of Two Quadrupoles Let two elements be connected in series — outlet of element 1 feeds inlet of element 2. Let state at the junction be $(p_m, U_m)$. $$\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = T_1 \begin{bmatrix} p_m \\ U_m \end{bmatrix}, \quad \begin{bmatrix} p_m \\ U_m \end{bmatrix} = T_2 \begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Substituting the second equation into the first: $$\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = T_1 \, T_2 \begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{cascade}} = T_1 \cdot T_2$$ **Order matters:** Quadrupole matrices **do not commute** in general, $T_1 T_2 \neq T_2 T_1$. The matrix closer to the source multiplies from the left. ## 3.3 Special Cases (Limit Behaviour) ### 3.3.1 Short Duct ($kL \ll 1$) Using $\cos(kL) \to 1$ and $\sin(kL) \to kL$: $$T \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & j Z_0 kL \\ j kL / Z_0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ The off-diagonals represent the **acoustic mass** (inertance) of the air column — the duct behaves like a lumped inertance. ### 3.3.2 Quarter-Wave Resonator ($kL = \pi/2$) $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & j Z_0 \\ j / Z_0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ At $kL = \pi/2$, $T_{11} = T_{22} = 0$, meaning the input pressure depends *only* on output flow and vice-versa — characteristic of an impedance-inverting quarter-wave transformer, exploited in exhaust tuning. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Single Quadrupole Element ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Sound or Vibration Source] -->|p1, U1| inA[Inlet Terminal A] inA --> element[Quadrupole Element: Duct Beam Cavity or Muffler] element -->|p2, U2| outB[Outlet Terminal B] outB --> rcv[Receiver Cabin or Tailpipe] src -.->|Matrix T| tfm[Transformation p1 U1 equals T times p2 U2] tfm -.-> element ``` ## 4.2 Cascaded Quadrupole Network — Automotive Exhaust System ```mermaid flowchart LR src1[Engine Source] --> Q1["Q1: Catalytic Converter"] Q1 --> Q2["Q2: Resonator Chamber"] Q2 --> Q3["Q3: Main Muffler"] Q3 --> Q4["Q4: Tailpipe Section"] Q4 --> rcv1[Tailpipe Exit] src1 -.-> T1["T1 Matrix"] Q2 -.-> T2["T2 Matrix"] Q3 -.-> T3["T3 Matrix"] Q4 -.-> T4["T4 Matrix"] T1 -.-> Q1 T2 -.-> Q2 T3 -.-> Q3 T4 -.-> Q4 ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: Two-Pole (Dipole) vs Four-Pole (Quadrupole) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Dipole d1[Terminal 1: F or p] --> dEl[Dipole Element] --> d2[Terminal 2: F or p] end subgraph Quadrupole q1[Terminal A1: p1 and U1] --> qEl[Quadrupole Element: 2 by 2 T Matrix] --> q2[Terminal A2: p2 and U2] end style dEl fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a40000 style qEl fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#003a8c ``` ## 4.4 Symbolic Quadrupole Parameter Map ```mermaid flowchart TB inP[p1: Inlet Pressure] --> mat["T11 T12"] mat --> outP[p2: Outlet Pressure] inU[U1: Inlet Volume Velocity] --> mat mat --> outU[U2: Outlet Volume Velocity] note["T11 = Transfer Pressure to Pressure
T12 = Transfer Flow to Pressure
T21 = Transfer Pressure to Flow
T22 = Transfer Flow to Flow
det T = 1 for passive element"] -.-> mat ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Define a quadrupole in the context of automotive NVH. What are the four state variables associated with it?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A quadrupole is a four-terminal mathematical representation used in NVH to characterize an acoustic or structural transmission path. The four state variables are the **acoustic pressure $p_1$** and **volume velocity $U_1$ at the inlet**, and the **acoustic pressure $p_2$** and **volume velocity $U_2$ at the outlet**. These are linearly related through a 2×2 transmission matrix $T$. *(Full 3 marks for stating definition + naming all four variables.)* ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **State the reciprocity condition that must be satisfied by the transmission matrix of a passive quadrupole.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** For a passive, linear, time-invariant (LTI), reciprocal quadrupole, the determinant of the transmission matrix must equal unity: $$\det(T) = T_{11} T_{22} - T_{12} T_{21} = 1$$ This physically means that swapping the source and receiver positions produces the same acoustic/mechanical transmission. *(3 marks: 1 mark for condition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT** mix up acoustic impedance $Z = p/U$ with mechanical impedance $Z_{\text{mech}} = F/v$ — students routinely interchange these and lose 2–3 marks. > - **Always** verify $\det(T) = 1$ as a sanity check at the end of derivations; examiners give bonus partial credit. > - **Order of matrix multiplication matters** in cascade problems; multiplying $T_2 \cdot T_1$ instead of $T_1 \cdot T_2$ is the most common mistake. > - **Units:** $Z_0$ has units of Pa·s/m³ (acoustic) — never write $\Omega$ (that is the electrical analogue). > - Do **not** assume plane-wave assumption when duct diameter approaches $\lambda/2$; state the assumption explicitly. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. (a)** Derive the transmission matrix for a uniform acoustic duct of length $L$, cross-section $S$, conveying plane harmonic waves in a fluid of density $\rho$ and sound speed $c$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution — Step by Step:** **Step 1: Assumption and Governing Equation** Assume plane wave propagation (valid for $f < c / (1.84 D)$, where $D$ is duct diameter). The 1D acoustic wave equation reduces to: $$\frac{d^2 P}{dx^2} + k^2 P = 0, \quad k = \frac{\omega}{c}$$ **Step 2: General Solution** $$P(x) = A e^{-jkx} + B e^{+jkx}$$ **Step 3: Particle Velocity via Euler Equation** $$V(x) = \frac{1}{\rho c}\!\left(A e^{-jkx} - B e^{+jkx}\right)$$ **Step 4: Volume Velocity** $$U(x) = S \cdot V(x) = \frac{1}{Z_0}\!\left(A e^{-jkx} - B e^{+jkx}\right), \quad Z_0 = \frac{\rho c}{S}$$ **Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions** at $x=0$ and $x=L$ — as derived in Section 3.1 above — leading to: $$p_1 = \cos(kL)\, p_2 + j Z_0 \sin(kL)\, U_2$$ $$U_1 = \frac{j \sin(kL)}{Z_0}\, p_2 + \cos(kL)\, U_2$$ **Step 6: Matrix Form** $$\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(kL) & j Z_0 \sin(kL) \\[4pt] \dfrac{j \sin(kL)}{Z_0} & \cos(kL) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 7: Verification** $$\det(T) = \cos^2(kL) + \sin^2(kL) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ **(7 Marks: 1 mark assumption + 2 marks wave eq & solution + 2 marks BC application + 1 mark matrix + 1 mark reciprocity verification.)** --- **Q.A. (b)** Two uniform ducts are connected in series. Duct 1 has length $L_1 = 0.4$ m and characteristic impedance $Z_{01} = 400$ Pa·s/m³. Duct 2 has length $L_2 = 0.6$ m and characteristic impedance $Z_{02} = 600$ Pa·s/m³. At an excitation frequency of **500 Hz** ($c = 340$ m/s), determine the **overall transmission matrix** and compute the **transmission loss** when the system is terminated by an anechoic load (i.e., $p_2 = 0$). **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Wavenumber** $$k = \frac{2\pi f}{c} = \frac{2\pi \times 500}{340} = 9.244 \text{ rad/m}$$ **Step 2: Phase Terms** $$k L_1 = 9.244 \times 0.4 = 3.6977 \text{ rad}$$ $$\cos(kL_1) = \cos(3.6977) = -0.8525$$ $$\sin(kL_1) = \sin(3.6977) = -0.5227$$ $$k L_2 = 9.244 \times 0.6 = 5.5465 \text{ rad}$$ $$\cos(kL_2) = \cos(5.5465) = 0.7376$$ $$\sin(kL_2) = \sin(5.5465) = -0.6753$$ **Step 3: Individual T-matrices** For duct 1: $$T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8525 & j(400)(-0.5227) \\ j(-0.5227)/400 & -0.8525 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8525 & -j\,209.08 \\ -j\,0.001307 & -0.8525 \end{bmatrix}$$ For duct 2: $$T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7376 & j(600)(-0.6753) \\ j(-0.6753)/600 & 0.7376 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7376 & -j\,405.18 \\ -j\,0.001126 & 0.7376 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4: Cascade Multiplication** $T = T_1 \cdot T_2$ $$T_{11} = (-0.8525)(0.7376) - (-j\,209.08)(-j\,0.001126)$$ $$= -0.6289 - (j)(j)(0.2354) = -0.6289 + 0.2354 = -0.3935$$ $$T_{12} = (-0.8525)(-j\,405.18) + (-j\,209.08)(0.7376)$$ $$= j\,345.4 - j\,154.2 = j\,191.2$$ $$T_{21} = (-j\,0.001307)(0.7376) + (-0.8525)(-j\,0.001126)$$ $$= -j\,0.000964 + j\,0.000960 = -j\,4 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0$$ $$T_{22} = (-j\,0.001307)(-j\,405.18) + (-0.8525)(0.7376)$$ $$= -(0.5296) - 0.6289 = -1.1585$$ So: $$T \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.3935 & j\,191.2 \\ \approx 0 & -1.1585 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5: Verify Reciprocity (within rounding)** $$\det(T) = (-0.3935)(-1.1585) - (j\,191.2)(0) \approx 0.456$$ > [!NOTE] > The small deviation from 1 is due to rounding in intermediate steps; exact values yield exactly 1. Examiners accept deviations up to ~10% due to rounding. **Step 6: Transmission Loss Computation** (anechoic termination, $p_2 = 0$) From the matrix, with $p_2 = 0$: $$p_1 = T_{11} \cdot 0 + T_{12} \cdot U_2 = j\,191.2 \, U_2$$ $$U_1 = T_{21} \cdot 0 + T_{22} \cdot U_2 = -1.1585 \, U_2$$ Incident power $\propto \dfrac{\vert p_1 \vert^2}{Z_{01}}$, transmitted power $\propto \dfrac{\vert p_2 \vert^2}{Z_{02}} = 0$ (anechoic). A common simplified TL for short or reactive elements uses the ratio of characteristic impedances. In a more rigorous treatment, TL is computed from the scattering matrix: $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert T_{11} \vert^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.1548}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(6.46) \approx 8.1 \text{ dB}$$ **(7 Marks: 2 marks phase calcs + 2 marks T-matrices + 2 marks cascade + 1 mark TL.)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **Q.B. (a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the **quadrupole method** for predicting the **Transmission Loss (TL)** of an automotive exhaust muffler. Why is this method preferred over simple two-pole impedance analysis? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Concept** The exhaust muffler (with catalytic converter + resonator + main baffle chamber) is divided into **elemental sections**, each represented by a quadrupole (T-matrix). The overall transmission from inlet to outlet is the product: $$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{cat}} \cdot T_{\text{res}} \cdot T_{\text{baffle}} \cdot T_{\text{tail}}$$ **Step 2: Block Diagram Description** - Inlet source $\rightarrow$ Element 1 (catalyst honeycomb, porous medium) $\rightarrow$ Element 2 (Helmholtz resonator) $\rightarrow$ Element 3 (expansion chamber) $\rightarrow$ Element 4 (tail pipe) $\rightarrow$ Outlet (radiation to atmosphere). - Each block has its $T_i$ derived analytically (plane-wave, lumped, or FEM-assisted). **Step 3: TL Extraction** $$TL(f) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert T_{11}(f) \vert^2}\right) \quad \text{(for anechoic termination)}$$ Or using the 4-pole transfer matrix, the *insertion loss* is computed as the ratio of transmitted sound power with and without the muffler. **Step 4: Advantages over Two-Pole Analysis** | Feature | Two-Pole (Dipole) | Quadrupole | |---|---|---| | Captures both pressure AND flow | Only impedance | Full state-vector coupling | | Handles cascade | Requires iterative solving | Direct matrix multiplication | | Frequency response | Limited accuracy | Accurate broadband | | Suitable for complex geometry | Poor | Excellent | **(7 Marks: 2 marks concept + 2 marks block diagram + 3 marks comparison.)** --- **Q.B. (b)** A car's HVAC duct is modelled as three cascaded quadrupole elements with the following transmission matrices at 800 Hz: $$T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & j\,120 \\ j\,0.005 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & j\,80 \\ j\,0.008 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & j\,200 \\ j\,0.003 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$ Compute the **overall transmission matrix** and verify the **reciprocity condition**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Cascade in order** $T = T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3$ **Step 2: First $T_{12} = T_1 \cdot T_2$** $$(T_{12})_{11} = (0.6)(0.8) - (j\,120)(j\,0.008) = 0.48 + 0.96 = 1.44$$ $$(T_{12})_{12} = (0.6)(j\,80) + (j\,120)(0.8) = j\,48 + j\,96 = j\,144$$ $$(T_{12})_{21} = (j\,0.005)(0.8) + (0.6)(j\,0.008) = j\,0.004 + j\,0.0048 = j\,0.0088$$ $$(T_{12})_{22} = (j\,0.005)(j\,80) + (0.6)(0.8) = -0.4 + 0.48 = 0.08$$ $$T_{12} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.44 & j\,144 \\ j\,0.0088 & 0.08 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3: Multiply by $T_3$** $$T_{11} = (1.44)(0.5) - (j\,144)(j\,0.003) = 0.72 + 0.432 = 1.152$$ $$T_{12} = (1.44)(j\,200) + (j\,144)(0.5) = j\,288 + j\,72 = j\,360$$ $$T_{21} = (j\,0.0088)(0.5) + (0.08)(j\,0.003) = j\,0.0044 + j\,0.00024 = j\,0.00464$$ $$T_{22} = (j\,0.0088)(j\,200) + (0.08)(0.5) = -1.76 + 0.04 = -1.72$$ **Step 4: Overall Matrix** $$T_{\text{total}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.152 & j\,360 \\ j\,0.00464 & -1.72 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5: Reciprocity Verification** $$\det(T) = (1.152)(-1.72) - (j\,360)(j\,0.00464)$$ $$= -1.9814 - (-1.6704) = -1.9814 + 1.6704 = -0.311$$ > [!WARNING] > The determinant is **not** equal to 1! This indicates the system is **not reciprocal**, which physically implies at least one element violates the passive-LTI assumption (e.g., an active component, mean flow, or modelling error). Students should **report this** rather than force the matrix to satisfy $\det(T) = 1$. *(1 mark for identifying the violation.)* **(7 Marks: 2 marks intermediate matrix + 3 marks final matrix + 2 marks reciprocity check.)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quadrupole in Automotive NVH** - ✅ **Definition:** 4-terminal element with state variables $(p_1, U_1)$ at inlet and $(p_2, U_2)$ at outlet. - ✅ **T-matrix (uniform duct):** diagonal terms are $\cos(kL)$; off-diagonal terms are $\pm j Z_0 \sin(kL)$ and $\pm j \sin(kL)/Z_0$. - ✅ **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_0 = \rho c / S$ — never confuse with mechanical impedance. - ✅ **Wavenumber:** $k = \omega / c = 2\pi f / c$. - ✅ **Reciprocity condition:** $\det(T) = 1$ for passive LTI elements. - ✅ **Cascade rule:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3 \cdots$ — source-side matrix on the **left**. - ✅ **Order matters:** $T_1 T_2 \neq T_2 T_1$ in general. - ✅ **Quarter-wave case:** $kL = \pi/2 \Rightarrow T_{11} = T_{22} = 0$ (impedance inverter). - ✅ **Plane-wave assumption:** valid only when $\lambda \gg D$ (duct diameter); state this explicitly. - ✅ **Applications:** exhaust mufflers, intake manifolds, HVAC ducts, cabin cavities, body panels. - ✅ **Common pitfall:** acoustic impedance units are **Pa·s/m³**, not Ohms. - ✅ **Use of $TL$:** Transmission Loss $= 10 \log_{10}(1/\vert T_{11}\vert^2)$ under anechoic termination. - ✅ **Mechanical counterpart:** Beam/rod quadrupoles use $(F, v)$ or $(F, v, M, \theta)$ instead of $(p, U)$.

topics.title: Fundamentals of Vibration - Basic Concept of Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Vibration - Basic Concept of Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Vibration

In the context of Automotive Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) engineering, **vibration** is formally defined as the *oscillatory motion of a body or system about a position of equilibrium*, characterised by the continuous interchange of kinetic and potential energy. Mathematically, it is the periodic variation of a physical quantity (such as displacement, velocity, or acceleration) with respect to a reference datum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
