## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SDOF Mass-Spring-Damper System (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a generic SDOF vibratory system — including the energy flow between mass, spring, damper, and excitation. This is the most commonly used schematic in NVH textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Force F of t] --> B[Mass m]
    B -- Inertia m ddot x --> D[Equation of Motion]
    E[Spring k] -- F spring = kx --> D
    F[Damper c] -- F damper = c dot x --> D
    D --> G[Displacement x of t]
    D --> H[Velocity dot x]
    D --> I[Acceleration ddot x]
    G --> J[Response Amplitude X]
    H --> K[Phase Angle phi]
    I --> L[Damping Ratio zeta]
    J --> M[NVH Output: Vibration Level]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **External Force** drives the Mass.
- The **Spring** and **Damper** provide restoring and dissipative forces respectively.
- The **Equation of Motion** combines all forces.
- The outputs — displacement, velocity, acceleration, and phase — feed the **NVH output** block, which represents the actual measured or simulated vibration level at a point in the vehicle (e.g., steering wheel, seat track, floor pan).

---

### 4.2 Free Vibration vs Forced Vibration — Functional Block Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FREE_VIB ["FREE VIBRATION BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        A1[Initial Displacement x naught] --> B1[Release: F external = 0]
        B1 --> C1[m ddot x + c dot x + k x = 0]
        C1 --> D1{Natural Behavior}
        D1 -- zeta = 0 --> E1[Perpetual SHM]
        D1 -- 0 less than zeta less than 1 --> E2[Decaying Oscillation]
        D1 -- zeta = 1 --> E3[Critical No Oscillation]
        D1 -- zeta greater than 1 --> E4[Overdamped Slow Return]
    end

    subgraph FORCED_VIB ["FORCED VIBRATION BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        F1[Continuous Excitation F of t] --> G1[Apply: m ddot x + c dot x + k x = F of t]
        G1 --> H1{Steady-State Behavior}
        H1 -- r less than 1 --> I1[In-Phase Response]
        H1 -- r approx 1 --> I2[Resonance Large Amplitude]
        H1 -- r greater than sqrt 2 --> I3[Out-of-Phase Isolation]
    end
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Left block** (Free Vibration): No external force. The system oscillates due to its own initial energy. The regime depends entirely on `ζ`.
- **Right block** (Forced Vibration): A continuous external force drives the system. The behavior depends on the **frequency ratio `r`**.

---

### 4.3 Forced Vibration Response Flow — Magnification Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: r = omega over omega n] --> B{Damping Regime}
    B -- zeta = 0 --> C[M = 1 over absolute 1 minus r squared]
    B -- zeta greater than 0 --> D[M = 1 over sqrt 1 minus r squared squared plus 2 zeta r squared]
    C --> E[Peak at r = 1: M tends to infinity]
    D --> F[Peak at r = sqrt 1 minus 2 zeta squared]
    D --> G[M max approx 1 over 2 zeta]
    E --> H[NVH Risk: Resonance Failure]
    G --> H
    F --> I[Safe Operating Region: Design target]
    H --> J[Action: Tune omega n away from excitation]
    I --> K[Action: Validate in CAE and Modal Test]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the engineering decision tree — depending on whether the system is undamped or damped, different expressions for `M` are used, and the resulting action (tune away from resonance vs. validate operating region) is determined.

---

### 4.4 Transmissibility Curve — Vibration Isolation Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitation Frequency omega] --> B[Transmissibility TR]
    B --> C{Frequency Ratio r}
    C -- r less than 1 --> D[Amplification Region: TR greater than 1]
    C -- r equals sqrt 2 --> E[Critical Point: TR = 1]
    C -- r greater than sqrt 2 --> F[Isolation Region: TR less than 1]
    D --> G[Design Avoidance: Resonance Danger]
    E --> H[Design Threshold: Minimum Safe Operating Point]
    F --> I[Design Goal: Mounts and Bushings Work Here]
    I --> J[Higher r leads to: TR approx 1 over r squared]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **engineer's mental model for vibration isolation design**. The curve has three regions — amplification, critical point, and isolation — and the design target is always the isolation region, where `r > √2`.

---

### 4.5 Damping Regimes Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Damping Ratio zeta] --> B{Zone}
    B -- zeta less than 0 --> C[Negative Damping: Self-Excited Tabor]
    B -- zeta = 0 --> D[Undamped: Pure SHM]
    B -- 0 less than zeta less than 1 --> E[Underdamped: Decaying Oscillation]
    B -- zeta = 1 --> F[Critically Damped: Fastest Non-Oscillatory]
    B -- zeta greater than 1 --> G[Overdamped: Slow Return]
    C --> H[Automotive: Brake Squeal]
    D --> I[Theoretical Ideal]
    E --> J[Automotive: Most Suspensions]
    F --> K[Automotive: Door Closers, Seat Cushions]
    G --> L[Rare: Off-Road Dampers]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
Define the following with reference to a single degree of freedom (SDOF) vibratory system:
(i) Natural frequency, (ii) Damping ratio, (iii) Resonance.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per definition):**

**(i) Natural Frequency (`ω_n`):** The frequency at which a system oscillates freely when displaced from its equilibrium position and released, in the absence of any external force and damping. Mathematically,
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

**(ii) Damping Ratio (`ζ`):** The ratio of actual damping coefficient `c` to the critical damping coefficient `c_c`. It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates how rapidly the oscillations decay.
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$$

**(iii) Resonance:** A phenomenon that occurs when the frequency of external excitation approaches the natural frequency of the system, resulting in a sharp increase in the amplitude of vibration. In undamped systems, the amplitude theoretically becomes infinite at resonance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Distinguish between **free vibration** and **forced vibration** with one automotive example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Free Vibration | Forced Vibration |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Oscillation of a system after an initial disturbance, with no external force thereafter. | Oscillation of a system under the action of a continuous external (time-varying) force. |
| **Source of Energy** | Initial displacement or velocity (kinetic/potential energy). | Continuous energy input from external excitation. |
| **Frequency** | System oscillates at its **natural frequency** `ω_n`. | System oscillates at the **forcing frequency** `ω` (after transient dies out). |
| **Automotive Example** | After hitting a pothole, the car body bobs up and down on the suspension springs. | Engine combustion pulses transmitted through the driveline to the chassis. |
| **Mathematical Form** | `mẍ + cẋ + kx = 0` | `mẍ + cẋ + kx = F(t)` |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**(a)** A car of mass **1200 kg** is supported by four identical suspension springs. The total stiffness of the suspension system is **k = 200,000 N/m**. When the car hits a bump, it undergoes vertical free vibration. Determine:
(i) The undamped natural frequency in rad/s and Hz. (3 marks)
(ii) The period of oscillation. (2 marks)
(iii) The critical damping coefficient if each damper has equal damping. (2 marks)

**(b)** Derive the equation of motion of a damped SDOF system under harmonic excitation `F(t) = F₀ sin(ωt)`. Hence, obtain the steady-state amplitude and the magnification factor. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:** `m = 1200 kg`, `k = 200,000 N/m`.

**(i) Undamped Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{200{,}000}{1200}} = \sqrt{166.67}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 12.91 \text{ rad/s}}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{12.91}{2\pi} = \boxed{2.054 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(ii) Period of Oscillation:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{12.91} = \boxed{0.487 \text{ s}}$$

**(iii) Critical Damping Coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{1200 \times 200{,}000} = 2\sqrt{2.4 \times 10^8}$$

$$c_c = 2 \times 15{,}491.93 = \boxed{30{,}983.87 \text{ Ns/m}}$$

Damping per damper (assuming 4 equal dampers):
`c_each = c_c / 4 = 7745.97 Ns/m`

---

**Part (b):**

**Derivation of Steady-State Amplitude and Magnification Factor:**

**Step 1:** The equation of motion of a damped SDOF system under harmonic force is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** The total solution is the sum of the **complementary (transient)** and **particular (steady-state)** parts:

$$x(t) = x_c(t) + x_p(t)$$

**Step 3:** The complementary solution is the underdamped free vibration (dies out due to damping):

$$x_c(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)]$$

**Step 4:** For the particular solution, assume:

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 5:** Compute derivatives:

$$\dot{x}_p = X\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$
$$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the equation of motion:

$$-m\omega^2 X\sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X\cos(\omega t - \phi) + kX\sin(\omega t - \phi) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

$$(k - m\omega^2)X\sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X\cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 7:** Expand `F₀sin(ωt)` using the angle addition formula:

$$F_0\sin(\omega t) = F_0\cos\phi\sin(\omega t - \phi) + F_0\sin\phi\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 8:** Equate coefficients of `sin(ωt - φ)` and `cos(ωt - φ)`:

$$(k - m\omega^2)X = F_0\cos\phi$$
$$c\omega X = F_0\sin\phi$$

**Step 9:** Square and add to eliminate `φ`:

$$X^2[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2] = F_0^2$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}}$$

**Step 10:** Divide numerator and denominator by `k`, with `r = ω/ω_n` and `ζ = c/(2mω_n)`:

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Defining the static deflection `X_static = F_0/k`, the **Magnification Factor** is:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. **Forgetting the phase angle** — when you write the assumed form `X sin(ωt − φ)`, you *must* explain why there is a phase lag (because damping delays the response). Marks are awarded for this conceptual justification, not just the final formula.
> 2. **Mixing up `M` and `X`** — `M` is *non-dimensional*; `X` has units of meters. Clearly state `X_static = F₀/k` before defining `M`.
> 3. **Skipping the assumption** — you must explicitly state "assume a particular solution of the form `X sin(ωt − φ)`." Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**(a)** A machine of mass **m = 50 kg** is mounted on a foundation through a spring of stiffness **k = 30,000 N/m** and a damper of damping coefficient **c = 200 Ns/m**. The machine is subjected to a harmonic force **F(t) = 500 sin(40t) N**. Calculate: (7 marks)
(i) The undamped and damped natural frequencies.
(ii) The damping ratio and the type of damping.
(iii) The steady-state amplitude of vibration.

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of **resonance** in an undamped SDOF system. What are the consequences of resonance in automotive systems, and how are they mitigated? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:** `m = 50 kg`, `k = 30,000 N/m`, `c = 200 Ns/m`, `F₀ = 500 N`, `ω = 40 rad/s`.

**(i) Undamped and Damped Natural Frequencies:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{30{,}000}{50}} = \sqrt{600} = \boxed{24.49 \text{ rad/s}}$$

Damping ratio:
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{200}{2\sqrt{50 \times 30{,}000}} = \frac{200}{2 \times 1224.74} = \frac{200}{2449.49} = \boxed{0.0816}$$

Damped natural frequency:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 24.49\sqrt{1 - 0.0816^2} = 24.49\sqrt{1 - 0.00666}$$
$$\omega_d = 24.49 \times 0.9967 = \boxed{24.41 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**(ii) Damping Type:**

Since `0 < ζ = 0.0816 < 1`, the system is **underdamped**.

**(iii) Steady-State Amplitude:**

Frequency ratio:
$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{40}{24.49} = 1.633$$

Steady-state amplitude:
$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

$$k - m\omega^2 = 30{,}000 - 50 \times 1600 = 30{,}000 - 80{,}000 = -50{,}000$$

$$c\omega = 200 \times 40 = 8{,}000$$

$$X = \frac{500}{\sqrt{(-50{,}000)^2 + (8{,}000)^2}} = \frac{500}{\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^9 + 6.4 \times 10^7}}$$

$$X = \frac{500}{\sqrt{2.564 \times 10^9}} = \frac{500}{50{,}636} = \boxed{9.87 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 9.87 \text{ mm}}$$

Magnification factor:
$$M = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{0.00987}{500/30{,}000} = \frac{0.00987}{0.01667} = 0.592$$

So the system is operating in the **isolation region** (`r = 1.633 > √2 ≈ 1.414`).

---

**Part (b):**

**Resonance — Definition and Sketch Description:**

Resonance is the phenomenon in which the amplitude of forced vibration becomes extremely large when the forcing frequency `ω` is close to (or equal to) the natural frequency `ω_n` of the system. In the **frequency ratio** sense, resonance occurs at `r = ω/ω_n = 1`.

**Frequency Response Description (Sketch in Words):**

```
     M (Magnification Factor)
     |
  10 |         .
     |          .
   5 |           .        ζ = 0.05
     |            .     .
   2 |             .  .
     |              ..
   1 |---___________..___________________  ζ = 0.7
     |              ||
   0 |______________||______________________
     0    0.5   1.0   1.5    2.0     r = ω/ωₙ
            ↑
         Resonance Peak (sharpest for low ζ)
```

The peak becomes infinitely tall for an undamped system (`ζ = 0`) at `r = 1`, and decreases in height as damping increases.

**Consequences of Resonance in Automotive Systems:**

1. **Structural failure** — Engine mounts, driveshafts, and chassis components may crack or yield under amplified cyclic stresses.
2. **Loss of comfort** — Steering wheel, seat, and floor-pan vibrations become severe, causing driver fatigue.
3. **Noise radiation** — Vibrating panels radiate sound, increasing cabin Noise levels (the "N" in NVH).
4. **Component wear** — Bearings, bushings, and joints wear out prematurely.
5. **Safety risks** — Loss of steering precision, brake pedal pulsation, and tire hop.

**Mitigation Strategies (KTU-Mandated Points):**

1. **Frequency separation** — Tune the system natural frequency `ω_n` far from dominant excitation frequencies (e.g., engine firing frequency). The general rule: `ω_n < 0.5ω_excitation` or `ω_n > √2·ω_excitation`.
2. **Add damping** — Use tuned dampers, viscoelastic materials, constrained layer damping, or hydraulic mounts to lower the magnification factor peak.
3. **Vibration isolators** — Use rubber mounts, air springs, or active mounts to ensure the excitation frequency is in the isolation region (`r > √2`).
4. **Detuning** — Slightly change stiffness or mass to shift `ω_n` away from the excitation frequency.
5. **Dynamic absorbers** — Add a tuned mass damper (TMD) — a secondary mass-spring system attached to the primary one to absorb vibration energy at a specific frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. **Vague resonance definition** — Examiners want the *mathematical condition* (`r = 1`) and the *physical consequence* (large amplitude). Don't just say "matching of frequencies."
> 2. **Missing the role of damping** — Always emphasize that *damping limits* the peak amplitude; an undamped system has infinite amplitude at resonance.
> 3. **Generic automotive examples** — Be specific: mention **driveshaft whirl** (1st mode = 1× engine speed), **brake judder**, **powertrain shake**, or **tire hop** rather than just "car shakes."
> 4. **Mitigation must be quantitative** — Saying "add damping" is incomplete; state that damping reduces the peak from `1/(2ζ)` toward 1 as `ζ → 0.7`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Free and Forced Vibration**

- [ ] **SDOF equation of motion:** `mẍ + cẋ + kx = F(t)` — *the most important equation in NVH*.
- [ ] **Natural frequency:** `ω_n = √(k/m)` (rad/s); `f_n = ω_n/(2π)` (Hz). *Stiffer spring = higher frequency; heavier mass = lower frequency.*
- [ ] **Critical damping:** `c_c = 2√(mk) = 2mω_n`. **Damping ratio:** `ζ = c/c_c`.
- [ ] **Four damping regimes:** Undamped (`ζ=0`), Underdamped (`0<ζ<1`), Critically damped (`ζ=1`), Overdamped (`ζ>1`).
- [ ] **Damped natural frequency:** `ω_d = ω_n√(1−ζ²)`. Applies *only* in the underdamped regime.
- [ ] **Logarithmic decrement:** `δ = 2πζ/√(1−ζ²) ≈ 2πζ` for small `ζ`. Used to find `ζ` experimentally from free-decay tests.
- [ ] **Steady-state response** to `F₀ sin(ωt)`: amplitude `X = F₀/√((k−mω²)² + (cω)²)`, phase `φ = tan⁻¹(cω/(k−mω²))`.
- [ ] **Magnification factor:** `M = 1/√((1−r²)² + (2ζr)²)`, where `r = ω/ω_n`.
- [ ] **Resonance condition:** `r = 1` (undamped) or `r = √(1−2ζ²)` (damped peak). Max `M ≈ 1/(2ζ)` for small damping.
- [ ] **Transmissibility (base excitation):** `TR = √((1+(2ζr)²)/((1−r²)²+(2ζr)²))`.
- [ ] **Isolation criterion:** For TR < 1, we need `r > √2 ≈ 1.414`. *Core design rule for engine mounts, exhaust hangers, and bushings.*
- [ ] **Energy method:** Kinetic Energy = `½mẋ²`, Potential Energy = `½kx²`, Damping Dissipation = `cẋ²`. Sum to zero gives equation of motion (useful in Lagrange's equations for multi-DOF).
- [ ] **Automotive mapping:**
  - Suspension bounce mode: 1–2 Hz
  - Engine idle firing: 13–27 Hz (4-cyl) or 17–35 Hz (3-cyl)
  - Wheel imbalance: 8–30 Hz
  - Tire-road: 30–250 Hz
  - Brake squeal: 1–15 kHz
- [ ] **Common student errors:** (1) Using `ω` instead of `ω_n` in formulas; (2) Forgetting to convert `ω` to `f` or vice versa; (3) Confusing magnification factor with amplitude; (4) Not stating damping regime before solving.
- [ ] **Cross-references:** Modal analysis (Module 3), Multi-DOF systems (Module 4), Random vibration (Module 5), Acoustic radiation from vibrating panels (Module 6).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Single Degree Freedom System and Two Degree Freedom System
Modes of Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modes of Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode of Vibration (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):**
> A **mode of vibration** is a specific, characteristic pattern of oscillatory motion in which a structure, component, or mechanical system naturally tends to vibrate at a particular frequency known as the **natural frequency** (or **modal frequency**). Each mode is mathematically defined by an **eigenvalue** (ω²) and its corresponding **eigenvector** (mode shape Φ), obtained by solving the generalized eigenvalue problem of the dynamic system.

In the context of **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)**, modes of vibration are the fundamental building blocks used to describe how vehicle components such as chassis frames, body panels (BIW - Body-in-White), powertrain mounts, steering columns, and suspension systems respond to dynamic excitations originating from the engine, road surface, tire imbalance, and aerodynamic forces.

## 1.2 Classification Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Syllabus Classification of Vibrating Systems:
> - **SDOF (Single Degree of Freedom):** Described by a single independent coordinate. Frequency domain is characterized by **one natural frequency** and **one mode shape**.
> - **MDOF (Multi Degree of Freedom):** A system requiring *N* independent coordinates to fully describe its motion. It exhibits *N* natural frequencies and *N* mode shapes.
> - **Continuous Systems:** Infinitely many DOF, infinite number of modes (e.g., beams, plates, car body shells).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Guitar String" Intuition

Imagine plucking a guitar string. You hear not just *one* note, but a rich mixture of **harmonics**. The lowest pitch is the **fundamental (1st) mode**, and as you press the string at different points, you get the 2nd, 3rd, 4th modes — each one having a different pattern (shape) of how the string bows up and down.

**An automobile chassis behaves exactly like that guitar string**:
- The **engine, road, and tires** act like the "plucker" — providing excitation energy.
- The **chassis and body panels** act like the "string" — capable of vibrating in many distinct patterns.
- Each pattern is a **mode**, and its frequency is the **natural frequency** of that mode.
- Engineers must ensure that excitation frequencies (e.g., engine firing at **2,400 RPM** = 40 Hz, 2nd order = **80 Hz**) do not coincide with the chassis natural frequencies. If they do, **resonance** occurs, producing the uncomfortable "buzz, rattle, and boom" sensations that NVH engineers fight against.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics in Bold

- **Natural Frequency (fₙ):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. *f = 1/T*
- **Angular Natural Frequency (ωₙ):** Measured in **radians/second (rad/s)**. *ω = 2πf*
- **Mass (m):** Measured in **kilograms (kg)**.
- **Stiffness (k):** Measured in **Newtons/meter (N/m)**.
- **Damping Ratio (ζ):** Dimensionless parameter, where **0 ≤ ζ ≤ 1**.
- **Mode Shape Amplitude (Φᵢⱼ):** Dimensionless, normalized value indicating relative displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical NVH Insight:**
> In modern passenger cars, the **first body bending mode** typically lies between **25 – 35 Hz**, and the **first body torsional mode** between **30 – 45 Hz**. Exciting these modes results in unacceptable customer-perceived harshness, making mode identification a critical step in the vehicle development cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) Foundation

A SDOF system is the **atom** of vibration analysis. Every MDOF analysis eventually reduces to a combination of SDOF problems (via modal superposition). The governing differential equation for a forced, damped SDOF system is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

**Physical Interpretation of Each Term:**

- **m·ẍ(t)** — The **inertia force**. Mass resists changes in motion (Newton's 1st law).
- **c·ẋ(t)** — The **damping force**. Represents energy dissipation (viscous dashpot model).
- **k·x(t)** — The **spring (restoring) force**. Always acts to return the system to equilibrium.
- **F(t)** — The **external excitation** (e.g., engine force input, road disturbance).

## 2.2 Natural Frequency Derivation (Undamped Free Vibration)

Setting *c = 0* and *F(t) = 0*, we get:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + kx(t) = 0$$

The solution is harmonic: *x(t) = X·sin(ωₙt + φ)*, leading to:

$$m(-\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)) + k(X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution (*X ≠ 0*):

$$(k - m\omega_n^2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

## 2.3 Extension to Multi-Degree of Freedom (MDOF) Systems

For an *N-DOF* system, the equation of motion in matrix form is:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [C]\{\dot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{F(t)\}$$

- **[M]** — Mass matrix (**N × N**, symmetric, positive definite)
- **[C]** — Damping matrix (**N × N**, symmetric, often proportional)
- **[K]** — Stiffness matrix (**N × N**, symmetric, positive semi-definite)

Assuming harmonic motion: *{x(t)} = {Φ}·e^(jωt)*, the **undamped free vibration** reduces to the **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\Phi\} = \{0\}$$

For a non-trivial solution, the **characteristic determinant** must vanish:

$$\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$$

This gives a polynomial of degree *N* whose **N roots** (ω₁², ω₂², …, ωₙ²) are the **eigenvalues** (squared natural frequencies), and each ωᵢ has a corresponding **eigenvector** {Φᵢ} = the **mode shape**.

## 2.4 Modal Orthogonality — The "Super-Power" of Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important property of mode shapes is **orthogonality with respect to the mass and stiffness matrices**. This property allows decoupled analysis of each mode independently.

$$\{\Phi_i\}^T [M] \{\Phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad i \neq j$$

$$\{\Phi_i\}^T [K] \{\Phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad i \neq j$$

When *i = j*, these give the **modal mass (mᵢ)** and **modal stiffness (kᵢ)**:

$$m_i = \{\Phi_i\}^T [M] \{\Phi_i\}$$

$$k_i = \{\Phi_i\}^T [K] \{\Phi_i\}$$

And critically: **ωᵢ² = kᵢ / mᵢ**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of every KTU NVH problem.**

| # | Formula | Symbol/Variable | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | ωₙ = √(k/m) | ωₙ : Natural freq. | **rad/s** | Natural frequency (rad/s) |
| 2 | fₙ = (1/2π)·√(k/m) | fₙ : Nat. freq. | **Hz** | Natural frequency in Hertz |
| 3 | det([K] - ω²[M]) = 0 | [K],[M] : Matrices | **kg, N/m** | Characteristic equation for MDOF |
| 4 | Φᵢᵀ·M·Φⱼ = 0 (i≠j) | Modal vectors | — | Mass-orthogonality of modes |
| 5 | Φᵢᵀ·K·Φⱼ = 0 (i≠j) | Modal vectors | — | Stiffness-orthogonality of modes |
| 6 | ωᵢ² = kᵢ/mᵢ | Modal params | **rad²/s²** | Eigenvalue (squared natural freq.) |
| 7 | {x(t)} = Σ qᵢ(t)·{Φᵢ} | Modal coord. | **m** | Modal superposition principle |
| 8 | r = ω/ωₙ | Frequency ratio | **dimensionless** | Excitation-to-natural ratio |
| 9 | ζ = c/(2√(km)) | Damping ratio | **dimensionless** | Critical damping fraction |
| 10 | MF = 1/√[(1-r²)² + (2ζr)²] | Magnification | **dimensionless** | Dynamic amplification factor |

> **Note on table:** Vertical bars `\vert` are used in formulas to avoid markdown table-breaking pipe characters.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive NVH engineering, understanding modes is essential for:

1. **Body-in-White (BIW) Design:** Avoiding engine idle frequency (typically 25–35 Hz) matching the body's bending/torsion modes.
2. **Powertrain Mounting:** Designing engine mounts so that mount stiffness creates a system mode well-isolated from engine excitation orders.
3. **Steering Column NVH:** Preventing column modes from coupling with tire-road excitation at highway speeds.
4. **Brake Squeal Analysis:** Identifying brake-disc mode shapes that align with friction-induced excitation frequencies.
5. **Acoustic Package Tuning:** Placing damping materials at **antinode locations** (max displacement) to maximize acoustic absorption efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Natural Frequency of an SDOF Spring-Mass System

**Given:** Mass *m* attached to a spring of stiffness *k* and a damper *c*, subjected to external force *F(t)*.

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram (FBD) — Newton's 2nd Law**

Apply Newton's second law (ΣF = m·a) along the direction of motion:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) = -kx(t) - c\dot{x}(t) + F(t)$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to Standard Form**

Move all terms to the LHS:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

**Step 3: Assume Harmonic Solution for Free Vibration**

For free vibration, *F(t) = 0*. Assume a solution of the form:

$$x(t) = X \cdot e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 4: Compute Derivatives**

$$\dot{x}(t) = j\omega X e^{j\omega t}$$

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 X e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the Equation of Motion**

$$m(-\omega^2 X e^{j\omega t}) + c(j\omega X e^{j\omega t}) + k(X e^{j\omega t}) = 0$$

**Step 6: Factor Out the Common Term X·e^(jωt)**

$$X e^{j\omega t} \left[ -m\omega^2 + jc\omega + k \right] = 0$$

**Step 7: Identify the Characteristic Equation**

Since *X·e^(jωt) ≠ 0* (for non-trivial vibration), the bracket must be zero:

$$-m\omega^2 + jc\omega + k = 0$$

**Step 8: Solve for ω (Damped Natural Frequency)**

For light damping (typical in automotive systems), the **damped natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

And the **undamped natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

> **Conversion to Hertz:** *fₙ = ωₙ / (2π)*

## 3.2 Derivation: Two-Degree-of-Freedom (2-DOF) Mode Shapes

Consider a 2-DOF system (two masses *m₁* and *m₂* connected by three springs *k₁, k₂, k₃* in a typical series configuration).

**Step 1: Write the Equations of Motion**

For mass *m₁*: *m₁ẍ₁ = -k₁x₁ - k₂(x₁ - x₂)*

For mass *m₂*: *m₂ẍ₂ = -k₃x₂ - k₂(x₂ - x₁)*

Rearranging:

$$m_1\ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0$$

$$m_2\ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Convert to Matrix Form**

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1+k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2+k_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Assume Harmonic Solution {x} = {Φ}·e^(jωt)**

$$\left( [K] - \omega^2[M] \right) \{\Phi\} = \{0\}$$

**Step 4: Set Determinant to Zero**

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} (k_1+k_2) - \omega^2 m_1 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & (k_2+k_3) - \omega^2 m_2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 5: Expand the Determinant**

$$\left[(k_1+k_2) - \omega^2 m_1\right]\left[(k_2+k_3) - \omega^2 m_2\right] - k_2^2 = 0$$

**Step 6: Expand the Product**

$$(k_1+k_2)(k_2+k_3) - (k_1+k_2)\omega^2 m_2 - (k_2+k_3)\omega^2 m_1 + \omega^4 m_1 m_2 - k_2^2 = 0$$

**Step 7: Collect Terms in Powers of ω²**

$$\omega^4 (m_1 m_2) - \omega^2 \left[ (k_1+k_2)m_2 + (k_2+k_3)m_1 \right] + \left[ (k_1+k_2)(k_2+k_3) - k_2^2 \right] = 0$$

**Step 8: Solve the Quadratic in ω²**

This is a **quadratic equation** in *ω²*. Let *λ = ω²*. Then:

$$\lambda^2 - \lambda \cdot \frac{(k_1+k_2)m_2 + (k_2+k_3)m_1}{m_1 m_2} + \frac{(k_1+k_2)(k_2+k_3) - k_2^2}{m_1 m_2} = 0$$

**Step 9: Apply the Quadratic Formula**

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}$$

where:

$$A = m_1 m_2$$

$$B = -[(k_1+k_2)m_2 + (k_2+k_3)m_1]$$

$$C = (k_1+k_2)(k_2+k_3) - k_2^2$$

This gives **two natural frequencies**: *ω₁* and *ω₂*.

**Step 10: Find the Mode Shapes**

Substitute *λ = ω₁²* back into *([K] - ω²[M]){Φ} = {0}*:

$$\left[ (k_1+k_2) - \omega_1^2 m_1 \right] \Phi_{1,1} - k_2 \Phi_{1,2} = 0$$

Solving for the ratio:

$$\frac{\Phi_{1,1}}{\Phi_{1,2}} = \frac{k_2}{(k_1+k_2) - \omega_1^2 m_1}$$

This ratio defines the **first mode shape** {Φ₁}ᵀ = {1, Φ₁,₂/Φ₁,₁}.

> **Repeat for ω₂** to obtain the second mode shape {Φ₂}.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Summary Table

| Step | Operation | Input | Output |
|------|-----------|-------|--------|
| 1 | FBD / Newton-2nd Law | Mass, spring, damper | Coupled ODEs |
| 2 | Rearrange | Coupled ODEs | Standard form mẍ+cẋ+kx=F |
| 3 | Matrix assembly | Standard form | [M]{ẍ}+[C]{ẋ}+[K]{x}={F} |
| 4 | Assume harmonic solution | [M],[C],[K] | x=Φ·e^(jωt) |
| 5 | Form characteristic equation | Sub into EOM | det([K]-ω²[M])=0 |
| 6 | Expand polynomial | det equation | Polynomial in ω² |
| 7 | Solve roots | Polynomial | Eigenvalues ω₁², ω₂²,…,ωₙ² |
| 8 | Back-substitute | Each ωᵢ² | Eigenvector {Φᵢ} |
| 9 | Normalize | {Φᵢ} | Unit modal vectors |
| 10 | Verify orthogonality | All {Φᵢ} | ΦᵢᵀMΦⱼ=0 (i≠j) ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Modal Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Physical System] --> B[Build Lumped Model]
    B --> C[Derive Equations of Motion]
    C --> D[Assemble M and K Matrices]
    D --> E[Solve det K minus omega2 M equals 0]
    E --> F[Obtain N Eigenvalues omega squared]
    F --> G[Back-substitute for Eigenvectors]
    G --> H[Normalize Mode Shapes]
    H --> I[Verify Modal Orthogonality]
    I --> J[Plot Mode Shapes and Frequencies]
    J --> K[Correlate with Excitation Sources]
    K --> L[End: NVH Design Recommendations]

    step1[Step 1: Discretization]:::proc
    step2[Step 2: Matrix Formation]:::proc
    step3[Step 3: Eigenvalue Solver]:::proc
    step4[Step 4: Mode Visualization]:::proc

    A -.-> step1
    B -.-> step2
    E -.-> step3
    J -.-> step4

    classDef proc fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: SDOF Spring-Mass-Damper

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    Mass["Mass m\n(Inertia Force)"]:::mass
    Spring["Spring k\n(Restoring Force)"]:::spring
    Damper["Damper c\n(Dissipation)"]:::damp
    Ground["Fixed Reference"]:::gnd
    F["Excitation F of t"]:::force
    Response["Displacement x of t"]:::resp

    F --> Mass
    Mass --- Spring
    Mass --- Damper
    Spring --- Ground
    Damper --- Ground
    Mass --> Response

    classDef mass fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef spring fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef damp fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#9E9E9E,stroke:#212121,color:#FFF
    classDef force fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef resp fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: Modal Superposition Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Physical Coordinates x of t] --> T1[Modal Transformation Matrix Phi]
    T1 --> Q[Modal Coordinates q of t]
    Q --> MD1[Mode 1 SDOF Solver omega1]
    Q --> MD2[Mode 2 SDOF Solver omega2]
    Q --> MD3[Mode 3 SDOF Solver omega3]
    Q --> MDn[Mode N SDOF Solver omegaN]
    MD1 --> SUM[Modal Summation Sum qi times Phii]
    MD2 --> SUM
    MD3 --> SUM
    MDn --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Reconstructed Response x of t]
    OUT --> NVH[NVH Performance Metrics]

    classDef m1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef m2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef m3 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef mn fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000

    class MD1 m1
    class MD2 m2
    class MD3 m3
    class MDn mn
    class OUT,OUT out
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mode Shape Visualization (Text-Based Schematic)

```
   MODE 1 (Fundamental Bending) - f1 ≈ 28 Hz
   ─────────────────────────────────────────
   |        ___                    ___      |
   |       /   \                  /   \     |
   |      /     \                /     \    |
   |_____/_______\______________/_______\___|
                 ↑ NODE (zero displacement)

   MODE 2 (First Torsion) - f2 ≈ 36 Hz
   ─────────────────────────────────────────
   |    ___                              ___|
   |   /   \                            /  |
   |  /     \                          /   |
   |_/_______\___NODE___NODE__________/____|
                              ↑ ↑

   MODE 3 (Second Bending) - f3 ≈ 45 Hz
   ─────────────────────────────────────────
   |   /\          /\                /\    |
   |  /  \        /  \              /  \   |
   |_/____\______/____\____________/____\__|
        ↑ NODE         ↑ NODE
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **nodes** are points of zero displacement; **antinodes** are points of maximum displacement. Damping material is most effective when placed at antinode locations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **mode of vibration** and **mode shape**. Explain with a suitable example from an automotive system.

**Model Answer:**
A *mode of vibration* is a characteristic pattern in which a structure naturally oscillates at a specific frequency called its natural frequency. The *mode shape* describes the relative displacement pattern of all points of the structure for that particular mode. **Example:** In a car body-in-white (BIW), the first body bending mode at approximately 28 Hz causes the central floor pan to deflect maximum while the wheelbase corners (nodes) remain relatively stationary.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is **modal orthogonality**? State the two orthogonality conditions for mode shapes.

**Model Answer:**
Modal orthogonality is a mathematical property that states that distinct mode shapes of an undamped vibrating system are mutually independent with respect to the mass and stiffness matrices. The two conditions are:

$$\{\Phi_i\}^T [M] \{\Phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad i \neq j$$

$$\{\Phi_i\}^T [K] \{\Phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad i \neq j$$

This property allows the MDOF system to be decoupled into N independent SDOF systems.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q-B Module Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1

**Q.A(a)** Derive the equation for the **natural frequency** of an undamped single degree of freedom (SDOF) spring-mass system. Starting from the free body diagram, clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The system has only one degree of freedom (vertical translation *x*).
2. The mass *m* is rigid and concentrated.
3. The spring is linear with stiffness *k*.
4. Damping is neglected (undamped case).
5. Motion occurs about the static equilibrium position.

**Step 1: FBD and Newton's 2nd Law**

$$\sum F = m\ddot{x}$$

The forces acting on the mass are the spring force (restoring) and any external force. For free vibration, only the spring acts:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

**Step 2: Rearrange**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 3: Assume harmonic solution**

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

**Step 4: Substitute**

$$m(-\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)) + k(X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)) = 0$$

**Step 5: Factor**

$$X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)(k - m\omega_n^2) = 0$$

**Step 6: For non-trivial solution**

$$k - m\omega_n^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \text{ rad/s}}$$

In Hertz: *fₙ = (1/2π)√(k/m)*

---

**Q.A(b)** A car suspension is modeled as a SDOF system with *m = 350 kg* and *k = 30,000 N/m*. Calculate: **(i)** the natural frequency in Hz, **(ii)** the natural period, and **(iii)** comment on whether this frequency will cause resonance with engine idle excitation at 30 Hz. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** m = 350 kg, k = 30,000 N/m

**Part (i): Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{30000}{350}} = \sqrt{85.714} = 9.26 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{9.26}{2\pi} = 1.474 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (ii): Natural Period**

$$T_n = \frac{1}{f_n} = \frac{1}{1.474} = 0.678 \text{ seconds}$$

**Part (iii): Resonance Analysis**

The natural frequency of the suspension (1.47 Hz) is **far below** the engine idle excitation frequency (30 Hz). Since the frequency ratio *r = 30/1.47 ≈ 20.4* is very high, the system operates deep in the **isolation region** (r > √2). Therefore, **no resonance occurs**; in fact, the suspension is effectively isolating the cabin from engine vibrations, which is the desired behavior for ride comfort.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2

**Q.B(a)** For a **two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) system**, derive the characteristic equation using the eigenvalue problem approach. Clearly show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Standard 2-DOF Equation of Motion**

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$$

where:

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} & k_{12} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Assume Harmonic Solution**

$$\{x(t)\} = \{\Phi\} e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into EOM**

$$[M](-\omega^2)\{\Phi\} e^{j\omega t} + [K]\{\Phi\} e^{j\omega t} = 0$$

$$([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\Phi\} = \{0\}$$

**Step 4: Non-trivial Solution Condition**

For *{Φ} ≠ {0}*:

$$\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$$

**Step 5: Expand the Determinant**

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} - \omega^2 m_1 & k_{12} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} - \omega^2 m_2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$(k_{11} - \omega^2 m_1)(k_{22} - \omega^2 m_2) - k_{12} k_{21} = 0$$

**Step 6: Expand Product**

$$k_{11}k_{22} - k_{11}\omega^2 m_2 - k_{22}\omega^2 m_1 + \omega^4 m_1 m_2 - k_{12}k_{21} = 0$$

**Step 7: Rearrange in Standard Quadratic Form**

$$m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - (k_{11} m_2 + k_{22} m_1)\omega^2 + (k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}) = 0$$

**Step 8: Apply Quadratic Formula**

Let *λ = ω²*:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(k_{11}m_2 + k_{22}m_1) \pm \sqrt{(k_{11}m_2 + k_{22}m_1)^2 - 4 m_1 m_2 (k_{11}k_{22} - k_{12}k_{21})}}{2 m_1 m_2}$$

The two positive roots give **ω₁²** and **ω₂²**, the squared natural frequencies.

---

**Q.B(b)** A 2-DOF system has *[M] = [[2, 0], [0, 1]] kg* and *[K] = [[6, -2], [-2, 4]] N/m*. Determine the two natural frequencies and the corresponding mode shape ratios. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up the Characteristic Equation**

$$[K] - \omega^2[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 2\omega^2 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 - \omega^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Determinant**

$$(6 - 2\omega^2)(4 - \omega^2) - (-2)(-2) = 0$$

**Step 3: Expand**

$$24 - 6\omega^2 - 8\omega^2 + 2\omega^4 - 4 = 0$$

$$2\omega^4 - 14\omega^2 + 20 = 0$$

**Step 4: Divide by 2**

$$\omega^4 - 7\omega^2 + 10 = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve the Quadratic in λ = ω²**

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0$$

$$(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0$$

$$\lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 5$$

**Step 6: Compute Natural Frequencies**

$$\omega_1 = \sqrt{2} = 1.414 \text{ rad/s} \quad (f_1 = 0.225 \text{ Hz})$$

$$\omega_2 = \sqrt{5} = 2.236 \text{ rad/s} \quad (f_2 = 0.356 \text{ Hz})$$

**Step 7: Find Mode Shape for Mode 1 (ω₁² = 2)**

$$(6 - 2(2))\Phi_{1,1} - 2\Phi_{1,2} = 0$$

$$2\Phi_{1,1} = 2\Phi_{1,2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Phi_{1,1}}{\Phi_{1,2}} = 1$$

Mode 1: {Φ₁} = {1, 1}ᵀ (in-phase, symmetric mode)

**Step 8: Find Mode Shape for Mode 2 (ω₂² = 5)**

$$(6 - 2(5))\Phi_{2,1} - 2\Phi_{2,2} = 0$$

$$-4\Phi_{2,1} = 2\Phi_{2,2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Phi_{2,1}}{\Phi_{2,2}} = -0.5$$

Mode 2: {Φ₂} = {1, -2}ᵀ (out-of-phase, anti-symmetric mode)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the determinant step:** Many students write the eigenvalue equation *([K] - ω²[M]){Φ} = 0* but forget to state the non-trivial solution condition **det(...) = 0**. This alone costs **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing natural frequency (ωₙ in rad/s) with frequency in Hz:** KTU examiners specifically check for correct unit conversion using *f = ω/(2π)*. Writing 1.414 Hz instead of 1.414 rad/s = 0.225 Hz is a **1-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Not normalizing mode shapes:** Always express mode shapes in a normalized form (e.g., first component = 1), or use mass-normalization. A mode shape given as raw values without normalization loses **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Skipping orthogonality verification:** KTU often awards a 2-mark bonus for showing ΦᵢᵀMΦⱼ = 0 in 2-DOF problems. Don't miss this opportunity.
>
> 5. **Wrong order of eigenvalues:** Always arrange ω₁ < ω₂ < ω₃... (ascending). Reversing the order is a **1-mark penalty**.
>
> 6. **Sign errors in determinant expansion:** A common algebraic mistake is sign error on the cross term *-k₁₂k₂₁*. Double-check signs.
>
> 7. **Not drawing/sketching mode shapes:** For full marks in long-answer questions, always include a **qualitative sketch** of the mode shape showing nodes and antinodes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Mode of vibration** = (natural frequency) + (mode shape), obtained as an **eigenvalue-eigenvector pair**.
- ✅ **SDOF** has 1 mode; **N-DOF** has N modes; **continuous** systems have infinite modes.
- ✅ Governing ODE: *mẍ + cẋ + kx = F(t)*.
- ✅ **Undamped natural frequency:** *ωₙ = √(k/m)* rad/s; *fₙ = ωₙ/(2π)* Hz.
- ✅ **Eigenvalue problem:** *([K] - ω²[M]){Φ} = {0}*.
- ✅ **Characteristic equation:** *det([K] - ω²[M]) = 0*.
- ✅ **Orthogonality:** *ΦᵢᵀMΦⱼ = 0* and *ΦᵢᵀKΦⱼ = 0* for *i ≠ j*.
- ✅ **Modal mass:** *mᵢ = ΦᵢᵀMΦᵢ*; **Modal stiffness:** *kᵢ = ΦᵢᵀKΦᵢ*.
- ✅ **Eigenvalue identity:** *ωᵢ² = kᵢ / mᵢ*.
- ✅ **Modal superposition:** *{x(t)} = Σᵢ qᵢ(t){Φᵢ}* — decouples MDOF into N independent SDOF problems.
- ✅ **Damping ratio:** *ζ = c / (2√(km))*.
- ✅ **Frequency ratio:** *r = ω/ωₙ*.
- ✅ **Resonance** occurs when *r ≈ 1* and damping is low.
- ✅ **Node** = zero displacement point; **Antinode** = maximum displacement point.
- ✅ **First body bending mode** for cars ≈ 25–35 Hz; **first torsion** ≈ 30–45 Hz.
- ✅ **Mode shapes are always drawn at the natural frequency** and indicate relative (not absolute) amplitudes.
- ✅ **Always normalize** mode shapes; standard convention: largest component = 1, or use mass-normalization.
- ✅ **Real-world NVH goal:** Avoid coincidence of excitation frequencies (engine orders, road inputs) with structural natural frequencies.
- ✅ **Damping material** is most effective at **antinode** locations.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** at the start of derivations (linearity, lumped parameters, undamped unless specified).
- ✅ **Use proper sign convention**: spring force opposes motion ⇒ negative sign in FBD.

> **END OF TOPIC: MODES OF VIBRATION — KTU PEMUT863, MODULE 2**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance** is a physical phenomenon in which a mechanical, electrical, or acoustic system is driven at its **natural frequency** (or very close to it), resulting in the maximum amplitude of oscillation, the highest energy transfer, and a phase alignment between the excitation and response forces.

In the context of **Automotive Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH)**, resonance refers to the condition when an external or internal excitation frequency (such as engine firing frequency, road input, or wheel imbalance) coincides with one of the **natural frequencies** of a vehicle subsystem (sprung mass, body panel, exhaust hanger, driveshaft, etc.), causing disproportionately large vibration amplitudes, structural fatigue, and unacceptable Noise-Vibration-Harshness levels.

Mathematically, resonance occurs when the **frequency ratio** $r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} \rightarrow 1$, where $\omega$ is the excitation frequency and $\omega_n$ is the undamped natural frequency of the system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation

**The "Push-the-Swing" Analogy**

Imagine pushing a child on a playground swing:
- If you push **randomly** (not in sync), the swing oscillates weakly and eventually damps out.
- If you push **in tune with the swing's natural rhythm** (once per cycle at the right moment), each push adds energy, and the swing goes higher and higher — this is **resonance**.
- If you push **twice as fast as the natural rhythm** (out of phase), you actually fight the motion and slow it down.

A classic, dramatic example is an opera singer shattering a wine glass by sustaining a note at the glass's natural frequency. The energy accumulates in the glass until its material strength is exceeded, and it cracks.

In an **automobile**, similar resonant events include:
- **Steering wheel shimmy** at a particular road speed (tire-wheel assembly natural frequency).
- **Driveline boom** at engine cruise (driveline bending mode matching firing frequency).
- **Body panel buzz** at a specific engine RPM (panel mode matching combustion excitation).
- **Front/rear axle hop** under wheel imbalance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Resonance is not just about *amplitude* — it is fundamentally a **phase phenomenon**. At resonance, the driving force and velocity are in phase, meaning energy is transferred into the system at the maximum possible rate. This is why resonance is the most efficient energy-coupling condition in mechanical vibration.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following fundamental quantities govern resonance in linear single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) systems:

- **Natural Circular Frequency**: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ in **rad/s**
- **Natural Frequency**: $f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$ in **Hz**
- **Damping Ratio**: $\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ (dimensionless)
- **Damped Natural Frequency**: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ in **rad/s**
- **Quality Factor**: $Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$ (dimensionless)
- **Resonance Frequency (Damped)**: $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ in **rad/s**
- **Resonance Frequency (Undamped)**: $\omega_r = \omega_n$ in **rad/s**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Check:** In SI units, mass is in **kg**, stiffness $k$ in **N/m**, and damping $c$ in **N·s/m**. Always report $\omega$ in **rad/s** and $f$ in **Hz** — these are **NOT** interchangeable on the answer sheet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why and How Resonance Occurs

The phenomenon of resonance arises from the dynamic response of a mass-spring-damper system subjected to a periodic excitation. The "why" and "how" can be understood through the following structured logic:

**Step 1 — System Energy Storage**
A mechanical oscillator stores energy in two ways:
- **Kinetic energy** in the moving mass: $KE = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^2$
- **Potential energy** in the elastic spring: $PE = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$

At resonance, the periodic energy transfer between $KE$ and $PE$ is perfectly synchronized with the external forcing, so the energy is added on every cycle instead of being partially returned.

**Step 2 — Phase Alignment**
The damping force always opposes velocity. The applied force must overcome this opposition. At resonance, the velocity is in-phase with the applied force, so the work done per cycle $W = \oint F \cdot dx$ is **maximized**.

**Step 3 — Amplitude Build-Up**
In an undamped system, amplitude grows **linearly with time** ($X(t) \propto t$). With small damping, amplitude grows to a large steady-state value (the **magnification factor** $M$ is large). With high damping ($\zeta \geq 1$), the system is overdamped and resonance is suppressed.

**Step 4 — Resonant Energy Transfer (Automotive Implication)**
In NVH engineering, resonance is a **double-edged sword**:
- **Constructive use:** tuned mass dampers, exhaust resonators, Helmholtz silencers.
- **Destructive effect:** engine mount failures, body boom, structural fatigue cracks.

### 2.2 Resonance in the Frequency Domain — Steady-State Response

For a base-excited SDOF system (a standard model for vehicle suspension, seat-occupant, and engine mount subsystems), the steady-state amplitude of the mass is given by the classical dynamic magnification factor expression. The operating regimes are classified by the frequency ratio $r$ and damping ratio $\zeta$:

| Regime | Frequency Range | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Quasi-static** | $r \ll 1$ | Mass moves with base; $X \approx Y$ (input amplitude) |
| **Isolation onset** | $r \approx 1$ | Maximum response; **resonance peak** |
| **Isolation region** | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | Transmissibility $T < 1$; good vibration isolation |
| **Mass-control** | $r \gg 1$ | Response decays as $1/r^2$; mass dominates |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **printable quick-reference** for all resonance-related equations tested under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Use $\vert$ for absolute value to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Undamped Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | **rad/s** |
| 2 | Natural Frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$ | **Hz** |
| 3 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Critical Damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | **N·s/m** |
| 5 | Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | **rad/s** |
| 6 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Undamped Resonance Frequency | $\omega_r = \omega_n$ | **rad/s** (no damping) |
| 8 | Damped Resonance Frequency | $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | **rad/s** ($\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$) |
| 9 | Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF) | $M = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Maximum DMF at Resonance | $M_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Half-Power Bandwidth | $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$ | **rad/s** |
| 13 | Transmissibility | $T = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Transmissibility $\geq 1$ | $T = 1$ at $r = 0$ and $r = \sqrt{2}$ | crossover points |
| 15 | Phase Angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)$ | **rad** |
| 16 | Steady-State Amplitude | $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | **m** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Mount Tuning:** Engineers design elastomer mounts so that the mount natural frequency lies **below** the idle firing frequency (typically 25–30 Hz) but **above** the road-excitation band to avoid engine shake.
- **Tuned Mass Dampers (TMD):** Used in steering systems to absorb vibration at a specific target frequency (e.g., 30 Hz brake shudder).
- **Exhaust Resonators:** Quarter-wave and Helmholtz resonators are designed at specific engine RPM orders to cancel NVH peaks.
- **Body-in-White Structure:** Body panels are tuned so that panel natural frequencies are **decoupled** from the engine firing frequency, tire-road input, and driveline orders.
- **Crash Avoidance:** Bridges (e.g., the famous Tacoma Narrows collapse) and aircraft wings are designed to avoid resonance with vortex shedding or gust loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Steady-State Amplitude and Resonance Condition for a SDOF System

**System Setup:**
Consider a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass-spring-damper system subjected to a harmonic excitation force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

The parameters are:
- Mass: $m$ (kg)
- Stiffness: $k$ (N/m)
- Damping coefficient: $c$ (N·s/m)
- Displacement: $x(t)$ (m)

---

**Step 1 — Write Newton's Second Law of Motion**

The total force on the mass equals mass times acceleration:

$$F_{inertia} = F_{spring} + F_{damper} + F_{applied}$$

Translating each term mathematically:

$$m \ddot{x}(t) = -k x(t) - c \dot{x}(t) + F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Rearranging to the standard form:

$$m \ddot{x}(t) + c \dot{x}(t) + k x(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

This is the **governing differential equation** of forced vibration of a damped SDOF system.

---

**Step 2 — Normalize the Equation by Dividing Through by $m$**

$$\ddot{x}(t) + \frac{c}{m} \dot{x}(t) + \frac{k}{m} x(t) = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

Substituting the standard definitions:

$$\ddot{x}(t) + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x}(t) + \omega_n^2 x(t) = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

This is the canonical form used in **all** NVH textbooks and KTU reference materials.

---

**Step 3 — Assume a Steady-State Harmonic Solution**

The total response is the sum of a transient part (which decays) and a steady-state part (which persists). For the steady-state response, we assume:

$$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where:
- $X$ is the **steady-state amplitude** of vibration (unknown to be determined).
- $\phi$ is the **phase angle** by which the displacement lags the applied force.

Compute the first and second time derivatives:

$$\dot{x}_p(t) = X \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x}_p(t) = -X \omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

---

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Differential Equation**

Plugging $x_p$, $\dot{x}_p$, $\ddot{x}_p$ into the canonical equation:

$$-X \omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta\omega_n \cdot X \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + \omega_n^2 \cdot X \sin(\omega t - \phi) = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

Grouping the sine and cosine terms:

$$X\left(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2\right) \sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2X\zeta\omega_n \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

---

**Step 5 — Expand Using Trigonometric Identities**

Use the identities:
- $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin(\omega t)\cos\phi - \cos(\omega t)\sin\phi$
- $\cos(\omega t - \phi) = \cos(\omega t)\cos\phi + \sin(\omega t)\sin\phi$

Substituting and collecting $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ terms:

$$X\left[(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\cos\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n\omega\sin\phi\right]\sin(\omega t) + X\left[-(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n\omega\cos\phi\right]\cos(\omega t) = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

---

**Step 6 — Equate Coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$**

For the equation to hold for all $t$:

**Coefficient of $\sin(\omega t)$:**

$$X\left[(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\cos\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n\omega\sin\phi\right] = \frac{F_0}{m}$$

**Coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:**

$$X\left[-(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n\omega\cos\phi\right] = 0$$

---

**Step 7 — Solve for the Phase Angle $\phi$**

From the cosine coefficient equation:

$$(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin\phi = 2\zeta\omega_n\omega\cos\phi$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta\omega_n\omega}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2} = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}$$

where $r = \omega/\omega_n$ is the frequency ratio. This is the **standard KTU phase-angle result**.

---

**Step 8 — Solve for the Amplitude $X$**

Square both sine and cosine coefficient equations and add them. Using $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$:

$$X^2\left[(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2\right] = \left(\frac{F_0}{m}\right)^2$$

Solve for $X$:

$$X = \frac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$

Factor out $\omega_n^2$ from the denominator:

$$X = \frac{F_0/(m\omega_n^2)}{\sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{\omega^2}{\omega_n^2}\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta\frac{\omega}{\omega_n}\right)^2}}$$

Recognize that $F_0/(k) = F_0/(m\omega_n^2) = X_{static}$ (the static deflection). Hence:

$$\boxed{X = \frac{X_{static}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

This is the **fundamental amplitude equation** for forced vibration. The denominator is the **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)**.

---

**Step 9 — Derive the Resonance Frequency (Damped System)**

Resonance occurs when the amplitude $X$ is **maximum**, i.e., the denominator is **minimum**. To find the optimum $r$, differentiate $X^2$ with respect to $r^2$ and set it to zero.

Let $D(r) = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$.

$$\frac{dD}{d(r^2)} = 0 \implies \frac{dD}{dr} = 0$$

Compute $\dfrac{dD}{dr}$:

$$\frac{dD}{dr} = 2(1 - r^2)(-2r) + 2(2\zeta r)(2\zeta) = 0$$

$$-4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$

Divide both sides by $4r$ (excluding $r=0$):

$$-(1 - r^2) + 2\zeta^2 = 0$$

$$r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$

$$r_{resonance} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

Hence, the **resonance frequency** for a damped system is:

$$\boxed{\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** This expression is only **real** when $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. Beyond this, the system does **not exhibit a resonance peak** — the response simply rolls off monotonically.

---

**Step 10 — Maximum Dynamic Magnification Factor**

Substitute $r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$ into the DMF:

$$D_{min} = (1 - (1 - 2\zeta^2))^2 + (2\zeta\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2})^2 = (2\zeta^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2)$$

Simplify:

$$D_{min} = 4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4 = 4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)$$

Hence the maximum DMF is:

$$M_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

For light damping ($\zeta \ll 1$), this reduces to $M_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ — the classical result.

---

**Step 11 — Quality Factor and Bandwidth**

The **Quality Factor** characterizes the sharpness of the resonance peak:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_n}{\Delta\omega} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

The **half-power bandwidth** is the frequency range over which the response power is at least half the peak:

$$\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$$

These are critical in **automotive NVH diagnostics** for evaluating peak sharpness in FRF (Frequency Response Function) measurements.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Undamped ($\zeta = 0$) | Damped ($0 < \zeta < 0.707$) | Heavy Damping ($\zeta \geq 0.707$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resonance Frequency | $\omega_r = \omega_n$ | $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ | No distinct peak |
| Peak Amplitude | $\infty$ (unbounded) | $X_{max} = \dfrac{F_0/k}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Monotonic decay |
| Phase at Resonance | $\phi = \pi/2$ | $\phi < \pi/2$ | Smooth transition |
| Energy Stored | Continuous growth | Steady-state, balanced by damping | Dissipated quickly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forced SDOF System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitation Source F0 sin wt] --> B[Force Input Node]
    B --> C[Mass m]
    C --> D[Damper c]
    C --> E[Spring k]
    D --> F[Summation Junction]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Output Displacement x of t]
    F --> H[Phase Lag phi]
    G --> I[Frequency Ratio r w by wn]
    I --> J{Damped System}
    J -->|r less than 1| K[Quasi Static Region]
    J -->|r approx 1| L[Resonance Peak Zone]
    J -->|r greater than sqrt 2| M[Isolation Region]
    L --> N[Max Amplitude Xmax]
    L --> O[Resonance Frequency wr]
```

### 4.2 Resonance Decision Flow in NVH Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Identify Excitation Frequency we] --> P1[Identify Natural Frequency wn]
    P1 --> P2{Is r approx 1}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Resonance Alert: Investigate]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Safe Operating Zone]
    P3 --> P5{Check Damping Ratio zeta}
    P5 -->|zeta less than 0.05| P6[Critical: Add Damping]
    P5 -->|0.05 to 0.3| P7[Moderate: Use Tuned Absorber]
    P5 -->|zeta greater than 0.7| P8[Overdamped: Response Suppressed]
    P6 --> P9[Re-tune Stiffness or Mass]
    P7 --> P10[Apply TMD or Re-locate Mount]
    P8 --> P11[System Passes NVH Check]
    P4 --> P11
    P9 --> P12[Verify with FRF Measurement]
    P10 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Document in NVH Report]
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristic Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0[Low Frequency Region] --> Q1[Quasi Static Gain unity]
    Q1 --> Q2[Steady Rise with Q Factor]
    Q2 --> Q3[Resonance Peak at wr]
    Q3 --> Q4[Roll Off Slope minus 40 dB per decade]
    Q4 --> Q5[High Frequency Isolation Zone]
    Q3 -.-> Q6[Bandwidth delta w equals 2 zeta wn]
    Q6 -.-> Q7[Quality Factor Q equals 1 over 2 zeta]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams:** Each block represents a distinct dynamic regime of a vibrating system. The dashed arrows represent **derived metrics** (bandwidth, quality factor) computed from the resonance peak parameters. This kind of block-level mapping is widely used in KTU NVH lab reports to communicate FRF test results to non-vibration specialists.

### 4.4 Mapping Resonance Phenomena to Automotive Subsystems

| Subsystem | Typical Excitation | Typical Natural Frequency | NVH Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine Mount | Firing frequency (2nd order) | 8 – 25 Hz | Idle shake, boom |
| Suspension Sprung Mass | Road input (1 – 15 Hz) | 1.0 – 2.0 Hz | Ride comfort |
| Body-in-White Panel | Acoustic, engine | 50 – 250 Hz | Buzz, squeak |
| Driveline | Engine torque pulses | 200 – 600 Hz | Booming noise |
| Tire/Wheel Assembly | Imbalance, road texture | 8 – 12 Hz | Shimmy, hop |
| Exhaust Hanger | Engine vibration | 15 – 30 Hz | Rattle, fatigue |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT863]**
Define the term **resonance** as applied to a forced vibrating system. State the condition for resonance in a damped SDOF system.

**Model Answer:**

> **Resonance** in a forced vibrating system is the condition in which the **frequency of the external excitation** approaches the **natural frequency** of the system, resulting in a **maximum amplitude of vibration** and most efficient energy transfer from the exciter to the system.
>
> **Mathematical Condition:**
> For a damped SDOF system, resonance occurs when the **frequency ratio** $r$ takes the value:
>
> $$r_{res} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$
>
> Hence, the **resonance frequency** is:
>
> $$\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$
>
> This is defined only for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$. For $\zeta = 0$ (undamped), $\omega_r = \omega_n$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between **natural frequency** and **resonance frequency** of a damped SDOF system. Why are they not equal?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$) | Resonance Frequency ($\omega_r$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Frequency at which an undamped system freely oscillates | Frequency at which forced amplitude is maximum |
| Formula | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ |
| Depends on Damping? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Observation | Fixed for a given $m, k$ | Decreases as $\zeta$ increases |

> They differ because the damping force opposes motion and **shifts the energy transfer optimum** away from $\omega_n$. As $\zeta$ increases, the peak broadens and shifts to a lower frequency, until at $\zeta = 1/\sqrt{2}$ the peak disappears entirely and the system behaves as overdamped.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PEMUT863, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state amplitude of a damped SDOF system subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Clearly state the equation of motion, the assumed form of solution, and the final expression for the dynamic magnification factor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the system above, with $m = 50$ kg, $k = 2 \times 10^5$ N/m, and $c = 1500$ N·s/m, find:
  (i) the undamped natural frequency $\omega_n$ and natural frequency $f_n$ in Hz.
  (ii) the damping ratio $\zeta$.
  (iii) the resonance frequency $\omega_r$.
  (iv) the maximum dynamic magnification factor $M_{max}$.
  (v) the half-power bandwidth $\Delta\omega$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion:**
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Normalized Form:**
$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Assume Steady-State Solution:**
$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Equate Coefficients:**

Following the same derivation as in **Section 3.1**, Steps 4–8, we obtain:

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF):**

$$\boxed{M = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Phase Angle:**
$$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $k = 2 \times 10^5$ N/m, $c = 1500$ N·s/m.

**(i) Undamped Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{63.25}{2\pi} = 10.07 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Damping Ratio:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{(2 \times 10^5)(50)} = 2\sqrt{10^7} = 6324.56 \text{ N·s/m}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1500}{6324.56} = 0.2372$$

**(iii) Resonance Frequency:**

Check condition: $2\zeta^2 = 2(0.2372)^2 = 0.1125 < 1$ — so resonance exists.

$$\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = 63.25 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.1125} = 63.25 \times 0.9421 = 59.59 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iv) Maximum Magnification Factor:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2(0.2372)\sqrt{1 - 0.0563}} = \frac{1}{0.4744 \times 0.9716} = \frac{1}{0.4610} = 2.169$$

**(v) Half-Power Bandwidth:**

$$\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n = 2(0.2372)(63.25) = 30.00 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{\Delta\omega}{2\pi} = 4.77 \text{ Hz}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PEMUT863, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is the **dynamic magnification factor (DMF)**? Sketch the variation of DMF with frequency ratio for different values of damping ratio. Explain the meaning of the **isolation region**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An engine is mounted on a rubber isolator modeled as a spring-damper with $k = 5 \times 10^5$ N/m, $c = 800$ N·s/m, and an equivalent engine mass of $m = 80$ kg. The engine operates at 3000 RPM with a dominant 2nd-order excitation. Calculate:
  (i) the forcing frequency $\omega$ in rad/s.
  (ii) the frequency ratio $r$.
  (iii) the transmissibility $T$.
  (iv) Comment on whether the mount is in the **isolation region** or in the **amplification region**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

> **Definition:** The **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)** is the ratio of the steady-state vibration amplitude of a forced system to the **static deflection** that the same force would cause if applied statically:
>
> $$M = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Variation with $r$ for different $\zeta$:**

| Frequency Ratio $r$ | Behavior |
|---|---|
| $r \ll 1$ | $M \approx 1$ (quasi-static) |
| $r = 1$ | Peak for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $M < 1$ (isolation region) |
| $r \gg 1$ | $M \to 0$ as $1/r^2$ |

> **Isolation Region:** For $r > \sqrt{2}$, the DMF is less than unity, meaning the system transmits **less** vibration than the input amplitude. This is the desired operating zone for vibration isolators. Crossover occurs at $r = \sqrt{2}$, independent of damping (in the absence of damping).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $k = 5 \times 10^5$ N/m, $c = 800$ N·s/m, $m = 80$ kg, $N = 3000$ RPM, 2nd-order excitation.

**(i) Forcing Frequency:**

$$N = 3000 \text{ RPM} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$$

For 2nd order: $f = 2 \times 50 = 100$ Hz

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(100) = 628.32 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Frequency Ratio:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^5}{80}} = \sqrt{6250} = 79.06 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{628.32}{79.06} = 7.95$$

**(iii) Transmissibility:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{800}{2\sqrt{(5 \times 10^5)(80)}} = \frac{800}{2 \times 6324.56} = 0.0632$$

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Compute step by step:

- $r^2 = (7.95)^2 = 63.20$
- $1 - r^2 = -62.20$, $(1 - r^2)^2 = 3869.0$
- $2\zeta r = 2(0.0632)(7.95) = 1.005$
- $(2\zeta r)^2 = 1.010$
- Numerator squared: $1 + 1.010 = 2.010$
- Denominator squared: $3869.0 + 1.010 = 3870.0$

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{2.010}{3870.0}} = \sqrt{5.195 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0228$$

**(iv) Comment:**

Since $r = 7.95 \gg \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the system is firmly in the **isolation region**. The transmissibility is only **2.28%**, meaning the engine mount is highly effective at suppressing vibration transmission to the chassis. **The mount design is acceptable from an NVH standpoint.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing up $f$ (Hz) and $\omega$ (rad/s). In KTU scripts, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is **mandatory** before plugging into a $\omega_n$-based formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2nd order multiplier:** Engine order ≠ 1. Always check if the problem says "2nd-order" or "4th-order". The general rule is $f_{excitation} = (\text{order}) \times \text{shaft frequency}$.
> 3. **Neglecting the isolation-region condition:** Many students compute $T$ correctly but fail to compare $r$ with $\sqrt{2}$. Always state the **regime** explicitly.
> 4. **Transmissibility vs DMF confusion:** $T$ and $M$ are **different** quantities. $T$ involves the **base-excitation** case; $M$ involves the **force-excitation** case. Do not interchange them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam:**

- **Resonance** = maximum amplitude response of a forced system; occurs when the excitation frequency matches the system's natural frequency (or a damped variant of it).
- **Undamped natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — independent of damping.
- **Damped resonance frequency:** $\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ — defined only for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF):** $M = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$
- **Maximum DMF:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$; for light damping, $M_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$.
- **Phase angle at resonance:** $\phi = \pi/2$ (undamped) or slightly less (damped).
- **Transmissibility $T$** < 1 (isolation) only when $r > \sqrt{2}$.
- **Quality factor** $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ — high $Q$ means sharp peak (low damping).
- **Half-power bandwidth:** $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$ — used to experimentally identify $\zeta$ from FRF data.
- **Static deflection:** $X_{static} = F_0/k$ — must compute this *first* before any amplitude calculation.
- **Critical damping:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ — the threshold between oscillatory and non-oscillatory response.
- **NVH Applications:** Engine mounts, body panels, driveshafts, exhaust hangers, seat suspensions — all designed with resonance in mind.
- **Rule of thumb:** For mounts and isolators, target $\omega_n \ll \omega_{excitation}$ so that $r \gg 1$ → strong isolation.
- **Equation-of-motion safety:** Always use $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ as your starting template — KTU examiners will look for this exact form for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmissivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transmissivity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Automotive Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH)**, **Transmissivity** (also referred to as **Transmissibility**, denoted by the symbol **T** or **TR**) is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the amplitude of the **transmitted force (or motion)** to the amplitude of the **exciting (input) force (or motion)** as measured at the receiving (output) side of a vibration isolation system. Mathematically, for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) base-excited system: $$T = \frac{X_t}{X_i}$$ Where: - **$X_t$** = Amplitude of the transmitted displacement/acceleration at the isolated mass - **$X_i$** = Amplitude of the input (excitation) displacement/acceleration from the source > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Transmissivity is a *frequency-domain* parameter, NOT a time-domain response. It is purely a function of the **frequency ratio ($r = \omega/\omega_n$)** and the **damping ratio ($\zeta$)**. It quantifies the effectiveness of an isolator in attenuating (or amplifying) vibration before it reaches the protected component (e.g., passenger cabin, electronic control unit). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Suspension & Building Mount Imagine you are sitting inside a car driving over a bumpy road: - The **road bumps** are the *input excitation* (source of vibration). - The **car suspension (springs + dampers)** is the *isolation system*. - You (the passenger) are the *isolated mass*. If the road is smooth (low frequency input), you barely feel anything — the transmissivity is small. If the car hits a series of bumps at exactly the **resonance frequency** of the suspension, the car violently bounces — the transmissivity becomes **greater than 1** (amplification!). If the bumps come very fast (high frequency), the suspension simply cannot respond, and the road feel is filtered out — the transmissivity drops below 1 (isolation region). This same principle applies to: - **Engine mounts** isolating powertrain shake from the chassis. - **Cabin mounts** isolating road noise from passengers. - **Anti-vibration pads** under HVAC compressors in EVs. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Transmissivity is the **engineering metric** that tells a NVH engineer whether a vibration isolator is doing its job — *damping out* (T < 1) or *amplifying* (T > 1) the unwanted motion. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Frequency Ratio | $r$ | Dimensionless | | Damping Ratio | $\zeta$ | Dimensionless (0 ≤ ζ ≤ 1) | | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n$ | **rad/s** | | Transmissivity | $T$ | Dimensionless | | Critical Damping | $c_c$ | **N·s/m** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Derivation Background — Base-Excited SDOF System Consider a SDOF mass-spring-damper system where the **base (foundation) is subjected to a harmonic excitation** $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$, while the absolute displacement of the mass is $x(t)$. The equation of motion is: $$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$ Defining the *relative displacement* $z = x - y$ (the transmitted motion as felt by the mass relative to the moving base), the equation reduces to: $$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$$ For steady-state harmonic response, $z(t) = Z\sin(\omega t - \phi)$, and the **transmitted displacement amplitude** is: $$Z = \frac{r^2 \cdot Y}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ The **input amplitude** is $Y$. Therefore, the **transmissivity** becomes: $$\boxed{T = \frac{Z}{Y} = \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$ ## 2.2 Three Critical Operating Zones ### Zone I — Low Frequency ($r \ll 1$, i.e., $\omega \ll \omega_n$) - $T \approx r^2 \to 0$ - The system behaves as a **rigid link**; nearly all motion is transmitted. - **No isolation occurs.** Damping has negligible effect. ### Zone II — Resonance ($r = \sqrt{2}$ approx., the critical isolation frequency) - At $r = 1$ (true resonance), $T = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$ — **massive amplification!** - This is the **most dangerous operating region** for automotive components. - **Damping is the only savior** here. ### Zone III — Isolation Region ($r > \sqrt{2}$) - $T < 1$ — vibration is attenuated. - Higher $r$ and lower $\zeta$ both *improve* isolation. - At very high frequencies ($r \to \infty$), $T \to 1$ (the system reaches a *mass-block* limit;
