## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SDOF Viscously Damped System — Free Body Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM[Mass m] -- Inertia Force m*x_ddot --> nodeS[Sum of Forces]
    nodeC[Damper c] -- Damping Force c*x_dot --> nodeS
    nodeK[Spring k] -- Spring Force k*x --> nodeS
    nodeF[Applied Force F of t] -- External Excitation --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeEOM[Equation of Motion m*x_ddot + c*x_dot + k*x = F of t]
    nodeEOM --> nodeResp[Displacement Response x of t]
    nodeResp -. Velocity x_dot .-> nodeC
    nodeResp -. Displacement x .-> nodeK
    nodeResp -. Acceleration x_ddot .-> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Energy Flow in a Viscously Damped SDOF System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[External Excitation F of t] --> node2[Input Energy E_in]
    node2 --> node3[Kinetic Energy of Mass 1/2 m v squared]
    node2 --> node4[Potential Energy in Spring 1/2 k x squared]
    node2 --> node5[Dissipated Energy in Damper pi c omega X squared]
    node3 --> node6[Heat Loss to Environment]
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node6
    node1 --> node7[Automotive Applications]
    node7 --> node7a[Suspension Dampers]
    node7 --> node7b[Engine Mounts]
    node7 --> node7c[Steering Column Dampers]
    node7 --> node7d[Hood and Trunk Dampers]
```

### 4.3 Damping Regime Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Given c m k] --> nodeCalc[Compute c_c = 2 times sqrt mk]
    nodeCalc --> nodeCompare{Compare c with c_c}
    nodeCompare -- c less than c_c --> nodeUnder[Underdamped zeta less than 1]
    nodeCompare -- c equals c_c --> nodeCrit[Critically Damped zeta equals 1]
    nodeCompare -- c greater than c_c --> nodeOver[Overdamped zeta greater than 1]
    nodeUnder --> nodeUResp[Oscillates with Decaying Amplitude]
    nodeCrit --> nodeCResp[Returns to Equilibrium Fastest No Oscillation]
    nodeOver --> nodeOResp[Returns Slowly Without Oscillation]
```

### 4.4 Viscous Damper Functional Block (Automotive Shock Absorber)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Piston Rod Input] --> nodeB[Upper Chamber Oil]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compression Valve Orifice]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Lower Chamber Oil]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Piston with Bleed Holes]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Rebound Valve Orifice]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Oil Reservoir]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Relative Velocity Across Piston]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Damping Force F = c times v]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Heat Dissipation to Environment]
    nodeA --> nodeK[Road Surface Excitation]
    nodeK --> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Insight:** The automotive shock absorber is essentially a **velocity-controlled hydraulic pump**. The damping coefficient $c$ is engineered by selecting:
> - Oil viscosity (typically 5–50 cSt)
> - Orifice diameter (typically 1–4 mm)
> - Number and arrangement of bleed holes
> - Valve spring preload for progressive damping

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT863]**

*Define viscous damping. State the mathematical expression for viscous damping force and explain its physical significance with an automotive example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Viscous damping is a form of energy dissipation in which the damping force is directly proportional to the relative velocity between two surfaces or across a fluid layer.

**Mathematical expression (1 mark):** The viscous damping force is given by:
$$F_d = c \cdot \dot{x}$$
where $c$ is the viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m) and $\dot{x}$ is the relative velocity (m/s). The negative sign indicates the force opposes motion.

**Automotive example (1 mark):** A **hydraulic shock absorber** in a car suspension is the prime example. As the piston moves through the oil, oil is forced through small orifices, generating a velocity-proportional resistive force that dissipates vibration energy as heat, improving ride comfort.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PEMUT863]**

*Define damping ratio. For an automotive suspension system, the values are $m = 300$ kg, $c = 1800$ N·s/m, and $k = 30000$ N/m. Compute the damping ratio and identify whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Damping ratio $\zeta$ is the ratio of the actual damping coefficient to the critical damping coefficient:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
- $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{300 \times 30000} = 2\sqrt{9{,}000{,}000} = 2 \times 3000 = 6000$ N·s/m
- $\zeta = \dfrac{1800}{6000} = 0.3$

Since $\zeta = 0.3 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**. (Note: A damping ratio of 0.3 is typical for a comfortable passenger car suspension.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system with viscous damping. Also derive the expression for the critical damping coefficient and the damping ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a SDOF system consisting of a mass $m$ supported by a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$ in parallel. Let $x(t)$ denote the displacement of the mass from the equilibrium position.

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram and Newton's Second Law (2 marks):**
Applying Newton's second law in the horizontal direction, the forces acting on the mass are:
- Spring restoring force: $F_s = -kx$
- Viscous damping force: $F_d = -c\dot{x}$
- Applied external force: $F(t)$

$$\sum F = m\ddot{x}$$

$$F(t) - c\dot{x} - kx = m\ddot{x}$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

This is the **equation of motion** of the viscously damped SDOF system.

**Step 3 — Characteristic Equation (2 marks):**
For free vibration ($F(t) = 0$), assume a solution $x(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

Solving:
$$s_{1,2} = \dfrac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

**Step 4 — Critical Damping Coefficient (1 mark):**
The system is critically damped when the discriminant is zero: $c^2 - 4mk = 0$.

$$\boxed{c_c = 2\sqrt{mk}}$$

Alternatively, using $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$:
$$c_c = 2m\omega_n$$

**Step 5 — Damping Ratio (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}}$$

Substituting back, the canonical form of the EOM is:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

---

**(b)** A vehicle suspension is modeled as a SDOF system with $m = 250$ kg, $c = 1000$ N·s/m, and $k = 25000$ N/m. The system is subjected to a base excitation of amplitude $0.05$ m at $5$ Hz. Calculate:
- (i) The natural frequency and damping ratio
- (ii) The frequency ratio
- (iii) The magnification factor and the steady-state amplitude of the mass **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Natural frequency and damping ratio (3 marks):**
- Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{25000}{250}} = \sqrt{100} = 10$ rad/s
- Natural frequency in Hz: $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{10}{2\pi} = 1.591$ Hz
- Critical damping: $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{250 \times 25000} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000$ N·s/m
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{1000}{5000} = 0.2$

**(ii) Frequency ratio (2 marks):**
- Excitation frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 5 = 10\pi \approx 31.416$ rad/s
- Frequency ratio: $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{31.416}{10} = 3.1416 \approx \pi$

**(iii) Magnification factor and steady-state amplitude (2 marks):**
- Magnification factor:
$$MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$
$$MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 9.87)^2 + (2 \times 0.2 \times 3.1416)^2}}$$
$$MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(-8.87)^2 + (1.257)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{78.68 + 1.58}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{80.26}} \approx \dfrac{1}{8.96} \approx 0.1117$$

- Static deflection: $X_{static} = 0.05$ m (input amplitude)
- Steady-state amplitude: $X = MF \times X_{static} = 0.1117 \times 0.05 \approx 0.00558$ m $\approx 5.58$ mm

The system is operating in the isolation region ($r > \sqrt{2}$), meaning the suspension effectively isolates the cabin from the road excitation — a desirable characteristic for ride comfort.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2025 Expected]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the logarithmic decrement $\delta$ for a viscously damped SDOF system. Show that for lightly damped systems, $\zeta \approx \delta/(2\pi)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Free Response (2 marks):**
For underdamped free vibration, the displacement response is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the damped natural frequency.

**Step 2 — Successive Peaks (2 marks):**
The peak amplitudes occur at times $t_n$ and $t_{n+1} = t_n + T_d$, where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ is the damped period. At the peaks, $\sin(\omega_d t + \phi) = 1$:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}$$

$$x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_n + T_d)}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio and Logarithm (2 marks):**
Forming the ratio:

$$\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$$

Substituting $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Light Damping Approximation (1 mark):**
For lightly damped systems ($\zeta \ll 1$), $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$, hence:

$$\boxed{\zeta \approx \dfrac{\delta}{2\pi}}$$

**Engineering Application:** This formula is the **standard experimental tool** in automotive NVH modal testing — engineers measure successive peak amplitudes from a bump-test decay curve and compute $\zeta$ directly, which is then used to update the simulation model (CAE correlation).

---

**(b)** An automotive shock absorber is modeled as a viscous damper with $c = 2500$ N·s/m. During a road test, the relative velocity across the damper follows $v(t) = 0.8 \sin(20\pi t)$ m/s. Calculate:
- (i) The peak damping force
- (ii) The energy dissipated per cycle
- (iii) The total energy dissipated over 500 cycles
- (iv) The power dissipation (average) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- Damping coefficient: $c = 2500$ N·s/m
- Velocity amplitude: $V = 0.8$ m/s
- Angular frequency: $\omega = 20\pi \approx 62.83$ rad/s

**(i) Peak Damping Force (2 marks):**

$$F_{d,\text{peak}} = c \cdot V = 2500 \times 0.8 = 2000 \text{ N} = 2 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Energy Dissipated Per Cycle (2 marks):**

Using the derived formula:

$$E_d = \pi c \omega V^2 = \pi \times 2500 \times (20\pi) \times (0.8)^2$$

$$E_d = \pi \times 2500 \times 62.83 \times 0.64$$

$$E_d = \pi \times 100{,}528 = 315{,}830 \text{ J} \approx 315.83 \text{ kJ}$$

> ⚠️ **Note for students:** Recompute carefully using $\omega = 20\pi$ exactly: $E_d = \pi \times 2500 \times 20\pi \times 0.64 = 16000\pi^2 \approx 157{,}913$ J $\approx 157.91$ kJ. (Examiners prefer exact $\pi$-form answers.)

**Exact expression:**
$$\boxed{E_d = 16{,}000\,\pi^2 \approx 157.91 \text{ kJ per cycle}}$$

**(iii) Total Energy Over 500 Cycles (1 mark):**

$$E_{total} = N \times E_d = 500 \times 157.91 \text{ kJ} = 78{,}955 \text{ kJ} \approx 78.96 \text{ MJ}$$

**(iv) Average Power Dissipation (1 mark):**
Period: $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = \dfrac{2\pi}{20\pi} = 0.1$ s

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_d}{T} = \dfrac{157{,}913}{0.1} = 1{,}579{,}130 \text{ W} \approx 1.58 \text{ MW}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Viscous Damping Problems:**
> 
> **1. Sign Error in Damping Force (–1 mark typical):** Students often write $F_d = c\dot{x}$ without the negative sign. The damping force *always opposes motion*; the negative sign is essential and frequently tested.
> 
> **2. Confusing $c_c$ with $c$ (–2 marks):** Many students compute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-c^2}$ instead of $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. The damping ratio $\zeta$ enters, not the raw coefficient $c$.
> 
> **3. Forgetting Units of $c$ (–1 mark):** The unit of viscous damping coefficient is **N·s/m** (or equivalently **kg/s**). Never write it as N/m or N/s.
> 
> **4. Wrong Application of Logarithmic Decrement Formula (–2 marks):** For multiple cycles, the correct formula is $\delta = \frac{1}{N}\ln(x_0/x_N)$, NOT $\delta = \ln(x_0/x_N)$. This is a *very* common trap.
> 
> **5. Treating $\zeta > 1$ as Oscillatory (–2 marks):** If a problem yields $\zeta > 1$, the system does NOT oscillate. Writing $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ for an overdamped system is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 
> **6. Missing the Isolation Region Criterion (–1 mark):** When $r > \sqrt{2}$, the magnification factor is less than 1, meaning the system acts as a vibration isolator. Always comment on this in base excitation problems.
> 
> **7. Calculation Slips in $E_d = \pi c \omega X^2$ (–1 mark):** Students often write $E_d = c\omega X^2$ (missing the $\pi$) or use $f$ (Hz) instead of $\omega$ (rad/s). Remember: $\omega = 2\pi f$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Viscous Damping (Module 3)**

**Core Equations to Memorize:**

- ✔️ **Viscous damping force:** $F_d = c\dot{x}$
- ✔️ **Equation of motion:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$
- ✔️ **Canonical form:** $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$
- ✔️ **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
- ✔️ **Critical damping:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$
- ✔️ **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_c = c/(2\sqrt{mk})$
- ✔️ **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- ✔️ **Underdamped response:** $x(t) = Xe^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$
- ✔️ **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \approx \dfrac{\delta}{2\pi}$ for light damping
- ✔️ **Multi-cycle formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{1}{N}\ln(x_0/x_N)$
- ✔️ **Energy per cycle:** $E_d = \pi c\omega X^2$
- ✔️ **Magnification factor:** $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$

**Key Conceptual Takeaways:**

- 🎯 Viscous damping is **linear and velocity-proportional** — the defining signature.
- 🎯 Energy dissipation is **irreversible** (converted to heat in fluid).
- 🎯 Passenger cars tune $\zeta$ between **0.2–0.4**; performance cars between **0.5–0.7**.
- 🎯 **Critical damping** ($\zeta = 1$) yields the **fastest non-oscillatory** return.
- 🎯 At $r > \sqrt{2}$, the system acts as a **vibration isolator**.
- 🎯 Logarithmic decrement is the **experimental tool** to extract $\zeta$ from decay curves.
- 🎯 Energy dissipated grows **linearly with frequency** but **quadratically with amplitude**.
- 🎯 Automotive applications: shock absorbers, hydraulic mounts, steering dampers, hood struts.

**Common Units Mastery:**

| Quantity | SI Unit |
|:---|:---:|
| Damping coefficient $c$ | N·s/m or kg/s |
| Damping ratio $\zeta$ | dimensionless |
| Natural frequency $\omega_n$ | rad/s |
| Frequency $f$ | Hz |
| Energy dissipated $E_d$ | J |
| Average power $P$ | W |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sandwich Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sandwich Construction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Sandwich Construction** is a structural composite configuration widely employed in automotive body-in-white (BIW) and chassis applications, consisting of two thin, high-modulus, dense **face sheets** (skins) adhesively or metallurgically bonded to a thick, low-density, shear-flexible **core material**. The arrangement forms a lightweight structural panel whose flexural rigidity is derived primarily from the geometric separation of the face sheets by the core, analogous to the upper and lower flanges of an I-beam. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT863 / Module 3):** Sandwich construction is treated as a primary *passive NVH control strategy* — it simultaneously delivers **mass-efficient stiffness** (vibration isolation) and **damped acoustic transmission loss** (noise attenuation) in automotive panels such as dash insulators, floor pans, roof modules, and hood laminates. ### 1.2 Constituent Materials — The Three-Layer Architecture A sandwich laminate is geometrically and materially a **three-layer system**: | Layer | Location | Function | Typical Automotive Materials | |---|---|---|---| | **Face Sheet 1** (Outer Skin) | Top/Outer side | Carries bending tensile/compressive stress; provides surface finish & impact resistance | **0.6 – 1.2 mm** Steel, Aluminium 5xxx/6xxx series, CFRP prepreg, GFRP | | **Core** | Middle (bulk) | Transfers shear between faces; provides thickness; contributes damping | **Polymer foam (PP, PU, PVC), honeycomb (Nomex/Al), balsa, corrugated steel** | | **Face Sheet 2** (Inner Skin) | Bottom/Inner side | Carries opposite bending stress; often the "constrained layer" for damping | Same as Face 1 — typically **0.5 – 0.8 mm** mild steel or Al | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "I-Beam on a Diet" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box:** Imagine holding a steel ruler flat between your palms and trying to bend it — it flexes easily. Now imagine the same ruler with a thick, light **balsa-wood block** glued to its underside. The ruler becomes *dramatically* stiffer in bending without adding much mass. This is the essence of a sandwich: by **spreading the two stiff skins apart** through a low-density spacer, the second moment of area ($I$) explodes (it scales as the *square* of the distance between skins), giving massive bending stiffness for negligible weight penalty. The core merely "holds the skins apart" while transmitting shear — like the web of an I-beam. ### 1.4 Why Sandwich Construction Matters in Automotive NVH - **Vibration isolation** at body-panel resonant frequencies (typically 60–500 Hz for trim panels). - **Acoustic transmission loss (TL)** in the coincidence and mass-law regions (500 Hz – 5 kHz). - **Mass de-coupling** — the structure is stiff but light, shifting resonant frequencies higher and out of critical excitation bands (engine 2nd, 3rd orders). - **Damping** — viscoelastic foam cores and adhesive interlayers convert vibrational energy into heat (loss factor $\eta \approx 0.05$–$0.30$ for constrained-layer sandwiches). ### 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted) - **Flexural Rigidity ($D$):** Units = **N·m²** — resistance to bending deformation. - **Shear Rigidity ($U$ or $S$):** Units = **N** — resistance to transverse shear deformation. - **Mass per unit area ($m''$):** Units = **kg/m²** — areal density governing TL. - **Loss factor ($\eta$):** Dimensionless — internal damping efficiency; **$\eta > 0.1$** is considered "highly damped." - **Critical frequency ($f_c$):** Units = **Hz** — onset of coincidence with sound waves. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanics of Load Transfer 1. **External load** (mechanical vibration, acoustic pressure, road-induced force) is applied to the outer face sheet. 2. The **outer face** develops **in-plane normal stresses** ($\sigma_f$) — tension on one side, compression on the other — because it is offset from the neutral axis by distance $d/2$. 3. These tensile/compressive forces are **transmitted as shear** ($\tau_c$) through the **core**, which acts as the "web" of the equivalent I-beam. 4. The **inner face** receives the opposite-sign in-plane stress, completing the force couple that resists the applied bending moment. 5. **Shear deformation of the core** contributes a *non-negligible* portion of total deflection — this is why sandwich deflection must consider **both bending AND shear** (Timoshenko beam theory, not Euler–Bernoulli). 6. **Damping** arises from viscoelastic shear strain in the core and the constrained-layer damping (CLD) action of the adhesive-skin interface. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Question:** Why use a sandwich instead of a single thick steel panel of equivalent mass? Because **bending stiffness scales as $t^3$** (cubic with thickness) for a monolithic plate but scales as $d^2$ (quadratic with skin separation) for a sandwich — and $d$ can be made very large with negligible mass penalty. A 1 mm steel + 20 mm foam + 1 mm steel sandwich has the **same mass** as a 2.2 mm solid steel sheet but **~21× higher** bending stiffness. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total panel thickness | $h = 2 t_f + t_c$ | m | $t_f$ = face thickness, $t_c$ = core thickness | | 2 | Distance between face centroids | $d = t_c + t_f$ | m | Critical spacing parameter | | 3 | Bending rigidity (sandwich) | $D \approx \dfrac{E_f \, b \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}$ | N·m² | Neglects own face-sheet $I$ (valid when $d \gg t_f$) | | 4 | Full bending rigidity (exact) | $D = \dfrac{E_f b t_f d^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{E_f b t_f^{3}}{6} + \dfrac{E_c b t_c^{3}}{12}$ | N·m² | Includes face and core contributions | | 5 | Shear rigidity | $U = \dfrac{G_c \, b \, d^{2}}{t_c}$ | N | $G_c$ = core shear modulus | | 6 | Bending stress in face | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M}{b \, t_f \, d}$ | Pa | $M$ = applied bending moment | | 7 | Core shear stress | $\tau_c = \dfrac{V}{b \, d}$ | Pa | $V$ = transverse shear force | | 8 | Total mid-span deflection (3-pt bend) | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{48 D} + \dfrac{P L}{4 U}$ | m | **Bending** + **shear** terms | | 9 | Critical (coincidence) frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{c^{2}}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{m''}{D}}$ | Hz | $c$ = speed of sound in air $\approx 343$ m/s | | 10 | Areal density | $m'' = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c$ | kg/m² | Governs mass-law TL | | 11 | Loss factor (constrained layer) | $\eta_s = \eta_f \cdot \dfrac{3 E_f t_f}{E_c t_c}$ (approx.) | — | Simplified CLD estimate | | 12 | Sound TL (mass law, sub-$f_c$) | $TL = 20 \log_{10}(m'' f) - 47$ | dB | $f$ in Hz, $m''$ in kg/m² | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Equation (8) is the **design cornerstone** of automotive sandwich panels — it explicitly shows that *deflection has two components* and that designers can tune $D$ (by face choice) and $U$ (by core choice) *independently*. This decoupling enables a "stiff but damped" panel — impossible with monolithic metal. ### 2.3 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Sandwiches - **Symmetric (balanced):** $t_{f1} = t_{f2}$, same material → no thermal warping, no bending–extension coupling. Preferred for hoods, doors, roof panels. - **Asymmetric (unbalanced):** $t_{f1} \neq t_{f2}$ or different materials → exhibits **bending–stretching coupling** ($B_{11} \neq 0$). Used deliberately in dash panels where one skin must be acoustically absorptive (porous) and the other reflective (solid foil). ### 2.4 Real-World Automotive Applications | Component | Sandwich Type | NVH Function | |---|---|---| | **Dash Insulator** | Heavy-layer (EPDM/sound-deadening steel) + foam + foil | Tuned mass absorber at ~200–400 Hz; broadband TL | | **Floor Pan** | Steel + PU foam + steel | Road noise attenuation; structural floor stiffness | | **Roof Module** | Al outer + honeycomb + Al inner | Weight reduction + rain impact damping | | **Hood / Bonnet** | Al + viscoelastic adhesive + Al | Engine NVH radiation reduction; pedestrian safety | | **Trunk Floor** | SMC composite + foam + SMC | Bass absorption; panel ringing suppression | | **Wheel House Liner** | PP honeycomb + felt + foil | Tyre cavity mode absorption | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Bending Rigidity $D$ for a Symmetric Sandwich **Starting Point:** The flexural rigidity of any laminated cross-section is defined by: $$D = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} E(z) \, z^{2} \, b \, dz$$ **Step 1 — Define the coordinate system.** Place the origin at the geometric mid-plane of the sandwich. The faces occupy the regions: $$-\frac{h}{2} \le z \le -\frac{h}{2}+t_f \quad \text{(bottom face, } -d/2 \le z \le -d/2 + t_f\text{)}$$ $$+\frac{h}{2}-t_f \le z \le +\frac{h}{2} \quad \text{(top face, } +d/2 - t_f \le z \le +d/2\text{)}$$ The core occupies $-d/2 + t_f \le z \le +d/2 - t_f$, where $d = t_c + t_f$. **Step 2 — Apply the parallel-axis theorem (Steiner's theorem).** For a thin face sheet whose centroid is at distance $d/2$ from the neutral axis, its own second moment of area is small compared with the parallel-axis term. Each face contributes: $$D_{\text{face}} = E_f \left[ \frac{b t_f^{3}}{12} + b t_f \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} \right]$$ **Step 3 — Sum both faces (two faces of identical material/thickness):** $$D_{\text{faces,total}} = 2 E_f \left[ \frac{b t_f^{3}}{12} + b t_f \frac{d^{2}}{4} \right]$$ **Step 4 — Add the core's own (small) contribution:** $$D_{\text{core}} = \frac{E_c \, b \, t_c^{3}}{12}$$ **Step 5 — Total flexural rigidity:** $$D = \frac{E_f b t_f d^{2}}{2} + \frac{E_f b t_f^{3}}{6} + \frac{E_c b t_c^{3}}{12}$$ > **Logic of conversion:** The first term dominates when $d \gg t_f$ (typical sandwich condition $d/t_f \ge 10$). The second term is the *own* moment of inertia of each face, the third is the core's. For typical automotive panels ($d/t_f \approx 20$), the **first term contributes >95 %** of $D$. **Step 6 — Simplified form (thin-face approximation):** $$D \approx \frac{E_f b t_f d^{2}}{2}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of Shear Rigidity $U$ The transverse shear force $V$ in a sandwich beam is carried *almost entirely* by the core, because the core is in shear between the two face sheets. For a uniform rectangular core section of area $b \times t_c$: $$U = \frac{G_c \, A_c}{t_c / d} \quad \text{(effective shear stiffness for sandwich)}$$ The factor $d/t_c$ arises because the shear strain in the core equals the *relative* sliding of the two faces normalised by their separation $d$. Therefore: $$U = \frac{G_c \, b \, t_c}{t_c / d} = \frac{G_c \, b \, d^{2}}{t_c}$$ > **Logic of conversion:** Shear strain $\gamma$ in the core is the *change in distance between faces* divided by the face separation $d$. The shear force–strain relationship $\tau = G_c \gamma$ multiplied by core area $b t_c$ gives the effective shear stiffness when expressed in terms of face-sliding displacement. ### 3.3 Derivation of Mid-Span Deflection Under 3-Point Bending For a simply-supported beam of span $L$ under central load $P$, the Euler–Bernoulli (bending) deflection is: $$\delta_{\text{bend}} = \frac{P L^{3}}{48 D}$$ The **shear-induced deflection** (Timoshenko contribution) under the same loading is: $$\delta_{\text{shear}} = \frac{P L}{4 U}$$ **Total deflection (linear superposition, valid for small deformations):** $$\delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{48 D} + \frac{P L}{4 U}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** For *thick* automotive sandwiches (e.g., dash insulators, $h \approx 20$ mm, $L \approx 600$ mm), the two terms are of **comparable magnitude** — neglecting shear is a common student error that underestimates compliance by up to 50 %. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A floor-pan sandwich panel has the following properties: - Face sheets: $t_f = 0.8$ mm, $E_f = 210$ GPa (steel), $\rho_f = 7850$ kg/m³ - Core: $t_c = 18$ mm, $G_c = 12$ MPa (PU foam), $\rho_c = 50$ kg/m³ - Width: $b = 1$ m, Span: $L = 1.2$ m - Central load: $P = 100$ N **Step A — Geometric parameters:** $$d = t_c + t_f = 0.018 + 0.0008 = 0.0188 \text{ m}$$ $$h = 2 t_f + t_c = 0.0016 + 0.018 = 0.0196 \text{ m}$$ **Step B — Flexural rigidity (thin-face approximation):** $$D = \frac{E_f \, b \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2} = \frac{(210 \times 10^{9})(1)(0.0008)(0.0188)^{2}}{2}$$ **Step C — Compute:** $$D = \frac{210 \times 10^{9} \times 0.0008 \times 3.5344 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = \frac{5.938 \times 10^{4}}{2}$$ $$D = 2.969 \times 10^{4} \text{ N·m}^{2}$$ **Step D — Shear rigidity:** $$U = \frac{G_c \, b \, d^{2}}{t_c} = \frac{(12 \times 10^{6})(1)(0.0188)^{2}}{0.018}$$ $$U = \frac{12 \times 10^{6} \times 3.5344 \times 10^{-4}}{0.018} = \frac{4241.3}{0.018}$$ $$U = 2.356 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$ **Step E — Bending deflection:** $$\delta_{\text{bend}} = \frac{P L^{3}}{48 D} = \frac{(100)(1.2)^{3}}{48 \times 2.969 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{172.8}{1.425 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\delta_{\text{bend}} = 1.213 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1213 \text{ mm}$$ **Step F — Shear deflection:** $$\delta_{\text{shear}} = \frac{P L}{4 U} = \frac{100 \times 1.2}{4 \times 2.356 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{120}{9.424 \times 10^{5}}$$ $$\delta_{\text{shear}} = 1.273 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1273 \text{ mm}$$ **Step G — Total deflection:** $$\delta_{\text{total}} = 0.1213 + 0.1273 = 0.2486 \text{ mm}$$ > **Result interpretation:** The **shear contribution is 51.2 %** of the total — confirming the warning that shear deformation is non-negligible in automotive sandwich panels. A naive Euler–Bernoulli analysis would under-predict deflection by 2×. **Step H — Areal density (for TL estimate):** $$m'' = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c = 2(7850)(0.0008) + (50)(0.018) = 12.56 + 0.90 = 13.46 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sandwich Panel for NVH ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["EXTERNAL EXCITATION
Engine Order Vibration
Road Induced Noise
Acoustic Pressure"] --> nodeB["OUTER FACE SHEET
High E modulus skin
Carries bending stress sigma_f
0.6 to 1.2 mm Steel or Al"] nodeB --> nodeC["ADHESIVE INTERLAYER
Viscoelastic bond
Provides Constrained Layer Damping
eta approx 0.1 to 0.3"] nodeC --> nodeD["CORE MATERIAL
PU foam or PP honeycomb
Transfers shear tau_c
Low density 30 to 100 kg per cubic metre"] nodeD --> nodeE["ADHESIVE INTERLAYER
Inner constrained layer
Shear strain energy dissipation"] nodeE --> nodeF["INNER FACE SHEET
Opposite sign bending stress
Reflects acoustic energy
0.5 to 0.8 mm Steel or Al"] nodeF --> nodeG["INTERIOR CABIN AIR SPACE
Receives damped attenuated signal
TL target greater than 30 dB"] nodeD -.-> nodeH["SHEAR DEFORMATION PATH
Major contributor to deflection
50 percent of total delta"] nodeB -.-> nodeI["BENDING DEFORMATION PATH
Face sheet carries M via d squared lever arm"] nodeG --> nodeJ["NVH PERFORMANCE OUTPUT
Panel natural frequency shifted up
Radiation efficiency reduced
Driver perceived NVH improved"] ``` ### 4.2 Stress Flow Diagram — Force Couple in a Sandwich Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Applied Bending Moment M"] --> nodeB["Outer face in TENSION
sigma_f = M divided by b t_f d"] nodeA --> nodeC["Inner face in COMPRESSION
sigma_f opposite sign"] nodeB --> nodeD["Core transmits SHEAR tau_c
tau_c = V divided by b d"] nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE["Resisting Force Couple
F x d approx M
High stiffness from large d"] nodeE --> nodeF["Equivalent I beam analogy
Flanges = faces
Web = core"] ``` ### 4.3 Material Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Target NVH Frequency Band"] --> nodeB{"Below 200 Hz
Structure borne"} nodeA --> nodeC{"200 to 1000 Hz
Mid band"} nodeA --> nodeD{"Above 1000 Hz
Air borne noise"} nodeB --> nodeE["Use HIGH DENSITY CORE
Constrained Layer Damping
EPDM heavy layer"] nodeC --> nodeF["Use HONEYCOMB CORE
High stiffness to weight ratio
Nomex or Al honeycomb"] nodeD --> nodeG["Use POROUS FOAM CORE
Acoustic absorption
Open cell PU or melamine"] nodeE --> nodeH["Recommended for DASH and FLOOR"] nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH ``` ### 4.4 Sandwich Panel Cross-Section (Schematic) ``` ┌─────────────────────────────┐ ← Outer face (t_f ≈ 0.8 mm, steel) │ σ = +M/(b·t_f·d) │ Tensile stress ├═════════════════════════════┤ ← Adhesive (viscoelastic, ~0.1 mm) │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│ │▒▒▒▒ CORE (PU foam) ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│ ← Core (t_c ≈ 18 mm, τ_c shear) │▒▒▒▒▒ G_c ≈ 10–20 MPa ▒▒▒▒▒│ │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│ ├═════════════════════════════┤ ← Adhesive │ σ = –M/(b·t_f·d) │ Compressive stress └─────────────────────────────┘ ← Inner face (t_f ≈ 0.8 mm) |←———————— d = t_c + t_f ——————→| |←————— h = 2·t_f + t_c ——————→| ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define sandwich construction in the context of automotive NVH. List the three primary functions of the core material.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Sandwich construction is a composite panel configuration consisting of two thin, high-stiffness face sheets bonded to a thick, low-density core, used in automotive body panels to achieve high bending stiffness and acoustic damping at low mass. The three primary functions of the core are: 1. **Geometric separation** of the face sheets to maximise the second moment of area (flexural rigidity $D \propto d^2$). 2. **Shear transfer** between the two face sheets, carrying the transverse shear force $V$ as core shear stress $\tau_c$. 3. **Damping and acoustic absorption** — viscoelastic foam cores dissipate vibrational energy through shear strain, increasing the loss factor $\eta$. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for three core functions = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State any three advantages of using sandwich panels over monolithic steel panels of equivalent mass in automotive applications.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Higher bending stiffness-to-weight ratio** — a sandwich achieves ~10–20× the flexural rigidity $D$ of a solid sheet of equal mass because $D$ scales as $d^2$ (skin separation) rather than $t^3$ (monolithic thickness). 2. **Built-in damping** — the viscoelastic core and adhesive interlayer provide constrained-layer damping ($\eta \approx 0.1$–$0.3$) that monolithic steel cannot offer ($\eta \approx 0.001$). 3. **Higher coincidence frequency** — the lighter areal density shifts the critical frequency $f_c$ upward, moving the coincidence dip out of sensitive interior acoustic bands. 4. *(Bonus, if asked)* Multi-functional integration — acoustic absorption, thermal insulation, and structural load-bearing in a single component. *(1 mark per advantage × 3 = 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PEMUT863]** **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction of a typical automotive sandwich panel. Discuss the roles of the face sheets, adhesive interlayer, and core. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A sandwich floor-pan has the following properties: - Face sheets: steel, $t_f = 0.7$ mm, $E_f = 210$ GPa, width $b = 1$ m - Core: PU foam, $t_c = 15$ mm, $G_c = 10$ MPa - Span $L = 1.0$ m, central load $P = 200$ N - Compute: (i) flexural rigidity $D$, (ii) shear rigidity $U$, (iii) total mid-span deflection. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction & roles — 7 Marks:** - **Sketch (2 marks):** Three-layer cross-section showing outer face, adhesive, core, adhesive, inner face with thickness labels and stress distribution. - **Outer & Inner Face Sheets (2 marks):** Made of high-modulus material (steel, Al, or composite). Carry in-plane bending stresses $\sigma_f = M/(b t_f d)$ on opposite sides. Provide surface finish and impact resistance. - **Adhesive Interlayer (1.5 marks):** Viscoelastic polymer (epoxy, acrylic, rubber-based). Provides constrained-layer damping — shear deformation dissipates vibrational energy. - **Core (1.5 marks):** Low-density material (PU/PP foam, Nomex honeycomb, balsa). Transfers shear between skins; provides thickness; contributes acoustic absorption. **(b) Numerical solution — 7 Marks:** *Step 1: Geometric parameters (1 mark)* $$d = t_c + t_f = 0.015 + 0.0007 = 0.0157 \text{ m}$$ *Step 2: Flexural rigidity (2 marks)* $$D = \frac{E_f b t_f d^{2}}{2} = \frac{(210 \times 10^{9})(1)(0.0007)(0.0157)^{2}}{2}$$ $$D = \frac{210 \times 10^{9} \times 0.0007 \times 2.4649 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = \frac{3.623 \times 10^{4}}{2} = 1.811 \times 10^{4} \text{ N·m}^{2}$$ *Step 3: Shear rigidity (1 mark)* $$U = \frac{G_c b d^{2}}{t_c} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{6})(1)(0.0157)^{2}}{0.015} = \frac{10^{7} \times 2.4649 \times 10^{-4}}{0.015} = 1.643 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$ *Step 4: Bending deflection (1.5 marks)* $$\delta_{\text{bend}} = \frac{P L^{3}}{48 D} = \frac{200 \times 1.0^{3}}{48 \times 1.811 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{200}{8.694 \times 10^{5}} = 2.301 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.230 \text{ mm}$$ *Step 5: Shear deflection (1 mark)* $$\delta_{\text{shear}} = \frac{P L}{4 U} = \frac{200 \times 1.0}{4 \times 1.643 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{200}{6.572 \times 10^{5}} = 3.043 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.304 \text{ mm}$$ *Step 6: Total deflection (0.5 marks)* $$\delta_{\text{total}} = 0.230 + 0.304 = 0.534 \text{ mm}$$ > **Result:** Shear contribution = 57 % of total — confirms Timoshenko beam analysis is essential. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT863]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the flexural rigidity $D$ of a symmetric sandwich beam, clearly stating all assumptions. Show that for typical automotive panels, the dominant term is $E_f b t_f d^2 / 2$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A car roof sandwich panel is made of two aluminium face sheets ($E_f = 70$ GPa, $t_f = 0.6$ mm) and a PP honeycomb core ($t_c = 25$ mm, $G_c = 30$ MPa). The panel width is $b = 1.2$ m. If a uniform distributed load of $q = 50$ N/m acts on a simply-supported span of $L = 1.5$ m, calculate the maximum deflection considering both bending and shear effects. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation — 7 Marks:** - **Assumption statement (1 mark):** Thin face sheets ($t_f \ll d$), symmetric geometry, linear-elastic materials, plane sections remain plane (Timoshenko), adhesive interlayer neglected in stiffness. - **Define D from first principles (1.5 marks):** $D = \int E(z) z^2 b \, dz$ over the cross-section. - **Apply to faces using parallel-axis theorem (2 marks):** Each face at distance $d/2$ from neutral axis contributes $E_f[b t_f^3/12 + b t_f (d/2)^2]$. - **Sum both faces + add core contribution (1.5 marks):** Obtain $D = E_f b t_f d^2/2 + E_f b t_f^3/6 + E_c b t_c^3/12$. - **Justify thin-face dominance (1 mark):** Show that when $d/t_f \ge 10$, the first term exceeds 95 % of the total — therefore $D \approx E_f b t_f d^2/2$. **(b) Numerical solution — 7 Marks:** *Step 1: Geometry (1 mark)* $$d = t_c + t_f = 0.025 + 0.0006 = 0.0256 \text{ m}$$ *Step 2: Flexural rigidity (1.5 marks)* $$D = \frac{E_f b t_f d^{2}}{2} = \frac{(70 \times 10^{9})(1.2)(0.0006)(0.0256)^{2}}{2}$$ $$D = \frac{70 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2 \times 0.0006 \times 6.5536 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = \frac{3.303 \times 10^{4}}{2} = 1.651 \times 10^{4} \text{ N·m}^{2}$$ *Step 3: Shear rigidity (1 mark)* $$U = \frac{G_c b d^{2}}{t_c} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{6})(1.2)(0.0256)^{2}}{0.025} = \frac{3 \times 10^{7} \times 1.2 \times 6.5536 \times 10^{-4}}{0.025} = 9.437 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$ *Step 4: Max bending deflection (UDL) (1.5 marks)* $$\delta_{\text{bend}} = \frac{5 q L^{4}}{384 D} = \frac{5 \times 50 \times 1.5^{4}}{384 \times 1.651 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{5 \times 50 \times 5.0625}{6.34 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\delta_{\text{bend}} = \frac{1265.6}{6.34 \times 10^{6}} = 1.996 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.200 \text{ mm}$$ *Step 5: Max shear deflection (UDL) (1.5 marks)* $$\delta_{\text{shear}} = \frac{q L^{2}}{8 U} = \frac{50 \times 1.5^{2}}{8 \times 9.437 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{112.5}{7.55 \times 10^{6}} = 1.490 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.0149 \text{ mm}$$ *Step 6: Total deflection (0.5 marks)* $$\delta_{\text{total}} = 0.200 + 0.0149 \approx 0.215 \text{ mm}$$ > **Result:** For the roof panel (thinner faces, stiffer core), shear contributes only ~7 % — bending dominates. **This contrasts with the floor-pan example** where the soft PU core made shear dominant. Engineers must check both terms for *every* application. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the shear term** in deflection — costs up to 4 marks when shear is the dominant contribution (soft foam cores). > 2. **Using $d$ instead of $h/2$** in the bending-stress formula $\sigma_f$ — students often write $\sigma_f = M y / I$ with $y = h/2$, which is incorrect for sandwiches; the correct lever arm is $d/2$ to the face centroid. > 3. **Confusing $E_c$ with $G_c$** — the core is specified by **shear modulus** $G_c$ for the *shear rigidity* $U$ and by **Young's modulus** $E_c$ for the (small) bending contribution. Mixing these loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for two faces in the $D$ expression. > 5. **Units mismatch** — using $E$ in MPa with $d$ in mm without consistency; KTU examiners expect SI units throughout. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Sandwich panel = 2 thin stiff skins + 1 thick soft core.** Bending stiffness scales as $d^2$ (skin separation), not as $t^3$ (monolithic thickness). - **Two independent rigidity terms:** $D$ (bending, face-controlled) and $U$ (shear, core-controlled). Tune separately. - **Total deflection = bending + shear.** Both terms are required for accurate automotive NVH panel design. - **Face stress:** $\sigma_f = M/(b t_f d)$. Core stress: $\tau_c = V/(b d)$. - **Damping source:** constrained-layer action of adhesive + viscoelastic core shear. $\eta_{sandwich} \gg \eta_{steel}$. - **Critical frequency $f_c$** shifts upward in sandwiches → moves coincidence dip out of sensitive bands. - **Applications remember:** Dash (heavy-layer + foam), Floor (steel + PU + steel), Roof (Al + honeycomb + Al), Hood (Al + visco + Al), Trunk (SMC + foam + SMC). - **Asymmetric sandwiches** exhibit bending–stretching coupling — useful for tailored acoustic absorption. - **Thin-face assumption** ($d/t_f \ge 10$) reduces $D$ to a single term — check this before using the simplified formula. - **Symmetric sandwich:** identical faces → no coupling, no warping; preferred for structural panels. - **Timoshenko, not Euler–Bernoulli,** is the correct beam theory for sandwiches with soft cores.

topics.title: Vibration Absorbers and Isolators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Absorbers and Isolators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of automotive noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH) engineering, **Vibration Absorbers** and **Vibration Isolators** are two distinct yet complementary classes of passive vibration control devices employed to manage unwanted oscillatory energy in mechanical systems.

A **Vibration Absorber** (also known as a **Tuned Mass Damper (TMD)** or **Dynamic Vibration Absorber (DVA)**) is a secondary mass-spring subsystem that is deliberately attached to a primary vibrating structure. It functions by storing and releasing vibrational energy out of phase with the primary system, effectively cancelling vibration at its tuned natural frequency.

A **Vibration Isolator**, in contrast, is a resilient support (spring/damper combination) placed *between* a vibration source and a receiver (or vice versa) to attenuate the transmission of force or motion across a structural boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT863 Module 3):**
> The module explicitly distinguishes between *absorption* (tuning a secondary system to a primary) and *isolation* (using stiffness/damping to decouple two systems). Examiners frequently test this distinction with a direct 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Building Sway Problem (Absorber):**
Imagine a tall office tower swaying dangerously during strong winds. Engineers attach a massive concrete block near the top, suspended on hydraulic pistons. As the building lurches left, the block lags and pulls right, cancelling the motion. This is exactly how a **Tuned Mass Damper** works — the famous example being the **Taipei 101 tower's 730-tonne pendulum**. In cars, the engine's balancer shaft system acts as a rotational TMD.

**Analogy 2 — The Mattress and the Bouncing Ball (Isolator):**
Consider a heavy washing machine vibrating on a wooden floor. Placing a thick rubber mat underneath reduces the vibration reaching the floor. The rubber mat is an **isolator** — it doesn't stop the machine from vibrating, but it *blocks* transmission of the vibration to the surroundings. Automotive engine mounts and chassis bushings are perfect examples of isolators.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the analysis of both devices:

- **Transmissibility (TR):** Dimensionless ratio of transmitted force/motion amplitude to input force/motion amplitude.
- **Frequency Ratio (r = ω/ωn):** The ratio of excitation frequency to natural frequency, **dimensionless**.
- **Damping Ratio (ζ = c / c_c):** Ratio of actual damping coefficient to critical damping, **dimensionless**, bounded as **0 ≤ ζ ≤ 1** for underdamped systems.
- **Critical Damping Coefficient (c_c = 2√(km)):** Units of **N·s/m**.
- **Standard Acceleration of Gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²):** Used when expressing stiffness in terms of static deflection (δ_st = mg/k).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system, isolation becomes *effective* only when the excitation frequency **ω exceeds √2 × ωn** (i.e., r > 1.414). Below this threshold, isolation actually amplifies the transmitted vibration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Undamped Dynamic Vibration Absorber (Frahm Absorber, 1909)

### 2.1.1 System Configuration

Consider a primary system of mass **M** and stiffness **K** subjected to harmonic excitation **F(t) = F₀ sin(ωt)**. A secondary mass **m** connected by a spring of stiffness **k** is attached to the primary mass.

### 2.1.2 Operating Principle — The "Why"

The undamped absorber works on the principle of **destructive interference**. When the natural frequency of the absorber subsystem is tuned *exactly* equal to the excitation frequency, the secondary mass oscillates with a phase that produces a spring force **k·X** on the primary mass that is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the applied force **F₀**. The net force on the primary becomes zero, freezing it in space.

### 2.1.3 Tuning Condition

For the primary mass to have **zero steady-state amplitude (X = 0)**, the absorber must satisfy:

$$
\omega_a = \omega \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{k}{m} = \omega^2\;}
$$

At this exact tuning, the secondary mass oscillates with amplitude:

$$
Y = \dfrac{F_0}{k}
$$

### 2.1.4 Frequency Response of the Primary (with Absorber)

The amplitude ratio of the primary mass is given by the classical Frahm result:

$$
\dfrac{X}{F_0/K} = \dfrac{1 - (\omega_a/\omega_n)^2}{\left[1 + \dfrac{m}{M} - \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}\right)^2\right]\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\omega_a}{\omega_n}\right)^2\right] - \dfrac{m}{M}}
$$

where **ωn = √(K/M)** is the natural frequency of the primary *without* the absorber.

**Key observation:** When the two natural frequencies of the *combined* system are computed, they are separated by an amount determined by the mass ratio **μ = m/M**. The wider the separation, the broader the absorption bandwidth.

## 2.2 The Damped Dynamic Vibration Absorber

### 2.2.1 Why Damping is Needed

The undamped Frahm absorber is **fragile** — it works perfectly at one exact frequency but produces *infinite* primary amplitude if the excitation drifts off the tuning point (anti-resonance failure). Adding damping to the absorber element trades peak performance for **robustness** across a small frequency band.

### 2.2.2 Den Hartog's Optimum Tuning

For a viscously damped absorber, **J.P. Den Hartog (1947)** established the optimum parameters that minimize the *maximum* amplitude of the primary over a frequency band. The classical formulas are:

$$
\mu_{opt} = \dfrac{m_{opt}}{M} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{F_2^2}{F_1^2}} \quad \text{(for undamped primary)}
$$

For a damped primary with light damping (ζ₁ ≈ 0), the well-known engineering formulas are:

$$
\boxed{\;f_{opt} = \dfrac{\omega_{a,opt}}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\zeta_{a,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1+\mu)^3}}\;}
$$

where **μ = m/M** is the mass ratio and **ζ_a** is the damping ratio of the absorber.

## 2.3 Vibration Isolation Theory

### 2.3.1 Two Scenarios

1. **Force Isolation:** A machine with rotating imbalance produces a force F(t) on its support. The support experiences a transmitted force F_T. The goal is to minimize F_T/F.
2. **Motion Isolation:** A base (e.g., road input) moves with displacement Y(t). A sensitive payload (e.g., instrument, passenger cabin) experiences transmitted displacement X(t). The goal is to minimize X/Y.

### 2.3.2 The Transmissibility Equation

For an SDOF system with mass m, stiffness k, and damping c, subjected to base excitation **y(t) = Y₀ sin(ωt)**, the steady-state transmissibility is:

$$
\boxed{\;TR = \dfrac{X}{Y} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}
$$

For *force* transmissibility from a rotating unbalance force, the formula is:

$$
\boxed{\;TR_F = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}
$$

The two equations are mathematically identical because they derive from the same SDOF response transfer function.

### 2.3.3 Critical Frequency Analysis

| Parameter | Value | Implication |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **r = 0** (quasi-static) | TR = 1 | No isolation at very low frequencies |
| **r = √2 ≈ 1.414** | TR = 1 (any ζ) | The **magic crossover** point |
| **r = 1** | TR = 1/(2ζ) | Resonance peak; maximum damage |
| **r → ∞** | TR → 0 | Perfect high-frequency isolation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Isolation:** Always operate at **r > √2**. Below this ratio, adding damping *increases* transmitted force; above it, adding damping *increases* transmitted force too. The minimum transmissibility for any given r > √2 occurs at a specific optimum damping value.

### 2.3.4 The Optimum Damping for Isolation (Absolute Minimum)

Setting **∂TR/∂ζ = 0** for r > √2 yields the damping ratio that gives the *lowest possible* transmissibility for a single r value:

$$
\zeta_{opt,iso} = \sqrt{\dfrac{r^2 - 1}{2r^2}}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | k (N/m), m (kg) | SDOF undamped |
| Critical damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ | k, m | Threshold value |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/c_c$ | c (N·s/m) | Dimensionless |
| Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | ω (rad/s) | Excitation ratio |
| Transmissibility | $TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | r, ζ | Universal SDOF |
| Frahm tuning | $\omega_a = \omega_{forcing}$ | ω_a = √(k/m) | Zero primary motion |
| Den Hartog tuning (freq) | $f_{opt} = 1/(1+\mu)$ | μ = m/M | Optimum absorber freq |
| Den Hartog damping | $\zeta_{a,opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/[8(1+\mu)^3]}$ | μ | Optimum absorber ζ |
| Optimum isolation damping | $\zeta_{opt} = \sqrt{(r^2-1)/(2r^2)}$ | r > √2 | Min TR for given r |
| Static deflection | $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ | g = **9.81 m/s²** | For mount design |
| Spring rate from δ_st | $k = mg/\delta_{st}$ | — | Automotive mount sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Automotive engine mounts must satisfy two contradictory requirements: *soft* enough to isolate engine firing-frequency vibration (typically 25–200 Hz) but *stiff* enough to control engine torque reaction (1–5 Hz). This is solved using **hydroelastic mounts** with frequency-dependent stiffness — a direct KTU board exam favourite.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transmissibility for Base Excitation

### Statement of the Problem

A mass **m** is supported on a spring of stiffness **k** and damper **c** in parallel. The base (foundation) is subjected to prescribed motion **y(t) = Y₀ sin(ωt)**. Find the steady-state amplitude of the absolute mass displacement **x(t)**.

### Step 1 — Draw the Free Body Diagram and Write Newton's Second Law

The forces acting on mass m are:
- Spring force: **−k(x − y)** (proportional to relative displacement)
- Damper force: **−c(ẋ − ẏ)** (proportional to relative velocity)

Newton's second law gives:

$$
m\ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange into Standard Form

Move all **x** terms to the left and all **y** terms to the right:

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{y} + ky
$$

### Step 3 — Assume Harmonic Steady-State Solution

Let **x(t) = X sin(ωt − φ)** and **y(t) = Y₀ sin(ωt)**. In phasor (complex) form, X, Y, and the equation become algebraic. Dividing both sides by the inertial term **m** and defining the standard ratios:

$$
\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k}{m}, \quad 2\zeta\omega_n = \dfrac{c}{m}, \quad r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}
$$

The transfer function (complex transmissibility) is:

$$
T(j\omega) = \dfrac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{k + jc\omega}{k - m\omega^2 + jc\omega}
$$

### Step 4 — Normalise by k

Factor out **k** from numerator and denominator:

$$
T(j\omega) = \dfrac{1 + j(2\zeta r)}{1 - r^2 + j(2\zeta r)}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Modulus

The magnitude of a complex ratio is the ratio of magnitudes. Compute numerator and denominator magnitudes:

$$
\vert 1 + j(2\zeta r)\vert = \sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

$$
\vert 1 - r^2 + j(2\zeta r)\vert = \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Transmissibility Expression

$$
TR = \dfrac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

This is the canonical KTU result. For a clean square-root form, we write:

$$
\boxed{\;TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frahm Absorber Tuning Condition

### Step 1 — Equations of Motion

For a primary mass **M** with absorber mass **m** connected by spring **k**, both moving in translation:

$$
M\ddot{x} + Kx + k(x - y) = F_0\sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
m\ddot{y} + k(y - x) = 0
$$

where **x** is primary displacement and **y** is absorber displacement.

### Step 2 — Assume Harmonic Steady-State

Let **x = X sin(ωt)** and **y = Y sin(ωt)**. Substitute and factor out **sin(ωt)**:

$$
(-M\omega^2 + K + k)X - kY = F_0
$$

$$
-kX + (-m\omega^2 + k)Y = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Linear System via Cramer's Rule

The determinant of the coefficient matrix is:

$$
\Delta = (-M\omega^2 + K + k)(-m\omega^2 + k) - k^2
$$

Solving for **X** (replacing first column with **F₀** and 0):

$$
X = \dfrac{F_0(-m\omega^2 + k)}{(-M\omega^2 + K + k)(-m\omega^2 + k) - k^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Zero-Amplitude Condition

Setting **X = 0** requires the numerator to vanish:

$$
-m\omega^2 + k = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = m\omega^2
$$

### Step 5 — Express in Terms of Natural Frequencies

Recognising the absorber natural frequency **ω_a² = k/m** and primary natural frequency **ω_n² = K/M**:

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_a = \omega_{forcing} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \dfrac{k}{m} = \omega^2\;}
$$

This is the **Frahm tuning condition** — the foundational result for all absorber design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimum Damping for Maximum Isolation

Starting from the transmissibility expression, we wish to find the damping ratio **ζ** that minimises TR for a fixed **r > √2**.

### Step 1 — Define the Square of Transmissibility

To eliminate the square root, work with TR²:

$$
TR^2 = \dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

Let **u = (2ζr)²** for compactness. Then **TR² = (1 + u) / [(1 − r²)² + u]**.

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to u

$$
\dfrac{d(TR^2)}{du} = \dfrac{[(1 - r^2)^2 + u] - (1 + u)}{(1 - r^2)^2 + u]^2} = \dfrac{(1 - r^2)^2 - 1}{[(1 - r^2)^2 + u]^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Set the Derivative to Zero

For **r ≠ 1**, the numerator vanishes only when:

$$
(1 - r^2)^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - r^2 = \pm 1
$$

The physically meaningful solution (giving a minimum) is **1 − r² = −1**, i.e., **r² = 2**, **r = √2**. But this is the *crossover* point, not the optimum for a fixed r. We need a different approach: differentiate with respect to **ζ** keeping **r** constant.

### Step 4 — Differentiate TR² with Respect to ζ

Let **A = (1 − r²)²** and **B = (2r)²** (constant for fixed r). Then:

$$
TR^2 = \dfrac{1 + B\zeta^2}{A + B\zeta^2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{d(TR^2)}{d\zeta} = \dfrac{2B\zeta(A + B\zeta^2) - 2B\zeta(1 + B\zeta^2)}{(A + B\zeta^2)^2} = \dfrac{2B\zeta(A - 1)}{(A + B\zeta^2)^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Set the Derivative to Zero (and A ≠ 1)

For r > √2, **A = (1 − r²)² > 1** (since r² > 2 implies (1 − r²)² > 1). So **A − 1 ≠ 0**, and the equation **d(TR²)/dζ = 0** gives **ζ = 0**. This means that for a *single* r > √2, **TR is monotonically increasing in ζ** — so the absolute minimum is at **ζ = 0**.

But practical systems have damping; therefore, we constrain **ζ** to a small value **ζ_s** (a specific design requirement) and find the **r** that minimises TR. This is the "optimum frequency for given damping" problem, leading to **Den Hartog-style** results for the isolator.

### Step 6 — The Practical Optimum Damping for "Acceptable" Trade-off

In practice, designers minimise the *maximum* TR over a frequency band. Following Den Hartog's approach for the *isolator* case, the optimum damping that minimises the peak transmissibility across a band of frequencies around r = √2 is:

$$
\zeta_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{r^2 - 1}{2r^2}} \quad \text{(for the given r)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** The optimum damping formula tells the designer how much damping to use so that the maximum transmissibility (the worst case) is the lowest possible for the *intended operating band* — balancing low steady-state TR against resonance overshoot.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A car engine (mass **M = 250 kg**) runs at a firing frequency of **30 Hz**. The engine is supported on rubber mounts with total stiffness **K = 200,000 N/m**. Compute:
1. The natural frequency of the engine on its mounts.
2. The transmissibility at the firing frequency.
3. The amplitude of engine vibration if the unbalanced force amplitude is **F₀ = 500 N**.

### Step 1 — Natural Frequency

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{800} = 28.284 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{28.284}{6.2832} = 4.502 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 2 — Frequency Ratio

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 30 = 188.496 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{188.496}{28.284} = 6.664
$$

Since **r = 6.664 > √2**, the system is in the isolation region. Good.

### Step 3 — Transmissibility (Assume ζ = 0.1 for rubber)

$$
(2\zeta r)^2 = (2 \times 0.1 \times 6.664)^2 = (1.333)^2 = 1.777
$$

$$
(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 44.41)^2 = (-43.41)^2 = 1884.4
$$

$$
TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + 1.777}{1884.4 + 1.777}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.777}{1886.2}} = \sqrt{0.001472} = 0.0384
$$

So only **3.84%** of the unbalanced force is transmitted — excellent isolation.

### Step 4 — Static Displacement and Vibration Amplitude

The static deflection of the mounts under the engine weight:

$$
\delta_{st} = \dfrac{Mg}{K} = \dfrac{250 \times 9.81}{200{,}000} = 0.01226 \text{ m} = 12.26 \text{ mm}
$$

The amplitude of the dynamic displacement of the engine:

$$
X = \dfrac{F_0}{K} \times TR = \dfrac{500}{200{,}000} \times 0.0384 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0384 = 9.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.096 \text{ mm}
$$

This is well within acceptable NVH limits (typically < 0.5 mm for engine mounts).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vibration Absorber System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitation Force F sin wt] --> B[Primary Mass M]
    B --> C[Primary Spring K]
    C --> D[Fixed Ground Support]
    B --> E[Absorber Spring k]
    E --> F[Absorber Mass m]
    F --> G[Absorber Damper c]
    G --> B

    classDef force fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef primary fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef absorber fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef ground fill:#808080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class A force
    class B,C primary
    class E,F,G absorber
    class D ground
```

**Reading Guide:** The **red path** is the primary system (machine to ground); the **blue path** is the secondary absorber (mass-spring-damper tuned to the excitation frequency). The absorber's mass **m** is intentionally *much smaller* than **M** (typically μ = 0.05 to 0.20).

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vibration Isolator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SourceSide[Source Side]
        P1[Vibration Source e.g. Engine or Road]
    end

    subgraph IsolatorBlock[Resilient Isolator]
        P2[Spring k]
        P3[Damper c in parallel with spring]
    end

    subgraph ReceiverSide[Receiver Side]
        P4[Protected Mass m e.g. Cabin or Instrument]
    end

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4

    P1 -. Force FT transmitted .-> P4

    classDef sourceNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef isoNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recvNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class P1 sourceNode
    class P2,P3 isoNode
    class P4 recvNode
```

**Reading Guide:** The **green block** is the isolator. Energy flows from the source, partially reflected back, partially absorbed by the damper, and partially transmitted to the receiver as **F_T**. The transmissibility **TR = F_T / F₀** is the design metric.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Selection Logic for Absorber vs. Isolator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vibration Problem Identified]) --> Q1{Is the excitation frequency KNOWN and CONSTANT?}

    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use VIBRATION ABSORBER TMD]
    Q2 --> Q3[Tune k and m such that wa equals forcing frequency]
    Q3 --> Q4[Apply Den Hartog optimum formulas for damping]

    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is the goal to BLOCK transmission to a receiver?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use VIBRATION ISOLATOR]
    Q6 --> Q7[Choose k to push wn well below lowest forcing frequency]
    Q7 --> Q8[Add minimum damping to control resonance]

    Q5 -- No --> End1[Re-evaluate the problem]
    Q4 --> End2([Design Complete])
    Q8 --> End2

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef terminal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class Q1,Q5 decision
    class Q2,Q3,Q4,Q6,Q7,Q8,End1 action
    class Start,End2 terminal
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Frequency Response Behaviour

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LowFreq: r less than 1
    LowFreq: Quasi Static Region
    LowFreq: TR approx 1
    LowFreq: No isolation
    LowFreq --> Resonance: r crosses 1
    Resonance: Resonance Region
    Resonance: TR peaks at 1 over 2 zeta
    Resonance: Maximum damage
    Resonance --> Isolation: r crosses sqrt 2
    Isolation: Isolation Region
    Isolation: TR less than 1
    Isolation: TR decreases as r increases
    Isolation --> [*]: High frequencies attenuated

    note right of Resonance: Damping critical here
    note right of Isolation: Damping hurts isolation
```

**Reading Guide:** This state diagram shows how the SDOF system transitions through three operational regimes as the excitation frequency increases. The **r = √2 crossover** is the boundary between amplification and attenuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Vibration Absorber
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
A **Vibration Absorber** is a passive vibration control device consisting of a secondary mass **m** connected to the primary vibrating system through a spring (and optionally a damper) of stiffness **k**. It is *tuned* so that its natural frequency **ω_a = √(k/m)** matches the offending excitation frequency. When properly tuned, the inertial force of the absorber mass cancels the excitation force on the primary, theoretically reducing its vibration amplitude to zero (Frahm's principle, 1909). In automobiles, a common example is the **balancer shaft** used to cancel second-order vibration in inline 4-cylinder engines.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for principle, 1 for automotive example)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between Force Isolation and Motion Isolation
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Force Isolation | Motion Isolation |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Source of excitation | Internal force (e.g., unbalanced rotating mass) | External kinematic input (e.g., road profile) |
| Quantity to be controlled | Force transmitted to support **F_T** | Motion transmitted to payload **X** |
| Typical application | Engine mount, motor foundation | Instrument table, vehicle suspension |
| Design metric | **TR_F = F_T / F₀** | **TR = X / Y** |
| Governing equation | Same SDOF transfer function | Same SDOF transfer function |

Although both use the same transmissibility formula, the *input* and *output* quantities differ. In force isolation, we reduce the force a vibrating machine sends to its surroundings; in motion isolation, we reduce the motion a vibrating base sends to a sensitive payload.

*(3 marks — 1 for clear distinction, 1 for examples, 1 for correct formula reference)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Design of a Tuned Mass Damper (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Actual Board Question Style]**

(a) **Derive the equation of motion for an undamped dynamic vibration absorber attached to a primary SDOF system and show that when the absorber natural frequency equals the excitation frequency, the primary mass has zero steady-state amplitude.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram and Equations of Motion:**

For the primary mass M, the forces are: excitation F₀ sin(ωt), primary spring force −Kx, and absorber spring force −k(x − y).

$$
M\ddot{x} + Kx + k(x - y) = F_0 \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

For the absorber mass m, the only force is the absorber spring:

$$
m\ddot{y} + k(y - x) = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume Harmonic Steady-State:**

Let **x = X sin(ωt)** and **y = Y sin(ωt)**. Substituting and cancelling sin(ωt):

$$
(-M\omega^2 + K + k)X - kY = F_0 \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

$$
-kX + (k - m\omega^2)Y = 0 \quad \text{...(4)}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve by Cramer's Rule:**

The determinant of the coefficient matrix:

$$
\Delta = (K + k - M\omega^2)(k - m\omega^2) - k^2
$$

For **X**, replace the first column with the RHS vector:

$$
X = \dfrac{F_0(k - m\omega^2)}{\Delta}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Zero-Amplitude Condition:**

Setting **X = 0** requires the numerator to vanish:

$$
k - m\omega^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = m\omega^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \omega_a
$$

Thus, when the absorber natural frequency equals the excitation frequency, the primary mass remains stationary. This is the **Frahm tuning condition**. *(7 marks — 1 each for FBD, equations, harmonic assumption, Cramer's rule, zero condition, conclusion, and neat presentation)*

---

(b) **A primary machine of mass 50 kg vibrates with an unbalanced forcing frequency of 50 Hz. Design an undamped vibration absorber (specify mass m and stiffness k) to eliminate this vibration. Assume a practical mass ratio μ = m/M = 0.1.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Excitation Frequency:**

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose Mass Ratio:**

Given **μ = 0.1**:

$$
m = 0.1 \times M = 0.1 \times 50 = 5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Frahm's Tuning Condition:**

For the primary to have zero amplitude:

$$
\omega_a = \omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = 314.16
$$

$$
\dfrac{k}{m} = (314.16)^2 = 98{,}696 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
k = 5 \times 98{,}696 = 493{,}480 \text{ N/m} \approx 4.93 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Combined System Natural Frequencies (Optional Check):**

The two natural frequencies of the combined 2-DOF system are:

$$
\omega_{1,2}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\omega_n^2(1 + \mu) + \omega_a^2\right] \mp \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\left[\omega_n^2(1 + \mu) - \omega_a^2\right]^2 - 4\mu\omega_n^2\omega_a^2}
$$

With **ω_n² = K/M** and assuming a primary stiffness K that gives **ω_n ≈ ω_a** (so that we are in the absorption band), the two natural frequencies split around 50 Hz.

**Design Summary:**
- Absorber mass: **m = 5 kg**
- Absorber stiffness: **k ≈ 4.93 × 10⁵ N/m**
- Verification: At 50 Hz excitation, primary mass M has zero amplitude.

*(7 marks — 1 each for ω computation, mass ratio application, tuning formula, k computation, design summary, units, and verification)*

---

### Question B: Vibration Isolator Design and Transmissibility Analysis (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Internal Choice Counterpart]**

(a) **Derive the transmissibility ratio TR for a single-degree-of-freedom system subjected to base excitation, clearly stating the assumptions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Linear, time-invariant SDOF system.
2. Viscous damping (force proportional to velocity).
3. Harmonic base motion **y(t) = Y₀ sin(ωt)**.
4. Steady-state response, transients neglected.

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion:**

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{y} + ky
$$

**Step 2 — Complex Phasor Representation:**

Let **x = X e^{jωt}**, **y = Y e^{jωt}**. Substituting:

$$
(-m\omega^2 + jc\omega + k)X = (jc\omega + k)Y
$$

**Step 3 — Transfer Function:**

$$
T(j\omega) = \dfrac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{k + jc\omega}{k - m\omega^2 + jc\omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize:**

Divide numerator and denominator by **k**, and substitute **2ζω_n = c/m** and **ω_n² = k/m**:

$$
T(j\omega) = \dfrac{1 + j(2\zeta r)}{1 - r^2 + j(2\zeta r)}
$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude:**

$$
TR = \vert T(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Special Cases:**
- At **r = 0** (quasi-static): **TR = 1** (no isolation).
- At **r = 1** (resonance): **TR = 1/(2ζ)** (peak amplification).
- At **r = √2**: **TR = 1** (crossover, independent of ζ).
- As **r → ∞**: **TR → 0** (perfect isolation).

**Conclusion:** For isolation, operate at **r > √2** with minimum damping.

*(7 marks — 1 each for assumptions, equation, phasor form, normalization, magnitude computation, special cases, and conclusion)*

---

(b) **A precision measuring instrument of mass 8 kg is to be isolated from floor vibrations of 60 Hz. The isolator has a total stiffness of 32,000 N/m and damping ratio 0.05. Calculate: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the frequency ratio, (iii) the transmissibility, and (iv) the percentage of vibration transmitted.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** M = 8 kg, k = 32,000 N/m, ζ = 0.05, f = 60 Hz.

**Step (i) — Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{32{,}000}{8}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.246 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n = \dfrac{63.246}{2\pi} = 10.066 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step (ii) — Frequency Ratio:**

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 60 = 376.99 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{376.99}{63.246} = 5.961
$$

**Step (iii) — Transmissibility:**

$$
2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.05 \times 5.961 = 0.5961
$$

$$
(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.3553
$$

$$
(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 35.53)^2 = (-34.53)^2 = 1192.3
$$

$$
TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + 0.3553}{1192.3 + 0.3553}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.3553}{1192.7}} = \sqrt{0.001136} = 0.03371
$$

**Step (iv) — Percentage Transmitted:**

$$
\% \text{ Transmitted} = TR \times 100\% = 3.371\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The isolator blocks **96.6%** of the floor vibration — a well-designed isolation system because the excitation frequency (60 Hz) is nearly 6 times the natural frequency (10 Hz), placing it firmly in the isolation region (r = 5.96 >> √2).

*(7 marks — 1 each for ω_n, f_n, r, (2ζr)², (1−r²)², TR value, and percentage interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU PEMUT863 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square r in (1 − r²)²:** A very common slip. The denominator is **(1 − r²)²**, not **(1 − r)²**. Half a mark is lost for this error, and the numerical answer becomes wildly wrong.
>
> 2. **Confusing TR with X/F₀:** Transmissibility is dimensionless. If you write **TR = X / F₀**, you've made a unit error worth 1 full mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Den Hartog's mass-ratio factor (1 + μ):** When asked for *optimum* absorber frequency, many students write **ω_a = ω_n** (Frahm condition) instead of **ω_a = ω_n / (1 + μ)**. This is the most-tested error in 14-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Misidentifying the absorber versus isolator:** An absorber *is attached to* the vibrating mass; an isolator is *placed between* source and receiver. Examiners award 0 marks for a correct derivation of the wrong concept.
>
> 5. **Not stating the design rule r > √2:** A complete answer must state the design condition, not just compute TR.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Always express stiffness in N/m, mass in kg, frequency in rad/s (or Hz with 2π conversion). Examiners check units before checking numbers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEMUT863 Module 3: Vibration Absorbers and Isolators**

### Core Distinctions
- **Absorber** = attached *to* the vibrating mass; cancels vibration at the tuned frequency.
- **Isolator** = placed *between* source and receiver; attenuates transmission across a frequency band.

### Absorber Key Formulas
- **Frahm tuning:** $k = m\omega^2$ (zero primary amplitude).
- **Den Hartog optimum frequency:** $f_{opt} = 1/(1+\mu)$.
- **Den Hartog optimum damping:** $\zeta_{a,opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/[8(1+\mu)^3]}$.

### Isolator Key Formulas
- **Transmissibility:** $TR = \sqrt{[1+(2\zeta r)^2]/[(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2]}$.
- **Crossover frequency:** $r = \sqrt{2}$ (TR = 1 regardless of ζ).
- **Isolation rule:** Operate at $r > \sqrt{2}$.
- **Optimum damping for given r:** $\zeta_{opt} = \sqrt{(r^2-1)/(2r^2)}$.

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorise
- **√2 ≈ 1.414**
- **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **ωn = √(k/m) = 2πfn**
- **cc = 2√(km)**

### Engineering Judgement Points
- Engine mounts are isolators (frequency ratio design).
- Balancer shafts are absorbers (tuned to firing frequency).
- Hydraulic engine mounts combine both (active + passive).
- Damping helps control resonance but *hurts* high-frequency isolation — a fundamental trade-off.

### High-Yield Keywords for KTU Theory Questions
- Transmissibility
- Frequency ratio
- Damping ratio
- Critical damping
- Anti-resonance
- Static deflection
- Frahm absorber
- Den Hartog optimum
- Force isolation
- Motion isolation
- Viscous damping

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** When faced with a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as: (1) stated assumptions, (2) free body diagram, (3) equation of motion, (4) derivation with each step labelled, (5) final boxed formula, (6) numerical substitution with units, (7) interpretation. KTU examiners award 2–3 marks for clear presentation and physical interpretation alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Measurement and Control - Sources of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Measurement and Control - Sources of Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Noise** is defined as any unwanted acoustic pressure fluctuation radiated from a vehicle or its subsystems into the surrounding environment (exterior noise) or into the passenger compartment (interior noise). According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for PEMUT863, the discipline of **Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH)** in automobiles treats noise as the *aero-acoustic, mechanical, or structural response* of the vehicle system to internal excitation sources (combustion, gear meshing, tire-road interaction) and external excitation sources (aerodynamic flow).

The standard metric used to quantify noise is the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**, measured in **decibels (dB)** relative to a reference pressure of $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). For human-perceived loudness, the frequency-weighted **A-weighted Sound Pressure Level (dBA)** is the globally accepted standard, because the human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Memorize):** *Noise in an automobile is the unwanted sound energy radiated by mechanical, aerodynamic, combustion, and structural sources, quantified by Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in dB, with A-weighted SPL (dBA) being the standard metric for human-perceived noise evaluation.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing next to a busy street. You hear the **honking horn** of a car, the **roar of its engine**, the **whistle of wind** past its windows, and the **hiss of tires on wet asphalt**. Each of these is a different *source* of automotive noise. Now, if you step inside the same car and close the windows, the sounds change — you hear the **engine ticking**, the **gearbox whining**, and **rattles from the dashboard**. These are *interior* noise sources.

Think of a vehicle as a complex **musical orchestra**:
- The **engine** plays the deep drums (combustion noise — low frequency).
- The **transmission** plays the violins (gear meshing — high frequency whine).
- The **tires** play the cymbals (road excitation — broadband).
- The **wind** plays the flute (aerodynamic — speed-dependent).

To an NVH engineer, the goal is to identify which instrument is too loud, at what frequency, and how to "tune it down" without breaking the car's performance — exactly like a sound engineer equalizing a live band.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$. This is the **standard reference** for all SPL calculations in air at 1 kHz, defined by the **IEC 61672** standard.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

Automotive noise sources are broadly classified into three major families based on their physical origin:

| Family | Physical Origin | Typical Frequency Range | Key Contributors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Powertrain Noise** | Combustion & mechanical | 20 Hz – 10 kHz | Engine, transmission, exhaust |
| **Road/Tire Noise** | Tire-pavement interaction | 200 Hz – 5 kHz | Tire tread, road texture, suspension |
| **Aerodynamic Noise** | Turbulent airflow | 500 Hz – 20 kHz | Windshield, mirrors, A-pillars, gaps |
| **Structure-Borne / Interior** | Panel vibration | 50 Hz – 1 kHz | Floor pan, dash panel, roof |
| **Auxiliary Systems** | Electric / mechanical auxiliaries | 100 Hz – 8 kHz | HVAC blower, alternator, pumps |

> [!TIP]
