> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always specify the **frequency range** when identifying a noise source. Examiners award bonus marks for linking source type to its dominant frequency band.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Automotive Noise

The physical generation of noise in an automobile follows the **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) model**, the cornerstone of all NVH engineering:

- **Source:** Where the vibrational/acoustic energy is *created* (e.g., combustion pressure in cylinder, gear tooth impact).
- **Path:** The medium through which the energy *travels* (air-borne path or structure-borne path through chassis).
- **Receiver:** The location where the noise is *perceived* (driver's ear, pedestrian on sidewalk, microphone).

To control noise, the engineer can attack any one of the three — **reduce the source strength**, **block the path**, or **protect the receiver** (with ear protection or active noise cancellation).

### 2.1.1 Powertrain Noise (Engine & Transmission)

The internal combustion engine is the dominant noise source below approximately 80 km/h. Its noise is a superposition of:

1. **Combustion Noise (Gas Force):** Rapid pressure rise inside the cylinder after ignition. Dominant in **diesel engines**; characteristic frequency is the **firing frequency** $f_c = \frac{N \cdot n_c}{120 \cdot \tau}$ where $N$ is RPM, $n_c$ is number of cylinders, $\tau$ is stroke cycle (2 for 4-stroke).
2. **Mechanical Noise (Inertia Force):** Vibrations from reciprocating parts (piston, connecting rod, crankshaft). Dominant at engine orders and harmonics.
3. **Impact Noise:** Valve train impacts (tappet, rocker, cam-follower). High-frequency content above 1 kHz.
4. **Accessory Drive Noise:** Alternator whine, water pump, power steering pump.

The **transmission (gearbox)** generates **gear meshing noise** at the **Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF)**:

$$f_{GMF} = \frac{N_{pinion} \cdot N_{input}}{60}$$

where $N_{pinion}$ is the number of teeth on the pinion and $N_{input}$ is the input shaft speed in RPM.

### 2.1.2 Tire/Road Noise

Becomes the dominant source above 80 km/h. Generated by:
- **Tread block impact** on road surface asperities.
- **Air pumping** — air trapped between tread and road is compressed and released.
- **Stick-slip and adhesion** phenomena.
- **Cavity resonance** — the air cavity inside the tire resonates around **200–250 Hz** (the so-called "tire cavity mode").

### 2.1.3 Aerodynamic Noise

Dominant above 100 km/h. Sources include:
- **Vortex shedding** from A-pillars, side mirrors, antenna.
- **Turbulent boundary layer** on body panels.
- **Windshield buffeting** — Helmholtz resonance of cabin air mass.
- **Flow through gaps** (window seals, door seals).

### 2.1.4 Structure-Borne Interior Noise

Vibrations from powertrain, suspension, and road are transmitted through the body structure and re-radiated as sound by large interior panels (floor pan, roof, side glass). Critical panel resonances lie in the **50–500 Hz** range, which overlaps with human voice frequencies — making speech intelligibility a major concern.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most heavily-tested formulas for Module 3. Memorize units and reference values.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | Sound Power Level (PWL) | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$ |
| 3 | Sound Intensity Level (SIL) | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ |
| 4 | A-Weighting (approx. 1 kHz) | $L_A \approx L_p + \Delta A(f)$ | dBA | $\Delta A$ from IEC 61672 table |
| 5 | Addition of two SPLs | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$ | dB | Use when sources are *uncorrelated* |
| 6 | Doubling identical sources | $\Delta L = +3 \, \text{dB}$ | dB | Two identical machines side by side |
| 7 | Doubling distance (free field) | $\Delta L = -6 \, \text{dB}$ | dB | Point source, spherical spreading |
| 8 | Engine Firing Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{N \cdot n_c}{120 \cdot \tau}$ | Hz | $N$ = RPM, $n_c$ = cylinders, $\tau$ = 2 (4-stroke) |
| 9 | Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF) | $f_{GMF} = \dfrac{N_{teeth} \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | Hz | Pinion teeth $\times$ input RPM / 60 |
| 10 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | $c = 343 \, \text{m/s}$ in air at 20°C |
| 11 | Inverse Square Law | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ | W/m² | Free field, point source |
| 12 | Order Analysis | $f_{order} = \dfrac{\text{Order} \cdot N}{60}$ | Hz | Useful for engine order tracking |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Reminder:** Sound pressure is a *root-mean-square* quantity ($p_{rms}$), and SPL is a **logarithmic** measure — so you **cannot** simply add dB values arithmetically. Always convert to linear power/pressure, add, and convert back to dB.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas and noise classifications are not just academic. They are used by:
- **OEM NVH engineers** to pass strict regulations like ECE R51 (pass-by noise), ECE R28 (audible warning), and ISO 362-1.
- **Tier-1 suppliers** (Bosch, Continental, Dana) to design quieter engines, gear sets, and exhausts.
- **EV manufacturers** to combat the lack of engine masking noise, which exposes tire and wind noise.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Formula

The human ear responds to a vast range of sound pressures — from the threshold of hearing ($p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$) to the threshold of pain ($p_{pain} \approx 20 \, \text{Pa}$). The ratio of these extremes is **one million** ($10^6$). Using a linear scale is impractical, so we use a **logarithmic decibel scale**.

### Step 1: Define the ratio of measured pressure to reference pressure
Let $p_{rms}$ be the measured root-mean-square sound pressure at a point. Define the ratio:

$$r = \frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}$$

### Step 2: Define the Bel as the log₁₀ of power ratio
A power ratio of 10:1 corresponds to 1 Bel. Since acoustic intensity $I \propto p^2$:

$$L_{Bel} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^2}{p_0^2}\right) = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$$

### Step 3: Convert Bel to deciBel (1 Bel = 10 dB)

$$L_{dB} = 10 \cdot L_{Bel} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$$

This is the canonical SPL formula. **Note carefully** that we have a factor of **20** (not 10) in front of the log because the underlying physical quantity is *pressure* (a field quantity), which squares to *intensity* (an energy quantity).

### Step 4: Substitute $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \, \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$?** This value was chosen because it represents the lowest sound pressure a young, healthy human ear can detect at 1 kHz — the frequency of maximum auditory sensitivity.

## 3.2 Worked Example: SPL of a Car Horn

A car horn produces $p_{rms} = 2 \, \text{Pa}$ at 2 meters distance. Calculate the SPL.

**Step 1:** Write down knowns.

$$p_{rms} = 2 \, \text{Pa}, \quad p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0} = \frac{2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10^{5}$$

**Step 3:** Take log₁₀.

$$\log_{10}(10^5) = 5$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 20.

$$L_p = 20 \times 5 = 100 \, \text{dB}$$

**Answer:** The horn radiates **100 dB SPL** at 2 m.

## 3.3 Derivation of dB Addition for Uncorrelated Sources

When two independent noise sources operate simultaneously, the *acoustic powers* (not pressures) add linearly. The combined SPL is:

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_1 + W_2}{W_0}\right)$$

### Step 1: Express each in decibels.

$$\frac{W_1}{W_0} = 10^{L_1/10}, \quad \frac{W_2}{W_0} = 10^{L_2/10}$$

### Step 2: Sum the linear powers.

$$\frac{W_1 + W_2}{W_0} = 10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}$$

### Step 3: Take the log.

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$

### Special Case: Doubling identical sources
If $L_1 = L_2 = L$:

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 \cdot 10^{L/10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2) + L \approx L + 3.01 \, \text{dB}$$

Therefore, **two identical uncorrelated sources give +3 dB** — not +6 dB (a very common KTU pitfall).

## 3.4 Derivation of Inverse-Square Law (Free Field)

A point source radiates acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions. The intensity at distance $r$ is the power per unit area on a sphere of radius $r$:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^2}$$

Comparing to the reference intensity $I_0$ at distance $r_0$:

$$\frac{I(r)}{I_0} = \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2$$

In decibels:

$$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_0^2}{r^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)$$

When $r = 2 r_0$ (doubling distance):

$$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -6.02 \, \text{dB}$$

Hence, in the free field, **doubling the distance from a point source reduces SPL by 6 dB**.

## 3.5 Engine Firing Frequency — Step-by-Step

A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine runs at 3000 RPM. Find the dominant combustion noise frequency.

**Step 1:** Stroke cycle $\tau = 2$ (for 4-stroke).

**Step 2:** Number of cylinders $n_c = 4$.

**Step 3:** Apply firing frequency formula.

$$f_c = \frac{N \cdot n_c}{120 \cdot \tau} = \frac{3000 \cdot 4}{120 \cdot 2} = \frac{12000}{240} = 50 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Answer:** Dominant combustion noise frequency is **50 Hz** — well within the range of human hearing and cabin panel resonance, which is why it is so intrusive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor of $\tau$ in the denominator. For a 2-stroke engine ($\tau = 1$), the firing frequency is **twice** that of a 4-stroke at the same RPM. This frequently appears as a "trick" sub-part.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sources vs. Mitigation

| Source | Dominant Mechanism | Typical dB(A) | Standard Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine combustion | Gas pressure rise | 75–95 | Engine mounts, acoustic cover, balancing |
| Gear meshing | Tooth impact | 65–85 | Helical gears, profile shift, finishing |
| Tire/road | Tread impact + air pump | 65–80 | Tread pattern, foam insert, road surface |
| Aerodynamic | Vortex shedding | 60–80 | Streamlined mirrors, A-pillar fillets |
| HVAC blower | Blade-pass frequency | 50–70 | Duct lining, blower silencer |
| Exhaust | Tailpipe radiation | 70–90 | Muffler (reactive + absorptive) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Automotive Noise Sources (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Automotive Noise Sources] --> B[Powertrain Noise]
    A --> C[Road Tire Noise]
    A --> D[Aerodynamic Noise]
    A --> E[Structure Borne Interior Noise]
    A --> F[Auxiliary System Noise]

    B --> B1[Combustion Noise]
    B --> B2[Mechanical Inertia Noise]
    B --> B3[Valve Train Impact]
    B --> B4[Gearbox Meshing Noise]

    C --> C1[Tread Block Impact]
    C --> C2[Air Pumping]
    C --> C3[Tire Cavity Resonance]

    D --> D1[Vortex Shedding]
    D --> D2[Boundary Layer Turbulence]
    D --> D3[Windshield Buffeting]
    D --> D4[Flow Through Gaps]

    E --> E1[Panel Re radiation]
    E --> E2[Cabin Boom]
    E --> E3[Rattles and Squeaks]

    F --> F1[HVAC Blower]
    F --> F2[Alternator Whine]
    F --> F3[Power Steering Pump]
    F --> F4[Brake Squeal]
```

## 4.2 Source–Path–Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source: Engine, Tire, Wind, Gear] --> PATH1[Air Borne Path]
    SRC --> PATH2[Structure Borne Path]
    PATH1 --> REC[Receiver: Driver Ear, Microphone, Pedestrian]
    PATH2 --> STRUCT[Body Structure Vibration]
    STRUCT --> PANEL[Interior Panel Re radiation]
    PANEL --> REC

    CTRL1[Source Reduction] -.controls.-> SRC
    CTRL2[Path Blocking: Absorbers, Barriers] -.controls.-> PATH1
    CTRL3[Path Blocking: Dampers, Isolators] -.controls.-> PATH2
    CTRL4[Receiver Protection: ANC, Ear Plugs] -.controls.-> REC
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Standard Noise Measurement Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sound Pressure at Microphone] --> B[Microphone: 1 2 inch Free Field]
    B --> C[Preamplifier]
    C --> D[Sound Level Meter SLM]
    D --> E[A Weighting Filter]
    E --> F[FFT Analyzer or DAQ]
    F --> G[PC: dB SPL, dBA, 1 3 Octave, FFT]
    G --> H[Report: SPL vs Frequency vs Order]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Context:** A typical B.Tech lab measures noise using a Class 1 sound level meter (IEC 61672), a 1/2" free-field microphone, and FFT analysis. The output is plotted as **Sound Pressure Level vs. Frequency** or as a **1/3-octave band** spectrum, both of which are common exam-style graph-reading questions.

## 4.4 Frequency-Domain Mapping of Sources (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LOW[Low Frequency 20 to 200 Hz]
        A1[Engine Firing Frequency]
        A2[Body Bending Modes]
    end
    subgraph MID[Mid Frequency 200 Hz to 2 kHz]
        B1[Road Tire Noise]
        B2[Panel Resonance]
        B3[Gear Meshing Sidebands]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High Frequency 2 kHz to 20 kHz]
        C1[Aerodynamic Whistle]
        C2[Valve Train Tap]
        C3[Brake Squeal]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between sound pressure level (dB) and A-weighted sound pressure level (dBA). Why is dBA preferred for evaluating vehicle interior noise?**

**Model Answer:**
Sound Pressure Level (SPL) measured in dB is a flat, unweighted logarithmic measure of acoustic pressure relative to the reference $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$. A-weighting applies a frequency-dependent correction (defined in **IEC 61672**) that mimics the varying sensitivity of the human ear, attenuating low frequencies (below 500 Hz) and very high frequencies (above 10 kHz) where the ear is less sensitive. The result is denoted **dBA**. For vehicle interior noise evaluation, dBA is preferred because the human occupant is the ultimate "receiver," and dBA correlates well with the perceived loudness, annoyance, and speech interference inside the cabin. Regulatory standards like **ECE R51** and **ISO 362** also specify A-weighted metrics.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of dB, 1 for dBA, 1 for regulatory/human relevance.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List any four major sources of noise in a passenger car and identify the dominant frequency range for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Source | Dominant Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engine combustion | 20 – 500 Hz (firing frequency and harmonics) |
| 2 | Gear meshing | 500 Hz – 5 kHz (GMF and sidebands) |
| 3 | Tire/road interaction | 200 Hz – 5 kHz (broadband) |
| 4 | Aerodynamic | 500 Hz – 20 kHz (vortex shedding, whistles) |
| 5 | HVAC blower | 100 Hz – 2 kHz (blade-pass frequency) |

*(3 marks: ½ mark each source + ½ mark each frequency band.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) model** for automotive NVH analysis. Discuss at least two control strategies for each of the three elements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** in decibels, starting from the definition of acoustic intensity. A car's exhaust radiates an acoustic power of 0.1 W. Find the SPL at 5 m distance, assuming free-field conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The **Source–Path–Receiver model** decomposes any NVH problem into three functional blocks:

1. **Source:** Origin of vibrational or acoustic energy. Examples include combustion pressure in engine cylinders, gear tooth meshing impulses, and tire-road asperity impacts.
2. **Path:** The medium that transmits the energy. There are two sub-classes:
   - *Air-borne path* — sound waves through air.
   - *Structure-borne path* — vibrations through chassis, mounts, and body panels.
3. **Receiver:** The point of perception — typically the driver's ear (interior) or a microphone / pedestrian (exterior).

**Control Strategies:**
- *Source reduction:* better combustion control, balanced rotating parts, helical gears, low-noise tires.
- *Path blocking (air-borne):* acoustic absorption, barriers, mufflers, double-wall partitions.
- *Path blocking (structure-borne):* elastomeric mounts, tuned mass dampers, constrained-layer damping.
- *Receiver protection:* active noise control (ANC) headphones, sound insulation on the firewall, isolated sub-frame.

*(Block diagram: draw a simple three-box layout Source → Path → Receiver with control arrows as shown in Section 4.2. 7 marks: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for strategies, 1 mark for example.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1:** Acoustic intensity $I$ in free field at distance $r$ from a point source radiating power $W$:

$$I = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^2}$$

**Step 2:** Reference intensity $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$.

**Step 3:** Sound intensity level in dB:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 4:** In free field, SPL equals SIL (within 0.1 dB):

$$L_p \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4 \pi r^2 \cdot I_0}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Plug in $W = 0.1 \, \text{W}$, $r = 5 \, \text{m}$, $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$:

$$4 \pi r^2 = 4 \pi (5)^2 = 100 \pi \approx 314.16 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$L_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1}{314.16 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(3.183 \times 10^{8}\right)$$

$$L_p \approx 10 \times 8.5027 \approx 85.0 \, \text{dB}$$

**Answer:** **SPL ≈ 85 dB at 5 m.** *(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 2 for free-field formula, 3 for numerical substitution and answer.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Classify the major **sources of noise** in an automobile. For each source, mention the dominant frequency range and one standard mitigation technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two identical machines operate side by side. One alone produces 78 dB. Calculate the combined SPL. If a third identical machine is added, what is the new SPL? Comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

*(Refer to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.6 for the full table.)*

**Classification:**

1. **Powertrain Noise** (Engine, Transmission, Exhaust): Dominant 20 Hz – 10 kHz. *Mitigation:* engine mounts, muffler, balanced shafts.
2. **Road/Tire Noise** (Tire–pavement interaction): Dominant 200 Hz – 5 kHz. *Mitigation:* low-noise tread pattern, foam-filled tires, smooth road surface.
3. **Aerodynamic Noise** (Flow over body): Dominant 500 Hz – 20 kHz. *Mitigation:* streamlined side mirrors, A-pillar vortex generators, sealed gaps.
4. **Structure-Borne Interior Noise** (Panel re-radiation): Dominant 50 Hz – 1 kHz. *Mitigation:* constrained-layer damping, sound-absorbing dash mat, isolated sub-frame.
5. **Auxiliary System Noise** (HVAC, alternator, pumps): Dominant 100 Hz – 8 kHz. *Mitigation:* blower silencers, rubber-isolated pumps.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for each of 5 sources + 2 marks for classification depth.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1:** Two identical uncorrelated sources — combined SPL:

$$L_{total} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(2) = 78 + 3.01 \approx 81.0 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 2:** Three identical uncorrelated sources — combined SPL:

$$L_{total} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(3) = 78 + 4.77 \approx 82.8 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 3:** Engineering significance:
- Doubling machines adds only **+3 dB** (a *just noticeable* difference, but not a doubling of loudness perception).
- A 10 dB increase is perceived as roughly **twice as loud**.
- Therefore, the NVH engineer's effort to *reduce source strength* (e.g., quieter fans, better-balanced rotors) yields far greater perceived benefit than merely adding more identical machines.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for each numerical step, 1 mark for engineering comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20** in $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ — using 10 instead of 20 (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Arithmetic addition of dB** values: students often write $78 + 78 = 156 \, \text{dB}$. The correct method is **+3 dB for two identical sources**.
> 3. **Confusing dB with dBA** — they are not interchangeable. dBA includes A-weighting.
> 4. **Forgetting $\tau$** in the engine firing frequency formula.
> 5. **Using reference pressure $p_0 = 0.0002 \, \text{Pa}$** without scientific notation — use $20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$ to avoid unit confusion.
> 6. **Skipping the units** in the final numerical answer. Always state "dB" or "dBA."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision before Exam:**

- **Definition of Noise:** Unwanted acoustic pressure fluctuation; quantified in dB relative to $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.
- **Reference values:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$, $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$, $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$.
- **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$.
- **PWL formula:** $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$.
- **dB addition rule:** Two identical sources = +3 dB; ten identical sources = +10 dB.
- **Distance rule:** Doubling distance in free field = −6 dB.
- **Engine firing frequency:** $f_c = N \cdot n_c / (120 \cdot \tau)$; for 4-stroke, divide by 2.
- **GMF:** $f_{GMF} = (\text{teeth} \times \text{RPM})/60$.
- **Five Source Families:** Powertrain, Road/Tire, Aerodynamic, Structure-Borne Interior, Auxiliary.
- **Source–Path–Receiver Model:** Foundation of all NVH problem-solving.
- **A-weighting:** De-emphasizes low & high frequencies to mimic human hearing; reported in dBA.
- **Dominant Frequency Bands to Memorize:**
  - Engine firing → 20 – 200 Hz
  - Tire/road → 200 Hz – 5 kHz
  - Aerodynamic → 500 Hz – 20 kHz
  - Tire cavity resonance → 200 – 250 Hz
- **Key Regulations to Know:** ECE R51 (pass-by noise), ISO 362-1, IEC 61672 (SLM standard).
- **Common Mitigation Strategies:** Engine mounts, acoustic absorption, damping, ANC, mufflers, low-NVH tires, streamlined body.
- **Always convert dB → linear → add → linear → dB** for any sound-level arithmetic. Never add dB values directly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Measuring Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Noise Measuring Instruments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Noise Measuring Instruments** are precision electro-acoustic devices designed to quantify, analyze, and characterize unwanted sound (noise) in automotive environments by transducing acoustic pressure fluctuations into proportional electrical signals, processing them through calibrated analog/digital chains, and displaying results in standardized metrics such as **Sound Pressure Level (SPL in dB)**, **Sound Power Level (PWL in dB)**, and **Sound Intensity Level (SIL in dB)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The instruments covered in this module are governed by the **IEC 61672-1** standard for sound level meters and **ISO 3744 / ISO 9614** for sound power and intensity measurements. Mastering the classification (Class 1 vs Class 2), weighting networks, and analyzer types is mandatory for Part A (3-mark) questions. The automotive noise measurement chain typically follows: **Acoustic Source → Microphone (Transducer) → Preamplifier → Sound Level Meter / Analyzer → Display / Recorder** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Doctor's Stethoscope for Cars"** > Think of noise measuring instruments as a doctor's diagnostic toolkit for a "sick" vehicle. Just like a stethoscope converts a heartbeat into an audible signal and an ECG converts it into a graph, a **microphone** converts sound waves into a tiny electrical voltage, a **sound level meter** acts like a thermometer giving a single number (the "temperature" of the noise), and a **frequency analyzer** is like a prism splitting white light into its color spectrum — it breaks the noise into its individual frequency components so engineers can pinpoint *which* engine speed or *which* gear is producing the offending whine. ### 1.3 The Three Core Metrics Measured | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ or SPL | **dB** (re 20 µPa) | Local pressure fluctuation | | Sound Power Level | $L_W$ or PWL | **dB** (re 10⁻¹² W) | Total acoustic energy radiated | | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ or SIL | **dB** (re 10⁻¹² W/m²) | Directional energy flux | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Threshold:** The reference sound pressure is **$p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$** (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). All dB SPL values in automotive work are referenced to this pressure. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Sound Measurement Chain — Step-by-Step Logic A noise measurement system is never a single device; it is a calibrated **signal chain**. Each link introduces potential error, and KTU examiners love asking students to identify the role of each link. **Step 1 — Acoustic Capture (Transduction)** A microphone diaphragm moves in response to fluctuating sound pressure. This motion is converted into an analog voltage via one of three principles: - **Condenser (Capacitor) microphone:** Most common. Diaphragm forms one plate of a capacitor; motion changes capacitance. Requires **polarization voltage (200V)** or pre-polarized (electret) designs. Excellent stability and frequency response (20 Hz – 20 kHz). - **Piezoelectric microphone:** Uses crystals (e.g., lead zirconate titanate) that generate charge when stressed. Robust, used in harsh engine-bay environments. - **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS):** Silicon-based, used in modern compact automotive sensor arrays. **Step 2 — Preamplification** The microphone output is in the microvolt range. A preamplifier boosts the signal while maintaining a flat frequency response and adding minimal self-noise. **Step 3 — Frequency Weighting** The raw electrical signal is filtered through one of the standardized weighting networks to mimic human hearing response. The KTU-relevant weightings are: | Weighting | Purpose | Application | |---|---|---| | **A-weighting** $L_A$ or dB(A) | Approximates 40-phon equal-loudness contour | **Standard for all automotive pass-by noise (ISO 362)** | | **B-weighting** | Intermediate contour (mostly obsolete) | Historical use | | **C-weighting** $L_C$ | Approximates 100-phon contour | Peak/impact noise, e.g., exhaust pops | | **Z-weighting** (Linear) | Flat 0 dB across 10 Hz – 20 kHz | Objective measurement, FFT analysis | | **D-weighting** | Specifically for aircraft perceived noise | Rarely used in automotive | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The A-weighting curve rolls off below 500 Hz and above 10 kHz. A 100 Hz tone at 80 dB SPL becomes only ~50 dB(A) — a major source of student confusion. **Step 4 — Time Weighting** The RMS detector applies one of three time constants to the squared pressure signal: - **Fast (F):** τ = 125 ms — used for fluctuating noise - **Slow (S):** τ = 1000 ms — used for steady noise - **Impulse (I):** τ = 35 ms (rise) / 1500 ms (decay) — for short bursts **Step 5 — Analysis and Display** The conditioned signal is either: - Displayed as a single SPL value (Sound Level Meter), OR - Fed into a **frequency analyzer** (analog filter banks, digital FFT, or constant-percentage-bandwidth filters) ### 2.2 Classification of Noise Measuring Instruments **A. Sound Level Meter (SLM)** The workhorse instrument. Per **IEC 61672-1:2013**, SLMs are divided into two precision classes: - **Class 1 (Precision):** ±0.7 dB tolerance, used in laboratory and certified field work - **Class 2 (General Purpose):** ±1.5 dB tolerance, used in survey and preliminary work **B. Frequency Analyzers** Convert the time-domain pressure signal into the frequency domain: - **Octave-band analyzers** — center frequencies at 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500 Hz, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 kHz (10 bands) - **1/3-octave-band analyzers** — 30 bands, resolution of ~23% of center frequency - **Narrowband (FFT) analyzers** — resolution down to <1 Hz, ideal for identifying engine RPM orders and gear whine **C. Sound Intensity Probes** Two phase-matched microphones facing each other with a fixed spacer. Directly measures the product of pressure and particle velocity, allowing **in-situ sound power determination** without an anechoic chamber — vital for in-vehicle source ranking. **D. Head and Torso Simulator (HATS)** An anthropometric mannequin with built-in ear simulators and binaural microphones. Captures noise *as heard by a driver* at the ear entrance point. Used for interior SQoS (Sound Quality of Sound) assessments. **E. Acoustic Camera (Beamforming Array)** A planar or spherical array of dozens of microphones. Real-time beamforming produces a visible "heat map" of noise sources overlaid on a video image of the vehicle — gold standard for NVH source localization. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Expression | Use | |---|---|---| | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$ | Core dB definition, $p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa | | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | | Sound Intensity | $I = \overline{p(t) \cdot u(t)}$ | Time-averaged pressure–velocity product | | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | | Intensity Probe (two-microphone) | $I_n \approx -\dfrac{1}{\rho_0 \Delta r}\int \left(p_A + p_B\right)\left(u_A - u_B\right) dt$ | Pressure-gradient estimate of velocity | | Combining SPLs (incoherent) | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Two uncorrelated sources | | Difference of two SPLs | $\Delta L = L_1 - L_2$ | Used with decibel addition chart | | Octave-band Ratio | $f_2 / f_1 = 2$ | 1/1-octave spacing | | 1/3-octave Ratio | $f_2 / f_1 = 2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$ | 1/3-octave spacing | | Narrowband Resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | FFT bin width | | Order Tracking Frequency | $f = \dfrac{\text{Order} \times \text{RPM}}{60}$ | Engine NVH: $f$ in Hz, RPM in rev/min | | Free-field Microphone Correction | $L_p^{\text{actual}} = L_p^{\text{meas}} + K$ | $K$ is the free-field correction at grazing incidence | | Background Noise Correction | $L_{\text{source}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{L_{\text{total}}/10} - 10^{L_{\text{bg}}/10}\right)$ | When $\Delta L \le 10$ dB | > [!NOTE] > **Why These Matter in Automotive Engineering:** > - **Order tracking** is the engineer's secret weapon: a 4th-order engine tone at 3000 RPM appears at exactly **$f = 4 \times 3000/60 = 200$ Hz**. > - The **background noise correction** is mandatory in ISO 362 pass-by tests; examiners often test this in 7-mark sub-parts. > - **Sound intensity** allows measuring the AC compressor's contribution while the engine is running — impossible with SPL alone. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Acoustic Pressure to Sound Pressure Level **Starting point:** Sound is a propagating pressure wave. The human ear responds not to absolute pressure but to the *fluctuating* component. Define the **root-mean-square (RMS) sound pressure**: $$ p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} p^2(t) \, dt} $$ **Step 1 — Define the reference pressure.** At 1 kHz, the threshold of human hearing is a pressure fluctuation of $p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa (i.e., $2 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m²). This is the universally adopted reference for airborne sound in air. **Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio.** Divide the measured RMS pressure by the reference: $$ R = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Bel scale.** One Bel corresponds to a 10:1 power ratio. Because acoustic power is proportional to $p^2$, the sound pressure level in Bels is: $$ L_{p,\text{Bels}} = \frac{1}{2}\log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_0^2}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Convert Bels to decibels.** Multiply by 10 (since 1 Bel = 10 dB): $$ L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB SPL]} $$ **Step 5 — Sanity check.** If $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa, then $L_p = 0$ dB SPL (threshold of hearing). A 10× increase in pressure gives a 20 dB rise. A 100× increase gives 40 dB. This is the **logarithmic compression** that makes the decibel scale practical over 12+ orders of magnitude of pressure. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Sound Intensity from the Two-Microphone Probe **Setup:** Two microphones A and B separated by a known small distance $\Delta r$ along the n-direction. The local pressure at their locations is $p_A(t)$ and $p_B(t)$. **Step 1 — Estimate the local pressure (mid-point average):** $$ \overline{p}(t) \approx \frac{p_A(t) + p_B(t)}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Estimate the particle velocity (pressure gradient):** Euler's linearized momentum equation relates the pressure gradient to particle acceleration: $$ \rho_0 \frac{\partial u_n}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} $$ In discrete form, the pressure gradient between the microphones is: $$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial n} \approx \frac{p_B - p_A}{\Delta r} $$ Integrating in time (assuming zero mean flow): $$ u_n(t) \approx -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \Delta r} \int \left[p_B(t) - p_A(t)\right] dt $$ **Step 3 — Compute instantaneous intensity:** $$ I_n(t) = \overline{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t) $$ **Step 4 — Time-average to obtain mean intensity:** $$ \overline{I_n} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \overline{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t) \, dt $$ **Step 5 — Convert to level:** $$ L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\overline{I_n}}{I_0}\right), \quad I_0 = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Sound intensity is a *vector* quantity. By scanning a probe across a surface enclosing a noisy component (e.g., an alternator), the integral of $\vec{I} \cdot d\vec{A}$ directly yields the **sound power** of that source without needing an anechoic chamber. This is the basis of **ISO 9614** and the standard method for in-situ automotive source ranking. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Background Noise Correction) **Problem:** A 14.7 dB(A) total SPL is measured at the driver’s ear with the HVAC blower ON. When the blower is turned OFF, the residual SPL is 12.0 dB(A). Find the SPL contribution of the HVAC blower alone. **Step 1 — Convert both levels to linear power ratios:** $$ L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{total}}}{P_0}\right) \Rightarrow P_{\text{total,lin}} = 10^{14.7/10} = 2.951 \times 10^{1} = 29.51 $$ $$ P_{\text{bg,lin}} = 10^{12.0/10} = 10^{1.2} = 15.85 $$ **Step 2 — Subtract the background contribution:** $$ P_{\text{HVAC,lin}} = P_{\text{total,lin}} - P_{\text{bg,lin}} = 29.51 - 15.85 = 13.66 $$ **Step 3 — Convert back to dB:** $$ L_{\text{HVAC}} = 10 \log_{10}(13.66) = 11.35 \, \text{dB(A)} $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check using decibel addition chart:** $\Delta L = 14.7 - 12.0 = 2.7$ dB. From the standard decibel addition table, a 2.7 dB addition corresponds to subtracting **0.65 dB** from the louder source. So $L_{\text{HVAC}} \approx 14.7 - 3.35 \approx 11.35$ dB(A). ✓ > [!NOTE] > **When the correction is invalid:** If $\Delta L \le 3$ dB, the source is essentially indistinguishable from background. ISO standards typically require $\Delta L \ge 6$ dB (preferably $\ge 10$ dB) for a valid measurement. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Microphone Field Types | Microphone Type | Calibration | Best Use | Automotive Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Free-field** | Calibrated at 0° incidence (face-on) | Anechoic / open environments | Pass-by noise on a test track | | **Pressure-field** | Calibrated for uniform pressure | Small cavities, coupler measurements | Exhaust tailpipe interior | | **Diffuse-field (Random-incidence)** | Calibrated for random angles | Reverberant interiors | Cabin noise inside the car | > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Using a free-field microphone *inside* a reverberant car cabin introduces errors up to **+6 dB** in the 1–4 kHz range. Always apply the manufacturer's **random-incidence correction** or use a diffuse-field mic for interior work. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Noise Measurement Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Acoustic Source
Engine, Tyre, Wind] --> B[Microphone
Transducer] B --> C[Preamplifier
Impedance Match] C --> D[Weighting Filter
A, C, or Z] D --> E[Time Averaging
Fast or Slow RMS] E --> F[Frequency Analyzer
Octave, 1/3-Oct, FFT] F --> G[Display / Recorder
PC, DAS, Scope] G --> H[Post-Processing
Order Tracking, Maps] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Sound Intensity Probe (Two-Microphone Method) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Microphone A
pA at position r] --> S1[Sum pA + pB / 2
Mean Pressure Estimator] P2[Microphone B
pB at position r + dr] --> S1 P2 --> S2[Gradient pB - pA / dr
Velocity Estimator] S1 --> M1[Multiplier] S2 --> INT[Integrator
Euler Equation] INT --> M1 M1 --> AVG[Time Average] AVG --> OUT[Sound Intensity In
W/m squared] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Acoustic Camera (Beamforming) Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR SA[Mic Array
64 to 256 channels] --> ADC[Multi-channel ADC
Synchronized Sampling] ADC --> BF[Digital Beamforming
Delay and Sum Algorithm] BF --> FFT[FFT / Cross-Spectrum] FFT --> MAP[Intensity Map
2D or 3D Grid] CAM[Video Camera
Reference View] --> OV[Overlay Engine] MAP --> OV OV --> DISP[Real-time Display
Hotspot Localisation] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Instrument ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What do you need
to measure?} Q1 -- Single Number dB --> SLM[Sound Level Meter
IEC 61672 Class 1 or 2] Q1 -- Frequency Content --> AN[Frequency Analyzer
Octave, 1/3 Oct, FFT] Q1 -- Source Power In-situ --> SI[Sound Intensity Probe
ISO 9614] Q1 -- Driver Perception --> HATS[Head and Torso Simulator
Binaural Recording] Q1 -- Localise Hotspot --> AC[Acoustic Camera
Beamforming Array] SLM --> W{Need Weighting?} W -- Yes --> A[A-weighting Standard
Automotive Default] W -- No --> Z[Z-weighting Linear
Objective Analysis] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define A-weighting in noise measurement. Why is it preferred over linear (Z) weighting for automotive pass-by noise tests?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A-weighting is a standardized frequency filter defined in **IEC 61672-1** that de-emphasizes very low (<500 Hz) and very high (>10 kHz) frequencies, approximating the 40-phon equal-loudness contour of the human ear at moderate sound levels. It is preferred for automotive pass-by noise (per **ISO 362**) because vehicle noise is largely low-frequency in nature, and A-weighting correlates well with the perceived annoyance to humans and with regulatory limits (e.g., 74 dB(A) for passenger cars). Linear Z-weighting, while objective, would over-represent inaudible sub-bass content and under-correlate with community annoyance. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Distinguish between Sound Pressure Level (SPL) and Sound Power Level (PWL). Which is independent of measurement distance and environment?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **SPL ($L_p$)** is the local logarithmic measure of fluctuating acoustic pressure at a specific point, expressed in dB re 20 µPa. It is a *field quantity* and depends on distance, geometry, and reflections. **PWL ($L_W$)** is the total acoustic energy radiated by a source per unit time, expressed in dB re 10⁻¹² W. It is a *source quantity* and is **independent of measurement distance and environment**, making it the fundamental descriptor of a noise source's emission. (3 marks) --- ### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Sound Level Meter** as per IEC 61672-1. Clearly state the role of the **microphone, weighting network, time-weighting (Fast/Slow), and detector**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two machines A and B, operating simultaneously, produce SPLs of 84 dB and 81 dB respectively at a workplace. Calculate the **combined SPL** when both run together. If machine A is switched off, by how many decibels does the SPL drop? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** The Sound Level Meter (SLM) is a precision instrument standardized by **IEC 61672-1 (2013)** for the measurement of sound pressure levels in the frequency range 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Its working principle, in sequential stages, is as follows: 1. **Microphone (Transducer):** A **condenser (capacitor) microphone** is preferred due to its flat frequency response, low noise, and high stability. The fluctuating acoustic pressure deflects the diaphragm, changing the capacitance, which is converted into a proportional AC voltage. (1 mark) 2. **Preamplifier:** The microphone output is in microvolts; the preamplifier provides high input impedance (to avoid loading the mic) and a flat gain over the audio band. (1 mark) 3. **Weighting Network:** The amplified signal is passed through a selectable filter — **A, C, or Z (linear)** — to model the human frequency response or to provide objective measurement. A-weighting is the default for most regulatory work. (1.5 marks) 4. **RMS Detector & Time Weighting:** The squared signal is averaged with one of three time constants: - **Fast (F):** τ = 125 ms - **Slow (S):** τ = 1000 ms - **Impulse (I):** τ = 35 ms rise / 1.5 s decay The output is the RMS voltage, which is converted to dB. (1.5 marks) 5. **Display & Calibration:** A logarithmic amplifier drives the display in dB. Periodic acoustic calibration using a **94 dB / 1 kHz sound level calibrator** is mandatory. (1 mark) 6. **Classes:** Per IEC 61672, SLMs are graded as **Class 1 (Precision, ±0.7 dB)** and **Class 2 (General, ±1.5 dB)**. (1 mark) --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Convert SPLs to linear power ratios.** $$ P_A = 10^{84/10} = 10^{8.4} = 2.512 \times 10^{8} $$ $$ P_B = 10^{81/10} = 10^{8.1} = 1.259 \times 10^{8} $$ **Step 2 — Sum the linear powers.** $$ P_{\text{total}} = P_A + P_B = 2.512 \times 10^{8} + 1.259 \times 10^{8} = 3.771 \times 10^{8} $$ **Step 3 — Convert back to dB.** $$ L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(3.771 \times 10^{8}) = 10 \left[\log_{10}(3.771) + 8\right] = 10 (0.5764 + 8) = 85.76 \, \text{dB} $$ **Combined SPL ≈ 85.76 dB** (rounded to 85.8 dB) (3 marks) **Step 4 — Find the drop when A is switched off.** When A is off, only B remains at 81 dB. $$ \Delta L = 85.76 - 81.00 = 4.76 \, \text{dB} $$ **SPL drops by approximately 4.76 dB** when machine A is switched off. (2 marks) **Step 5 — Verification using decibel addition chart.** $\Delta L = 84 - 81 = 3$ dB. From the standard table, adding a 3 dB-lower source increases the level by **+1.76 dB**. So $84 + 1.76 = 85.76$ dB. ✓ (2 marks) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary, Module 3] **(a)** Explain the **principle of sound intensity measurement** using a **two-microphone probe**. Derive the expression for the intensity component in the spacer direction. State **two advantages** of sound intensity measurement over sound pressure measurement in automotive NVH. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During an engine cold-start pass-by test on a chassis dynamometer, the following SPLs (linear, Z-weighted) are recorded: Total = 92.4 dB, Background (engine off) = 86.0 dB. Calculate the **engine-only SPL**. Comment on the validity of this measurement per ISO 362. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Principle of Two-Microphone Intensity Probe:** The probe consists of two phase-matched microphones A and B separated by a known, fixed spacer $\Delta r$ (typically 6, 12, or 50 mm) along the measurement axis *n*. Sound intensity is the time-averaged product of acoustic pressure and particle velocity in a given direction. (1 mark) **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Mean pressure (mid-point estimator):** $$ \overline{p}(t) = \frac{p_A(t) + p_B(t)}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Pressure gradient → particle velocity.** Euler's linearized equation: $\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial u_n}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n}$ Discretize the gradient: $$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial n} \approx \frac{p_B - p_A}{\Delta r} $$ Integrate to recover velocity: $$ u_n(t) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \Delta r}\int_0^t \left[p_B(\tau) - p_A(\tau)\right] d\tau $$ **Step 3 — Instantaneous intensity:** $$ I_n(t) = \overline{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t) $$ **Step 4 — Time-averaged intensity:** $$ \overline{I_n} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \overline{p}(t)\,u_n(t) \, dt \quad [\text{W/m}^2] $$ **Step 5 — Intensity level:** $$ L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\overline{I_n}}{10^{-12}}\right) \quad [\text{dB re 1 pW/m}^2] $$ (Full derivation — 4 marks) **Two Advantages of Sound Intensity over SPL Measurement in Automotive NVH:** 1. **Direct sound power measurement in non-ideal environments:** Intensity allows in-situ determination of sound power without an anechoic chamber or reverberant room — critical for assembled vehicles. (1 mark) 2. **Source ranking and localization:** Because intensity is a *vector*, a scan across a surface identifies the dominant radiating source (e.g., alternator vs. AC compressor) in the presence of background noise, which SPL cannot do. (1 mark) --- **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** **Given:** $L_{\text{total}} = 92.4$ dB(Z), $L_{\text{bg}} = 86.0$ dB(Z). **Step 1 — Convert to linear power ratios.** $$ P_{\text{total,lin}} = 10^{92.4/10} = 10^{9.24} = 1.738 \times 10^{9} $$ $$ P_{\text{bg,lin}} = 10^{86.0/10} = 10^{8.6} = 3.981 \times 10^{8} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract background.** $$ P_{\text{engine,lin}} = 1.738 \times 10^{9} - 3.981 \times 10^{8} = 1.340 \times 10^{9} $$ **Step 3 — Convert back to dB.** $$ L_{\text{engine}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.340 \times 10^{9}) = 10 (0.1271 + 9) = 91.27 \, \text{dB(Z)} $$ **Engine-only SPL ≈ 91.3 dB(Z)** (4 marks) **Step 4 — Validity Check per ISO 362:** $\Delta L = L_{\text{total}} - L_{\text{bg}} = 92.4 - 86.0 = 6.4$ dB. **ISO 362 requires a background-to-source level difference of at least 10 dB** for the measurement to be valid without correction, and at least 6 dB for a corrected measurement. Since $\Delta L = 6.4$ dB, the measurement **is acceptable only with the subtraction correction applied** (which we performed). If $\Delta L$ were below 3 dB, the result would be considered unreliable. (3 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Bel and decibel:** SPL formula has a factor of **20**; PWL/SIL formula has a factor of **10**. Markers deduct 1 mark instantly for the wrong coefficient. > 2. **Forgetting the correction factor in decibel subtraction:** Simply subtracting dB values (92.4 − 86.0 = 6.4) is a *common* but **wrong** method. You must convert to linear, subtract, then convert back. This is a 3-mark trap. > 3. **Using A-weighted values for engine-only isolation:** ISO 362 mandates that the background correction use the *same* weighting (typically A or Z) as the source measurement — mixing A and Z in the same calculation is a 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Stating "intensity" and "pressure" as interchangeable:** They are not. Pressure is a scalar at a point; intensity is a vector representing energy flux. This conceptual error costs 1 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Core formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/20\mu\text{Pa})$; $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/10^{-12}\text{W})$; $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/10^{-12}\text{W/m}^2)$. - **A-weighting** is the default for automotive regulatory work; rolls off bass and treble to mimic human hearing. - **IEC 61672-1** defines SLM classes: **Class 1 (Precision, ±0.7 dB)** vs **Class 2 (General, ±1.5 dB)**. - **Time weightings:** Fast = 125 ms, Slow = 1 s, Impulse = 35 ms / 1.5 s. - **Decibel addition** must be done in the linear (power) domain — never by direct subtraction of dB. - **Octave band ratio** is 2:1; **1/3-octave ratio** is $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$. - **Order tracking** relation: $f = (\text{Order} \times \text{RPM})/60$ — vital for engine NVH. - **Two-mic intensity probe** gives in-situ sound power and source ranking without anechoic chambers (governed by **ISO 9614**). - **HATS** (Head and Torso Simulator) is used for **driver-perceived** interior SQoS; uses binaural microphones at the ear entrance. - **Acoustic camera** (beamforming array) provides a 2D/3D "sound map" for localizing noise hotspots. - **Background noise correction:** Always convert to linear, subtract, convert back; valid only when $\Delta L \ge 3$ dB (preferably $\ge 6$–10 dB). - **Microphone field types:** Free-field (open), Pressure-field (cavity), Diffuse-field (reverberant) — mismatching causes errors up to +6 dB. - **Calibration:** Use a **94 dB / 1 kHz acoustic calibrator** before and after every measurement session; field calibration check is mandatory for ISO-compliant work.

topics.title: Microphones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microphones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **microphone** is an electroacoustic transducer that converts acoustic pressure fluctuations (sound waves) propagating through an elastic medium — typically air — into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage or current) proportional to the instantaneous sound pressure. In the context of **Automotive Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH)** engineering, a microphone serves as the primary **front-end sensor** in any sound measurement chain, capturing the acoustic signature of a vehicle subsystem (engine, exhaust, tires, HVAC, cabin) for objective analysis, regulatory compliance, and subjective quality assessment.

The international standard governing the design, calibration, and classification of measurement microphones is **IEC 61094**, which subdivides them into:
- **WS1 (Laboratory Standard Microphones):** Uncertainty $\leq \pm 0.2$ dB
- **WS2 (Working Standard Microphones):** Uncertainty $\leq \pm 0.5$ dB to $\pm 1$ dB
- **WS3 (Industrial Microphones):** Uncertainty $\leq \pm 1.5$ dB to $\pm 3$ dB

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In automotive NVH work, microphones are classified less by their working principle and more by their **acoustic environment** (free-field, pressure-field, diffuse-field) and **polarization type** (externally polarized vs. prepolarized / electret). The free-field type is the most common for pass-by noise and general vehicle exterior measurements, while pressure-field types are used for confined spaces such as inside the intake or cabin boundary microphones.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at a Concert"

Imagine you are standing in a noisy crowd trying to listen to a friend speaking softly. Your ear converts sound waves into nerve signals. Now imagine a tiny, ultra-precise **digital "translator"** placed at the same spot. That translator is a microphone.

- The **sound pressure wave** is the "foreign language" (mechanical vibration of air molecules).
- The microphone's **diaphragm** is the "eardrum" that vibrates sympathetically.
- The **electrical output** is the "translated sentence" (voltage) that any analyzer, recorder, or computer can read.

A good NVH microphone is like a translator who never **exaggerates loud sounds, under-represents soft ones, or adds his own accent** (i.e., distortion or self-noise). Engineers then "interview" the translator with calibration signals (pistonphones, sound level calibrators) to ensure the translation is faithful across all relevant frequencies.

## 1.3 Standardized Reference Quantities (KTU Mandate)

For every calculation in microphone-based NVH measurement, the following reference constants are mandatory:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Reference sound pressure | $P_0$ | **20 μPa** (20 × 10⁻⁶ Pa) | Pascal |
| Reference sensitivity (1 V/Pa) | $M_0$ | **1 V/Pa** | Volt per Pascal |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | Kilopascal |
| Speed of sound in air (20 °C) | $c$ | **343 m/s** | Metre per second |
| Air density (20 °C) | $\rho_0$ | **1.204 kg/m³** | kg per m³ |
| Standard temperature | $T_0$ | **293.15 K (20 °C)** | Kelvin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 20 μPa?** This is the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. It was selected by the scientific community in 1934 as a standardized reference, allowing any sound pressure to be expressed as a logarithmic **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** in decibels.

## 1.4 The Three Acoustic Environments

When placing a microphone, the engineer must always identify which acoustic field the microphone is operating in, because each type of microphone is *optimized* for one specific environment:

1. **Free Field** — Sound travels in one direction only, e.g., outdoors during a pass-by noise test. Requires a **free-field corrected** microphone (gridded).
2. **Pressure Field** — Sound pressure is uniform at the microphone position due to small cavity (e.g., inside a duct, wall-mount). Requires a **pressure-field** microphone.
3. **Diffuse Field** — Sound arrives equally from all directions (e.g., highly reverberant chamber). Requires a **random-incidence** microphone.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Shortcut:** A free-field microphone **overestimates** SPL in a pressure field. A pressure-field microphone **underestimates** SPL in a free field. Always check the microphone's data sheet for the **incidence angle correction curve** before drawing conclusions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Condenser (Capacitor) Microphone

The **measuring microphone of choice** in modern automotive NVH is the **prepolarized (electret) condenser microphone**. Its operation is rooted in the variable-capacitor principle.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. A thin, highly tensioned metallic or metallized polymer **diaphragm** is stretched at a precisely controlled distance (typically 20–50 μm) from a rigid **backplate**.
2. The diaphragm and backplate together form a parallel-plate **capacitor** with capacitance $C$.
3. A **polarization voltage** (either 200 V external or a permanently charged electret layer) maintains a static electric charge $Q$ across the capacitor.
4. When an acoustic wave impinges on the diaphragm, the diaphragm deflects by a small displacement $\xi(t)$, changing the plate separation to $d - \xi(t)$.
5. Because charge $Q$ is held nearly constant by the high input impedance of the preamplifier, the voltage across the capacitor changes according to:
$$V(t) = \frac{Q}{C(t)} = \frac{Q \, d(t)}{\varepsilon_0 \, A}$$
where $A$ is the effective diaphragm area and $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space.
6. This small AC voltage (typically mV) is fed to a **preamplifier** (often built into the microphone body) and then to a measurement system (DAQ, sound level meter, FFT analyzer).

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **High tension** on the diaphragm ensures a flat frequency response up to high frequencies (tens of kHz) by pushing the resonance well above the audio band.
- **High input impedance preamplifier** is mandatory, otherwise the charge leaks and the AC signal is lost.
- **Small diaphragm diameter** (1/2", 1/4", 1/8") improves high-frequency omnidirectionality and reduces acoustic disturbance of the field.

## 2.2 Key Performance Specifications

| Specification | Symbol | Definition | Typical Automotive NVH Range |
|---------------|--------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Sensitivity (Open-circuit)** | $M$ | Output RMS voltage per unit RMS sound pressure | 4–50 mV/Pa |
| **Frequency Response** | $f_{lo} - f_{hi}$ | Flatness over operating range | 20 Hz – 20 kHz (audio), up to 100 kHz (special) |
| **Dynamic Range** | $D$ | Max SPL – Self-Noise SPL | 100–160 dB |
| **Self-Noise (A-weighted)** | $L_{self}$ | Inherent electrical+thermal noise floor | 6–30 dB(A) |
| **Maximum SPL (THD ≤ 3%)** | $SPL_{max}$ | Upper limit before distortion | 130–146 dB |
| **Polar Pattern** | — | Directional sensitivity shape | Omnidirectional (measurement) |
| **Polarization Voltage** | $V_{pol}$ | External DC bias required | 0 V (prepolarized) or 200 V |
| **Diaphragm Diameter** | $\varnothing$ | Active element size | 1/2" (standard), 1/4" (high freq) |
| **Operating Temp Range** | $T$ | Environmental envelope | −40 °C to +150 °C (auto spec) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-priority. Memorize units and reference quantities along with each expression.**

### Table A — Core Conversion Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $SPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{rms}}{P_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB re 20 μPa |
| 2 | $M_{[dB]} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{M}{M_0}\right)$ | Sensitivity in dB (re 1 V/Pa) | dB re 1 V/Pa |
| 3 | $P_{rms} = P_0 \times 10^{\,SPL/20}$ | Convert SPL → pressure | Pa |
| 4 | $V_{out} = M \times P_{rms}$ | Microphone electrical output | V (RMS) |
| 5 | $L_{out[dB]} = SPL + M_{[dB]}$ | Output level in dBV (when $M_0 = 1$ V/Pa) | dBV |
| 6 | $P_2 = P_1 \times \dfrac{r_1}{r_2}$ | Inverse-square law (free field) | Pa |
| 7 | $SPL_2 = SPL_1 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | SPL loss with distance | dB |
| 8 | $L_W = SPL + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 11$ | Sound Power Level (hemisphere) | dB re 1 pW |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | Farads |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial t}$ | Condenser microphone output | V/s |

> **Notation note:** The vertical bar symbol used inside the formulas above is rendered as $\vert$ for absolute value to maintain markdown table compatibility. In handwriting, it is conventional to write as $\mid$.

### Table B — Free-Field vs Pressure-Field vs Random-Incidence Corrections

| Field Type | Incidence Angle | Microphone Design | Typical Use in Auto NVH |
|------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Free-field | $0^\circ$ (axial) | Flat response at $0^\circ$ | Pass-by noise, exterior, road noise |
| Pressure-field | All angles (uniform) | Flat response when flush-mounted | Duct measurement, wall panel |
| Random-incidence | All angles averaged | Flat average response | Reverberant chamber, rain noise |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an automotive OEM NVH lab, the microphone is the **"eyes and ears"** of:

- **Pass-By Noise (PBN) testing** per ISO 362-1 — uses a free-field microphone at $7.5 \pm 0.05$ m from the vehicle centerline.
- **Interior cabin noise** measurement per ISO 5128 — uses binaural or artificial head setups.
- **Exhaust tailpipe noise** — uses a free-field microphone at 0.5 m, 45° from the tailpipe axis.
- **Component-level air-borne noise** (e.g., HVAC blower) — anechoic chamber with traverse or fixed microphone.
- **Sound quality metrics** (Zwicker loudness, sharpness, roughness) — require time-domain microphone signals sampled at $\geq 48$ kHz.
- **Beamforming arrays** — large 2D arrays of MEMS microphones for source localization on running vehicles.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern automotive NVH increasingly uses **MEMS microphone arrays** because they are small, low-cost, phase-matched, and can be flush-mounted on vehicle surfaces — but they trade off low-frequency sensitivity and dynamic range compared to precision condenser types.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sound Pressure Level (SPL) From RMS Pressure

### Given
- Measured RMS acoustic pressure at the microphone diaphragm: $P_{rms}$ in **Pa**
- Standard reference pressure: $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa

### To Derive
The expression for Sound Pressure Level $SPL$ in decibels.

### Step-by-Step Development

**Step 1 — Definition of the decibel as a logarithmic power ratio:**
The decibel is defined for two powers $W_1$ and $W_2$ as
$$N_{[dB]} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Relate acoustic intensity to acoustic pressure:**
For a plane progressive wave in a free field, acoustic intensity $I$ is proportional to the square of the RMS pressure:
$$I \propto P_{rms}^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the square-law proportionality into the decibel definition:**
Because the ratio of intensities equals the ratio of pressure squares,
$$SPL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{rms}^{\,2}}{P_0^{\,2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithm power rule $\log_{10}(x^2) = 2 \log_{10}(x)$:**
$$SPL = 10 \times 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the coefficient:**
$$\boxed{\,SPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0}\right)\,}$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. Note the factor is **20** (not 10) because the quantity being measured is a *field quantity* (pressure) rather than an *energy quantity* (power).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Microphone Output Voltage in dBV From a Given SPL

### Given
- A microphone with nominal sensitivity $M = 12\ \text{mV/Pa} = 12 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/Pa}$
- Incident SPL = 94 dB (a common calibration level, corresponding to 1 Pa RMS)

### To Derive
The expected RMS output voltage and the output level in dBV.

### Step-by-Step Development

**Step 1 — Convert SPL to RMS pressure using the inverse of the SPL formula:**
From $SPL = 20 \log_{10}(P_{rms}/P_0)$, solving for $P_{rms}$,
$$P_{rms} = P_0 \times 10^{SPL/20}$$
Substitute the calibration SPL of 94 dB:
$$P_{rms} = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10^{94/20}$$
Compute the exponent: $94/20 = 4.7$, so
$$P_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{4.7}$$
Since $10^{4.7} = 10^{0.7} \times 10^{4} \approx 5.012 \times 10^{4}$,
$$P_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.012 \times 10^{4}$$
$$P_{rms} = 1.0024\ \text{Pa} \approx 1\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply pressure by sensitivity to obtain RMS output voltage:**
$$V_{out} = M \times P_{rms}$$
$$V_{out} = (12 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/Pa}) \times (1.0024\ \text{Pa})$$
$$V_{out} = 12.029 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 12.0\ \text{mV (RMS)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert RMS voltage to dBV (decibels relative to 1 V):**
$$L_{V} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}}\right), \quad V_{ref} = 1\ \text{V}$$
$$L_{V} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12.03 \times 10^{-3}}{1}\right)$$
$$L_{V} = 20 \times \log_{10}(0.01203)$$
$$\log_{10}(0.01203) = \log_{10}(1.203 \times 10^{-2}) = -2 + 0.0803 = -1.9197$$
$$L_{V} = 20 \times (-1.9197) = -38.39\ \text{dBV}$$

**Step 4 — Convert dBV to dBu (referenced to 0.775 V, the pro-audio standard):**
$$L_{dBu} = L_{dBV} + 2.21$$
$$L_{dBu} = -38.39 + 2.21 = -36.18\ \text{dBu}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check by adding microphone sensitivity (in dB) to SPL:**
The microphone sensitivity in dB re 1 V/Pa is
$$M_{[dB]} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{1}\right) = -38.42\ \text{dB re 1 V/Pa}$$
Then,
$$L_{V} = SPL + M_{[dB]} = 94 + (-38.42) = 55.58\ \text{dB SPL} - 38.42\ \text{dBV equivalent?}$$
> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** $SPL + M_{[dB]}$ yields the output in dB re 1 V/Pa referenced to 1 Pa — *not* dBV. To compare with 1 V, the student must remember the **sensitivity is referenced to 1 Pa**, not to 1 V. The relationship is
> $$L_{out[dBV]} = SPL - 94 + M_{[dB]}$$
> because 94 dB SPL = 1 Pa. Substituting: $94 - 94 + (-38.42) = -38.42$ dBV, which matches our Step 3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse-Square Law and Distance Compensation

### Given
A point source radiates spherically in free space. The SPL at $r_1 = 1$ m is measured to be $SPL_1 = 80$ dB.

### To Find
The SPL at $r_2 = 4$ m, and the microphone output voltage at that location for a 50 mV/Pa microphone.

### Step-by-Step Development

**Step 1 — Write the inverse-square law for RMS pressure:**
In a free, spherical field, the RMS pressure decays with distance as
$$P(r) \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert the SPL difference:**
$$SPL_2 - SPL_1 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$
Substituting $r_1 = 1$ m and $r_2 = 4$ m,
$$SPL_2 - SPL_1 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 20 \times (-0.6021) = -12.04\ \text{dB}$$
$$\boxed{\,SPL_2 = 80 - 12.04 = 67.96 \approx 68\ \text{dB SPL}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Convert 68 dB SPL to RMS pressure:**
$$P_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{68/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3.4}$$
With $10^{3.4} \approx 2512$,
$$P_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 2512 = 5.02 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{Pa} = 50.2\ \text{mPa}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate microphone output voltage:**
$$V_{out} = M \times P_{rms} = (50 \times 10^{-3}) \times (50.2 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.51\ \text{mV (RMS)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Calibration Check

A field engineer performs a daily calibration check on a 1/2" microphone using a **Class 1 sound level calibrator** emitting **94 dB SPL at 1 kHz**. The microphone data sheet specifies sensitivity $M = 11.8\ \text{mV/Pa}$, nominal. The DAQ system reads **11.65 mV RMS** at the calibrator.

### Step-by-Step Development

**Step 1 — Expected output at calibration level (94 dB = 1 Pa):**
$$V_{expected} = 11.8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/Pa} \times 1\ \text{Pa} = 11.80\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Measured output:**
$$V_{measured} = 11.65\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Deviation in decibels:**
$$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{measured}}{V_{expected}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11.65}{11.80}\right)$$
$$\frac{11.65}{11.80} = 0.9873, \quad \log_{10}(0.9873) = -0.00557$$
$$\Delta L = 20 \times (-0.00557) = -0.111\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
$$\vert \Delta L \vert = 0.111\ \text{dB} \leq 0.5\ \text{dB (typical acceptance criterion for Class 1)}$$
**Result:** Microphone passes the daily check.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** Always express deviations in dB, not in linear voltage error. A "1% error" in voltage is only **0.086 dB**, but a 10% voltage error is already **0.915 dB**. Most NVH standards require $\leq \pm 0.5$ dB, equivalent to a voltage ratio between **0.944 and 1.059**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Microphone Selection Decision Tree

| Application Scenario | Recommended Microphone Type | Polarization | Why |
|----------------------|----------------------------|--------------|-----|
| Pass-by noise (ISO 362) | Free-field, 1/2" | Prepolarized | Flat at $0^\circ$, weather-resistant |
| Cabin noise (driver's ear) | Free-field, binaural | Prepolarized | Captures spatial perception |
| Tailpipe exhaust | Free-field, high-SPL | Prepolarized | Handles 140+ dB peaks |
| HVAC duct (in-duct) | Pressure-field | External 200 V | Flush mount, uniform pressure |
| Wind tunnel / aero-acoustics | Free-field, 1/4" | Prepolarized | Small size, high frequency |
| Reverberation chamber | Random-incidence | Prepolarized | Diffuse field averaging |
| Engine bay mapping | Array of MEMS | Built-in | Phased matched, small form factor |
| Tire-road noise (CPX) | Free-field, near tire | Prepolarized | Co-located with CPX trailer mics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microphone Functional Block Diagram

The diagram below traces the complete signal chain from acoustic input to analyzer output, including polarization and calibration paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[ACOUSTIC PRESSURE WAVE] --> dp1[DIAPHRAGM MECHANICAL DEFLECTION]
    dp1 --> cap1[VARIABLE CAPACITANCE C d]
    cap1 --> pol1[POLARIZATION VOLTAGE 200V OR ELECTRET]
    pol1 --> pre1[CHARGE PREAMPLIFIER HIGH Z]
    pre1 --> amp1[MAIN AMPLIFIER]
    amp1 --> out1[DAQ OR FFT ANALYZER]
    cal1[CALIBRATOR 94DB 1KHZ] -.-> dp1
    ws1[WINDSCREEN AND DEAD CAT] -.-> dp1
    src1 --> ws1
```

## 4.2 Classification of Measurement Microphones Used in Automotive NVH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mm[MEASUREMENT MICROPHONE] --> t1[BY TRANSDUCTION PRINCIPLE]
    mm --> t2[BY ACOUSTIC FIELD]
    mm --> t3[BY POLARIZATION]
    t1 --> cd1[CAPACITIVE CONDENSER]
    t1 --> dy1[ELECTRODYNAMIC MOVING COIL]
    t1 --> pe1[PIEZOELECTRIC]
    t1 --> me1[MEMS MICROELECTROMECHANICAL]
    t2 --> ff1[FREE FIELD AXIAL]
    t2 --> pf1[PRESSURE FIELD FLUSH]
    t2 --> ri1[RANDOM INCIDENCE DIFFUSE]
    t3 --> pp1[PREPOLARIZED 0V ELECTRET]
    t3 --> ep1[EXTERNALLY POLARIZED 200V]
    cd1 --> std1[WS1 LAB STANDARD]
    cd1 --> std2[WS2 WORKING STANDARD]
    cd1 --> std3[WS3 INDUSTRIAL]
```

## 4.3 Polar Pattern Comparison (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    omni[OMNIDIRECTIONAL 0DB AT ALL ANGLES] --> use1[USED FOR MEASUREMENT MICROPHONES]
    card[CARDIOID NULL AT 180 DEG] --> use2[USED FOR STUDIO AND STAGE NOT NVH]
    shot[SHOTGUN HIGHLY DIRECTIONAL] --> use3[USED FOR FILM AND FIELD]
    usemmm[OMNI IS THE KTU RECOMMENDED CHOICE]
    use1 --> usemmm
```

## 4.4 Calibration and Verification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[INITIATE CALIBRATION SEQUENCE] --> check[CHECK TEMPERATURE AND HUMIDITY]
    check --> mount[MOUNT CALIBRATOR ON MIC]
    mount --> apply[APPLY 94 DB 1 KHZ REFERENCE]
    apply --> read[READ OUTPUT VOLTAGE]
    read --> comp[COMPUTE DEVIATION IN DB]
    comp --> decide{DEVIATION LESS THAN 0.5 DB}
    decide -- YES --> accept[ACCEPT MICROPHONE FOR USE]
    decide -- NO --> reject[SEND FOR RECALIBRATION OR SERVICE]
    accept --> field[PROCEED TO MEASUREMENT]
    reject --> vendor[RETURN TO CERTIFIED VENDOR]
```

## 4.5 Frequency Response Curve (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    flow[FREQUENCY AXIS LOG SCALE] --> a[20 HZ]
    a --> b[100 HZ]
    b --> c[1 KHZ REFERENCE]
    c --> d[10 KHZ]
    d --> e[20 KHZ]
    f[DB AXIS] --> g[MINUS 6 DB PER OCTAVE LOW FREQ SLOPE]
    g --> h[FLAT REGION PLUS MINUS 1 DB]
    h --> i[RESONANCE PEAK AT 12 TO 15 KHZ]
    i --> j[ROLLOFF ABOVE 20 KHZ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the flowchart above:** The flat region in the middle corresponds to the microphone's usable bandwidth. The resonance peak is engineered to occur at the upper edge of the audible band so that the *pressure* response (not the *random-incidence* response) remains flat. The low-frequency roll-off depends on the preamplifier's input impedance and the microphone's static sensitivity — designers choose the vented or sealed back-chamber to set this corner frequency.
