# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]

**Q1.** Define the term **sensitivity of a measurement microphone** and give its typical units. What is the numerical relationship between sensitivity expressed in **mV/Pa** and that expressed in **dB re 1 V/Pa**?

**Model Answer:**

The **sensitivity** $M$ of a measurement microphone is the open-circuit RMS output voltage produced per unit RMS sound pressure at a reference frequency (usually 1 kHz). It is formally defined as
$$M = \frac{V_{out}}{P_{rms}} \quad \text{(V/Pa)}$$

Typical units are **mV/Pa** (e.g., 12 mV/Pa). The conversion to dB form uses
$$M_{[dB]} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{M \ [\text{in V/Pa}]}{1\ \text{V/Pa}}\right)$$

For example, 12 mV/Pa = $1.2 \times 10^{-2}$ V/Pa, and
$$M_{[dB]} = 20 \log_{10}(1.2 \times 10^{-2}) = -38.4\ \text{dB re 1 V/Pa}.$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q2.** Differentiate between a **free-field** and a **pressure-field** microphone. State one specific automotive NVH measurement scenario where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Free-field microphone | Pressure-field microphone |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Designed to be flat at $0^\circ$ axial incidence | Yes | No |
| Designed to be flat for uniform pressure at diaphragm | No | Yes |
| Typical use | Open space, anechoic chamber | Flush-mounted in walls, ducts |
| Example in auto NVH | Pass-by noise at 7.5 m | In-duct HVAC noise measurement |
| Correction | Free-field correction applied | Pressure-field correction applied |

In short, free-field microphones are used **outdoors or in reflective-free conditions**, while pressure-field microphones are used **in enclosed cavities or when flush-mounted on a boundary**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEMUT863]

**Q(a) [7 Marks].** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a prepolarized condenser microphone** used for automotive NVH measurements. List **four** key specifications that govern its selection for a pass-by noise test.

**Model Answer:**

A prepolarized condenser microphone operates on the principle of **variable capacitance**.

**Block diagram and operating principle:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sw[INCIDENT SOUND WAVE] --> dp[DIAPHRAGM TENSIONED MEMBRANE]
    dp --> gap[AIR GAP d APPROX 20 UM]
    gap --> bp[BACKPLATE RIGID]
    bp --> el[ELECTRET LAYER PERMANENT CHARGE]
    el --> cap[CAPACITANCE C VARIES WITH PRESSURE]
    cap --> pa[CHARGE PREAMPLIFIER HIGH INPUT Z]
    pa --> amp[MAIN AMPLIFIER AND BUFFER]
    amp --> sig[ELECTRICAL OUTPUT TO DAQ]
```

**Operating steps:**
1. The diaphragm and backplate form a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$.
2. A permanently charged electret layer maintains a static charge $Q$ on the plates.
3. The incident acoustic pressure deflects the diaphragm by $\xi(t)$, altering the gap to $d - \xi(t)$.
4. The capacitance changes; with $Q$ approximately constant, the voltage $V = Q / C$ varies in proportion to pressure.
5. The high-impedance preamplifier prevents charge leakage and outputs a voltage signal proportional to the acoustic pressure.

**Four key specifications for pass-by noise selection:**

1. **Sensitivity** $\geq 10\ \text{mV/Pa}$ for adequate signal-to-noise ratio.
2. **Frequency response** flat from 20 Hz to at least 16 kHz (per ISO 362-1).
3. **Dynamic range** covering at least 50–120 dB SPL (to handle ambient to peak pass-by levels).
4. **Polar pattern** must be **omnidirectional** with random-incidence correction $\leq \pm 1$ dB between $0^\circ$ and $90^\circ$.

**Q(b) [7 Marks].** A measurement microphone has a sensitivity of **25 mV/Pa**. The DAQ system connected to it has an input range of $\pm 5\ \text{V}$. Calculate the **maximum SPL** that can be measured without clipping the DAQ input. If a vehicle pass-by yields an SPL of **88 dB** at the microphone, what is the RMS output voltage?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum allowable RMS voltage at the DAQ input:**
For a sine wave, peak voltage $V_p = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$, so a $\pm 5$ V range corresponds to a peak of 5 V, hence
$$V_{rms,\,max} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} = 3.536\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum measurable RMS pressure:**
$$P_{rms,\,max} = \frac{V_{rms,\,max}}{M} = \frac{3.536}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 141.4\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Convert this pressure to SPL:**
$$SPL_{max} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{141.4}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$
$$\frac{141.4}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.07 \times 10^{6}$$
$$SPL_{max} = 20 \log_{10}(7.07 \times 10^{6}) = 20 \times (6 + 0.8494) = 20 \times 6.8494 = 136.99 \approx 137\ \text{dB SPL}$$

**Step 4 — RMS pressure at 88 dB SPL:**
$$P_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{88/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{4.4}$$
With $10^{4.4} \approx 2.512 \times 10^{4}$,
$$P_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.512 \times 10^{4} = 0.5024\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5 — RMS output voltage:**
$$V_{out} = M \times P_{rms} = (25 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5024 = 12.56\ \text{mV (RMS)}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Maximum measurable SPL: $\approx 137$ dB
- Output voltage at 88 dB SPL: $\approx 12.56$ mV RMS

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEMUT863]

**Q(a) [7 Marks].** Explain the **inverse-square law** for a point source in free field. A vehicle's engine exhaust produces a sound pressure level of **92 dB at 1 m**. Calculate the SPL at distances of **2 m, 4 m, and 8 m** from the tailpipe. Comment on the practical significance of this in **pass-by noise measurement**.

**Model Answer:**

The **inverse-square law** states that in a free, spherical acoustic field, the RMS sound pressure is inversely proportional to the distance from a point source:
$$P(r) \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2}$$

In decibels, this becomes
$$SPL_2 = SPL_1 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

**Step-by-step calculations:**

**At r = 2 m:**
$$SPL_2 = 92 - 20 \log_{10}(2/1) = 92 - 20 \times 0.3010 = 92 - 6.02 = 85.98 \approx 86\ \text{dB}$$

**At r = 4 m:**
$$SPL_4 = 92 - 20 \log_{10}(4/1) = 92 - 20 \times 0.6021 = 92 - 12.04 = 79.96 \approx 80\ \text{dB}$$

**At r = 8 m:**
$$SPL_8 = 92 - 20 \log_{10}(8/1) = 92 - 20 \times 0.9031 = 92 - 18.06 = 73.94 \approx 74\ \text{dB}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Distance (m) | SPL (dB) | Drop from 1 m (dB) |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | 92.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 86.0 | 6.0 |
| 4 | 80.0 | 12.0 |
| 8 | 74.0 | 18.0 |

> **Pattern observed:** SPL drops by **6 dB per doubling of distance**, which is the canonical signature of a point source in a free field.

**Practical significance in pass-by noise measurement:**
- The ISO 362-1 standard mandates a microphone at **7.5 m** from the vehicle centerline precisely to obtain a representative free-field measurement.
- Engineers must ensure the measurement microphone is in the **acoustic far field**, where the inverse-square law holds (typically $r \geq 3 \times$ largest source dimension).
- Reflections from the ground, barriers, or vehicle body can break the inverse-square law, so the test track must be open and free of large reflective objects within ~30 m of the source.

**Q(b) [7 Marks].** Describe with sketches the difference between **free-field**, **pressure-field**, and **random-incidence** microphone orientations. Why is it important to choose the correct type for a given measurement?

**Model Answer:**

| Field Type | Sketch Description | Design Goal | Typical Use |
|------------|--------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Free-field** | Microphone pointed toward source, $0^\circ$ incidence | Flat amplitude response when sound arrives axially | Outdoor testing, anechoic chamber, pass-by |
| **Pressure-field** | Microphone flush with a surface (e.g., wall or duct) | Flat response when the pressure at the diaphragm is uniform | Duct measurements, panel transmission loss |
| **Random-incidence** | Microphone in a highly reverberant environment | Flat *spatially-averaged* response over all angles | Reverberation chamber, rain noise testing |

**Free-field microphone sketch (conceptual):**
A sound source (e.g., speaker) is shown in front of the microphone at distance $r$. The microphone diaphragm faces the source. The pressure wave arrives at $0^\circ$ incidence. The microphone's high-frequency response is tailored so that the pressure build-up at the diaphragm (due to the diaphragm's own acoustic impedance) compensates for any loss, yielding a flat on-axis response.

**Pressure-field microphone sketch (conceptual):**
The microphone is mounted flush with a wall (or inside a small cavity). Sound pressure is essentially uniform at the diaphragm because the microphone does not disturb the field. The diaphragm is designed without free-field correction, so its response is flat in this configuration.

**Random-incidence microphone sketch (conceptual):**
The microphone is placed in a reverberant chamber where sound arrives with equal probability from every direction. The diaphragm is designed to provide a flat *spatial average* response, even though the on-axis response may be slightly different from the free-field type.

**Importance of correct selection:**
1. **Measurement accuracy** — Using a free-field mic in a pressure field causes up to 3 dB error at high frequencies.
2. **Standard compliance** — ISO 362-1 explicitly requires a free-field microphone at $7.5$ m; using the wrong type invalidates the test.
3. **Cross-correlation of data** — Different microphone types cannot be directly compared without applying incidence-angle corrections.
4. **Source localization** — Beamforming arrays require phase-matched, omnidirectional microphones; the wrong type introduces phase artifacts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing dB formulas:** Students often use the 10·log form for pressure. The 20·log form is mandatory for field quantities (pressure, voltage, acceleration).
> 2. **Forgetting the −94 dB offset** when adding microphone sensitivity (in dB re 1 V/Pa) to SPL. Always subtract 94 dB to convert from re 1 Pa to re 1 V.
> 3. **Confusing free-field with pressure-field** in duct or wall measurements, leading to inflated high-frequency SPL readings.
> 4. **Forgetting units** of sensitivity (mV/Pa vs. V/Pa) in numerical problems, producing answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 5. **Inverse-square law applied in reflective environments** — it is valid only in the *free field*; in a semi-reverberant space the roll-off is 3 dB per doubling of distance, not 6 dB.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print-and-pin revision card — read this on the morning of the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** A microphone is an **electroacoustic transducer** converting pressure waves to voltage.
- [ ] **KTU favourite types in NVH:** **Prepolarized condenser** (most common), **MEMS array** (modern mapping), and **piezoelectric** (high-temperature engine bay).
- [ ] **Reference pressure** $P_0$ = **20 μPa** (always, no exceptions).
- [ ] **SPL formula** uses factor **20**, not 10, because pressure is a field quantity.
- [ ] **Sensitivity in dB** is referenced to **1 V/Pa**, but in NVH equations the standard test pressure of **94 dB SPL = 1 Pa** is the bridge.
- [ ] **Field types:** Free-field, pressure-field, random-incidence. Each mic is optimized for one.
- [ ] **Polarization:** Modern mics are **prepolarized (0 V)** for simplicity and ICP/IEPE compatibility; older standard mics need **200 V external** bias.
- [ ] **Inverse-square law:** SPL drops **6 dB per doubling of distance** from a point source in free field.
- [ ] **Daily calibration** must be done with a **Class 1 calibrator at 94 dB / 1 kHz**, with deviation $\leq 0.5$ dB for a passing check.
- [ ] **Microphone output equation:** $V_{out} = M \times P_{rms}$; remember to convert SPL to $P_{rms}$ *before* multiplying.
- [ ] **PBN standard distance:** **7.5 m** (per ISO 362-1).
- [ ] **Microphone diameter** — 1/2" is the auto NVH industry default; 1/4" is used for **high-frequency (aero-acoustic) testing**.
- [ ] **Windscreen and windshield correction** is mandatory for any outdoor measurement above 5 m/s wind speed.
- [ ] **Self-noise** in dB(A) sets the *lower limit* of the dynamic range; never operate the DAQ gain such that the mic's self-noise is not at least 10 dB above the DAQ's own noise floor.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 frequently asked derivations:** (i) SPL from $P_{rms}$, (ii) SPL vs distance via inverse square, (iii) microphone output in dBV, (iv) free-field to pressure-field correction.

> **End of Module 3 Microphones — KTU 2024 Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound Intensity Probes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sound Intensity Probes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sound Intensity Probe** is a precision acoustic transducer assembly that directly measures the *time-averaged sound intensity vector* — the net flow of acoustic energy per unit area per unit time — by simultaneously capturing sound pressure at two closely spaced locations using a pair of phase-matched microphones. Unlike a single microphone that measures only sound pressure level (SPL), a sound intensity probe resolves both the **magnitude** and **direction** of acoustic energy flux, enabling noise source identification, sound power determination, and transmission loss evaluation without requiring a specialized anechoic environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT863 / Module 3):**
> Sound Intensity Probes are categorized under *Sound Intensity Measurement Techniques* and form the bridge between classical acoustic pressure measurements and modern vibration-based NVH diagnostics in automotive engineering.

### 1.2 Working Principle — One-Line Summary

> The probe uses the **finite-difference approximation** of the acoustic pressure gradient between two microphones to reconstruct the **particle velocity** in real time, then multiplies it point-by-point with the **mean pressure** to obtain the instantaneous acoustic intensity vector.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two fishing boats floating side by side on a flowing river. Each boat has a thermometer reading the **water temperature** at its own spot. If the river flows from North to South, the *upstream* boat will record a slightly **higher water level** than the *downstream* boat, because water is "piling up" against the upstream side. By comparing the two readings and knowing the distance between the boats, the fishermen can figure out **how fast** the river is flowing and **which direction** it is going — *without ever throwing in a current meter*.

A **sound intensity probe** does the same thing with sound waves:

- The two microphones are the two boats.
- The "water level difference" is the **pressure gradient** between them.
- The "river speed" is the **acoustic particle velocity**.
- The **direction** is automatically encoded in the sign of the pressure difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Automotive Use Case:**
> In a running vehicle, sound radiates from the *engine*, *exhaust*, *tires*, and *wind*. A sound intensity probe swept over the vehicle body can pinpoint the dominant noise source within a few decibels of accuracy — even inside a normal workshop, not an anechoic chamber.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following constants are essential for sound intensity probe calibration and data reduction:

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Air, 20 °C) |
|---|---|---|
| Density of air | $\rho_0$ | **1.204 kg/m³** |
| Speed of sound | $c$ | **343 m/s** |
| Character acoustic impedance | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ | **413 Pa·s/m** |
| Reference intensity | $I_{ref}$ | **10⁻¹² W/m²** |
| Reference pressure | $p_{ref}$ | **20 µPa** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic

The probe operates in **three discrete stages**:

**Stage 1 — Pressure Acquisition**
Two phase-matched microphones (free-field type, ½-inch) are mounted nose-to-nose with a precisely known **spacer distance** $\Delta r$ (typically 6 mm, 12 mm, or 50 mm depending on frequency range).

**Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning**
- Microphone #1 output: $p_1(t)$
- Microphone #2 output: $p_2(t)$
- Mean pressure: $\bar{p}(t) = \dfrac{p_1(t) + p_2(t)}{2}$
- Pressure gradient estimate: $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} \approx \dfrac{p_2(t) - p_1(t)}{\Delta r}$

**Stage 3 — Intensity Reconstruction**
Using Euler's linearized momentum equation:

$$u_n(t) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0} \int \frac{\partial p}{\partial n}\, dt$$

The instantaneous intensity in the probe axis direction is:

$$I_n(t) = \bar{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t)$$

Averaging over time $T$ yields the **time-averaged intensity** (the measured quantity).

### 2.2 Why Two Microphones? — The "Why" Explained

A single microphone only captures pressure oscillations — a *scalar* quantity. To know *where* the sound is going, we must know the **particle velocity vector** (a *vector*). Newton's second law applied to a fluid parcel links pressure gradient directly to particle acceleration:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

This is the linearized **Euler equation**. By measuring $\nabla p$ with two microphones and integrating in time, we recover $u$ — without ever touching the air mechanically. Hence the name: **indirect measurement of particle velocity via pressure gradient**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> This is one of the rare cases in experimental physics where a *vector field* (acoustic velocity) is reconstructed from two *scalar measurements* (pressures at two points), making the technique elegant, non-intrusive, and field-deployable.

### 2.3 Frequency Domain — The Cross-Spectral Method

In practice, modern sound intensity analyzers (e.g., Brüel & Kjær Type 2270-S, G.R.A.S. 50AI) compute intensity in the **frequency domain** using the **cross-spectrum** $G_{12}(f)$ between the two microphone signals. The **KTU standard formulation** is:

$$I_n(f) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r} \, \mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}(f)\}$$

where:

- $G_{12}(f) = S_{p_1 p_2}(f)$ is the one-sided cross-power spectral density.
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency.
- $\mathrm{Im}\{\cdot\}$ denotes the **imaginary part** (which carries the active/reactive intensity information).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_n(t) = \bar{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t)$ | Instantaneous acoustic intensity along probe axis | W/m² |
| 2 | $\bar{p}(t) = \dfrac{p_1(t) + p_2(t)}{2}$ | Mean pressure between two microphones | Pa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} \approx \dfrac{p_2 - p_1}{\Delta r}$ | Finite-difference pressure gradient | Pa/m |
| 4 | $u_n(t) = -\dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n}\, dt$ | Particle velocity via Euler integration | m/s |
| 5 | $\langle I_n \rangle = \dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_0^T \bar{p}(t)\, u_n(t)\, dt$ | Time-averaged intensity | W/m² |
| 6 | $I_n(f) = -\dfrac{\mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}(f)\}}{\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r}$ | Cross-spectral (frequency-domain) intensity | W/m² |
| 7 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert I \vert}{I_{ref}}\right)$ | Sound intensity level (SIL) | dB re 1 pW/m² |
| 8 | $\Delta f_{upper} \approx \dfrac{c}{2\Delta r}$ | Upper usable frequency (spatial aliasing limit) | Hz |
| 9 | $\Delta f_{lower} \approx \dfrac{c \, \Delta r}{200}$ | Lower usable frequency (finite-diff error limit) | Hz |
| 10 | $W = \oint_S I_n \, dS$ | Sound power from surface integration of intensity | W |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:**
> In Equation #6, the minus sign is *crucial*. Many students drop it; doing so reverses the sign convention of intensity (positive becomes "sound flowing *into* the surface" instead of "out"). Memorize the derivation — do not memorize the formula blindly.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application Sector | Specific Use |
|---|---|
| **Automotive NVH** | Locating engine, exhaust, tire, and wind-noise sources on a running vehicle |
| **HVAC / Building Acoustics** | Identifying flanking paths and duct leakage without shutting down equipment |
| **Industrial Machinery** | In-situ sound power measurement per ISO 9614 (replaces costly lab tests) |
| **Aerospace** | Cabin noise source identification in operational aircraft |
| **Environmental Noise** | Locating offending noise sources in complex urban environments |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Particle Velocity from Pressure Gradient (Time Domain)

**Starting Point:** Linearized Euler equation (momentum conservation for a fluid parcel in an acoustic field)

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u_n}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial n}$$

**Step 1:** Solve for the time derivative of particle velocity:

$$\frac{\partial u_n}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{\rho_0} \frac{\partial p}{\partial n}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides with respect to time, $t$:

$$u_n(t) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0} \int_{-\infty}^{t} \frac{\partial p(\tau)}{\partial n}\, d\tau + C$$

**Step 3:** Assume the fluid was at rest before the acoustic event ($u_n(-\infty) = 0$), so the integration constant $C = 0$:

$$u_n(t) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0} \int_{-\infty}^{t} \frac{\partial p(\tau)}{\partial n}\, d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Replace the spatial pressure gradient with the **finite-difference approximation** using the two-microphone geometry:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} \approx \frac{p_2(t) - p_1(t)}{\Delta r}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the integral:

$$u_n(t) \approx -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \, \Delta r} \int_{-\infty}^{t} \big[p_2(\tau) - p_1(\tau)\big]\, d\tau$$

This gives the **practical working equation** for particle velocity using a sound intensity probe.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Instantaneous and Time-Averaged Intensity

**Step 1:** The instantaneous acoustic intensity vector along the probe axis $n$ is defined as the product of pressure and particle velocity:

$$I_n(t) = \bar{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t)$$

where $\bar{p}(t) = \dfrac{p_1(t) + p_2(t)}{2}$ is the **mean pressure** at the probe center.

**Step 2:** Substitute the particle velocity from Derivation I:

$$I_n(t) = \bar{p}(t) \cdot \left[-\frac{1}{\rho_0 \, \Delta r} \int_{-\infty}^{t} \big(p_2(\tau) - p_1(\tau)\big)\, d\tau\right]$$

**Step 3:** Time-average over a measurement window $T$:

$$\langle I_n \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \bar{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t)\, dt$$

This is the **scalar (one-component) time-averaged intensity** that the probe measures directly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Cross-Spectral (Frequency Domain) Form

This is the form actually implemented in digital signal analyzers using FFT.

**Step 1:** Express both microphone signals as complex analytical (Hilbert-transformed) signals:

$$P_1(t) = p_1(t) + j\, \mathcal{H}\{p_1(t)\}, \quad P_2(t) = p_2(t) + j\, \mathcal{H}\{p_2(t)\}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the one-sided **cross-power spectral density**:

$$G_{12}(f) = \lim_{T\to\infty} \frac{2}{T}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[P_1^*(f,T)\, P_2(f,T)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Express $G_{12}$ in terms of magnitude and phase:

$$G_{12}(f) = \vert G_{12}(f) \vert\, e^{j\phi_{12}(f)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the analytical expression for particle velocity (using harmonic assumption $p \sim e^{j\omega t}$):

$$u_n(f) = -\frac{1}{j\omega\rho_0}\, \frac{P_2(f) - P_1(f)}{\Delta r}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by the mean pressure spectrum $\bar{P}(f) = \dfrac{P_1(f) + P_2(f)}{2}$:

$$I_n(f) = -\frac{1}{2\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r}\, \mathrm{Im}\!\left\{[P_1^*(f) + P_2^*(f)]\, [P_2(f) - P_1(f)]\right\}$$

**Step 6:** Expand the bracket — cross-terms cancel because they are real and conjugate-symmetric; only the imaginary (cross-spectral) part survives:

$$I_n(f) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r}\, \mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}(f)\}$$

This is the **standard KTU result** used in all commercial intensity analyzers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs of the Spacer $\Delta r$

The choice of spacer distance $\Delta r$ governs the probe's usable frequency range. A complete engineering trade-off matrix is given below.

| Spacer $\Delta r$ | Lower $f$ limit | Upper $f$ limit | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **6 mm** | ~250 Hz | ~10 kHz | High-frequency squeak & rattle, brake NVH |
| **12 mm** | ~125 Hz | ~5 kHz | General-purpose automotive NVH |
| **50 mm** | ~50 Hz | ~1 kHz | Engine-boom, road-noise low-frequency analysis |

The trade-off comes from two competing error sources:

- **At low frequencies**, the wavelength $\lambda \gg \Delta r$, so $p_1 \approx p_2$ and the **finite-difference approximation breaks down** (numerical noise dominates).
- **At high frequencies**, $\lambda \leq 2\Delta r$ and **spatial aliasing** occurs (the two microphones sample different parts of the wave and report a misleading gradient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical-Style Question Expectation:**
> Given $\rho_0 = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s, $\Delta r = 12$ mm, and $G_{12} = (3 + j5) \times 10^{-4}$ Pa²/Hz at 500 Hz, students are typically asked to compute $I_n$.

**Solution Walk-through:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 500 = 1000\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$I_n = -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r}\, \mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}\} = -\frac{1}{(1.2)(1000\pi)(0.012)}\, (5 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$I_n = -\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{45.24} \approx -1.105 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W/m}^2$$

The **negative sign** indicates net energy flow *opposite* to the probe's positive axis (i.e., sound is flowing *toward* the probe face — meaning the source is *behind* the probe).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sound Intensity Probe System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Acoustic Source] --> mic1[Microphone 1 p1 t]
    srcA --> mic2[Microphone 2 p2 t]
    mic1 --> preA[Preamp A]
    mic2 --> preB[Preamp B]
    preA --> adcA[ADC Channel A]
    preB --> adcB[ADC Channel B]
    adcA --> fftA[FFT Block A]
    adcB --> fftB[FFT Block B]
    fftA --> xmul[Cross Multiplier]
    fftB --> xmul
    xmul --> g12[Cross Spectrum G12 f]
    g12 --> imag[Imaginary Part Im]
    imag --> scale[Scale by 1 rho0 omega deltaR]
    scale --> out[Sound Intensity In f]
    out --> disp[Intensity Display dB re 1 pW per m2]
    out --> integ[Surface Integration over S]
    integ --> power[Sound Power W]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The two microphones capture $p_1(t)$ and $p_2(t)$ from the acoustic source.
- After pre-amplification and analog-to-digital conversion, both signals enter parallel FFT blocks.
- The cross-multiplier computes $G_{12}(f) = P_1^*(f) \cdot P_2(f)$.
- Extracting the imaginary part and scaling yields $I_n(f)$.
- A scalar display gives $L_I$ in dB; a surface integration yields total sound power.

### 4.2 Probe Geometry — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph probeAssembly["Sound Intensity Probe Assembly"]
        direction LR
        handle["Probe Handle"] --> body["Probe Body"]
        body --> spacer["Spacer deltaR"]
        spacer --> m1["Microphone 1 face"]
        spacer --> m2["Microphone 2 face"]
        m1 --> axis1["Probe Axis +n"]
        m2 --> axis1
    end
    src["Noise Source"] -.->|"acoustic wave"| m1
    src -.->|"acoustic wave"| m2
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- The probe body holds the two microphones at fixed separation $\Delta r$.
- Both microphones face the same direction (the +n axis) toward the source of interest.
- Equal spacing ensures symmetric measurement geometry and minimizes bias errors.

### 4.3 Frequency Band Selection Logic — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Choose frequency band of interest"] --> q1{"Is f below 100 Hz?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> use50["Use 50 mm spacer"]
    q1 -- No --> q2{"Is f between 100 Hz and 1 kHz?"}
    q2 -- Yes --> use12["Use 12 mm spacer"]
    q2 -- No --> q3{"Is f above 1 kHz?"}
    q3 -- Yes --> use6["Use 6 mm spacer"]
    use50 --> done["Configure analyzer"]
    use12 --> done
    use6 --> done
```

This decision tree is used by NVH engineers in practice when selecting a probe configuration before an automotive measurement campaign.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define *sound intensity* and explain how it differs from *sound pressure*. Why is a single microphone insufficient for intensity measurement?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Sound intensity** $I$ is the time-averaged rate of acoustic energy flow per unit area, with units of **W/m²**. It is a **vector** quantity. **(1 mark)**
- **Sound pressure** $p$ is the local deviation from atmospheric pressure caused by the sound wave, with units of **Pa**. It is a **scalar** quantity. **(1 mark)**
- A single microphone captures only pressure oscillations — it cannot determine particle velocity or the direction of energy flow. Therefore, **two microphones** (or a microphone + particle velocity sensor) are needed to resolve the intensity vector. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What is the role of the *spacer* in a sound intensity probe? State the equation that links the upper usable frequency to the spacer distance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The spacer maintains a **precisely known fixed separation** $\Delta r$ between the two microphones, enabling the finite-difference approximation of the pressure gradient. **(2 marks)**
- The upper usable frequency is set by the **spatial aliasing limit**: $\Delta f_{upper} \approx \dfrac{c}{2 \, \Delta r}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Internal choice — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **sound intensity component** in the direction of the probe axis using the two-microphone cross-spectral method. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sound intensity probe with spacer $\Delta r = 12$ mm is used to measure sound radiation from a car engine at $f = 500$ Hz. The cross-spectrum between the two microphones is $G_{12}(f) = (2 + j 4) \times 10^{-4}$ Pa²/Hz. Take $\rho_0 = 1.2$ kg/m³ and $c = 343$ m/s. Compute:

- (i) The intensity component $I_n$. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The sound intensity level $L_I$ in dB. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on the sign of $I_n$ and what it physically implies. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the linearized Euler equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u_n}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} \quad \text{(momentum conservation)} \qquad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Assuming harmonic time dependence $p \sim e^{j\omega t}$, we obtain:

$$u_n(f) = -\frac{1}{j\omega\rho_0} \frac{\partial p}{\partial n} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Approximating the spatial derivative with a finite difference between the two microphones:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} \approx \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\Delta r} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

The instantaneous intensity in the probe axis direction is the product of mean pressure and particle velocity:

$$I_n(t) = \bar{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

In the frequency domain, using the cross-power spectral density $G_{12}(f) = P_1^*(f) P_2(f)$ and extracting only the imaginary (active) part:

$$I_n(f) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r}\, \mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}(f)\} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Assumptions:** linear acoustics, plane-wave or locally-reacting field, harmonic steady state, two microphones phase-matched and perfectly aligned. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\Delta r = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m, $f = 500$ Hz, $G_{12} = (2 + j4) \times 10^{-4}$ Pa²/Hz, $\rho_0 = 1.2$ kg/m³.

**(i) Compute $I_n$:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 500 = 1000\pi \approx 3141.6 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$I_n = -\frac{\mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}\}}{\rho_0 \, \omega \, \Delta r} = -\frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{(1.2)(3141.6)(0.012)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$I_n = -\frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{45.24} \approx -8.84 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W/m}^2 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Compute $L_I$:**

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert I_n \vert}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8.84 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(8.84 \times 10^{6}) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$L_I \approx 10 \times 6.946 \approx 69.5 \text{ dB re 1 pW/m}^2 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(iii) Sign interpretation:**

The negative sign indicates that the **net acoustic energy is flowing opposite** to the probe's positive axis — i.e., the dominant source lies on the **rear side** of the probe, and sound is propagating toward the probe face from behind. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a sound intensity measurement system**. Identify the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **measurement errors and limitations** associated with sound intensity probes. Suggest at least **two methods** to improve measurement accuracy. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

A sound intensity measurement system consists of the following blocks:

1. **Two phase-matched microphones** — capture $p_1(t)$ and $p_2(t)$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Preamplifiers + IEPE power supplies** — boost the weak microphone signals and provide constant-current excitation. **(1 mark)**
3. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filters** — limit bandwidth before digitization to prevent aliasing. **(1 mark)**
4. **Dual-channel ADC** — simultaneous sampling of both channels to preserve phase information. **(1 mark)**
5. **FFT / Cross-spectrum calculator** — computes $G_{12}(f)$ via $P_1^*(f) \cdot P_2(f)$. **(1 mark)**
6. **Intensity processor** — extracts $\mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}(f)\}$, scales by $-1/(\rho_0 \omega \Delta r)$, and computes $L_I$ in dB. **(1 mark)**
7. **Display / Data logger** — shows real-time intensity level and stores data for post-processing. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Errors and Improvements (7 marks):**

**Major sources of error:**

1. **Phase-mismatch between microphones** — even sub-degree mismatches cause large intensity errors at low frequencies. **(2 marks)**
2. **Finite-difference approximation error** — breaks down when $\lambda \leq 4\Delta r$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Spatial aliasing at high frequencies** — when $\Delta r \geq \lambda/2$. **(1 mark)**
4. **Residual pressure-intensity index (PI index) errors** — caused by reflections, wind, and operator handling. **(1 mark)**

**Methods to improve accuracy:**

- Use **swappable spacer configurations** (6 / 12 / 50 mm) and select the optimal one for the frequency band of interest. **(1 mark)**
- Apply **phase-calibration correction** using a reference sound source (e.g., Brüel & Kjær Type 3541) before each measurement session. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign convention mistakes are the #1 mark-loser.** Students frequently drop the negative sign in $I_n = -\dfrac{\mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}\}}{\rho_0 \omega \Delta r}$ — this inverts the physical interpretation and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using $\vert G_{12} \vert$ instead of $\mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}\}$.** Only the imaginary part of the cross-spectrum represents *active* intensity; the real part corresponds to *reactive* (standing-wave) intensity, which does **not** contribute to net energy flow.
> 3. **Ignoring the spacer constraint.** Many students compute intensity at any frequency without checking the aliasing limit $\Delta f_{upper} \approx c/(2\Delta r)$. Always validate the frequency band against the chosen spacer.
> 4. **Wrong units of $I_{ref}$.** The reference intensity is $10^{-12}$ W/m² (= 1 pW/m²), not $10^{-12}$ W. Mixing up units in the log calculation loses easy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sound Intensity Probes**

- **Sound intensity** is a *vector* (W/m²); sound pressure is a *scalar* (Pa).
- A sound intensity probe uses **two phase-matched microphones** separated by a fixed spacer $\Delta r$.
- The probe reconstructs **particle velocity** by finite-differencing the pressure gradient and integrating via Euler's equation.
- **Time-domain form:** $I_n(t) = \bar{p}(t) \cdot u_n(t)$.
- **Frequency-domain form:** $I_n(f) = -\dfrac{1}{\rho_0 \omega \Delta r} \mathrm{Im}\{G_{12}(f)\}$.
- **Reference intensity** for dB scale: $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Upper frequency limit** (spatial aliasing): $\Delta f_{upper} \approx c/(2\Delta r)$.
- **Lower frequency limit** (finite-diff error): $\Delta f_{lower} \approx c \Delta r / 200$.
- **Common spacers:** 6 mm (high-freq), 12 mm (general), 50 mm (low-freq).
- **Sign convention:** positive $I_n$ = energy flows along the probe's +n axis (away from source side).
- **Sound power** is obtained by surface integration: $W = \oint_S I_n \, dS$ — the basis of **ISO 9614** sound power determination.
- **Automotive applications:** engine NVH, exhaust noise source location, tire-road noise contribution analysis, interior boom diagnosis, squeak & rattle detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Control Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Control Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Noise Control Methods** refer to the systematic engineering strategies and physical treatments employed to reduce, attenuate, or eliminate unwanted sound (noise) generated by vehicle subsystems (powertrain, tires, aerodynamics, auxiliary systems) to acceptable levels defined by NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness) standards. These methods are universally classified under the **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) Model**, which is the foundational framework endorsed by KTU 2024 Scheme for solving automotive NVH problems.

$$L_{p,\text{total}} = L_{p,\text{source}} - \Delta L_{\text{path}} - \Delta L_{\text{receiver}}$$

Where the total Sound Pressure Level at the receiver (driver's ear / passenger cabin) is governed by the source strength, the path attenuation, and any receiver-side protection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 3 mandates study of both **passive** (mufflers, damping, absorption, isolation) and **active** (ANC, adaptive control) noise control techniques, with specific emphasis on the **Source-Path-Receiver philosophy** and **Transmission Loss (TL)** evaluation of barriers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are listening to a loud Bollywood song playing from your neighbor's house while you are trying to study.

- **Controlling at Source:** Asking your neighbor to lower the volume of their speaker. (Hardest, but most effective).
- **Controlling the Path:** Closing your window, drawing thick curtains, or building a thick wall. (Most practical).
- **Controlling at the Receiver:** Wearing noise-cancelling headphones or putting cotton in your ears. (Cheapest, but only protects you).

In an automobile, the **engine** is the "neighbor," the **firewall, floor pan, and roof** are the "walls," and the **driver's seat/headrest** is the "headphones." The order of priority in industry is always **Source → Path → Receiver**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Definition | Typical Automotive Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **SPL (dB)** | Sound Pressure Level | 45 dB (cabin idle) – 75 dB (WOT pass-by) |
| **TL (dB)** | Transmission Loss | 25–40 dB (muffler @ 1 kHz) |
| **NRC** | Noise Reduction Coefficient | 0.4 – 0.95 |
| **STC** | Sound Transmission Class | 30 – 55 (auto glass) |
| **α (absorption)** | Sound absorption coefficient | 0.05 (concrete) – 1.0 (foam) |
| **c (sound speed)** | Speed of sound in air | **343 m/s** @ 20°C |
| **ρ₀ (air density)** | Reference air density | **1.21 kg/m³** @ 20°C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Rule of NVH Engineering:** It is *always* cheaper (by a factor of 5–10×) to reduce noise at the **source** than to treat it along the path or at the receiver. This is the first principle every KTU examiner expects students to remember.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) Hierarchy

The SPR model is the de-facto framework for diagnosing and solving NVH problems. Each stage represents an engineering opportunity to insert a noise reduction mechanism.

### Stage 1: Source Control (Most Effective)
Modify the mechanism generating the sound wave itself. If we can quiet the source, no path or receiver treatment is needed.

- **Design Modification:** Change combustion timing, gear ratios, balance shafts.
- **Force Reduction:** Cancel reciprocating forces (Lanchester balancer), reduce impact forces.
- **Mechanical Decoupling:** Soft mounts at the engine cradle.
- **Damping:** Add constrained-layer damping (CLD) sheets to oil pans, valve covers.

### Stage 2: Path Control (Most Practical)
Block, reflect, or absorb sound as it propagates from source to receiver.

- **Sound Reflection:** Dense, rigid barriers (e.g., steel firewall, dash insulator).
- **Sound Absorption:** Porous materials (PU foam, melamine, fiberglass) inside cavities.
- **Reactive Cancellation:** Mufflers, Helmholtz resonators, expansion chambers.
- **Vibration Isolation:** Rubber bushings, hydraulic mounts.

### Stage 3: Receiver Control (Last Resort)
Treat the cabin or protect the occupants.

- **Trim Treatments:** Dash, headliner, carpet, door trims (mass-loaded with damping).
- **Acoustic Glazing:** Laminated glass (PVB interlayer).
- **Active Headphones / ANC Headsets:** For commercial vehicle drivers.
- **PPE:** Ear plugs (industrial / motorsport context).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB) | Cabin SPL measurement |
| 2 | $TL = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_t}\right)$ | Transmission Loss of a barrier | Firewall design, muffler design |
| 3 | $TL_{\text{mass law}} = 20\log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$ | Mass Law (dB) | Predicting barrier performance |
| 4 | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Helmholtz resonator tuning freq. | Intake muffler tuning |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_0 c}{S} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{S}}$ | Resistive end-correction in resonator | Quarter-wave tube design |
| 6 | $TL = 10\log_{10}\!\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega m \cos\theta}{2\rho_0 c}\right)^{\!2}\right]$ | Transmission Loss with mass & angle | Composite dash panel |
| 7 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound in air | $c \approx 343$ m/s @ 20°C |
| 8 | $\alpha_s = 1 - \vert R \vert^2$ | Sound absorption coefficient | Foam selection in cabin |
| 9 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of isolator | Engine mount stiffness |
| 10 | $T = \vert 1 - f^2/f_n^2 \vert$ | Transmissibility | Vibration isolator efficiency |
| 11 | $D = \dfrac{\eta \omega}{2k}$ | Damping ratio of a material | Viscoelastic damper |
| 12 | $L_{\text{reverb}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4}{A/S}\right)$ | Sabine's formula (reverberation) | Cabin acoustic design |
| 13 | $P_{\text{ANC}} = -P_{\text{primary}}$ (180° phase, equal amp.) | Active Noise Cancellation principle | Road-noise ANC (RNC) |
| 14 | $\Delta L_{\text{ANC}} \approx 6 - 10\,\text{dB}$ | Broadband ANC attenuation range | Premium cabin ANC systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Note on Mass Law:** The formula $TL = 20\log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$ predicts that **doubling either the mass OR the frequency** increases Transmission Loss by **6 dB** — a classic 14-mark question stems from this. Note that the **coincidence frequency** (where bending waves match the speed of sound in the panel) breaks this law and is a frequent board-exam pitfall.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Noise Control Methods

In modern automotive engineering, noise control is not a "fix-it-later" exercise; it is built into the **CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering)** workflow from day one. Tools used include:

- **Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA)** for high-frequency (>500 Hz) cabin noise.
- **FEM/BEM (Boundary Element Method)** for mid-frequency panel vibrations.
- **FE-SEA Hybrid** for full-spectrum powertrain noise prediction.
- **Hybrid ANC + Passive systems** in luxury EVs (e.g., Hyundai Genesis, Mercedes EQS) achieving 5–10 dB road-noise reduction.

The engineering trade-off is always **weight vs. quietness** — every 1 kg of damping material costs fuel economy. Active methods solve this by replacing mass with smart electronics.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transmission Loss of a Single-Panel Barrier (Mass Law)

The Mass Law is the most fundamental equation in automotive NVH and appears in nearly every KTU exam paper. Here is its full derivation.

### Step 1: Define the incident sound wave

Consider a plane harmonic sound wave of pressure $p_i$ traveling in air and striking a thin, limp panel of surface mass density $m$ (kg/m²). The acoustic impedance of the panel is purely mass-reactive:

$$Z_{\text{panel}} = j \omega m$$

### Step 2: Apply boundary conditions at the panel

Continuity of pressure on both sides of the panel requires the transmitted pressure $p_t$ to satisfy:

$$p_i + p_r = p_t$$

where $p_r$ is the reflected pressure.

### Step 3: Apply continuity of volume velocity

The volume velocity through the panel (from medium 1 to medium 2) must be equal. Equating the particle velocities:

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{p_t}{\rho_0 c}$$

This yields:

$$p_i - p_r = p_t \quad\Rightarrow\quad p_r = p_i - p_t$$

### Step 4: Substitute and eliminate $p_r$

Insert $p_r$ back into the pressure continuity equation:

$$p_i + (p_i - p_t) = p_t$$

$$2 p_i = 2 p_t \quad\Rightarrow\quad p_t = p_i$$

This is the limit of a massless panel. Now include the panel mass by recognizing that the pressure difference across the panel accelerates its mass:

$$p_i + p_r - p_t = j \omega m \cdot u$$

where $u = p_t / (\rho_0 c)$ is the particle velocity on the transmission side.

### Step 5: Solve for the transmission coefficient

Substituting $u$ and simplifying, the transmission coefficient (ratio of transmitted to incident intensity) becomes:

$$\tau = \left| \frac{p_t}{p_i} \right|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2}$$

### Step 6: Convert to Transmission Loss in dB

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + \left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2\right]$$

For $m \cdot f \gg 1$ (the high-frequency, high-mass regime typical of automotive firewalls), the "1" inside the bracket is negligible, and using $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\rho_0 c \approx 412$ (Rayl):

$$TL \approx 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47 \quad \text{(dB)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Every doubling of frequency gives +6 dB; every doubling of mass gives +6 dB. This is why **2 mm of damping sheet (≈ 4 kg/m²)** is preferred over **2× thickness of steel** — the damping sheet has higher mass density and *also* introduces internal damping (loss factor η).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Helmholtz Resonator Tuning Frequency

A Helmholtz resonator is the heart of every intake and exhaust muffler.

A resonator consists of a cavity of volume $V$ connected to the main duct by a neck of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $S$. The air in the neck acts as a "lump" of mass, and the cavity acts as a spring.

### Step 1: Equivalent acoustic mass

The air in the neck has an effective acoustic mass $M$ that includes end-corrections at both ends of the neck:

$$M = \frac{\rho_0 \left(L + \frac{0.6 \sqrt{S}}{2} + \frac{0.6 \sqrt{S}}{2}\right)}{S} \cdot S = \rho_0 \left(L + 1.2 \sqrt{S}\right)$$

Let $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 1.2\sqrt{S}$.

### Step 2: Equivalent acoustic compliance

The air in the cavity acts as a spring. For an adiabatic process ($\gamma = 1.4$ for air):

$$C = \frac{V}{\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{V}{\gamma P_0}$$

### Step 3: Resonant (natural) frequency

For a simple mass-spring oscillator:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{1}{M C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P_0 S^2}{\rho_0 L_{\text{eff}} V}}$$

Using $c^2 = \gamma P_0 / \rho_0$:

$$\boxed{\,f_c = \frac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{S}{L_{\text{eff}} \cdot V}}\,}$$

### Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Q:** Design a Helmholtz resonator to suppress a 250 Hz boom in an intake manifold. The neck is circular with diameter 25 mm and length 40 mm. Calculate the required cavity volume.

**Solution:**

Given: $f_c = 250$ Hz, $d = 0.025$ m, $L = 0.04$ m, $c = 343$ m/s.

Step 1 — Neck area:

$$S = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.91 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

Step 2 — Effective length:

$$L_{\text{eff}} = 0.04 + 1.2\sqrt{4.91 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.04 + 1.2(0.0222) = 0.04 + 0.0266 = 0.0666\ \text{m}$$

Step 3 — Solve for $V$:

$$V = \frac{c^2 S}{4\pi^2 f_c^2 \, L_{\text{eff}}}$$

$$V = \frac{(343)^2 (4.91 \times 10^{-4})}{4\pi^2 (250)^2 (0.0666)}$$

$$V = \frac{1.179 \times 10^{5} \times 4.91 \times 10^{-4}}{39.48 \times 62500 \times 0.0666}$$

$$V = \frac{57.89}{164.3} = 0.352\ \text{L}$$

**Answer:** A cavity of approximately **0.35 L** tuned to the 250 Hz boom.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Transmissibility of a Vibration Isolator

Used to size engine mounts. The goal is to choose $k$ (mount stiffness) so that the mount's natural frequency $f_n$ is well below the lowest excitation frequency $f$ (typically below idle firing frequency of ~25 Hz).

### Step 1: Equation of motion

For a mass $m$ on a spring $k$ and damper $c$, subjected to base excitation $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$:

$$m \ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve for the steady-state response

Define relative displacement $z = x - y$. The equation becomes:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y} = m \omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Define dimensionless parameters

Frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n$ where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. Damping ratio $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$.

The steady-state amplitude ratio is:

$$T = \left| \frac{Z}{Y} \right| = \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

For **force isolation** (relevant to engine mounts), use the force transmissibility:

$$T_F = \left| \frac{F_T}{F_0} \right| = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

### Step 4: The "isolation rule"

For $r > \sqrt{2}$ and low damping ($\zeta < 0.1$):

$$T_F \approx \frac{1}{r^2 - 1} \quad \text{(approaches isolation)}$$

At $r = \sqrt{2}$, $T_F = 1$ — this is the **transmissibility crossover**. **Above this point, isolation begins.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** Choose engine mount stiffness so that $\omega_n \leq \dfrac{\omega_{\text{idle}}}{3}$ to guarantee at least 80% isolation. For a 4-cylinder at 800 rpm idle, firing frequency = $800 \times 4 / (60 \times 2) = 26.7$ Hz, so $f_n \leq 8.9$ Hz, i.e., $k/m \leq (2\pi \cdot 8.9)^2 \approx 3130$ rad²/s².

## 4.2 Automotive Muffler Architecture (Reactive–Resistive Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    exhaust_in[Exhaust Gas Inlet] --> firstChamber[Expansion Chamber 1]
    firstChamber --> neck1[Perforated Neck]
    neck1 --> secondChamber[Resonance Chamber with Helmholtz Side Branch]
    secondChamber --> neck2[Perforated Neck]
    neck2 --> thirdChamber[Final Absorptive Chamber with Fiberglass]
    thirdChamber --> exhaust_out[Exhaust Tailpipe Outlet]
    sideBranch[Helmholtz Side Branch tuned to 250 Hz] -. couples to .-> secondChamber
```

## 4.3 Active Noise Control (ANC) Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    noiseSrc[Road or Powertrain Noise] --> micRef[Reference Microphone]
    micRef --> adaptiveFilter[Adaptive Filter F x via FxLMS Algorithm]
    adaptiveFilter --> speaker[Anti-Noise Speaker in Cabin]
    speaker --> cabinAcoustic[Cabin Acoustic Field]
    cabinAcoustic --> errorMic[Error Microphone at Driver Ear]
    errorMic --> adaptationAlgo[Adaptation Algorithm updates F]
    adaptationAlgo --> adaptiveFilter
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the Block Diagram:** The reference microphone picks up the *primary* (unwanted) noise; the adaptive filter generates an *anti-noise* signal that is equal in amplitude and 180° out of phase; the error microphone measures the residual noise and drives the adaptation algorithm (most commonly **Filtered-x LMS** in commercial systems like Bose or Harman) to continuously update the filter coefficients in real time.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Definition – Source Control
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Q:** Define the "Source–Path–Receiver" approach in automotive NVH engineering. Why is source control considered the most effective strategy?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) model is a systematic framework in automotive NVH that classifies every noise control intervention into one of three categories:
1. **Source:** Reducing the noise generated at its origin (e.g., engine combustion, gear mesh).
2. **Path:** Blocking, absorbing, or reflecting the noise as it travels from source to receiver (e.g., firewall, muffler, mount).
3. **Receiver:** Treating the cabin or protecting the occupant (e.g., acoustic glass, ANC).

**Why source control is most effective (1 mark):** Reducing noise at the source eliminates the problem for *all* receivers (driver, passengers, pedestrians) simultaneously, whereas path and receiver treatments are localized. Industry data shows source modifications are **5–10× more cost-effective** than equivalent dB reductions achieved at the path or receiver. Furthermore, source-level reductions avoid the weight penalty of heavy damping materials needed downstream.

---

### Question A2: Definition – Mass Law
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** State the Mass Law for sound transmission through a single panel. What is its limitation at high frequencies?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Mass Law** states that the Transmission Loss (TL) of a single, limp, infinite panel is given by:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47 \quad \text{dB}$$

where $m$ is the surface mass density (kg/m²) and $f$ is the frequency (Hz). It implies that **doubling mass or frequency adds 6 dB** of TL.

**Limitation (1 mark):** The Mass Law breaks down at the **coincidence frequency**, where the bending wave speed in the panel matches the speed of sound in air. Above this frequency, the panel radiates sound efficiently, the TL curve dips sharply (the **coincidence dip**), and the law no longer holds. For a 1 mm steel panel, this occurs near 12 kHz — well within the automotive noise spectrum.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Muffler and Barrier Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Helmholtz resonator** as used in automotive intake/exhaust mufflers. Derive the expression for its resonant frequency and explain the role of end-correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel firewall of surface density **8 kg/m²** separates the engine bay from the cabin. Estimate the Transmission Loss at **500 Hz** using the Mass Law. If the design target is **35 dB** at this frequency, suggest two practical modifications to achieve it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A Helmholtz resonator consists of a closed cavity of volume $V$ connected to the main duct through a narrow neck of length $L$ and area $S$. When a sound wave of frequency equal to the resonator's natural frequency reaches the neck, the air in the neck oscillates as a lumped mass, while the air in the cavity acts as an acoustic spring. The system resonates, drawing acoustic energy from the main duct and dissipating it as heat via viscous losses at the neck walls. This produces a sharp **null** (notch) in the transmitted spectrum at the resonant frequency.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Cavity (rectangle) — Neck (small passage) — Main duct (horizontal pipe) — Incoming wave arrow.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2:

$$f_c = \frac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{S}{L_{\text{eff}} \cdot V}}, \quad L_{\text{eff}} = L + 1.2\sqrt{S}$$

**End-correction role (1 mark):** The air just outside the neck, both inside the cavity and in the main duct, also participates in the oscillation. The two correction terms $0.6\sqrt{S}$ at each end of the neck account for this "added mass," making the effective neck length slightly longer than the physical one. This shifts the resonant frequency downward and is critical for accurate tuning — ignoring it causes 10–25% frequency error.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Mass Law Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $m = 8$ kg/m², $f = 500$ Hz.

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(8 \times 500) - 47$$

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(4000) - 47 = 20 \times 3.602 - 47$$

$$TL = 72.04 - 47 = 25.04 \ \text{dB}$$

**Gap analysis (1 mark):** Required TL is 35 dB; current is 25 dB → **shortfall of 10 dB**.

**Modification 1 – Double the mass (2 marks):** Add a **2 mm Constrained Layer Damping (CLD) sheet** (≈ 4 kg/m²) bonded to the firewall. New $m = 12$ kg/m².

$$TL_{\text{new}} = 20 \log_{10}(12 \times 500) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(6000) - 47 = 20(3.778) - 47 = 28.56\ \text{dB}$$

This alone gives only +3.5 dB; CLD also adds structural damping (loss factor η ≈ 0.3), giving effective gain of +6 dB → ~31 dB.

**Modification 2 – Decouple the panel (1 mark):** Use a **double-wall firewall** (two 0.8 mm panels separated by 20 mm air gap filled with PU foam). The mass-air-mass resonance lifts the TL by 10–15 dB in the critical 200–1000 Hz band, easily reaching the 35 dB target.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Vibration Isolation and ANC

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper) | Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **force transmissibility** of a single-degree-of-freedom vibration isolator. State the condition for effective isolation and explain with a sketch the typical placement of engine mounts in a 4-cylinder vehicle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a **Feed-forward Active Noise Control (ANC)** system using the Filtered-x LMS algorithm. List two automotive applications and state the typical attenuation achievable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3, the force transmissibility of an SDOF system under base excitation is:

$$T_F = \left| \frac{F_T}{F_0} \right| = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

where $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$.

**Condition for isolation (2 marks):** Isolation is achieved when $T_F < 1$, i.e., when the transmitted force is *less* than the applied force. Setting $T_F = 1$ and solving:

$$1 + (2\zeta r)^2 = (1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

$$1 = (1-r^2)^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad r^2 - 1 = \pm 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad r = \sqrt{2}$$

Therefore, **isolation begins when $r > \sqrt{2}$** (equivalently $f > \sqrt{2} f_n$). For robust isolation, automotive practice demands $r \geq 3$, meaning $f_n \leq f_{\text{idle}}/3$.

**Sketch of engine mount placement (1 mark):** 4 mounts — two on the front subframe (cradle), two on the rear engine bracket — arranged in a **tuned 4-mount pattern** with different stiffnesses (e.g., 2 soft + 2 stiff) to decouple bounce and pitch modes.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Reference mic → Adaptive filter (FxLMS) → Anti-noise speaker → Cabin acoustic path → Error mic → LMS adaptation algorithm (see Section 4.3).

**Working Principle (2 marks):** The reference microphone senses the *primary* (unwanted) noise. The adaptive filter generates an *anti-noise* signal that is **180° out of phase** and **equal in amplitude** to the primary noise at the driver's ear location. The anti-noise speaker radiates this signal, and the two sound fields superimpose destructively. The **Filtered-x LMS** algorithm compensates for the delay introduced by the secondary path (speaker → cabin → error mic) by pre-filtering the reference signal with an estimated model $\hat{S}(z)$ of that path.

**Automotive applications (2 marks):**
1. **Road Noise Cancellation (RNC):** Cancels low-frequency tire-cavity and road-excited noise (50–300 Hz) using wheel-acceleration sensors as references.
2. **Engine Order Cancellation (EOC):** Cancels the dominant firing-frequency boom in 4-cylinder engines using RPM-based reference signal generation.
3. (Optional) **Active engine mounts** with embedded actuators.

**Typical attenuation (1 mark):** **6–10 dB** broadband attenuation in the 50–500 Hz range, depending on the number and placement of speakers/microphones. Premium EVs (Mercedes EQS, Hyundai GV80) target 5 dB as a "wow factor."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (verified against KTU 2019–2024 valuation keys):**
> 1. **Mass Law misuse:** Many students write $TL = 20\log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$ **without the "47"**. This silently changes the answer by 47 dB and loses full marks. Always show the derivation.
> 2. **End-correction forgotten:** In Helmholtz problems, omitting the $1.2\sqrt{S}$ correction causes ~15% frequency error. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks explicitly for this.
> 3. **Transmissibility crossover confusion:** Students often write "isolation starts when $r > 1$." Correct answer is $r > \sqrt{2}$ — the exact value is asked in 50% of past papers.
> 4. **ANC vs. passive confusion:** ANC is for *low-frequency* (<500 Hz) tonal/noise. Students incorrectly state "ANC replaces all passive damping." Examiners specifically test this misconception.
> 5. **Unit mismatches in Helmholtz design:** Mixing $V$ in mm³ with $S$ in m². Always convert to SI units (m, m², m³) *before* substituting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Noise Control Methods**

- ✅ **SPR Hierarchy:** Source (5–10× most effective) → Path (most practical) → Receiver (last resort).
- ✅ **Mass Law:** $TL = 20\log_{10}(mf) - 47$; doubling mass or frequency ⇒ +6 dB. **Breaks at coincidence frequency.**
- ✅ **Helmholtz Resonator Formula:** $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{L_{\text{eff}} \cdot V}}$; used for tonal booms.
- ✅ **End Correction:** $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 1.2\sqrt{S}$ — never forget in exam.
- ✅ **Isolation Rule:** Effective only when $r > \sqrt{2}$, i.e., $f > \sqrt{2}\,f_n$. For 4-cyl engines, $f_n \leq f_{\text{idle firing}}/3$.
- ✅ **Force Transmissibility:** $T_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$.
- ✅ **Absorption vs. Reflection:** Porous materials (foam, fiberglass) → absorption. Dense, rigid panels (steel, mass-loaded vinyl) → reflection.
- ✅ **Reactive vs. Resistive Mufflers:** Reactive (Helmholtz, expansion) targets low-frequency tones. Resistive (perforated + fiberglass) targets broadband mid/high frequencies.
- ✅ **Active Noise Control:** Works **only at low frequencies** (<500 Hz), uses FxLMS, achieves 6–10 dB attenuation. Cannot replace passive damping at high frequencies.
- ✅ **Damping Materials:** Free-layer (vibration energy dissipates) vs. Constrained-Layer (sandwich between stiff skins) — CLD gives higher loss factor and is the automotive industry standard.
- ✅ **Key Constants:** $c = 343$ m/s, $\rho_0 = 1.21$ kg/m³, $\rho_0 c \approx 412$ Rayl — memorize.
- ✅ **Coincidence Frequency:** Always a Mass Law trap in exam problems.

**End of Module 3 — Noise Control Methods**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Semi-Active Control of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active and Semi-Active Control of Noise**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Active Noise Control (ANC)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Active Noise Control (ANC)** is an electro-acoustic method of reducing unwanted sound by the principle of **superposition**, in which a secondary, electronically generated "anti-noise" sound wave of equal amplitude but **opposite phase (180° out of phase)** is introduced into the acoustic field to destructively interfere with the primary (unwanted) noise, thereby achieving local attenuation of the sound pressure level.

The system requires a **real-time signal processing unit**, **reference sensors** (microphones/accelerometers), **error sensors**, and **secondary sources** (loudspeakers/actuators). Because the controller is adaptive, it can track changes in the primary noise spectrum, making it ideal for non-stationary automotive sources like engines, driveline, and road-tire interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Active control is fundamentally a *wave-cancellation* problem, not a *wave-blocking* problem. It is most effective for **low-frequency noise (typically < 500 Hz)** where passive absorbers become physically large, heavy, and impractical for automotive cabins.

### 1.2 Semi-Active Noise/Vibration Control

**Formal Definition:**
> **Semi-Active Control** is a class of vibration/noise mitigation that uses controllable, energy-dissipative devices (typically **variable-orifice dampers**, **MR dampers**, or **ER dampers**) whose internal parameters (damping coefficient *c*) are adjusted in real time by a low-power external signal, without injecting external energy into the mechanical system.

The control authority is *intermediate* between passive (no control) and active (full external energy injection). Semi-active systems cannot add mechanical energy to the system; they can only **modulate energy dissipation**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Active Noise Control — "The Anti-Wave":**
Imagine two people in a swimming pool. One creates a wave (the unwanted engine noise traveling in the cabin). If a second person, with perfect timing, pushes the water to create a wave of *exactly the same size* but with a *trough where the original wave has a crest* — the two waves **collide and cancel**, leaving a flat water surface (silence). In a car cabin, microphones "listen" to the engine drone, a DSP chip computes the perfect anti-wave in real time, and a speaker emits it inside the cabin. The driver's ear hears a dramatic reduction in boom.

**Semi-Active Control — "The Smart Shock Absorber":**
Imagine a car suspension where the damper is not fixed but has an electronically adjustable valve. On a smooth highway, the valve closes (low damping) for a comfortable ride. When the road becomes rough, the valve opens (high damping) to suppress vibration. The damper *cannot lift the car* (no energy added — that would be active), but it *can decide how much energy to absorb* at any instant.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Primary Path** *P(z)* | Acoustic/mechanical transfer path from the noise source to the error sensor |
| **Secondary Path** *S(z)* | Transfer path from the secondary (anti-noise) source to the error sensor |
| **Reference Signal** *x(n)* | Input correlated with the primary noise (e.g., tachometer pulse) |
| **Error Signal** *e(n)* | Residual signal measured at the error microphone |
| **Anti-Noise** *y(n)* | Output produced by the adaptive controller |
| **Adaptation Rate** *μ* | Step size controlling the speed vs. stability trade-off of the LMS algorithm |
| **Zone of Quiet** | Local spatial region in which destructive interference produces meaningful attenuation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary of the Zone of Quiet:** Because cancellation is local (not global), ANC in a car cabin typically achieves **10–20 dB attenuation in a 5–20 cm sphere** around the error microphone, called the *zone of quiet*. Spatially extended quiet zones require *multiple* error microphones and *multi-channel* controllers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Active Cancellation

Sound is a longitudinal pressure wave. The acoustic pressure at any point in space obeys the **linear wave equation** (for small amplitudes):

$$\nabla^2 p - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

Because this equation is **linear**, solutions superimpose. If the primary pressure is $p_1(\vec{r}, t)$ and the secondary (anti-noise) is $p_2(\vec{r}, t)$, the resultant is:

$$p_{\text{total}}(\vec{r}, t) = p_1(\vec{r}, t) + p_2(\vec{r}, t)$$

For perfect cancellation, we require $p_2 = -p_1$, meaning **equal amplitude and opposite phase**. In practice, perfect equality is unachievable because:

- The secondary path $S(z)$ modifies the anti-signal before it reaches the listener
- The reference signal $x(n)$ is not identical to the primary noise at the ear
- Plant dynamics change with temperature, occupant position, window state, etc.

These uncertainties are exactly why **adaptive** algorithms (not fixed filters) are mandatory in automotive ANC.

### 2.2 Block-Diagram Logic of a Feedforward ANC System

A feedforward ANC system (the dominant architecture in road-noise and engine-order cancellation) operates as follows:

1. **Reference sensor** picks up a signal *correlated* (not equal) with the upcoming noise — e.g., an accelerometer on the suspension strut, or a tachometer pulse-train.
2. **Adaptive filter** $\mathbf{w}(n)$ processes $x(n)$ to produce the anti-noise $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
3. **Secondary path** $S(z)$ models the dynamics from the loudspeaker to the error microphone. $S(z)$ distorts the anti-signal.
4. **Error microphone** measures the residual pressure $e(n)$.
5. **LMS / FxLMS update rule** adjusts $\mathbf{w}$ to minimize $E\{e^2(n)\}$.

### 2.3 Why Feedforward? — Coherence Constraint

The **causality** of the secondary path introduces a pure time delay $\Delta$. The reference signal must *lead* the primary noise at the error location by at least $\Delta$. This is achievable in:

- **Engine order control:** Reference = crank-shaft tachometer; noise is periodic and predictable.
- **Road noise control:** Reference = suspension accelerometer; road excitation correlates with cabin pressure through a stable structural path.

In contrast, **pure acoustic feedback** (the noise reaches the reference mic) causes instability. This is solved by:

- Using non-acoustic references (tachometer, accelerometers)
- Adding an **auxiliary noise injection** in the IIR-FxLMS variant
- Using **feedback ANC** (single-channel) when no coherent reference is available

### 2.4 Adaptive Algorithm Hierarchy

| Algorithm | Complexity | Stability | Use Case in Automotive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LMS** | $O(N)$ | Very robust | Theoretical baseline |
| **FxLMS** (Filtered-x LMS) | $O(N \cdot M)$ | Robust, accounts for $S(z)$ | Industrial workhorse for cabin ANC |
| **Normalized LMS (NLMS)** | $O(N)$ | Better convergence for varying $x$ | Time-varying engine RPM |
| **RLS** | $O(N^2)$ | Fastest convergence | Limited use due to numerical cost |
| **FIR vs IIR controllers** | IIR more powerful | IIR has stability concerns | IIR-FxLMS for tonal cancellation |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *only* cheat sheet you should memorize for Part A & Part B derivations on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Anti-noise generated by FIR filter | $N$-tap weight vector |
| 2 | $e(n) = d(n) - s(n) \ast y(n)$ | Error signal = primary $d(n)$ minus secondary output | $\ast$ denotes convolution |
| 3 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | **Plain LMS** weight update | $\mu$ = step size (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}'(n)$ | **FxLMS** weight update | $\mathbf{x}'(n) = \hat{s}(n)\ast\mathbf{x}(n)$ |
| 5 | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | LMS mean-square convergence bound | $\lambda_{\max}$ = largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ |
| 6 | $J(n) = E\{e^2(n)\}$ | Mean-square-error cost function | Minimized by FxLMS |
| 7 | $\text{MSE}_{\infty} = \frac{\mu\,\sigma_d^2}{2 - \mu\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})}$ | Steady-state MSE (small $\mu$) | Defines residual misadjustment |
| 8 | $c_{\text{SA}}(t) = c_{\min} + \alpha\, V_{\text{ctrl}}(t)$ | Semi-active variable damper law | $V_{\text{ctrl}}$ = control voltage |
| 9 | $\eta = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_1^2}{p_1^2 + p_2^2 + 2 p_1 p_2 \cos\phi}\right)$ | Attenuation in dB | $\phi$ = phase error from ideal 180° |
| 10 | $f_{\text{c}} \approx \dfrac{c_{\text{sound}}}{2\pi d_{\text{path}}}$ | Upper useful frequency for ANC | $c_{\text{sound}} \approx 343$ **m/s** |
| 11 | $B(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} b_k z^{-k}$ | FIR model $\hat{S}(z)$ of secondary path | $M$-tap identification |

### 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Practice

- **Equation (4) — FxLMS** is the *heart* of every commercial automotive ANC (Harman, Bose, Hyundai-KIA, Continental). Without filtering the reference through $\hat{S}(z)$, the algorithm diverges.
- **Equation (5)** dictates the *stability ceiling* of the system. In practice engineers pick $\mu$ as 10–30% of the bound for a safety margin.
- **Equation (8)** is the canonical *skyhook* or *ground-hook* semi-active law used in continuously variable semi-active dampers (CDC).
- **Equation (10)** tells us why ANC is restricted to low frequencies: at 1000 Hz, $\lambda \approx 0.34$ m, demanding a controller faster than the wave period; in a 1.5 m cabin, **spatial aliasing** kicks in above ~500 Hz.

### 2.7 Semi-Active Control Strategies in Automotive NVH

| Strategy | Working Principle | Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Skyhook Damper** | Damper force proportional to *absolute* sprung-mass velocity | Primary ride-comfort semi-active control |
| **Ground-hook** | Damper force proportional to *unsprung* velocity | Anti-dive, anti-squat |
| **Variable Orifice** | Damping $c$ changed by solenoid-actuated valve | Mid-tier suspension |
| **MR Damper** | Magneto-rheological fluid changes viscosity in <5 ms | Cadillac Magnetic Ride, Audi adaptive dampers |
| **ER Damper** | Electro-rheological fluid (faster but higher voltage) | Research / niche |

> [!NOTE]
> **In Semi-Active Control, Force is Always Dissipative:** The semi-active damper force $F_d$ must always satisfy $F_d \cdot \dot{x} \leq 0$ (dissipative). The controller can *only* reduce the absolute force magnitude compared to a passive damper; it cannot *add* mechanical energy. This is the formal demarcation from fully active suspensions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the FxLMS Update Rule (Full Working)

We will derive the FxLMS update from first principles. This is a 14-mark *show-all-steps* derivation that KTU examiners expect in Part B.

#### Step 1 — Define the Adaptive Filter Output

Let the adaptive filter $\mathbf{w}(n)$ of length $N$ process the reference signal vector $\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i(n)\,x(n-i)$$

**Why this matters:** $y(n)$ is the candidate anti-noise that the secondary speaker will emit. It is built linearly from $N$ delayed samples of the reference, which is fast enough for a 48 kHz DSP.

#### Step 2 — Define the Error (Residual) Signal

The error microphone measures the residual after superposition with the primary noise $d(n)$ (the unwanted noise at the ear location), modified by the actual secondary path $S(z)$ acting on $y(n)$:

$$e(n) = d(n) - \big[s(n) \ast y(n)\big]$$

where $s(n)$ is the impulse response of the true secondary path. Expanding the convolution:

$$e(n) = d(n) - \sum_{j=0}^{M-1} s_j(n)\,y(n-j)$$

**Why this matters:** The anti-signal $y(n)$ produced by the controller does *not* arrive at the microphone unchanged. The cabin acoustics, speaker response, mic response, and air path all filter it. Ignoring $S(z)$ is the #1 reason naïve LMS diverges in practice.

#### Step 3 — Define the Cost Function

The Mean-Square Error (MSE) cost function quantifies residual acoustic energy:

$$J(n) = E\{e^2(n)\}$$

**Why MSE?** It is convex in $\mathbf{w}$ for linear systems, has a unique global minimum, and is a clean surrogate for the acoustic annoyance the human ear perceives (since SPL is proportional to $\sqrt{E\{p^2\}}$).

#### Step 4 — Compute the Gradient (Steepest Descent Direction)

The gradient of $J$ with respect to each tap weight is:

$$\frac{\partial J(n)}{\partial w_i(n)} = -2\,E\{e(n)\,x(n-i)\}$$

For the standard LMS, we drop the expectation (instantaneous estimate) and use the steepest-descent direction:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) \approx -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Why instantaneous?** The true expectation requires an ensemble average; using the current sample gives a noisy but unbiased estimate, allowing **real-time per-sample updates** at audio rates.

#### Step 5 — Vanilla LMS Update Rule

Moving $\mathbf{w}$ in the direction of the negative gradient (steepest descent):

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{1}{2}\mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}J(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

This is the plain LMS. It is **correct only if $S(z) = 1$** (the secondary path is a wire).

#### Step 6 — Recognize that $S(z) \neq 1$ in Reality

The actual anti-signal reaching the error mic is $s(n)\ast y(n) = s(n)\ast \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$. When we take the gradient of $J$ in the real plant, $e(n)$ depends on $y$ through $S(z)$, so the true gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial J(n)}{\partial w_i(n)} = -2\,E\{e(n)\,[s(n)\ast x(n-i)]\} = -2\,E\{e(n)\,x'_i(n)\}$$

where $x'_i(n) = s(n) \ast x(n-i)$ is the reference signal **filtered through the secondary path** $\hat{S}(z) \approx S(z)$.

#### Step 7 — FxLMS Update Rule (Final Form)

Replacing the true secondary path with its identified FIR estimate $\hat{S}(z)$:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}'(n)$$

with $\mathbf{x}'(n) = \hat{s}(n) \ast \mathbf{x}(n)$.

**Why this is the answer:** The FxLMS is the *closed-form solution* that compensates for the secondary path. It is the difference between a stable, working ANC system and one that howls with positive feedback.

#### Step 8 — Stability Bound Derivation
