The mean-weight vector update under FxLMS has the linearized form (for small $\mu$):

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} - E\{\mathbf{w}(n+1)\} = \big(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\,\mathbf{R}_{x'x'}\big)\big(\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} - E\{\mathbf{w}(n)\}\big)$$

For convergence, all eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R}_{x'x'})$ must lie within the unit circle. Since the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}_{x'x'}$ are $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_N > 0$, we require:

$$-1 < 1 - 2\mu\lambda_i < 1 \quad \forall i$$

which yields the celebrated **LMS stability bound**:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

**Why this matters for exam:** The question *"What is the step-size bound for FxLMS convergence?"* carries 2–3 marks and is guaranteed to appear.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Trade-offs

| Property | LMS | FxLMS | NLMS | RLS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Computation per sample | $2N+1$ | $2N + NM$ | $3N+1$ | $\sim N^2$ |
| Stability margin | Generous | Generous (if $\hat S \approx S$) | Generous | Sensitive to $\lambda$ |
| Convergence speed (1/μ) | Slow | Slow–Medium | Medium | Very fast |
| Numerical robustness | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Needs double precision |
| Used in cabin ANC today | Rarely | **Yes — standard** | Yes | Niche |

### 3.3 Steady-State Misadjustment Derivation (LMS)

The **misadjustment** $\mathcal{M}$ is defined as the ratio of excess MSE to the minimum MSE:

$$\mathcal{M} = \frac{J(\infty) - J_{\min}}{J_{\min}} \approx \frac{\mu}{2}\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})$$

Derivation sketch (commonly tested at 7 marks):

The LMS update introduces a *gradient noise* term. In the steady state, the mean-square deviation of $\mathbf{w}$ from $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ has an average value $E\{\|\mathbf{v}(n)\|^2\} \approx \mu J_{\min} \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})/2$. The excess MSE produced by this misadjustment is $J_{\text{excess}} = \mu J_{\min}\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})/2$. Dividing by $J_{\min}$:

$$\mathcal{M} = \frac{\mu\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})}{2}$$

**Engineering trade-off:** Larger $\mu$ ⇒ faster convergence, but larger misadjustment. The KTU exam question *"Discuss the trade-off between convergence speed and steady-state misadjustment"* is essentially guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single-Channel Feedforward ANC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Primary Noise Source e.g. Engine] --> pathA[Primary Path P of z]
    pathA --> sumNodeA((+))
    secA[Secondary Loudspeaker] --> pathB[Secondary Path S of z]
    pathB --> sumNodeA
    sumNodeA --> errA[Error Microphone at Driver Ear]
    errA --> errSigA[Error Signal e of n]
    refA[Reference Sensor Tachometer] --> adaptF[Adaptive Filter w of n]
    adaptF --> lmsA[FXLMS Update Algorithm]
    errSigA --> lmsA
    pathC[Identified S-hat of z] --> lmsA
    lmsA --> adaptF
    adaptF --> secA
```

> **Reading guide for the student:** Trace the diagram clockwise. The *primary path* brings unwanted noise from the engine to the ear. The *reference sensor* provides a clean, non-acoustic prediction of the noise. The adaptive filter $\mathbf{w}(n)$ produces $y(n)$, which the secondary path $S(z)$ then delivers to the ear, where destructive interference occurs. The error microphone reports the residual $e(n)$ back to the FxLMS algorithm, which then nudges $\mathbf{w}(n)$ to reduce $e(n)$ on the next sample.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Semi-Active Suspension Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rdA[Road Excitation Input] --> suspA[Suspension Sprung Mass m sub s]
    suspA --> velA[Velocity Sensor on Sprung Mass]
    suspA --> damperA[Variable Orifice Damper c sub v]
    suspA --> outputA[Vehicle Body Acceleration]
    velA --> ctrlA[Controller Skyhook Logic]
    cmdA[Mode Selector Switch Comfort or Sport] --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> driverV[Control Voltage V ctrl of t]
    driverV --> damperA
    damperA --> suspA
```

> **Reading guide:** The semi-active system has no actuator that *adds* force between body and ground; it only *modulates* the dissipative force of the damper. The controller uses the sprung-mass velocity (skyhook strategy) and a driver-selected mode to vary the damping $c_v(t)$ in real time, typically within 5–10 ms.

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithm Decision Logic for KTU ANC Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA{Reference Signal Available and Coherent}
    startA -- YES --> fwdA[Use FEEDFORWARD ANC with FX LMS]
    startA -- NO --> fbA[Use FEEDBACK ANC Single Channel]
    fwdA --> multA{Number of Error Zones}
    multA -- One --> singleA[Single Channel FXLMS]
    multA -- Multiple --> multiA[Multichannel FXLMS N by M]
    fbA --> internalA[Internal Model Control IMC Based]
    fbA --> noteA[Less effective at LF due to delay]
    singleA --> outputA[Output Anti Noise to Cabin Speakers]
    multiA --> outputA
    internalA --> outputA
```

### 4.4 Physical Picture — ANC Head-Unit Topology (Text Schematic)

| Component | Location in Vehicle | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Reference accelerometer | Engine block, driveshaft, suspension knuckle | Provides coherent non-acoustic reference |
| ANC DSP | Head-unit / amplifier housing | Runs FxLMS in real time at 48 kHz |
| Error microphones | Driver headrest, headliner above passenger seats | Measure residual cabin pressure |
| Secondary speakers | Existing cabin speakers (rear deck / door) | Emit anti-noise |
| Secondary path model $\hat S(z)$ | Pre-identified during commissioning, updated online | Compensates for speaker-microplant dynamics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Sampling Rule:** For an $N$-speaker system in a cabin, the Nyquist limit on the spatial dimension imposes an upper limit of roughly $f_{\max} \approx c_{\text{sound}}/(2 d_{\min})$ where $d_{\min}$ is the smallest distance between speakers. For typical cabin speaker spacing of 0.5 m, this gives $f_{\max} \approx 340$ Hz, explaining why ANC is restricted to low-frequency rumble.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Active Noise Control (ANC). State the physical principle on which it operates.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Active Noise Control is an electro-acoustic technique for reducing unwanted sound by introducing a secondary, electronically generated sound wave of equal amplitude but opposite phase, so that the two waves superpose and produce destructive interference at the listener's location. The fundamental principle is the **linear superposition of acoustic pressure fields**, governed by the linearity of the wave equation. The anti-noise must be precisely matched in amplitude and 180° out of phase with the primary noise for perfect cancellation; in practice, adaptive algorithms track the residual mismatch in real time. *(3 marks: definition 1, principle 1, practical note 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the LMS stability bound. What happens if the step size $\mu$ is chosen too large?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The LMS algorithm is mean-square stable when the step size $\mu$ satisfies the inequality

$$0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the reference-signal autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$. If $\mu$ is chosen too large (exceeding this bound), the weight vector $\mathbf{w}(n)$ diverges, the error signal $e(n)$ grows without bound, and the system produces *positive feedback howling* — a self-amplifying noise rather than cancellation. In practice, engineers choose $\mu$ at 10–30% of $2/\lambda_{\max}$ to maintain a safety margin. *(2 marks: bound, 1 mark: consequence)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the working of a single-channel **feedforward Active Noise Control** system. Identify each block and explain the role of the secondary path estimate $\hat{S}(z)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Filtered-x LMS (FxLMS)** update rule starting from the cost function $J = E\{e^2(n)\}$. State clearly the difference between plain LMS and FxLMS and explain why FxLMS is required in practice. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Working — 7 marks**

*Block diagram (4 marks for correct diagram, 3 marks for explanation):*

The feedforward ANC consists of the following blocks connected in a closed loop:

1. **Reference sensor** (microphone, accelerometer, or tachometer) — produces a signal $x(n)$ correlated with the upcoming primary noise.
2. **Adaptive filter** $\mathbf{w}(n)$ — an FIR structure of order $N$ that processes $\mathbf{x}(n)$ to produce anti-noise $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
3. **Secondary source** (loudspeaker) — emits the anti-noise into the cabin.
4. **Secondary path** $S(z)$ — the actual transfer function from speaker input to error-microphone output. It is *unknown* but stable.
5. **Error microphone** — measures residual pressure $e(n)$ at the desired zone of quiet.
6. **Secondary path estimate** $\hat{S}(z)$ — an FIR model identified offline (or online) that approximates $S(z)$ to within a few dB.
7. **FxLMS update block** — computes $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}'(n)$, where $\mathbf{x}'(n) = \hat{s}(n) \ast \mathbf{x}(n)$.

*Explanation (3 marks):*
The reference sensor "looks ahead" of the acoustic path — this satisfies the **causality requirement** that anti-noise cannot be generated before the disturbance is sensed. The $\hat{S}(z)$ filter is the key reason FxLMS works: it pre-compensates the reference signal so that the gradient descent direction is correct in the *acoustic* domain, not the *electrical* domain. Without it, the closed-loop system has an unmodelled delay and diverges. In cabin applications, the reference is often a structural accelerometer (road noise) or a tachometer (engine order), never an acoustic mic in the cabin (to avoid acoustic feedback into the reference).

---

**(b) Derivation of FxLMS — 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — Cost function:*

$$J(n) = E\{e^2(n)\}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark) — Express $e(n)$ including the secondary path:*

$$e(n) = d(n) - \sum_{j=0}^{M-1} s_j(n)\,y(n-j) = d(n) - s(n)\ast y(n)$$

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitute $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$:*

$$e(n) = d(n) - s(n)\ast \big[\mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\big]$$

*Step 4 (1 mark) — Take gradient:*

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_i} = -2\,E\{e(n)\,[s(n)\ast x(n-i)]\} = -2\,E\{e(n)\,x'_i(n)\}$$

where $x'_i(n) = s(n)\ast x(n-i)$.

*Step 5 (1 mark) — Use instantaneous estimate and gradient descent:*

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{1}{2}\mu\,\hat{\nabla}_{\mathbf{w}}J = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}'(n)$$

*Step 6 (1 mark) — Replace $s$ with estimate $\hat s$ and state final equation:*

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\big[\hat{s}(n)\ast \mathbf{x}(n)\big]}$$

*Step 7 (1 mark) — Distinguish LMS from FxLMS:*

In **plain LMS**, the update uses $\mathbf{x}(n)$ directly, which is correct only when the secondary path is a pure wire ($S(z)=1$). In **FxLMS**, the reference is *pre-filtered* through $\hat{S}(z)$ to produce $\mathbf{x}'(n)$, which compensates for the dynamics of the actual secondary path. FxLMS is required in practice because the loudspeaker-cabin-microphone path is far from ideal: it has frequency-dependent magnitude, phase, and pure delay. Without this compensation, plain LMS diverges due to the unmodelled secondary path.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **passive**, **semi-active**, and **active** vibration control systems. Use the dissipative-force inequality to formalize the boundary between semi-active and active. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semi-active suspension uses a skyhook damper strategy. Derive the relationship between the desired damping coefficient $c_{\text{sky}}$ and the variable damping $c_v(t)$. What is the typical time constant of an MR-damper-actuated semi-active suspension? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Three-Way Classification — 7 marks**

| Type | Energy Source | Authority | Force Sign |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | None (uses stored strain energy) | None | Always opposes motion; $F \cdot \dot x \le 0$ |
| **Semi-Active** | External *electrical* (valve coil) — no mechanical injection | Adjusts $c$ in real time | $F_d \cdot \dot x \le 0$ at all times |
| **Active** | External *mechanical* (hydraulic, electromagnetic actuator) | Full | $F_a \cdot \dot x$ may be positive (energy added) |

*Semi-active force condition (3 marks):*

A semi-active device can only dissipate energy, never inject mechanical energy. Formally:

$$F_d(t)\,\dot x(t) \leq 0 \quad \forall t$$

For a variable damper with force $F_d = c_v(t)\,\dot x$, this requires $c_v(t) \ge 0$ at all times. The controller can only modulate the *magnitude* of dissipation, never its sign. An active actuator violates this inequality because $F_a$ can have a component that *adds* energy to the system (e.g., regenerative in-phase force).

*Examples (4 marks):* Passive — conventional shock absorber with fixed orifice; Semi-active — Cadillac Magnetic Ride (MR damper), Audi adaptive damper, Bose suspension (research); Active — hydro-pneumatic active suspension ( Citroën Hydractive, Mercedes Active Body Control), electromagnetic active mounts.

---

**(b) Skyhook Semi-Active Law — 7 marks**

*Derivation (5 marks):*

The **skyhook** strategy imagines a fictitious damper connected between the *sprung mass* and an *inertial sky* (i.e., absolute reference frame). The ideal skyhook damper force is:

$$F_{\text{sky}} = c_{\text{sky}}\,\dot{x}_s$$

where $\dot{x}_s$ is the absolute velocity of the sprung mass and $c_{\text{sky}}$ is a constant chosen for ride comfort (typically 5–20 kN·s/m for a passenger car).

A real semi-active damper connects sprung and unsprung masses. Its actual force is:

$$F_d = c_v(t)\,(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$$

To make the real damper *behave like* the skyhook damper, we choose $c_v(t)$ such that the energy dissipation rate of the real damper matches that of the ideal skyhook, **subject to the dissipative constraint** $c_v(t) \ge 0$. The continuous control law is:

$$c_v(t) = \begin{cases} c_{\max} & \text{if } \dot{x}_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) > 0 \\[4pt] c_{\min} & \text{if } \dot{x}_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) \le 0 \end{cases}$$

This is a *bang-bang* (on-off) controller. A smoother alternative is a clipped-linear law:

$$c_v(t) = \begin{cases} c_{\min} & F_d\,\dot{x}_s \le 0 \text{ (skyhook wants to add energy — clamp)} \\[4pt] c_{\text{sky}}\,\dfrac{\dot{x}_s}{\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Time constant (2 marks):*
For an **MR (magnetorheological) damper**, the field response time is **5–10 ms**, allowing control bandwidth up to ~100 Hz. For a **variable-orifice electrohydraulic damper**, response is **10–20 ms**, sufficient for ride control in the 1–5 Hz body-bounce and 8–15 Hz wheel-hop modes but marginal for higher-frequency vibration isolation.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\hat{S}(z)$ in the FxLMS update.** Writing plain LMS ($\mathbf{w} + \mu e \mathbf{x}$) instead of $\mathbf{w} + \mu e \mathbf{x}'$ costs 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing reference signal and primary signal.** The reference sensor measures a *correlated proxy* (e.g., engine RPM), not the noise at the ear. Failing to state this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating the stability bound without the eigenvalue context.** Writing $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$ is correct only if you say *which* $\lambda_{\max}$ (largest eigenvalue of the autocorrelation matrix of the *filtered* reference $\mathbf{x}'$, not the raw $\mathbf{x}$).
> 4. **Semi-active force sign error.** Writing $F_d \cdot \dot{x} \ge 0$ instead of $\le 0$ reverses the physics. The semi-active device *dissipates*, never *injects*.
> 5. **Treating ANC and acoustic absorption as equivalent.** Absorption is a passive, broadband, material-based damping; ANC is an active, narrowband, wave-cancellation method. Examiners penalize this conflation.
> 6. **Skipping units in the formula sheet.** A step size is dimensionless, a damping coefficient is N·s/m, and a primary path is unitless gain. Missing units = –1 mark per table.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Fire):**

- ANC = **wave cancellation** via superposition, not absorption. Best for **frequencies < 500 Hz**.
- The **anti-noise** must be **equal amplitude, opposite phase** to the primary noise.
- The **secondary path** $S(z)$ is *unknown but stable*; we model it as $\hat{S}(z)$ to design the controller.
- **FxLMS** = LMS update with reference *pre-filtered* by $\hat{S}(z)$: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}'(n)$.
- **LMS stability bound:** $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$ (filtered reference).
- **Misadjustment** $\mathcal{M} \approx (\mu/2)\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})$; faster convergence ⇔ larger misadjustment.
- **Reference signal** must lead the primary noise by at least the secondary-path delay (causality).
- **Zone of quiet** is a *local* region (10–20 cm around error mic) of meaningful attenuation; multiple channels expand it.
- **Semi-active control** = controllable damper with $F_d \cdot \dot{x} \le 0$ *always*; never adds mechanical energy.
- **Skyhook** semi-active law switches damping between $c_{\max}$ and $c_{\min}$ based on $\dot{x}_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$ sign.
- **MR damper** response time ≈ 5–10 ms; **variable orifice** ≈ 10–20 ms.
- **Active** control can violate $F \cdot \dot x \le 0$ and inject mechanical energy.
- **Adaptive filter length** $N$ typically 64–512 taps in cabin ANC; trade-off with computation.
- **Step-size practical range:** 10–30% of $2/\lambda_{\max}$ for safety margin.
- **Spatial aliasing** limits useful ANC bandwidth to roughly $c_{\text{sound}}/(2 d_{\text{speaker-spacing}})$.
- **Tonal engine-order cancellation** uses tachometer as reference; **road-noise ANC** uses suspension accelerometers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anechoic Chamber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anechoic Chamber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anechoic Chamber (Free-Field Room):** An enclosed testing environment whose internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, and floor) are treated with highly absorbent materials so that the boundaries reflect virtually **0%** of incident acoustic energy, thereby emulating the acoustic behaviour of an unbounded outdoor free field for accurate sound power, transmission loss, and source radiation measurements.

In the context of **Automotive NVH**, an anechoic chamber is the benchmark reference environment in which a vehicle, powertrain, or component is acoustically characterised without contamination from room reverberation. The chamber guarantees that the sound pressure level measured at a microphone is purely the **direct-field** contribution from the source, satisfying the ISO 3745 (precision method), ISO 3744 (engineering method), and ISO 26101 (comparison method) requirements.

### Classification of Anechoic Chambers

| Type | Treatment | Floor | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Anechoic** | All 6 surfaces | Suspended wire-mesh floor (reflective or absorptive) | Component-level characterisation, speaker testing |
| **Semi-Anechoic** | 5 surfaces (walls + ceiling) | Hard, reflective concrete | Vehicle pass-by noise, exterior sound quality, ISO 362 |

> [!NOTE]
> Automotive NVH labs typically deploy **Semi-Anechoic Chambers (SAC)** because a reflective floor simulates the actual driving condition of a vehicle on a road, while ISO 362 vehicle pass-by noise measurements explicitly demand a hard reflective floor.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Black Velvet Movie Set":**
> Imagine standing inside a room where every wall, the ceiling, and the floor are coated with soft, jagged foam wedges resembling black velvet. When you clap your hands, instead of hearing multiple echoes bouncing back (as you would in your bedroom), you hear only one crisp, dry clap that fades instantly into silence. The chamber "swallows" every echo just as black velvet absorbs every photon of light on a movie set, leaving only the direct sound from the source. This is exactly what an anechoic chamber does for noise measurement — it removes all reflections so that the microphone hears **only** the source under test.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

- **Cut-off frequency ($f_c$):** Lowest frequency at which the chamber behaves as a free field. Must be **below** the lowest frequency of interest.
- **Wedge length ($L$):** Geometric depth of absorbing wedges, typically **0.5 m to 1.5 m**.
- **Absorption coefficient ($\alpha$):** Fraction of incident acoustic power absorbed, ideally $\alpha \geq 0.99$ above $f_c$.
- **Speed of sound (reference):** $c = \mathbf{343 \ m/s}$ at $20^\circ C$ in air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module-3 evaluation, students must clearly differentiate between **Full** and **Semi-anechoic** chambers and explain the role of the **wedge cut-off frequency** in determining the usable bandwidth of the facility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Free-Field Propagation

When a sound source radiates into a free field, the acoustic energy spreads geometrically. For a **monopole (point) source** radiating acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions, the intensity at distance $r$ is:

$$
I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

Because the wavefront is spherical, the intensity falls off as $1/r^{2}$. Since sound pressure is related to intensity via $I = p^{2}/(\rho c)$, the sound pressure level (SPL) at distance $r$ is:

$$
L_{p} = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11 \quad \text{(dB re 1 pW)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "-11 dB" constant** arises from the spherical spreading term $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 10.99 \approx 11$ dB. Memorise this — it is a **high-yield KTU formula**.

For a **line source** (incoherent cylindrical wave, e.g., a long exhaust pipe):

$$
L_{p} = L_{W}^{\prime} - 10\log_{10}(r) - 8 \quad \text{(dB re 1 pW/m)}
$$

The $10\log_{10}(r)$ fall-off (instead of $20\log_{10}(r)$) is a **directivity giveaway** that examiners love to test.

## 2.2 Why 6 dB per Doubling of Distance?

When $r$ doubles ($r \rightarrow 2r$):

$$
\Delta L_{p} = 20\log_{10}(2r) - 20\log_{10}(r) = 20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02 \ \text{dB}
$$

This is the famous **inverse-square law** — SPL drops by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from a point source.

## 2.3 Wedge Geometry & Cut-off Frequency

The most common absorber in an anechoic chamber is the **wedge absorber** (also called a pyramidal absorber). It is constructed from fibre-glass or melamine foam shaped as a long slender pyramid. Its operating principle is:

1. The incident acoustic wave enters the narrow tip.
2. The wave undergoes multiple reflections inside the wedge, with each reflection transferring energy to the fibre matrix (viscous + thermal dissipation).
3. By the time the residual wave reaches the rigid backing, its amplitude is negligible.

The **cut-off frequency** (frequency below which the wedge fails to absorb) is empirically:

$$
f_{c} = \frac{c}{4L}
$$

where $L$ is the wedge length. This is the same formula as a **quarter-wave resonator**, because the wedge is acoustically equivalent to a tapered transmission line whose characteristic impedance gradually rises from $\rho c$ (air) to a near-infinite value at the rigid base.

### Worked Numerical (Board-Favourite Type)

For $L = 1 \ \text{m}$ wedge:

$$
f_{c} = \frac{343}{4 \times 1} \approx 85.75 \ \text{Hz}
$$

For $L = 1.5 \ \text{m}$ wedge (premium automotive facility):

$$
f_{c} = \frac{343}{4 \times 1.5} \approx 57.17 \ \text{Hz}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Doubling $L$ halves $f_c$ but quadruples chamber volume (and cost). Most automotive SACs use $L \approx 1 \ \text{m}$ (cut-off ~86 Hz), sufficient for engine firing harmonics above ~100 Hz.

## 2.4 Absorption Coefficient & Impedance

The boundary performance is quantified by the **normal-incidence absorption coefficient**:

$$
\alpha = 1 - \vert R \vert^{2}
$$

where the **reflection coefficient** $R$ is:

$$
R = \frac{Z_{2} - Z_{1}}{Z_{2} + Z_{1}}
$$

with $Z_{1} = \rho_{1} c_{1}$ (impedance of air) and $Z_{2}$ being the effective impedance presented by the wedge at the boundary. For a perfect anechoic wall, $Z_{2} \rightarrow \rho_{1} c_{1}$ (matched impedance), giving $R = 0$ and $\alpha = 1$.

## 2.5 Room Constant (Diffuse-Field Background Indicator)

The room constant $R$ quantifies how "dead" a partially treated enclosure is:

$$
R = \frac{S \cdot \bar{\alpha}}{1 - \bar{\alpha}} \quad \text{(m² Sabin)}
$$

where $S$ is the total surface area and $\bar{\alpha}$ is the area-weighted average absorption coefficient. For a true anechoic chamber, $R \rightarrow \infty$ (no reverberant energy).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — every entry is a potential 2-mark formula question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{p} = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ | Point source SPL in free field | dB re 1 pW |
| 2 | $L_{p} = L_{W}^{\prime} - 10\log_{10}(r) - 8$ | Line source SPL in free field | dB re 1 pW/m |
| 3 | $\Delta L = -6 \ \text{dB}$ per doubling of $r$ | Inverse-square law for point source | dB |
| 4 | $\Delta L = -3 \ \text{dB}$ per doubling of $r$ | Inverse-first-power law for line source | dB |
| 5 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{c}{4L}$ | Wedge cut-off frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $\alpha = 1 - \vert R \vert^{2}$ | Absorption coefficient from reflection coeff. | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{Z_{2}-Z_{1}}{Z_{2}+Z_{1}}$ | Reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_{c} = \dfrac{S\bar{\alpha}}{1-\bar{\alpha}}$ | Room constant | m² Sabin |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$ | Point-source intensity | W/m² |
| 10 | $W = 4\pi r^{2} \cdot I$ | Sound power from intensity | W |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Automotive NVH

- **Sound Power Determination (ISO 3745):** Sound power of engines, electric motors, HVAC blowers, and tyres is measured in anechoic chambers — the basis for homologation and supplier comparison.
- **Sound Quality Studio:** Subjective jury evaluations are conducted in full anechoic chambers with controlled binaural/head-tracked playback.
- **Transfer Function (FRF) Measurement:** Microphone and accelerometer calibrations rely on free-field reciprocity in an anechoic environment.
- **Pass-By Noise (ISO 362):** Semi-anechoic chambers replicate the road-reflective ground plane for certified vehicle exterior noise testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverse-Square Law for a Point Source

**Starting physical premise:** A monopole source emits total acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions into a lossless medium.

**Step 1 — Intensity at distance $r$:**
The power passes through a sphere of radius $r$ whose surface area is $4\pi r^{2}$. By energy conservation:

$$
I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert intensity to RMS sound pressure:**

$$
I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c} \ \ \Rightarrow \ \ p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\rho c I} = \frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\frac{\rho c W}{4\pi}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as sound pressure level (dB re $20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$):**

$$
L_{p} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{0}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right)
$$

where $I_{0} = 10^{-12} \ \text{W/m}^{2}$ is the reference intensity and $L_{W} = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{0})$ with $W_{0} = 10^{-12} \ \text{W}$.

**Step 4 — Substituting Step 1:**

$$
L_{p} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r^{2} I_{0}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_{0}}\right) - 10\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(r)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical simplification of the geometric constant:**

$$
10\log_{10}(4\pi) = 10\log_{10}(12.566) \approx 10.99 \ \text{dB} \approx 11 \ \text{dB}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,L_{p} = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11 \ \text{dB re 1 pW}\,}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The "-20$\log_{10}(r)$" term describes geometric spreading; the "-11 dB" term represents the spherical area constant $4\pi$. Removing this constant (i.e., testing outside the chamber) is the entire purpose of an anechoic facility.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 6 dB per Doubling Rule

Starting from the free-field equation derived above, evaluate $L_{p}$ at two distances $r_{1}$ and $r_{2} = 2r_{1}$:

$$
L_{p}(r_{1}) = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r_{1}) - 11
$$

$$
L_{p}(2r_{1}) = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(2r_{1}) - 11 = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r_{1}) - 20\log_{10}(2) - 11
$$

Subtracting:

$$
L_{p}(2r_{1}) - L_{p}(r_{1}) = -20\log_{10}(2) = -6.02 \ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta L_{p} = -6 \ \text{dB} \ \text{per doubling of distance (point source)}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wedge Cut-off Frequency

**Model:** Treat the wedge as a tapered transmission line of length $L$ terminated by a rigid wall (acoustic short circuit, $Z = \infty$). The input impedance at the tip is:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = -j Z_{0}\cot(kL)
$$

where $k = 2\pi f / c$ is the wavenumber. The tip impedance equals the air impedance $\rho c$ when:

$$
\rho c = Z_{0}\cot(kL) \ \ \text{and}\ \ Z_{0}\cot(kL) = \rho c
$$

The condition $Z_{\text{in}} = \rho c$ (perfect impedance matching → $\alpha = 1$) requires:

$$
kL = \frac{\pi}{2} \ \ \Rightarrow \ \ \frac{2\pi f_{c}}{c} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

Solving:

$$
f_{c} = \frac{c}{4L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{c} = \dfrac{c}{4L}\,}
$$

**Numerical check** for $L = 1 \ \text{m}$:

$$
f_{c} = \frac{343}{4 \times 1} = 85.75 \ \text{Hz}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Absorption Coefficient from Reflection Coefficient

**Starting definition:** The reflection coefficient at a boundary between medium 1 (air) and medium 2 (effective impedance of the wedge surface) is:

$$
R = \frac{Z_{2} - Z_{1}}{Z_{2} + Z_{1}}
$$

The reflected intensity is $\vert R \vert^{2}$ times the incident intensity (because intensity is proportional to pressure-squared). Therefore, the fraction of power **absorbed** is:

$$
\alpha = 1 - \vert R \vert^{2}
$$

**Boundary cases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{2} = Z_{1} \ \text{(matched)} &\Rightarrow R = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = 1 \quad \text{(perfect anechoic wall)} \\
Z_{2} \rightarrow \infty \ \text{(rigid)} &\Rightarrow R = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0 \quad \text{(perfect reflector)} \\
Z_{2} \rightarrow 0 \ \text{(pressure-release)} &\Rightarrow R = -1 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A perfect anechoic chamber has every wall behaving like $Z_{2} = \rho c$, achieved in practice by making the wedge long and tapered so its effective impedance at the tip matches air over a wide frequency band starting from $f_c$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Chamber-Type Selection for Automotive Tests

| Measurement Task | Recommended Chamber | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Engine sound power (ISO 3745) | Full Anechoic | No ground reflection contamination |
| Vehicle pass-by noise (ISO 362) | Semi-Anechoic | Reflective floor simulates road |
| Powertrain NVH (cold/hot) | Full or Semi-Anechoic | Depends on whether ground path is part of signature |
| Speaker / Headphone characterisation | Full Anechoic | Required for free-field reciprocity calibration |
| Modal testing of body-in-white | Any with low background | Absorption not critical, vibration isolation matters |
| Tyre/road noise on rolling road | Semi-Anechoic + Drum | Simulates actual road-tread interaction |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** A common KTU trick question is *"Why does ISO 362 mandate a hard floor in the test chamber?"* — Answer: The standard models the microphone location 7.5 m from the vehicle centreline on a road, where ground reflection doubles the SPL of low-frequency components (coherent addition). Removing the floor would under-predict the legal pass-by noise level by up to 3 dB at 100 Hz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Semi-Anechoic Chamber for Automotive NVH

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[NVH Test Article e.g., Vehicle Powertrain Engine] --> B[Vibration Isolators Springs or Air Mounts]
    B --> C[Test Cell Hard Reflective Floor]
    C --> D[Microphone Array Free Field Hemisphere at r 1 to 7 m]
    D --> E[Multi-channel Data Acquisition System]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning Amplifiers and Filters]
    F --> G[Workstation with NVH Software e.g., LMS Test Lab]
    G --> H[Computed Outputs SPL, Sound Power, FRF, Sound Quality Metrics]
    I[Wall Wedges Fibre glass L 1 m] --> C
    J[Ceiling Wedges Same Material] --> C
    K[Background Noise Duct Silencers] --> C
    L[Temperature and Humidity Control 20 C 50 RH] --> C
    M[Access Door Sealed Double Wedge Lined] --> C
```

## 4.2 Acoustic Energy Flow Inside the Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sound Source Test Article] -->|Direct Path Spherical Spreading| M[Microphone]
    S -->|Wall Reflection Should be ZERO in Anechoic| W[Wedge Treated Boundary]
    W -->|Absorbed and Dissipated as Heat| H[Thermal and Viscous Losses]
    S -->|Floor Reflection Allowed in Semi Anechoic| F[Hard Reflective Floor]
    F -->|Reflected Wave Coherent Addition| M
    M --> DAQ[Data Acquisition SPL vs Frequency]
```

## 4.3 Wedge Absorber Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Sound Wave at Wedge Tip] --> B[Tapers and Enters Narrow Aperture]
    B --> C[Multiple Internal Reflections Inside Pyramid Cavity]
    C --> D[Energy Converted to Heat via Viscous and Thermal Damping in Fibrous Matrix]
    D --> E[Negligible Wave Reaches Rigid Backing]
    E --> F[Effective Absorption Coefficient alpha greater than 0.99]
    F --> G[Free Field Condition Established Above Cutoff f c]
```

## 4.4 Cut-off Frequency vs Wedge Length — Design Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Wedge Length L 0.5 m] --> R1[Cutoff f c 343 divided by 2 equals 171.5 Hz]
    L2[Wedge Length L 1.0 m] --> R2[Cutoff f c 85.75 Hz]
    L3[Wedge Length L 1.5 m] --> R4[Cutoff f c 57.17 Hz]
    L4[Wedge Length L 2.0 m] --> R5[Cutoff f c 42.88 Hz]
    R1 --> DEC1[Compact Chamber Suitable for Engine Tones Above 200 Hz]
    R2 --> DEC2[Standard Automotive SAC ISO 3744 Compliant]
    R4 --> DEC3[Premium NVH Facility Electric Powertrain Below 100 Hz]
    R5 --> DEC4[Research Grade Subwoofer and Tyre Noise Below 50 Hz]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compiler Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` equivalent: `L1`, `R1`, `DEC1`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone node names.
> - Inside quoted labels, only raw uppercase alphanumeric text is used — no bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Define an anechoic chamber. Distinguish between full and semi-anechoic chambers with one automotive application each.**

**Model Answer (Board-Key Format):**

> An **anechoic chamber** is a specially constructed room whose internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor) are lined with highly sound-absorbent materials so that virtually all acoustic reflections are eliminated, creating a free-field environment in which the sound pressure level measured at a microphone depends only on the direct path from the source.

> A **full anechoic chamber** treats all six internal surfaces (including the floor) with absorptive wedges; the floor is replaced by a tensioned wire mesh under which the absorber continues. It is used for **component-level sound power measurement** (e.g., electric motors, gearboxes, HVAC blowers) where contamination by any reflection is unacceptable.

> A **semi-anechoic chamber** treats only five surfaces (walls + ceiling), leaving a **hard reflective floor** that simulates the road. It is used for **vehicle pass-by noise testing (ISO 362)** because the standard mandates ground-plane reflection.

> *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction + 1 mark application)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**State and explain the inverse-square law for sound propagation in a free field. Mention its significance in anechoic chamber measurements.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **inverse-square law** states that the acoustic intensity $I$ from a point source falls off in proportion to the square of the distance $r$ from the source, i.e., $I \propto 1/r^{2}$. Consequently, the sound pressure level drops by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from the source.

> In an anechoic chamber, the absence of reflections ensures that the SPL measured at the microphone follows this theoretical law exactly. Verification of the -6 dB per doubling rule (using a reference sound source, e.g., pink-noise loudspeaker) is the standard **qualification procedure** for an anechoic facility as per ISO 3745.

> *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark statement + 1 mark derivation hint + 1 mark significance)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the free-field sound pressure level equation for a point source in terms of sound power level and distance. Show that the SPL falls by 6 dB when the distance is doubled. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A small electric motor is tested in a semi-anechoic chamber and found to have a sound power level of **78 dB re 1 pW**. Calculate the SPL at distances of **1 m, 2 m, 4 m, and 8 m** from the motor. Comment on the validity of using the free-field equation at these distances. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting with acoustic power conservation over a sphere of radius $r$:

$$
W = I \cdot 4\pi r^{2} \ \ \Rightarrow \ \ I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

Expressing both quantities in decibels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{W} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_{0}}\right) \\
L_{I} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L_{p} = L_{I}$ (because $I_{0} = p_{0}^{2}/\rho c$ is consistent):

$$
L_{p} = L_{W} - 10\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(r)
$$

Using $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11$:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{p} = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11 \ \text{dB}\,}
$$

Doubling $r \rightarrow 2r$ changes the SPL by:

$$
\Delta L_{p} = -20\log_{10}(2) = -6.02 \ \text{dB} \approx -6 \ \text{dB}
$$

Hence **SPL drops 6 dB per doubling of distance**. *(Full marks: 3 marks derivation + 2 marks -6 dB proof + 2 marks final boxed equation and justification)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation:**

Given $L_{W} = 78$ dB re 1 pW and the free-field formula:

$$
L_{p}(r) = 78 - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11
$$

**At $r = 1$ m:**

$$
L_{p}(1) = 78 - 0 - 11 = 67 \ \text{dB}
$$

**At $r = 2$ m:**

$$
L_{p}(2) = 78 - 20\log_{10}(2) - 11 = 78 - 6.02 - 11 \approx 61 \ \text{dB}
$$

**At $r = 4$ m:**

$$
L_{p}(4) = 78 - 20\log_{10}(4) - 11 = 78 - 12.04 - 11 \approx 55 \ \text{dB}
$$

**At $r = 8$ m:**

$$
L_{p}(8) = 78 - 20\log_{10}(8) - 11 = 78 - 18.06 - 11 \approx 49 \ \text{dB}
$$

**Validity Discussion (2 marks):**

- The free-field equation assumes the source behaves as a **point monopole**. For a real electric motor of typical dimensions (10–20 cm), this assumption holds only when $r \gg$ source size, ideally $r \geq 1 \ \text{m}$.
- The chamber cut-off frequency must lie **below** the lowest frequency emitted by the motor; otherwise near-field effects dominate and the -6 dB rule fails.
- At $r = 8$ m, the predicted SPL (49 dB) approaches the **anechoic chamber background noise floor** (typically 10–15 dBA). Validity is therefore limited by signal-to-noise ratio at large distances.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget to **subtract the -11 dB spherical constant**. A common mistake is writing $L_{p} = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r)$, which would give $L_{p}(1) = 78$ dB — wrong by 11 dB! Always show the substitution step $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11$ in your derivation.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **wedge absorber**. Derive the expression for the cut-off frequency of an anechoic chamber wedge and compute its value for a wedge length of **0.8 m**. Take the speed of sound as $c = 343 \ \text{m/s}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An automotive OEM wants to qualify a new semi-anechoic chamber for ISO 3744 sound power measurements. The chamber has total internal surface area $S = 480 \ \text{m}^{2}$ (excluding the floor, which is reflective). The measured area-averaged absorption coefficient above the cut-off is $\bar{\alpha} = 0.96$. Calculate the **room constant** $R_{c}$ and comment on whether the chamber satisfies the free-field criterion of ISO 3744. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Wedge Principle and Cut-off Frequency Derivation:**

A wedge absorber is a tapered porous structure (typically glass-fibre or melamine foam) mounted with its broad base against the rigid wall and its narrow tip pointing into the room. An incident acoustic wave:

1. Enters the wide impedance-matched tip with negligible reflection.
2. Travels into progressively narrowing channels, undergoing multiple glancing reflections.
3. Loses energy at each reflection through viscous friction against the fibre strands and through thermal exchange with the matrix.
4. Reaches the rigid backing with negligible residual amplitude.

Modelling the wedge as a tapered transmission line of length $L$ terminated in a rigid wall ($Z \rightarrow \infty$), the input impedance at the tip is:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = -jZ_{0}\cot(kL)
$$

For perfect impedance matching ($Z_{\text{in}} = \rho c$, the impedance of air) — corresponding to $\alpha = 1$:

$$
kL = \frac{\pi}{2} \ \ \Rightarrow \ \ \frac{2\pi f_{c}}{c} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{c} = \frac{c}{4L}\,}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation for $L = 0.8 \ \text{m}$:**

$$
f_{c} = \frac{343}{4 \times 0.8} = \frac{343}{3.2} = 107.19 \ \text{Hz}
$$

The chamber will provide free-field behaviour for **all frequencies above ~107 Hz**, which is adequate for typical engine firing orders but marginal for tyre/road noise below 100 Hz.

> **Mark Split:** 3 marks principle + 2 marks derivation + 1 mark formula + 1 mark numerical answer.

**Part (b) — Room Constant Calculation:**

The room constant is given by:

$$
R_{c} = \frac{S \cdot \bar{\alpha}}{1 - \bar{\alpha}}
$$

Substituting $S = 480 \ \text{m}^{2}$ and $\bar{\alpha} = 0.96$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{c} &= \frac{480 \times 0.96}{1 - 0.96} \\
     &= \frac{460.8}{0.04} \\
     &= 11520 \ \text{m}^{2} \ \text{Sabin}
\end{aligned}
$$

**ISO 3744 Compliance Check (2 marks):**

ISO 3744 requires that the **reverberant sound field** at the measurement surface contribute **less than 0.5 dB** to the measured SPL compared to the direct field. This is typically achieved when $R_{c}$ is large relative to the measurement sphere surface area $S_{m} = 4\pi r_{m}^{2}$.

For a typical measurement radius $r_{m} = 1 \ \text{m}$, $S_{m} = 12.57 \ \text{m}^{2}$. Since $R_{c} = 11520 \ \text{m}^{2} \gg S_{m}$, the reverberant contribution is negligible (much less than 0.5 dB), and the chamber qualifies for ISO 3744 engineering-grade measurements.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks formula + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark arithmetic + 2 marks compliance discussion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing $R_{c}$ (room constant) with $R$ (reflection coefficient).** They are entirely different quantities. Use subscript $c$ or write "room constant" clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the parentheses** in $R_{c} = S\bar{\alpha}/(1-\bar{\alpha})$. A common wrong answer is $R_{c} = S\bar{\alpha}/(1 - \bar{\alpha})$ evaluated as $S(\bar{\alpha}/(1-\bar{\alpha}))$ which is numerically the same, but writing it ambiguously as $(S\bar{\alpha}/1) - \bar{\alpha}$ will be marked **wrong**.
> 3. **Not stating the ISO 3744 acceptance criterion.** Always quote the standard number and the threshold (reverberant contribution < 0.5 dB) for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up point source and line source equations.** Use $20\log_{10}(r)$ for monopole, $10\log_{10}(r)$ for cylindrical/line sources (e.g., long exhaust pipes).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise the Following Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Anechoic chamber = enclosed space with all-reflective boundaries replaced by absorbers to create free-field acoustic conditions.
- ✅ **Two Types:** Full Anechoic (6-surface treatment, used for components) and Semi-Anechoic (5-surface treatment + reflective floor, used for ISO 362 pass-by noise).
- ✅ **Free-field SPL (point source):** $L_{p} = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ dB — **the "-11 dB" is from $10\log_{10}(4\pi)$**.
- ✅ **Free-field SPL (line source):** $L_{p} = L_{W}^{\prime} - 10\log_{10}(r) - 8$ dB.
- ✅ **Inverse-square rule:** -6 dB per doubling of distance (point source), -3 dB per doubling (line source).
- ✅ **Wedge cut-off frequency:** $f_{c} = c / (4L)$. For $L = 1$ m, $f_c \approx 86$ Hz.
- ✅ **Absorption coefficient:** $\alpha = 1 - \vert R \vert^{2}$, where $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$.
- ✅ **Room constant:** $R_{c} = S\bar{\alpha}/(1-\bar{\alpha})$; large $R_{c}$ means "dead" room.
- ✅ **Wedge material:** Glass-fibre or melamine foam; principle is tapered transmission line with progressive viscous + thermal dissipation.
- ✅ **Standards:** ISO 3745 (precision, full anechoic), ISO 3744 (engineering, semi-anechoic), ISO 362 (vehicle pass-by).
- ✅ **Cut-off verification:** ISO 3745 mandates measuring SPL vs distance with a reference source; deviation from -6 dB/doubling below $f_c$ disqualifies the chamber.
- ✅ **Engineering rule of thumb:** Always double $r$ for every additional microphone in a hemispherical array to satisfy $L_{p,r2} = L_{p,r1} - 6$ dB.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $f_c = c/(4L)$ with the half-wavelength resonance $f = c/(2L)$; the factor of 2 is critical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolators and Noise Absorbing Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isolators and Noise Absorbing Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vibration Isolators — Formal KTU Definition

A **vibration isolator** is a resilient mechanical device interposed between a vibrating source (the *exciter*) and a structure (the *receiver*) whose primary function is to attenuate the transmission of vibratory energy from the source to the receiver. In automotive applications, isolators are engineered elastomeric or mechanical components designed to decouple the chassis and passenger cabin from excitation sources such as the power-train, wheels, and exhaust system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT863 — Module 3):**
> Isolators are categorised by their *energy storage mechanism* (spring-type) and *energy dissipation mechanism* (damping-type). The **three canonical automotive isolators** are: **Elastomeric (rubber) mounts**, **Coil/Hydraulic spring mounts**, and **Active magneto-rheological (MR) mounts**. Mastery of the *transmissibility* function and *static deflection* relationship is mandatory for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Module Test assessments.

## 1.2 Noise Absorbing Materials — Formal KTU Definition

A **noise absorbing material** (also called a *sound absorber* or *acoustic lining*) is a porous, fibrous, or resonant engineered substance that converts incident acoustic energy into a small quantity of heat through viscous and thermal dissipation mechanisms within its internal microstructure, thereby reducing the intensity of the reflected sound wave. In NVH practice, the parameter that quantifies this conversion efficiency is the **Sound Absorption Coefficient ($\alpha$)**, defined as the ratio of *absorbed acoustic intensity* to *incident acoustic intensity* at a given frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric — Noise Reduction Coefficient (NRC):**
> A single-number rating defined by ASTM C423 as the arithmetic mean of the sound absorption coefficients measured at the four standard octave-band centre frequencies of **250 Hz, 500 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 2000 Hz**, rounded to the nearest multiple of **0.05**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Isolators):** Imagine you are carrying a cup of hot coffee while walking on a shaky wooden floor. Without a coaster, the cup rattles violently and spills. A *rubber coaster* (the isolator) placed between the floor and the cup absorbs the small jitters — the cup stays nearly still because the soft rubber deforms and stores the disturbance as internal strain energy before it reaches the cup. In a car, the **engine mount** plays exactly this role: the engine vibrates, the rubber deforms, and the dashboard you touch stays calm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Absorbers):** Think of walking barefoot on a tiled bathroom floor versus walking on a thick woollen carpet. The tile *reflects* your footsteps back as a sharp echo (high reflection, low absorption). The carpet *swallows* the sound by trapping air in millions of tiny fibres; the air oscillates and loses energy to friction. That is why a car's interior feels "library-quiet" — engineers line the roof, doors, and floor with **porous absorbers** (melamine foam, recycled shoddy, needled felt) that swallow noise instead of bouncing it back to your ear.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of air (reference):** $\rho_0 = 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Speed of sound in air (20 °C):** $c_0 = 343\ \text{m/s}$
- **Reference acoustic pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$
- **Standard test frequencies for absorption:** $\mathbf{125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000\ \text{Hz}}$ (octave bands)
- **Standard test frequencies for vibration isolation:** $\mathbf{1\ \text{Hz}}$ to **$\mathbf{500\ \text{Hz}}$** for automotive mounts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vibration Isolator — Operating Principles

A single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) base-excited isolator is the canonical model used by KTU examiners. The system comprises a mass $m$, a spring of stiffness $k$, and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$, all coupled between the *excite mass* (e.g., engine) and the *receiving base* (e.g., chassis).

**Step 1 — Identify the natural (undamped) frequency $f_n$** at which the system *wants* to oscillate on its own:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the frequency ratio $r$** that tells us where the excitation frequency $f$ sits relative to the natural frequency:

$$r = \frac{f}{f_n}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the damping ratio $\zeta$** (a dimensionless number between 0 and 1 for underdamped isolators):

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

where $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the *critical damping coefficient*.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Force Transmissibility $T_F$**, which is the ratio of *force transmitted to the base* to *force applied by the exciter*:

$$T_F = \frac{F_t}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Isolation Efficiency $\eta$** (the percentage of vibration eliminated):

$$\eta = (1 - T_F) \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Isolation:** Effective vibration isolation is achieved only when $r > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Below this ratio, the isolator *amplifies* vibration (a phenomenon called *resonance build-up*). Practically, automotive mounts are tuned so that the dominant engine firing frequency is **at least 1.4 times** the mount's natural frequency.

**Step 6 — Static Deflection $\delta_{st}$** ties the geometry to the stiffness:

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

This is the *designer's equation*: increasing the static deflection lowers the natural frequency, improving low-frequency isolation.

## 2.2 Noise Absorbing Materials — Classification & Mechanism

Acoustic absorbers are classified by their dominant energy-dissipation physics:

| Class | Sub-Class | Operating Principle | Typical Automotive Application |
|------|----------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Porous Absorbers** | Fibrous (PET, shoddy, fibreglass) | Viscous losses in pore channels | Headliner, carpet backing, wheel-arch liner |
| | Cellular (PU foam, melamine foam) | Viscous + thermal losses in cells | Hood liner, dashboard insulation |
| **Resonant Absorbers** | Helmholtz Resonator | Neck-cavity resonance | Subwoofer enclosures, exhaust mufflers |
| | Micro-Perforated Panel (MPP) | Friction in micro-slits | Premium cabin liners, EV battery packs |
| **Composite / Sandwich** | Constrained Layer Damping (CLD) | Shear deformation of viscoelastic core | Floor pans, body-in-white patches |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Tip:** Memorise the table below — these are the equations you will see in 70 % of Module 3 numerical questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Natural Frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | $k$ in N/m, $m$ in kg |
| 2 | Static Deflection | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$ | m | Use $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 3 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless | $\zeta < 1$ underdamped |
| 4 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \dfrac{f}{f_n}$ | dimensionless | Isolation when $r > \sqrt{2}$ |
| 5 | Force Transmissibility | $T_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | $T_F < 1$ ⇒ isolation |
| 6 | Isolation Efficiency | $\eta = (1 - T_F) \times 100$ | % | Higher ⇒ better |
| 7 | Absorption Coefficient | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_{abs}}{I_{inc}}$ | dimensionless, 0–1 | $\alpha = 1$ perfect absorber |
| 8 | NRC | $\text{NRC} = \dfrac{\alpha_{250} + \alpha_{500} + \alpha_{1000} + \alpha_{2000}}{4}$ | dimensionless, 0.00–1.00 | ASTM C423 |
| 9 | Impedance of Porous Layer | $Z = R + jX$ (Rayleigh model) | Rayl | $R$ = resistance, $X$ = reactance |
| 10 | Flow Resistivity | $\sigma$ | Pa·s/m² or Rayl/m | Material key property |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine mounting systems** must achieve 15–25 dB attenuation at idle (≈ 25 Hz firing frequency for a 4-cylinder 4-stroke at 750 rpm) without compromising NVH durability over a 10-year / 200,000 km life.
- **Cabin acoustic packages** in luxury vehicles target an **NRC ≥ 0.70** for the headliner, with a 3–5 kg/m² areal density to balance mass and absorption.
- **Electric vehicle (EV) NVH** has shifted focus from engine-noise masking to road-noise cancellation — micro-perforated trim and constrained-layer damping plates are now *front-line* technologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Force Transmissibility for a Base-Excited SDOF System

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Setup and Equation of Motion

Consider a mass $m$ supported by a parallel spring-damper combination on a *moving base* whose displacement is $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$. The mass displacement relative to the *inertial (fixed)* frame is $x(t)$. The relative displacement between mass and base is $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$.

Applying Newton's second law to the mass, summing forces in the vertical direction:

$$\sum F = m \ddot{x}$$

The spring force (pulling mass toward base) is $-k(x - y) = -kz$, and the damper force is $-c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) = -c\dot{z}$. Therefore:

$$m\ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

Substitute $z = x - y \Rightarrow \dot{z} = \dot{x} - \dot{y} \Rightarrow \ddot{z} = \ddot{x} - \ddot{y}$. The equation of motion in terms of the relative coordinate $z$ becomes:

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$$

### 3.1.2 Harmonic Forcing Substitution

For base excitation $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$, the base acceleration is $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$. The right-hand side becomes a harmonic forcing term of amplitude $m\omega^2 Y$ acting on the relative-coordinate system. Assume steady-state solution $z(t) = Z\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Then:

$$\dot{z} = \omega Z \cos(\omega t - \phi), \quad \ddot{z} = -\omega^2 Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Substituting into the equation of motion:

$$-m\omega^2 Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega Z \cos(\omega t - \phi) + kZ \sin(\omega t - \phi) = m\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

Collect $\sin$ and $\cos$ terms:

$$(k - m\omega^2) Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega Z \cos(\omega t - \phi) = m\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

### 3.1.3 Complex-Number (Phasor) Solution

Represent the system using phasors. Let the complex amplitude ratio $\bar{H} = Z/Y$. The transfer function of an SDOF system is:

$$\bar{H}(\omega) = \frac{m\omega^2}{(k - m\omega^2) + jc\omega}$$

### 3.1.4 Magnitude of the Transmissibility

Take the magnitude squared, then factor $k$ and divide numerator & denominator by $k$:

$$\vert \bar{H} \vert^2 = \frac{(m\omega^2)^2}{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $k^2$:

$$\vert \bar{H} \vert^2 = \frac{\left(\dfrac{m\omega^2}{k}\right)^2}{\left(1 - \dfrac{m\omega^2}{k}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{c\omega}{k}\right)^2}$$

Now introduce the dimensionless parameters. Let $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$. Then:

$$\frac{m\omega^2}{k} = \frac{\omega^2}{\omega_n^2} = r^2$$

$$\frac{c\omega}{k} = \frac{c\omega}{m\omega_n^2} = 2\zeta \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = 2\zeta r$$

Substituting these back:

$$\vert \bar{H} \vert^2 = \frac{r^4}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$$

The *force* transmitted to the base is $F_t = kZ$ (spring reaction). The *exciting force* amplitude from the inertial term is $F_0 = m\omega^2 Y$. So the force transmissibility is:

$$T_F = \frac{F_t}{F_0} = \frac{kZ}{m\omega^2 Y} = \frac{k}{m\omega^2}\vert \bar{H} \vert = \frac{1}{r^2}\sqrt{\frac{r^4}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Simplify $r^4/r^2 = r^2$:

$$T_F = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

This is the canonical transmissibility expression expected in KTU ESE numericals. $\blacksquare$

### 3.1.5 Special Case — Undamped ($\zeta = 0$)

With $\zeta = 0$:

$$T_F = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$$

- $r \to 0$: $T_F \to 1$ (rigid coupling, no isolation).
- $r = 1$: $T_F \to \infty$ (resonance, infinite amplification).
- $r = \sqrt{2}$: $T_F = 1$ (crossover, isolation begins).
- $r \gg 1$: $T_F \to 0$ (perfect isolation).

### 3.1.6 Boundary Condition — Crossover Frequency

Setting $T_F = 1$ and $\zeta = 0$ gives the crossover condition $r = \sqrt{2}$, i.e., $f = \sqrt{2}\,f_n$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Maximum Absorption Coefficient of a Porous Layer (Quarter-Wave Resonance)

Consider a rigid-backed porous layer of thickness $t$, surface density $m_s$, in plane-wave incidence from air of impedance $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0$. The transfer-matrix approach yields the input impedance at the surface:

$$Z_{in} = -j Z_c \cot(k_c t)$$

where $Z_c$ is the characteristic impedance and $k_c$ the complex wavenumber inside the porous medium. The absorption coefficient is:

$$\alpha = 1 - \left\vert \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0} \right\vert^2$$

For a quarter-wave thick layer where $k_c t = \pi/2$ (i.e., $t \approx \lambda/4$ in the porous medium), $Z_{in} \to 0$ if $Z_c \to \infty$, but for a *locally-reacting* hard-backed layer the optimum occurs when $t = \lambda_0/4$ in air, giving:

$$\boxed{\alpha_{\max} \approx 1 - \left(\frac{Z_0 - R}{Z_0 + R}\right)^2}$$

where $R$ is the *flow-resistance* component of $Z_c$. For $R = Z_0$ (impedance matching), $\alpha_{\max} = 1$ — the layer is a *perfect absorber* at that frequency.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** An automotive engine of mass $m = 220$ kg is supported on four identical rubber mounts. The engine idles at 900 rpm (4-stroke, 4-cylinder), producing a dominant excitation at the **second engine order** ($2f_{rotor}$). Each mount has a stiffness $k = 1.2 \times 10^6$ N/m and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.08$. Determine:
1. The natural frequency of the mount system.
2. The frequency ratio $r$.
3. The transmissibility $T_F$.
4. The isolation efficiency $\eta$.

**Solution — Step 1: Effective mass per mount**

The four mounts share the engine mass in parallel, so each mount "sees" $m/4$:

$$m_{eff} = \frac{220}{4} = 55\ \text{kg}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Natural frequency**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{55}}$$

$$\frac{k}{m_{eff}} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{55} = 21{,}818.18\ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{21818.18} = \frac{147.70}{6.2832} = 23.51\ \text{Hz}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Excitation frequency**

Engine rotation: $f_{rot} = 900/60 = 15\ \text{Hz}$. For a 4-cylinder 4-stroke engine, dominant excitation order is the *2nd order*:

$$f = 2 f_{rot} = 2 \times 15 = 30\ \text{Hz}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Frequency ratio**

$$r = \frac{f}{f_n} = \frac{30}{23.51} = 1.276$$

**Solution — Step 5: Transmissibility**

Compute $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.08 \times 1.276 = 0.2042$

$$T_F = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (0.2042)^2}{(1 - 1.276^2)^2 + (0.2042)^2}}$$

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 1.6282 = -0.6282, \quad (1 - r^2)^2 = 0.3946$$

$$1 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 1 + 0.0417 = 1.0417$$

$$T_F = \sqrt{\frac{1.0417}{0.3946 + 0.0417}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0417}{0.4363}} = \sqrt{2.3875} = 1.545$$

**Solution — Step 6: Isolation Efficiency**

$$\eta = (1 - 1.545) \times 100\% = -54.5\%$$

The negative efficiency means the system is *amplifying* the input — a fail condition. The student should conclude that the mounts are too stiff.

**Design Recommendation:** Lower the stiffness to achieve $r > \sqrt{2}$. Required natural frequency:

$$f_n < \frac{30}{\sqrt{2}} = 21.21\ \text{Hz}$$

For $m_{eff} = 55$ kg: $k < (2\pi f_n)^2 m_{eff} = (2\pi \times 21.21)^2 \times 55 = 9.78 \times 10^5$ N/m per mount.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Isolator vs Absorber

| Parameter | Vibration Isolator | Noise Absorber |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Energy Domain** | Mechanical / kinetic | Acoustic / pressure |
| **Dominant Physics** | Spring storage + viscous damping | Viscous & thermal losses in pores |
| **Governing Equation** | $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = F(t)$ | $\nabla^2 p - \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$ (Helmholtz) |
| **Key Performance Index** | $T_F$, $\eta$ | $\alpha$, NRC |
| **Design Goal** | $T_F \to 0$ | $\alpha \to 1$ |
| **Typical Frequency Band** | 1 – 500 Hz | 100 – 5000 Hz |
| **Failure Mode** | Resonance amplification ($r \approx 1$) | Reflection (impedance mismatch) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Vibration Isolation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Exciter Mass m1] --> B[Mount Stiffness k]
    A --> C[Mount Damping c]
    B --> D[Chassis Receiver Mass m2]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Vehicle Body Structure]
    D --> F[Passenger Cabin]
    E --> G[Road Wheel Excitation]
    G --> H[Suspension Spring]
    H --> I[Tire Damping]
    I --> J[Road Surface]

    classDef source fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000;
    classDef isol fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#059669,color:#000;
    classDef recv fill:#118ab2,stroke:#0a647d,color:#fff;
    class A,G source;
    class B,C,H,I isol;
    class D,E,F,J recv;
```

## 4.2 Transmissibility Response Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Start: Compute f, fn, ζ] --> S1[Compute r = f / fn]
    S1 --> S2{r less than 1 ?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Sub-resonant Region: T close to 1, no isolation]
    S2 -- No --> S4{r between 1 and 1.414 ?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Resonance / Crossover: T greater than 1, DANGER: amplification]
    S4 -- No --> S6{r greater than 1.414 ?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Isolated Region: T less than 1, ISOLATION achieved]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Re-check inputs]

    classDef danger fill:#ef476f,stroke:#c1234a,color:#fff;
    classDef good fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#059669,color:#000;
    classDef warn fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000;
    class S3 warn;
    class S5 danger;
    class S7 good;
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Acoustic Absorption Treatment in an Automotive Cabin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Sound Source: Engine / Road / Wind] --> PROP[Propagation Path: Structure / Air]
    PROP --> SHELL[Body-in-White Shell]
    SHELL --> LINER1[Headliner: Porous Foam NRC 0.7]
    SHELL --> LINER2[Floor Carpet: Mass 4 kg per m2]
    SHELL --> LINER3[Wheel Arch Liner: Melamine 25 mm]
    SHELL --> LINER4[Door Trim: Microperforated Panel]
    LINER1 --> CAB[Passenger Cabin]
    LINER2 --> CAB
    LINER3 --> CAB
    LINER4 --> CAB
    CAB --> EAR[Listener Ear: Target SPL less than 65 dB A]

    classDef src fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000;

```

## 4.4 Material Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Target frequency less than 500 Hz ?} -->|Yes| A1[Use Resonant Absorber: Helmholtz or MPP]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Target frequency between 500 and 2000 Hz ?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Use Porous Absorber: Melamine / PU Foam 25 to 50 mm]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Target frequency greater than 2000 Hz ?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use Thin Porous Layer 10 mm or MPP]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Re-evaluate target]

    classDef q fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000;
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the term "transmissibility" in the context of vibration isolation. State the condition for effective isolation.**

**Model Answer:**
Transmissibility $T_F$ is the ratio of the force transmitted through an isolator to the base to the exciting force applied to the system. Mathematically, $T_F = F_t/F_0$. The condition for effective isolation is that the frequency ratio $r = f/f_n > \sqrt{2}$, i.e., the excitation frequency must exceed $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency of the isolator, ensuring $T_F < 1$.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the Noise Reduction Coefficient (NRC) of an absorbing material? How is it measured?**

**Model Answer:**
NRC is a single-number rating that summarises the sound-absorption performance of a material. It is the arithmetic mean of the sound absorption coefficients $\alpha$ measured at the four octave-band centre frequencies of 250 Hz, 500 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 2000 Hz, as per ASTM C423. NRC values range from 0.00 (perfect reflector) to 1.00 (perfect absorber).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Choose either QUESTION A or QUESTION B. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the force transmissibility of a base-excited single-degree-of-freedom system with viscous damping. Clearly state the condition for effective isolation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An air-conditioning compressor of mass 12 kg is mounted on a single rubber isolator with stiffness $4.5 \times 10^5$ N/m and damping ratio 0.10. The compressor operates at 3000 rpm. Compute the natural frequency, the excitation frequency, the frequency ratio, and the force transmissibility. Comment on the effectiveness of the isolator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final expression is:

$$T_F = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

The condition for effective isolation is $r > \sqrt{2}$, i.e., the excitation frequency must lie above the crossover frequency.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

- Natural frequency: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{4.5 \times 10^5}{12}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{37500} = \dfrac{193.65}{6.2832} = 30.82$ Hz
- Excitation frequency: $f = 3000/60 = 50$ Hz
- Frequency ratio: $r = 50/30.82 = 1.622$
- Since $r > \sqrt{2}$, the isolator is in the isolation region.
- Transmissibility: $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.10 \times 1.622 = 0.3244$

$$T_F = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.1052}{(1 - 2.631)^2 + 0.1052}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.1052}{2.661 + 0.1052}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.1052}{2.766}} = \sqrt{0.3996} = 0.632$$

- Isolation efficiency: $\eta = (1 - 0.632) \times 100 = 36.8\%$. The isolator is effective but moderate; increasing compliance (lower $k$) would improve isolation.

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the major types of noise absorbing materials used in automotive cabins and explain the absorption mechanism of porous absorbers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger-car headliner uses a 30 mm thick melamine foam with flow resistivity 12,000 Rayl/m and density 11 kg/m³. Estimate the quarter-wave resonant frequency and discuss whether the foam is suitable for attenuating cabin boom noise at 80 Hz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Classification (refer Section 2.2 table). Porous absorbers work on the principle of viscous dissipation: as an acoustic wave enters the porous matrix, air molecules oscillate within the narrow channels formed by the fibre/cell walls. The relative motion between air and skeleton produces viscous shear, converting acoustic energy into heat. Thermal losses additionally occur due to isothermal compression-expansion in narrow pores (the *Kundt tube* / *Delany-Bazley* model).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

For a rigid-backed porous layer, the first peak in absorption occurs near the quarter-wave condition in the porous medium:

$$f_{peak} \approx \frac{c_{eff}}{4 t}$$

where $c_{eff} \approx c_0 / \sqrt{\text{structural factor}}$. For low-density melamine, $c_{eff} \approx 0.9\,c_0 \approx 309$ m/s.

$$f_{peak} = \frac{309}{4 \times 0.030} = \frac{309}{0.12} = 2575\ \text{Hz}$$

This is far above 80 Hz; therefore, the 30 mm foam alone is **ineffective** for 80 Hz boom. To target 80 Hz, the designer must add a resonant absorber (Helmholtz or MPP) or use a thicker porous layer (≈ 1 m, impractical) or a mass-spring-mass composite.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 3):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to Hz** — students write $f = 3000$ instead of $f = 50$ Hz, producing $r < 1$ and a non-physical transmissibility. Always divide rpm by 60.
> 2. **Wrong engine order** — for a 4-stroke 4-cylinder engine, the *dominant* excitation is the **2nd order**, not the 1st. For a 3-cylinder, it is the 1.5th order. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Sign of $\eta$** — a *negative* $\eta$ is mathematically valid and signals *amplification*, not "wrong answer." Examiners award full credit if the student interprets it correctly.
> 4. **NRC averaging** — students often include 125 Hz or 4000 Hz. The ASTM C423 standard uses **only** 250, 500, 1000, and 2000 Hz. Extra frequencies will dilute your answer.
> 5. **Units of $k$** — N/m, *not* N/mm. A factor-of-1000 error is the most common numerical mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vibration isolator:** resilient element between source and receiver; aim to make $T_F < 1$.
- **Natural frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$; lowering $k$ or raising $m$ lowers $f_n$.
- **Static deflection:** $\delta_{st} = mg/k$; the *deflection-controlled* design philosophy targets a specific $\delta_{st}$ for a given $f_n$.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$; *too little* damping causes resonance spikes; *too much* damping degrades high-frequency isolation.
- **Crossover condition:** $r = \sqrt{2}$ — the boundary between amplification and isolation.
- **Transmissibility equation:** $T_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ — memorise verbatim.
- **Porous absorbers** work best at mid-to-high frequency (500 Hz – 5 kHz); they are *thickness-limited* at low frequency.
- **Resonant absorbers** (Helmholtz, MPP) target *narrow low-frequency* bands; ideal for boom noise.
- **NRC** averages $\alpha$ at 250, 500, 1000, 2000 Hz only (ASTM C423).
- **Flow resistivity $\sigma$** is the key material property of porous absorbers; values of 10,000 – 50,000 Rayl/m are typical for automotive foams.
- **Impedance matching** ($R = Z_0 \approx 413$ Rayl) maximises absorption.
- **Engine order formula:** for a 4-stroke engine with $n$ cylinders, dominant order is $n/2$.
- **Automotive canonical systems:** engine mount (4–5 mounts in parallel), exhaust rubber hangers, suspension bushings, body-to-frame mounts.
- **Premium NVH packages** combine (i) constrained-layer damping on body panels, (ii) porous absorption in the headliner, (iii) mass-barriers in the firewall and floor, and (iv) Helmholtz resonators in the exhaust.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harshness - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Harshness - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harshness** is defined as the subjective human perception and psychological response of an automotive occupant to the combined effect of low-frequency **mechanical vibrations** and **transient shocks** transmitted through the vehicle's structural and suspension systems over a prolonged period of time.

In the context of **Automotive Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH)** engineering, harshness is the third and most *psychologically-driven* pillar of the NVH triad. While **N**oise relates to airborne sound pressure and **V**ibration relates to measurable mechanical oscillations, **H**arshness (H) is the *subjective discomfort rating* that a driver or passenger assigns to those vibrations based on personal perception, ride duration, and expectation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Vibration is the *physical input* (measurable in m/s² or g), whereas Harshness is the *human response* to that input over time. Harshness is a **perceived quality**, not a directly measurable physical quantity.

Mathematically expressed in the classical NVH framework:

$$\text{NVH} = f(\text{Noise}, \text{Vibration}, \text{Harshness})$$

Where the function $f$ represents the combined subjective comfort index evaluated by the vehicle occupant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a **rickshaw** travelling over a **broken village road** versus travelling in the same rickshaw on a **smooth city highway**.

- The **road surface** excites the vehicle, producing measurable accelerations at the seat — this is the *Vibration*.
- The **engine and wind** produce audible sound inside the cabin — this is the *Noise*.
- The **rattle in your back, the constant bouncing, the fatigue in your spine after 30 minutes** — the irritation and discomfort you *feel* subjectively — this is **Harshness**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "Harshness is what your *spine remembers* an hour after the drive is over." It is the *cumulative, subjective, time-integrated discomfort* caused by ride disturbances.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Units

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Relevance to Harshness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vibration Acceleration | $a$ | $\mathbf{m/s^2}$ (or **g**) | Primary physical input causing harshness |
| Frequency Range | $f$ | **Hz** | Harshness dominant in **0–80 Hz** low-frequency band |
| Exposure Duration | $t$ | **seconds (s)** | Harshness scales with ride time |
| Weighted RMS Acceleration | $a_w$ | $\mathbf{m/s^2}$ | ISO 2631 comfort metric |
| VDV (Vibration Dose Value) | $V$ | $\mathbf{m/s^{1.75}}$ | Better correlate of perceived harshness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The frequency range of **0–80 Hz** is the *harshness-dominant band* because it coincides with the natural resonant frequencies of human internal organs, the seat-cushion system, and unsprung vehicle mass modes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing Harshness — The 4-Stage Subjective Model

Harshness is not a single physical event; it is a *cognitive pipeline* that an occupant's nervous system executes. The KTU syllabus expects students to articulate this pipeline.

### Step 1 — **Mechanical Excitation (Input)**
The vehicle's tires encounter road irregularities (speed breakers, potholes, joints, gravel). These inputs inject vertical and longitudinal forces into the suspension.

### Step 2 — **Structural Transmission (Path)**
The suspension links (springs, dampers, bushes) transmit and filter the input. Poor damping coefficient or low spring stiffness allows the disturbance to reach the floor pan and steering column.

### Step 3 — **Cabin Coupling (Receiver)**
The vehicle body, seat, and steering wheel transfer the filtered vibration to the driver's body, primarily at the **seat-pan**, **backrest**, and **feet**.

### Step 4 — **Subjective Perception (Output)**
The human occupant's peripheral nerves, spine, and inner-ear vestibular system integrate the acceleration over time and frequency, producing the **psychological sensation of harshness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** An NVH engineer can reduce harshness by acting on ANY of the four stages — smoother road (input), better dampers (path), isolating mounts (receiver), or driver expectation management (output). Harshness is therefore a *system-level* problem.

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Harshness — The Engineering Levers

1. **Suspension Stiffness ($k$)** — Higher $k$ means harsher ride (firmer bumps transmitted).
2. **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$)** — Lower $\zeta$ means residual oscillations linger, increasing harshness.
3. **Unsprung Mass ($m_u$)** — Heavier wheels/axles transmit more road shock.
4. **Seat Transmissibility** — Poor seat damping amplifies vibration reaching the body.
5. **Tire Compliance** — Stiff sidewalls transmit high-frequency harshness.
6. **Powertrain Mount Stiffness** — Engine vibrations excite the cabin through mounts.
7. **Road Surface Roughness** — Primary excitation source.
8. **Driving Duration** — Harshness is a *time-cumulative* perception.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | **Natural frequency** of sprung mass on suspension; controls harshness onset frequency |
