(1 mark) **Step 7 — Per-Band Fluctuation Strength:** $$F_{CB,i} = 0.008 \cdot \Delta L_i^{\,2} \cdot g(f_{mod,i}) \cdot k(z_i)$$ (1 mark) **Step 8 — Quadratic Summation:** $$F = \sqrt{ \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{CB,i}^{\,2} }$$ (1 mark) ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Given:** | Band (Bark) | $f_{mod}$ (Hz) | $\Delta L$ (dB) | $k(z)$ | |---|---|---|---| | 6 | 4 | 4.0 | 1.0 | | 8 | 4 | 3.0 | 1.0 | | 10 | 2 | 2.5 | 0.8 | **Step 1 — Compute $g(f_{mod})$ for each band:** - Band 6: $g(4) = \frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5$ - Band 8: $g(4) = 0.5$ - Band 10: $g(2) = \frac{0.5}{0.25 + 4} = \frac{0.5}{4.25} = 0.1176$ **Step 2 — Compute $F_{CB}$ for each band:** $$F_{CB,1} = 0.008 \times 4^{2} \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.008 \times 16 \times 0.5 = 0.0640 \text{ vacil}$$ $$F_{CB,2} = 0.008 \times 3^{2} \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.008 \times 9 \times 0.5 = 0.0360 \text{ vacil}$$ $$F_{CB,3} = 0.008 \times 2.5^{2} \times 0.1176 \times 0.8 = 0.008 \times 6.25 \times 0.1176 \times 0.8$$ $$F_{CB,3} = 0.008 \times 6.25 \times 0.0941 = 0.00470 \text{ vacil}$$ **Step 3 — Quadratic summation:** $$F = \sqrt{ (0.0640)^{2} + (0.0360)^{2} + (0.00470)^{2} }$$ $$F = \sqrt{ 0.00410 + 0.00130 + 0.00002 } = \sqrt{0.00542}$$ $$\boxed{F \approx 0.0736 \text{ vacil}}$$ **Step 4 — Comment:** Since $F = 0.074$ vacil is well below **1 vacil (JND threshold)**, the noise is **not perceived as fluctuating** and is unlikely to draw a customer complaint for *boom/pulse*. The band 6 contribution dominates (≈ 87% of energy), indicating that the 200–250 Hz spectral region (Bark 6) is the primary target if a modulation issue arises. (7 marks total) --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the **modulation-frequency weighting function** $g(f_{mod})$ used in Zwicker's Fluctuation Strength model. Show that it peaks at $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tire cavity noise modulates the cabin SPL with a linear index $m = 0.25$ at a modulation frequency of 4 Hz in the 200 Hz critical band ($z = 8$ Bark, $k(z) = 1.0$). Calculate: - (i) The modulation depth in dB. - (ii) The per-band Fluctuation Strength. - (iii) The change in $F_{CB}$ if the modulation frequency is shifted to 8 Hz (keeping $\Delta L$ constant). **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Step 1 — Define the normalized variable:** $$u = \frac{f_{mod}}{4}$$ **Step 2 — Construct the band-pass weighting:** We require $g \to 0$ as $u \to 0$ and as $u \to \infty$, with a maximum near $u = 1$. The symmetric band-pass form is: $$g(f_{mod}) = \frac{u}{u^{2} + u^{-2}} = \frac{f_{mod}/4}{(f_{mod}/4)^{2} + (4/f_{mod})^{2}}$$ (2 marks) **Step 3 — Differentiate to find the maximum:** $$\frac{dg}{du} = \frac{(u^{2} + u^{-2}) - u(2u - 2u^{-3})}{(u^{2} + u^{-2})^{2}}$$ Setting numerator to 0: $$u^{2} + u^{-2} - 2u^{2} + 2u^{-2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -u^{2} + 3u^{-2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; u^{4} = 3$$ (2 marks) **Step 4 — Note on empirical 4 Hz anchor:** The exact analytical maximum occurs at $u = 3^{1/4} \approx 1.316$ (i.e., $f_{mod} \approx 5.27$ Hz). However, Zwicker **rounded this empirically to 4 Hz** by absorbing the small correction into the constant **0.008**. The final form of the equation therefore *peaks exactly* at $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz when the empirical constant is fitted to the calibration data (1 vacil at 3 dB, 60 dB SPL). (1.5 marks) **Step 5 — Verify peak value:** $$g(4) = \frac{1}{1^{2} + 1^{2}} = 0.5$$ (1.5 marks) ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **(i) Modulation depth in dB:** $$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10} \!\left( \frac{1+0.25}{1-0.25} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(1.6667)$$ $$\boxed{\Delta L = 20 \times 0.2218 = 4.44 \text{ dB}}$$ (2 marks) **(ii) Per-band Fluctuation Strength at 4 Hz:** $$g(4) = 0.5$$ $$F_{CB} = 0.008 \times (4.44)^{2} \times 0.5 \times 1.0$$ $$F_{CB} = 0.008 \times 19.71 \times 0.5 = 0.0788 \text{ vacil}$$ $$\boxed{F_{CB, 4Hz} = 0.079 \text{ vacil}}$$ (2.5 marks) **(iii) Per-band Fluctuation Strength at 8 Hz:** $$g(8) = \frac{8/4}{(8/4)^{2} + (4/8)^{2}} = \frac{2}{4 + 0.25} = \frac{2}{4.25} = 0.4706$$ $$F_{CB,8Hz} = 0.008 \times 19.71 \times 0.4706 \times 1.0$$ $$\boxed{F_{CB, 8Hz} = 0.0742 \text{ vacil}}$$ (1.5 marks) **Change:** $\Delta F = 0.079 - 0.074 = 0.005$ vacil — a reduction of only **~6%** when going from 4 Hz to 8 Hz. The 200 Hz-band tire noise is still within the fluctuation region at 8 Hz, and the perceived "boom" may not be eliminated by frequency-shifting alone — *modulation depth* and *spectral spreading to other bands* must also be controlled. (1 mark for interpretation) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Points (KTU Board):** > 1. **Forgetting quadratic summation** — many students add $F_{CB}$ linearly. The correct formula is $F = \sqrt{\sum F_{CB}^{2}}$. Lose 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing Fluctuation Strength (0.5–20 Hz) with Roughness (20–300 Hz)** — always check the modulation frequency first. Lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting to convert linear modulation index $m$ to $\Delta L$ (dB)** before plugging into the formula. Lose 1 mark. > 4. **Using $f_{mod}$ in rad/s** instead of Hz in $g(f_{mod})$. Lose 1 mark. > 5. **Not stating the unit "vacil"** in the final answer. Lose 0.5 mark. > 6. **Writing "Fluctuation" without the word "Strength"** — terminology matters. Lose 0.5 mark. > 7. **Missing the calibration anchor** (1 vacil = 3 dB at 4 Hz, 60 dB SPL). Lose 1 mark if not stated in derivations. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Fluctuation Strength (F)** is a *time-varying* psychoacoustic metric, measured in **vacils**. - It quantifies the *perceived magnitude of slow loudness modulations* — independent of average Loudness. - It is **only valid for modulation frequencies between 0.5 Hz and ~20 Hz**. - **Peak sensitivity occurs at 4 Hz** — directly relevant to tire/road and HVAC modulation. - The core equation per critical band is $F_{CB} = 0.008 \cdot \Delta L^{2} \cdot g(f_{mod}) \cdot k(z)$. - The **modulation-frequency weighting** $g(f_{mod}) = (f_{mod}/4) / [ (f_{mod}/4)^{2} + (4/f_{mod})^{2} ]$ peaks at 4 Hz. - Modulation depth must be in **dB**; convert via $\Delta L = 20\log_{10}[(1+m)/(1-m)]$. - **Total F is obtained by quadratic summation across critical bands** — *never linear addition*. - **Do not confuse** Fluctuation Strength with **Roughness** (asper, 20–300 Hz) or with **Loudness** (sone, DC magnitude). - **Calibration reference:** 1 vacil = 3 dB depth at 4 Hz, 60 dB SPL. - **Automotive relevance:** tire cavity resonance, HVAC blower, exhaust pulse, door buffeting. - **Design lever:** shifting the modulation *frequency* away from 4 Hz, or *reducing* the modulation depth $\Delta L$ by 1–2 dB, dramatically reduces customer complaints. - **Standard:** ISO 532-1 / DIN 45631 (Zwicker method). - **Unit symbol to use in KTU answer scripts:** write **"vacil"** in lowercase italic or upright — both accepted.

topics.title: Psychoacoustic Indexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Psychoacoustic Indexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Psychoacoustic Indexes?

**Psychoacoustic Indexes** (also called **Sound Quality Metrics** or **Perceptual Noise Descriptors**) are quantitative engineering parameters that describe *how humans subjectively perceive* a sound. They bridge the gap between **objective physical quantities** (sound pressure, frequency spectrum) and the **subjective human auditory response** (annoyance, sharpness, roughness, loudness).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> Psychoacoustic indexes are mathematical models derived from psychoacoustic experiments that transform an objective acoustic signal (sound pressure vs. time/frequency) into a single- or multi-dimensional perceptual quantity representing human auditory sensation.

The standard perceptual dimensions universally accepted in NVH engineering are:

| Dimension | Perceived Attribute | Primary Stimulus |
|---|---|---|
| **Loudness (N)** | How strong/loud | Sound pressure level |
| **Sharpness (S)** | How "bright" or piercing | High-frequency content |
| **Roughness (R)** | How "harsh" or modulating | Fast amplitude modulation (15–300 Hz) |
| **Fluctuation Strength (F)** | How "pulsating" | Slow amplitude modulation (< 20 Hz) |
| **Tonality (K, PNR, TNR)** | Presence of tonal components | Sinusoidal peaks in spectrum |
| **Impulsiveness** | How "sharp"/"clicking" | Fast rise-time transients |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why We Need Them

Imagine two cars producing the **same sound pressure level of 75 dB(A)** at the driver's ear:
- Car A emits a low-frequency rumble (like a diesel truck).
- Car B emits a high-frequency whine (like a turbocharger whistle).

A simple **dB(A) meter** would say both are equally "loud." But to a human, **Car B feels dramatically more annoying and piercing**. 

A standard sound-level meter treats the ear like a dumb microphone. **Psychoacoustic indexes treat the ear like the complex biological organ it actually is** — applying models of the cochlea's frequency resolution (critical bands), masking, and temporal integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight for Automotive NVH:**
> Modern premium vehicle manufacturers (BMW, Mercedes, Lexus) do NOT tune cabin sound for "lowest dB." They tune for the *psychoacoustic profile* — a balance of low sharpness, controlled roughness, appropriate loudness, and pleasing tonality (e.g., a "sporty" engine note vs. a "luxury" hushed cabin).

## 1.3 The Foundation — The Critical Band Scale (Bark)

All major psychoacoustic indexes (loudness, sharpness, roughness) are calculated on the **Critical Band Rate scale (Bark)**, NOT the linear frequency scale (Hz). This is because the human cochlea performs a kind of mechanical **Fourier analysis** — each location on the basilar membrane responds to a band of frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 Bark = Width of one critical band on the basilar membrane** (≈ 100 Hz at low frequencies, up to ~ 4 kHz at high frequencies).
> The audible range (20 Hz – 20 kHz) maps to **0 – 24 Bark**.

The standard transformation (Zwicker, 1961) from frequency $f$ (in Hz) to critical band rate $z$ (in Bark) is:

$$
z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f) + 3.5 \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2
$$

This transforms any 1/3-octave or FFT spectrum into 24 critical bands — the native domain of all subsequent psychoacoustic calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Psychoacoustic Modeling

Every major psychoacoustic index is built on **three biological facts** about the human ear:

1. **Frequency Selectivity (Critical Bands):** The ear resolves sound into ~24 independent bands (Bark). Two tones in the same band mask each other; tones in different bands are heard separately.
2. **Frequency-Dependent Sensitivity:** The ear is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz. A 1 kHz tone at 40 dB is heard louder than a 100 Hz tone at 40 dB.
3. **Temporal Integration:** The ear integrates energy over a time window (~ 100–200 ms for loudness) but can also detect fast modulations (giving rise to roughness/fluctuation strength).

## 2.2 Detailed Theory of the Major Indexes

### 2.2.1 LOUDNESS (N) — Zwicker Stationary Method
- **Purpose:** Quantifies the perceived intensity of a steady sound.
- **Standard:** ISO 532-1 (Zwicker method), ISO 532-2 (Stevens Mark VII).
- **Unit:** **Sone** (1 sone = loudness of a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL).
- **Doubled Sone = +10 phons** (perceived doubling of loudness).

The procedure:
1. Convert SPL spectrum into **excitation level** $L_E(z)$ in each critical band $z$.
2. Apply **lower curve of masking** (threshold in quiet + internal noise).
3. Compute **specific loudness** $N'(z)$ — a function representing loudness density per Bark.
4. Integrate over all Bark:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{24\ \text{Bark}} N'(z)\, dz
$$

### 2.2.2 SHARPNESS (S) — DIN 45692
- **Purpose:** Quantifies the "high-frequency content" or "piercing" quality of a sound.
- **Unit:** **Acum** (1 acum = sharpness of a narrowband noise at 1 kHz, 60 dB).
- **Reference:** A 1 kHz narrowband noise has S = 1 acum.
- **Application in Automotive:** Door-close sound sharpness; tire-road hiss; brake squeal.

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z) \cdot z\, dz \quad \Big/ \int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot dz
$$

where $g(z)$ is a high-frequency weighting function that amplifies the contribution of bands above 8 Bark.

### 2.2.3 ROUGHNESS (R) — Daniel & Weber
- **Purpose:** Quantifies the perceived "harshness" of a sound modulated at 15–300 Hz (typically 70 Hz is the most uncomfortable modulation frequency).
- **Unit:** **Asper** (1 asper = 60 dB, 1 kHz tone, 100% AM at 70 Hz).
- **Application in Automotive:** Engine NVH, gear whine, electric motor torque-ripple-induced acoustic modulation.

$$
R \approx 0.0084 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} \frac{\Delta L_E(z) \cdot f_{\text{mod}}}{f_{\text{mod}}^2 + f_0^2}\, dz
$$

where $\Delta L_E$ is the modulation depth in the excitation level and $f_0 \approx 70$ Hz.

### 2.2.4 FLUCTUATION STRENGTH (F)
- **Purpose:** Quantifies the perceived "slow pulsation" of a sound modulated below 20 Hz (typically 4 Hz most noticeable).
- **Unit:** **Vacil** (1 vacil = 60 dB, 1 kHz tone, 100% AM at 4 Hz).
- **Application:** HVAC fan noise, road surface texture noise, wind buffeting.

### 2.2.5 TONALITY (K, TNR, PNR)
- **Purpose:** Quantifies the perceived "tonal" or "whistling" content.
- **Common Metrics:**
  - **Tone-to-Noise Ratio (TNR)** = $L_{\text{tone}} - L_{\text{noise floor}}$ in the same critical band (in dB).
  - **Prominence Ratio (PR):** More perceptually relevant; TNR penalized for low-frequency bands (where hearing is less sensitive).
  - **Tonality Index (K, Aures):** Combines TNR with sharpness and loudness effects.
- **Application in Automotive:** Gear whine, belt squeal, HVAC whistle, turbo whistle.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 4 (Psychoacoustic Indexes).

| Index | Formula (Stationary) | Variable Definitions | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Band Rate | $z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan(f/7500)^2$ | $f$ = frequency (Hz), $z$ = critical band rate (Bark) | Zwicker, 1961 |
| Loudness (Zwicker) | $N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz$ | $N'$ = specific loudness, $N$ = total loudness | ISO 532-1 / **Sone** |
| Phon-Loudness | $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | $L_N$ = loudness level in **Phon** | Stevens |
| Sharpness | $S = \dfrac{0.11 \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz}{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz}$ | $g(z)$ = high-frequency weighting, $z$ = Bark | DIN 45692 / **Acum** |
| Roughness (Daniel & Weber) | $R = c \int_0^{24} \dfrac{\Delta L_E(z)\, f_{\text{mod}}}{f_{\text{mod}}^2 + f_0^2}\, dz$ | $\Delta L_E$ = modulation depth (dB), $f_{\text{mod}}$ in Hz, $f_0 \approx 70$ Hz | **Asper** |
| Fluctuation Strength | $F \sim \int_0^{24} \dfrac{\Delta L_E(z)}{f_{\text{mod}}/4 + 4/f_{\text{mod}}}\, dz$ | For $f_{\text{mod}} < 20$ Hz | **Vacil** |
| Tonality (Prominence Ratio) | $\text{PR} = L_{\text{tone}} - L_{\text{masked}} + \text{penalty}(f)$ | $L_{\text{tone}}$ = tone level, penalty increases at low $f$ | **dB** |
| Articulation Index | $\text{AI} = \sum_i w_i \cdot (L_i - N_i)/30$ | $w_i$ = band importance weight, $L_i$ = band SPL, $N_i$ = noise in band | **0 to 1 (fraction)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Pitfall:** Loudness uses Sone (linear), Loudness Level uses Phon (log). Sharpness uses Acum, Roughness uses Asper, Fluctuation Strength uses Vacil. **All are 1-D numerical descriptors, but they have unique units.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Psychoacoustic Indexes |
|---|---|
| **Automotive NVH** | Engine sound "sound design," door-close sound tuning, brake squeal evaluation, EV motor whine masking, HVAC tonal control |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Tuning smartphone speaker quality, headphone/earphone sound signature |
| **Home Appliances** | Reducing perceived harshness of vacuum cleaners, refrigerators, washing machines |
| **HVAC / Building Acoustics** | Designing quiet ventilation (low fluctuation strength), avoiding tonal hum |
| **Psychoacoustics Research** | Hearing aid design, tinnitus research, auditory neuroscience |

In **automotive NVH specifically**, psychoacoustic indexes are now used in the **objective sound design** process — engineers specify a *target Sharpness, Loudness, and Roughness profile* for cabin sound before any subjective listening tests are conducted.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Loudness from Specific Loudness (Zwicker Method)

The Zwicker method is the most frequently examined derivation in KTU Module 4. We derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1: Spectral Input
We start with a sound pressure spectrum $L_p(f)$ in 1/3-octave bands or a narrowband FFT. We convert each frequency $f$ to its critical band rate $z$ (Bark) using the Zwicker transformation.

$$
z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Excitation Level $L_E(z)$
The excitation level accounts for the **absolute threshold of hearing** and the **internal noise of the ear**. For each Bark band:

$$
L_E(z) = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{0.1\,L_p(z)} + 10^{0.1\,L_{\text{thr}}(z)}\right)
$$

where $L_{\text{thr}}(z)$ is the threshold in quiet (well-known tabulated function: 0 dB at 4 Bark, rising sharply at both ends).

### Step 3: Apply the Lower Curve of Masking
The ear's internal masking raises the effective threshold in proportion to the loudness of nearby tones. The "lower curve" $L_q(z)$ (in dB) is empirically:

$$
L_q(z) = 10 \log_{10}\left( \max\left[1,\ 10^{0.1(L_E(z) - 5)} \cdot 10^{0.1\,\text{mask}(z)} \right] \right)
$$

The masking function (in dB) is piecewise:

$$
\text{mask}(z) = \begin{cases} -27 + 0.367\, z & z < 6.5 \\ -50 + 1.273\, z & 6.5 \le z \le 12 \\ -5.2 + 0.16\, z & z > 12 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Specific Loudness $N'(z)$
The specific loudness is a **non-linear function** of the corrected excitation level:

$$
N'(z) = \begin{cases} 10^{0.1\, L_q(z)} / 4 & \text{for low levels} \\ 10^{0.046\, L_q(z)} \cdot \text{const} & \text{for high levels} \end{cases}
$$

The exact Zwicker piecewise formula (widely tabulated):

$$
N'(z) = 0.0635 \cdot 10^{0.0295\, L_q(z)} \quad [\text{Sone/Bark}]
$$

This single expression maps each excitation level to its perceptual loudness density.

### Step 5: Integrate to Get Total Loudness
Loudness is the total "perceptual energy" integrated over all 24 Bark bands:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{Sone}]
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Board Style)
**Problem:** A 1 kHz pure tone is presented at 60 dB SPL. Compute the loudness.

**Solution:**
1. Convert 1 kHz → Bark: $z = 13 \arctan(0.76) + 3.5 \arctan(0.133)^2 \approx 8.5$ Bark.
2. Threshold at $z=8.5$ is about 6 dB. Excitation level: $L_E \approx 60$ dB (tone is far above threshold).
3. Apply masking correction: $L_q \approx L_E - 0.5 \approx 59.5$ dB.
4. Specific loudness: $N'(8.5) = 0.0635 \times 10^{0.0295 \times 59.5} = 0.0635 \times 10^{1.755} \approx 4.55$ Sone/Bark.
5. Approximate integration (assume narrow peak at 8.5 Bark with effective width 0.5 Bark due to critical band):

$$
N \approx 4.55 \times 0.5 \approx 2.27\ \text{Sone}
$$

This is **approximately 2.3 Sone**, which corresponds to ~50 phons — a sound perceived as "moderately loud." ✓ Matches reference data.

## 3.2 Derivation — Sharpness as a Weighted Centroid

### Step 1: Motivation
Sharpness must be **dimensionless** and **independent of loudness** (loudness is already accounted for by the denominator).

### Step 2: Define the Weighting Function
A high-frequency emphasis function $g(z)$ is defined as:

$$
g(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z \le 13.3 \\ 10^{1.25(z - 13.3)} & z > 13.3 \end{cases}
$$

This means at $z = 13.3$ Bark (≈ 2.5 kHz), $g=1$. Above this, the weight **increases exponentially** at 1.25 dB/Bark (in linear units, 17.8 dB per Bark).

### Step 3: Sharpness as a Centroid
The ratio of weighted specific loudness to total loudness gives the **centroid** of loudness density along the Bark axis. Multiplying by 0.11 calibrates the result so that a 1 kHz narrowband noise at 60 dB gives S = 1 acum.

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz}{\displaystyle\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Sharpness is the **loudness-weighted average critical band rate** (with high-frequency boost), scaled. So a sound with loudness density concentrated at high Bark has high sharpness.

## 3.3 Derivation — Roughness from Modulation Depth

### Step 1: Modulation Signal Model
Let the excitation level in a critical band vary sinusoidally:

$$
L_E(t) = \overline{L_E} + \frac{\Delta L_E}{2} \sin(2\pi f_{\text{mod}} t)
$$

where $\Delta L_E$ is the peak-to-peak modulation depth (in dB) and $f_{\text{mod}}$ is the modulation frequency (Hz).

### Step 2: Ear's Sensitivity to Modulation
The ear is **most sensitive to modulations around 70 Hz**. A first-order bandpass model gives the weighting function:

$$
w(f_{\text{mod}}) = \frac{f_{\text{mod}}}{f_{\text{mod}}^2 + f_0^2}, \quad f_0 \approx 70\ \text{Hz}
$$

Maximum of $w(f_{\text{mod}})$ occurs at $f_{\text{mod}} = f_0 = 70$ Hz (take derivative and set to zero — straightforward calculus).

### Step 3: Total Roughness
Summing across all critical bands (modulation in each band contributes independently):

$$
R = 0.0084 \int_0^{24} \Delta L_E(z) \cdot w(f_{\text{mod}})\, dz \quad [\text{Asper}]
$$

The constant 0.0084 is empirical — chosen so that 100% AM at 70 Hz on a 60 dB 1 kHz tone yields R = 1 asper.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Indexes

| Property | Loudness | Sharpness | Roughness | Fluctuation | Tonality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Sone | Acum | Asper | Vacil | dB (or Aures) |
| **Spectral weighting** | Internal (Zwicker) | High-frequency boost | None (modulation-based) | None (modulation-based) | Tonal peak vs. noise floor |
| **Temporal domain** | Time-averaged | Time-averaged | Modulation-based | Modulation-based | Spectral |
| **Primary input** | 1/3-octave SPL | 1/3-octave SPL | Modulation spectrum | Modulation spectrum | Tonal peak levels |
| **Calibration reference** | 1 kHz, 40 dB → 1 sone | 1 kHz NB, 60 dB → 1 acum | 1 kHz, 100% AM at 70 Hz → 1 asper | 1 kHz, 100% AM at 4 Hz → 1 vacil | 1 dB prominence |
| **Typical automotive value** | 5–40 sone | 1–5 acum | 0.1–5 asper | 0.1–3 vacil | TNR 3–10 dB |
| **Perceptual meaning** | "How loud" | "How piercing" | "How harsh" | "How throbbing" | "How tonal/whistly" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Psychoacoustic Index Calculation Pipeline

The following block diagram maps the universal signal processing chain used to compute any psychoacoustic index from a raw acoustic input.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Signal p t] --> B[FFT / 1/3-Octave Analysis]
    B --> C[SPL Spectrum L p f in dB]
    C --> D[Hz to Bark Conversion]
    D --> E[Excitation Level L E z with Threshold of Hearing]
    E --> F[Lower Curve of Masking L q z]
    F --> G[Specific Loudness N prime z]
    G --> H{Index Selection}
    H -->|Loudness| I1[Integrate N prime over 0 to 24 Bark]
    I1 --> J1[Total Loudness N in Sone]
    H -->|Sharpness| I2[Weighted Centroid using g z and z]
    I2 --> J2[Sharpness S in Acum]
    H -->|Roughness| I3[Modulation Depth per Band times w f mod]
    I3 --> J3[Roughness R in Asper]
    H -->|Fluctuation| I4[Slow Modulation Integration f mod under 20 Hz]
    I4 --> J4[Fluctuation Strength F in Vacil]
    H -->|Tonality| I5[Prominence Ratio per Critical Band]
    I5 --> J5[Tonality PR in dB]
    J1 --> K[Sound Quality Vector]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    J5 --> K
    K --> L[Subjective Mapping via Jury Tests]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every psychoacoustic index follows the same upstream pipeline (steps A → G). Only the final computation differs based on which perceptual dimension is being extracted. This is why all indexes are *compatible* — they can be computed simultaneously from a single spectral analysis.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Index Selection Based on Stimulus Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acoustic Stimulus] --> B{Dominant Characteristic}
    B -->|Steady Broadband| C[Loudness N]
    B -->|High Frequency Content| D[Sharpness S]
    B -->|Fast Amplitude Modulation 15 to 300 Hz| E[Roughness R]
    B -->|Slow Amplitude Modulation under 20 Hz| F[Fluctuation Strength F]
    B -->|Discrete Tonal Peaks| G[Tonality PR or TNR]
    C --> H[Application Cabin Engine NVH]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 Bark Scale Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[0 Bark 0 to 100 Hz] --> B[5 Bark 100 to 500 Hz]
    B --> C[10 Bark 500 to 2000 Hz]
    C --> D[15 Bark 2000 to 5000 Hz]
    D --> E[20 Bark 5000 to 12000 Hz]
    E --> F[24 Bark 12000 to 20000 Hz]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Bark scale is **non-linear**: 0–5 Bark spans only ~500 Hz, but 20–24 Bark spans ~8 kHz. This is because the human cochlea resolves low frequencies finely (high frequency resolution at low frequencies) and high frequencies coarsely.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Sound Quality Engineering Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Setting Customer Requirement] --> B[Objective Measurement Recording]
    B --> C[Psychoacoustic Index Computation Loudness Sharpness Roughness]
    C --> D[Subjective Evaluation Jury Tests]
    D --> E[Correlation Objective vs Subjective]
    E --> F{Target Met}
    F -->|No| G[Design Modification]
    G --> B
    F -->|Yes| H[Sign Off Sound Quality Approval]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Point:** Modern automotive OEMs use this closed-loop workflow. The "sound design target" is a **vector of psychoacoustic indexes**, e.g., *{Loudness < 25 sone, Sharpness < 3 acum, Roughness < 2 asper}* — specified *before* physical prototyping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**Define Loudness. Explain the difference between Sone and Phon.**

**Model Answer:**
Loudness is the perceptual intensity of a sound — how strong a sound appears to a human listener.

- **Sone** is the **linear** unit of loudness. 1 sone = loudness of a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL. A sound of 2 sones is perceived as **twice as loud** as 1 sone. Mathematical: $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$.
- **Phon** is the **logarithmic** unit of loudness level. It is the dB SPL of an equally loud 1 kHz reference tone. By definition, phon = dB SPL at 1 kHz.

**Key Conversion:** A doubling of Sone corresponds to a **+10 phon** increase in loudness level.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is Sharpness? How is it calculated and what does it indicate about a sound?**

**Model Answer:**
Sharpness (S) is a psychoacoustic index that quantifies the **"high-frequency content"** or **"piercing"** quality of a sound.

- **Unit:** Acum.
- **Reference:** 1 kHz narrowband noise at 60 dB → S = 1 acum.
- **Formula (DIN 45692):**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz}{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz}
$$

where $g(z)$ is the high-frequency weighting function. A high sharpness value (> 3 acum) typically indicates an **annoying, screeching, or whining sound** (e.g., brake squeal, gear whine, turbo whistle).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 4 questions carry **internal choice**. The student must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Loudness & Sharpness in Depth

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Zwicker method of calculating loudness** for a stationary sound. List the five steps and the role of the **lower curve of masking**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **DIN 45692 sharpness formula** and explain why sharpness is independent of loudness. Give two **automotive applications** where sharpness is the dominant perceptual metric. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Zwicker method (ISO 532-1) computes loudness in **5 steps**:

1. **Spectral Analysis:** Measure the SPL in 1/3-octave bands $L_p(f)$.
2. **Hz → Bark Conversion:** Transform each frequency to critical band rate:

$$
z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2
$$

3. **Excitation Level $L_E(z)$:** Add the energy of the external sound and the internal threshold noise:

$$
L_E(z) = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{0.1 L_p(z)} + 10^{0.1 L_{\text{thr}}(z)}\right)
$$

4. **Apply Lower Curve of Masking $L_q(z)$:** The lower curve accounts for **upward masking** — a loud tone in one critical band raises the effective threshold in nearby bands, suppressing softer sounds. This models the **mechanical coupling of the basilar membrane**. The piecewise masking function is:

$$
\text{mask}(z) = \begin{cases} -27 + 0.367 z & z < 6.5 \\ -50 + 1.273 z & 6.5 \le z \le 12 \\ -5.2 + 0.16 z & z > 12 \end{cases}
$$

The corrected level is $L_q(z) = L_E(z) + \text{mask}(z)$ (approximately).

5. **Specific Loudness and Integration:** Specific loudness is computed as $N'(z) = 0.0635 \cdot 10^{0.0295\, L_q(z)}$ in Sone/Bark. Total loudness:

$$
N = \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{Sone}]
$$

**Role of Lower Curve:** It captures the fact that the ear is not a perfect analyzer — strong tones suppress weaker tones in adjacent critical bands. Without it, loudness would be overestimated in complex spectra.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation of Sharpness:**

Starting from the requirement that sharpness must be (i) a single scalar, (ii) high-frequency-weighted, and (iii) independent of loudness, we define:

- The weighting function $g(z) = 1$ for $z \le 13.3$ Bark, and $g(z) = 10^{1.25(z-13.3)}$ for $z > 13.3$ Bark. This boosts high-Bark contributions.
- The **un-normalized sharpness** is the moment of the specific loudness distribution about $z = 0$:

$$
S' = \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz
$$

- Dividing by total loudness (denominator) removes the loudness dependency:

$$
S'_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz}{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz}
$$

- The factor 0.11 is the **calibration constant**, chosen so that 1 kHz NB noise at 60 dB gives S = 1 acum.

$$
\boxed{S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz}{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz}}
$$

**Why Loudness-Independent:** Because of the denominator $\int N'(z)\, dz$, doubling the overall sound level (and thus doubling $N'$) leaves the ratio unchanged. Sharpness only depends on the **shape** of the loudness distribution along the Bark axis.

**Automotive Applications:**
1. **Door-Close Sound:** Premium cars are tuned for a "solid thunk" (low sharpness ~ 1.5 acum), not a "tinny clack" (high sharpness > 3 acum).
2. **Brake Squeal:** Brake NVH evaluation uses sharpness as the primary metric — high sharpness values correlate strongly with customer complaints of "annoying" brake noise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Roughness, Fluctuation & Tonality

**(a)** With suitable equations, explain **Roughness (R)** and **Fluctuation Strength (F)**. Compare their modulation frequency ranges and units. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **Tonality**? Explain the **Prominence Ratio (PR)** and **Aures Tonality Index (K)**. Why is tonality critical in evaluating HVAC and turbocharger noise in automobiles? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Roughness (R):**
- **Perceptual meaning:** Perceived "harshness" or "modulation" of sound.
- **Modulation range:** $f_{\text{mod}}$ = 15 Hz to 300 Hz (peak sensitivity at 70 Hz).
- **Unit:** **Asper.**
- **Formula (Daniel & Weber):**

$$
R = 0.0084 \int_0^{24} \Delta L_E(z) \cdot \frac{f_{\text{mod}}}{f_{\text{mod}}^2 + f_0^2}\, dz, \quad f_0 = 70\ \text{Hz}
$$

The weighting $f_{\text{mod}}/(f_{\text{mod}}^2 + f_0^2)$ is **maximum at 70 Hz** — i.e., 70 Hz AM is the most "rough."

**Fluctuation Strength (F):**
- **Perceptual meaning:** Slow "pulsing" or "throbbing" sensation.
- **Modulation range:** $f_{\text{mod}}$ < 20 Hz (peak sensitivity at 4 Hz).
- **Unit:** **Vacil.**
- **Formula:**

$$
F = \frac{0.008 \int_0^{24} \Delta L_E(z)\, dz}{f_{\text{mod}}/4 + 4/f_{\text{mod}}}
$$

The denominator is **minimum at 4 Hz** — i.e., 4 Hz AM is the most "fluctuating."

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Roughness | Fluctuation Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation range | 15–300 Hz | 0.5–20 Hz |
| Peak sensitivity | 70 Hz | 4 Hz |
| Unit | Asper | Vacil |
| Perceptual feel | Harsh, grating | Pulsing, throbbing |
| Automotive example | Engine combustion modulation | Fan blade passing frequency |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Tonality** is the perceived prominence of **discrete tonal components** in a sound. A sound with strong tones (e.g., a whistle) is described as "tonal," while broadband noise is "atonal."

**Prominence Ratio (PR):**
Defined for each critical band:

$$
\text{PR}(z) = L_{\text{tone}}(z) - L_{\text{noise}}(z) + \text{penalty}(f)
$$

where $L_{\text{tone}}$ is the SPL of the tonal peak, $L_{\text{noise}}$ is the average SPL in neighboring bands (the masking noise floor), and the **penalty** reduces the PR for low-frequency tones (because the ear is less sensitive at low frequencies — equivalent to A-weighting conceptually).

- A sound is considered "tonal" if PR > 5–7 dB.

**Aures Tonality Index (K):**
A dimensionless index (0 to ~ 1) combining prominence ratio with sharpness and loudness effects:

$$
K = \frac{\sum_z w_z \cdot \text{PR}_z}{\sum_z w_z}
$$

where weights $w_z$ depend on the bandwidth of each tone and the critical bandwidth. It correlates well with **subjective tonal annoyance**.

**Why Tonality is Critical in Automotive NVH:**

1. **HVAC Noise:** Airflow over evaporator/condenser fins generates discrete tones. A prominent tone at 800 Hz (1× blade passing frequency) is judged highly annoying, even if overall SPL is low. PR is used to set target limits (typically PR < 5 dB).
2. **Turbocharger Whistle:** Turbo "siren" at 1500–3000 Hz is a tonal peak. Customer perception is dominated by the *tonality*, not the broadband level. Aures K is used to evaluate cabin microphone recordings.
3. **Gear Whine / Belt Squeal:** Both produce strong narrowband tones; Aures K provides a single number for complaint likelihood.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Confusing Sone and Phon** — Sone is linear, Phon is logarithmic. Equation $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ is REQUIRED.
> 2. **Forgetting the denominator in Sharpness** — without dividing by $\int N' dz$, sharpness becomes loudness-dependent (wrong!).
> 3. **Mixing up Roughness and Fluctuation frequency ranges** — 70 Hz is for Roughness, 4 Hz is for Fluctuation.
> 4. **No credit for units** — every index must be reported with its unit (Sone, Acum, Asper, Vacil, dB).
> 5. **Skipping the Bark scale** — almost every formula above uses $z$ (Bark), not $f$ (Hz). Students who write $f$ instead of $z$ in the loudness integral lose full credit.
> 6. **Not distinguishing TNR and PR** — TNR is purely level-based; PR applies a frequency-dependent penalty.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Psychoacoustic Indexes**

### Conceptual Foundations
- [ ] Psychoacoustic indexes quantify **subjective perception**, not physical SPL.
- [ ] The **Bark scale (0–24)** is the natural domain of all major indexes.
- [ ] The ear is modeled with **critical bands, internal noise, and masking**.

### Loudness (Sone)
- [ ] Zwicker method = ISO 532-1; Stevens Mark VII = ISO 532-2.
- [ ] 1 Sone = 1 kHz tone at 40 dB; 2 Sone = +10 Phon.
- [ ] Sone → Phon: $L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N)$.
- [ ] Loudness requires the **5-step pipeline** (FFT → Bark → $L_E$ → masking → $N'$ → integrate).

### Sharpness (Acum)
- [ ] DIN 45692 is the global standard.
- [ ] High-frequency weighting $g(z)$ boosts bands above 13.3 Bark (~ 2.5 kHz).
- [ ] Formula has 0.11 calibration constant — DO NOT forget.
- [ ] Door-close sound and brake squeal use sharpness as primary metric.

### Roughness (Asper)
- [ ] 15–300 Hz modulation, **peak at 70 Hz**.
- [ ] Daniel & Weber model uses $f_{\text{mod}}/(f_{\text{mod}}^2 + 70^2)$ weighting.
- [ ] Dominant in **engine combustion NVH and motor whine**.

### Fluctuation Strength (Vacil)
- [ ] Below 20 Hz, **peak at 4 Hz**.
- [ ] Dominant in **HVAC fan pulsation and road rumble**.

### Tonality (dB / K)
- [ ] **TNR** is purely level-based; **PR** adds frequency penalty; **Aures K** is the full perceptual index.
- [ ] A tonal component is "perceived" when **PR > 5–7 dB**.
- [ ] Critical for HVAC, turbo, gear whine, belt squeal.

### KTU Exam Quick Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] $z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan(f/7500)^2$
- [ ] $N = \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz$
- [ ] $S = 0.11 \int N' g z\, dz \big/ \int N'\, dz$
- [ ] $R = 0.0084 \int \Delta L_E \cdot w(f_{\text{mod}})\, dz$

### Units (Memorize All)
- [ ] **Sone** (Loudness), **Phon** (Loudness Level)
- [ ] **Acum** (Sharpness)
- [ ] **Asper** (Roughness), **Vacil** (Fluctuation Strength)
- [ ] **dB** (Tonality TNR/PR), **dimensionless 0–1** (Aures K, Articulation Index)

### Engineering Insight
- [ ] Modern automotive sound design specifies **psychoacoustic targets** (vectors of {Loudness, Sharpness, Roughness, Tonality}) **before** subjective jury tests.
- [ ] Same SPL ≠ same annoyance. Always compute psychoacoustic indexes for customer-relevant evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptable Degree of Harshness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Acceptable Degree of Harshness ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Harshness** in the context of Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness) refers to the subjective human response to the **short-duration, transient, and high-frequency mechanical disturbances** transmitted from the vehicle's drivetrain, suspension, and road interface to the passenger compartment. While *Vibration* refers to periodic/continuous oscillatory motion and *Noise* refers to airborne acoustic pressure fluctuations, **Harshness** specifically addresses the *quality* of impact events, bumps, and shock transients as perceived by vehicle occupants. The **Acceptable Degree of Harshness** is therefore defined as the quantified boundary of mechanical inputs (acceleration magnitude, jerk rate, frequency content, and exposure duration) below which the **majority of human occupants (typically the 50th percentile rating)** judge the ride experience as *acceptable*, *comfortable*, or *not objectionable*, and above which the ride is rated *uncomfortable*, *fatiguing*, or *intolerable*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Acceptable degree of harshness is **not a single fixed number** — it is a multi-parameter envelope defined by ISO 2631 / ISO 8041 standards combining *frequency-weighted RMS acceleration*, *Vibration Dose Value (VDV)*, and *Crest Factor* thresholds, cross-referenced with subjective jury ratings from SAE J1060 / J1490 procedures. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine you are sitting on a wooden chair and someone taps the seat from below with their finger: - A **soft, gentle tap** (low acceleration, low jerk) → you barely notice → **Acceptable** - A **moderate tap** (noticeable but not painful) → you shrug it off → **Borderline Acceptable** - A **hard hammer strike** (sharp jerk, high peak) → you wince and shift uncomfortably → **Unacceptable Harshness** Now scale this finger-tap to a car driving over a pothole, expansion joint, or rumble strip. The vehicle's suspension, tires, and seat mounts all act as **mechanical filters** that determine how much of that "tap" reaches your spine. The **Acceptable Degree of Harshness** is the maximum allowable "tap strength" after all filtering, that an average human will tolerate without complaint during a typical 30–60 minute commute. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Summary:** Harshness ≈ *how "sharp" a bump feels*, Acceptability ≈ *the threshold beyond which the sharp bump becomes annoying rather than merely noticeable*. ### 1.3 Critical Distinctions KTU Examiners Love | Term | Domain | Time Character | Frequency Range | Perception Type | |------|--------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------| | Noise | Acoustic (airborne) | Continuous/transient | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | Aural (ears) | | Vibration | Structural/mechanical | Periodic/continuous | 0.5 – 80 Hz | Tactile (body/seat) | | **Harshness** | Structural/mechanical | **Transient/impact** | **1 – 80 Hz (peak shock)** | **Tactile + Emotional (comfort)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** *Harshness* is the *quality dimension* of vibration — it depends not only on magnitude but on the **rate of change of acceleration (jerk)**, the **transient peak**, and the **spectral sharpness**. A 0.5 g sinusoidal vibration feels "smooth"; a 0.5 g impact shock feels "harsh". ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ - **Human body resonance (vertical):** **4 – 8 Hz** (most sensitive band for discomfort) - **Human body resonance (horizontal):** **1 – 2 Hz** (motion sickness band) - **ISO 2631-1 health guidance caution boundary:** $a_{w,rms} = 0.5 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (8-hour exposure) - **ISO 2631-1 comfort boundary (reduced comfort):** $a_{w,rms} = 0.315 \, \text{m/s}^2$ - **VDV comfort limit:** $\text{VDV} \le 8.5 \, \text{m/s}^{1.75}$ - **Crest factor threshold for VDV applicability:** $\text{CF} > 9$ ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Acceptable Harshness Assessment The Acceptable Degree of Harshness is determined by the intersection of three pillars: **Pillar 1 — Objective Measurement (Physics-Based)** Mechanical quantities measured at the seat-pan, backrest, and feet using accelerometers (per ISO 8041): - Frequency-weighted RMS acceleration - Vibration Dose Value (VDV) - Crest Factor - Peak Transient Acceleration (PTA) **Pillar 2 — Subjective Rating (Human-Based)** Standardized jury evaluations using bipolar or unipolar rating scales: - SAE J1060 / J1490 scale: 1 (unacceptable) → 10 (excellent) - Subjective Rating Threshold: ≥ **6.0/10** typically denotes "acceptable" **Pillar 3 — Statistical Correlation (Engineering Mapping)** Regression equations linking objective metrics to subjective scores, e.g.: $$\text{SR} = f(a_{w,rms}, \text{VDV}, \text{CF}, f_{dominant})$$ ### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown #### Step 1 — Signal Acquisition Tri-axial accelerometers (one vertical, two horizontal) are mounted on the **seat-pan** (where the occupant's pelvis rests — dominant vibration input) and the **floor pan** (vehicle-side reference). Signals are sampled at ≥ **100 Hz** (Nyquist criterion for harshness band) and anti-alias filtered. #### Step 2 — Frequency Weighting Raw acceleration is filtered using standardized frequency-weighting networks to reflect human perception: - $W_k$ weighting — vertical (z-axis) seat-pan vibration - $W_d$ weighting — horizontal (x,y axes) seat-pan vibration - $W_b$ weighting — higher frequency (0.5–80 Hz) whole-body vibration - $W_c$ weighting — vertical motion sickness (0.1–0.5 Hz) The weighted acceleration is: $$a_w(t) = W(f) \cdot a(t)$$ #### Step 3 — RMS Computation For continuous (vibratory) inputs: $$a_{w,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t)\, dt}$$ #### Step 4 — VDV Computation For transient/shock (harshness) inputs, RMS under-predicts discomfort. The **fourth-power Vibration Dose Value** is used: $$\text{VDV} = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_w^4(t)\, dt\right]^{1/4} \quad \left[\text{m/s}^{1.75}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why 4th power?** VDV's fourth-power weighting heavily penalizes **peaks and transients** — exactly what defines harshness. A short-duration 2 g shock contributes far more to VDV than a long 0.3 g steady vibration. #### Step 5 — Crest Factor Check $$\text{CF} = \frac{a_{w,peak}}{a_{w,rms}}$$ - If $\text{CF} < 9$ → RMS method is sufficient - If $\text{CF} \geq 9$ → VDV method **must** be used (harshness territory) #### Step 6 — Comparison to ISO 2631 Limits The measured $a_{w,rms}$ or VDV is compared against the ISO 2631-1 comfort/reduction/health boundaries (see Formula Sheet). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application | |---|---------|----------------|------|-------------| | 1 | $a_{w,rms} = \left[\dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t)\,dt\right]^{1/2}$ | Frequency-weighted RMS acceleration | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Steady-state vibration | | 2 | $\text{VDV} = \left[\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} a_w^4(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$ | Vibration Dose Value (4th root) | $\text{m/s}^{1.75}$ | Transient harshness | | 3 | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{a_{w,peak}}{a_{w,rms}}$ | Crest Factor | dimensionless | Transient detection | | 4 | $a_{eq} = \left[\dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t)\,dt\right]^{1/2}$ | Equivalent acceleration | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Equivalent steady exposure | | 5 | $\text{SR} = k_0 - k_1 \cdot a_{w,rms} - k_2 \cdot \text{VDV}$ | Subjective Rating regression | score (1–10) | Jury ↔ objective mapping | | 6 | $j(t) = \dfrac{da_w}{dt}$ | Jerk (rate of change of acceleration) | $\text{m/s}^3$ | Sharpness of harshness event | | 7 | $\text{ISO comfort boundary}: a_{w,rms} \le 0.315$ | Reduced comfort threshold | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Comfort judgement | | 8 | $\text{ISO health caution}: a_{w,rms} \le 0.5$ | 8-hr exposure limit | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Health & safety | | 9 | $\text{VDV comfort limit}: \text{VDV} \le 8.5$ | ISO 2631 comfort threshold | $\text{m/s}^{1.75}$ | Comfort judgement | | 10 | $\text{Seat Transmission Factor}: \text{STF} = \dfrac{a_{seat,rms}}{a_{floor,rms}}$ | Seat amplification/attenuation | dimensionless | Suspension design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions:** All ISO 2631 limits assume the subject is **seated upright with back supported**, exposure between **0.5–80 Hz**, and values reported as **weighted RMS** in $\text{m/s}^2$ (not g). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Suspension Tuning:** Engineers tune spring/damper rates so that road-induced seat-pan $a_{w,rms}$ stays below 0.315 $\text{m/s}^2$ on random roads. - **Tire Selection:** Stiff sidewall tires reduce harshness at the cost of road-noise isolation — quantifying acceptable harshness enables trade-off. - **Seat Foam Development:** Multi-density foam + seat suspension modules target STF between **0.6–0.8** in the 4–8 Hz band. - **Driveline Calibration:** Torque fluctuations in 2nd–3rd gear (1–4 Hz) cause shake; harshness thresholds limit calibration freedom. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Acceleration to VDV (Harshness Metric) **Given:** A seat-pan vertical acceleration signal $a(t)$ sampled over $T = 30$ s during a road-event test. The signal contains a transient shock of peak $1.8 \, \text{m/s}^2$ superimposed on 0.3 $\text{m/s}^2$ RMS background. Determine whether the harshness is acceptable. **Step 1 — Apply $W_k$ Frequency Weighting** The $W_k$ filter has a transfer function approximated by: $$ W_k(s) = \frac{(1+s/2\pi \cdot 12.92)(1+s/2\pi \cdot 2.34)}{(1+s/2\pi \cdot 2.05)(1+s/2\pi \cdot 15.91)^2} $$ After filtering, the weighted signal becomes $a_w(t)$. For our derivation, assume ideal filtering yields the simplified piecewise table: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At } f = 1\,\text{Hz} &: W_k = 0.612 \\ \text{At } f = 4\,\text{Hz} &: W_k = 0.951 \\ \text{At } f = 8\,\text{Hz} &: W_k = 0.880 \\ \text{At } f = 20\,\text{Hz} &: W_k = 0.388 \\ \text{At } f = 80\,\text{Hz} &: W_k = 0.046 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute RMS Acceleration** $$ \begin{aligned} a_{w,rms} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t)\, dt} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{30}\int_{0}^{30} a_w^2(t)\, dt} \end{aligned} $$ Assume integral evaluates to (numerical integration): $$ \int_{0}^{30} a_w^2(t)\, dt = 2.7 \;\; (\text{m/s}^2)^2 \cdot \text{s} $$ Therefore: $$ a_{w,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{2.7}{30}} = \sqrt{0.09} = 0.30 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Peak and Crest Factor** $$ \begin{aligned} a_{w,peak} &= 1.80 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 \\ \text{CF} &= \frac{a_{w,peak}}{a_{w,rms}} = \frac{1.80}{0.30} = 6.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Check CF Threshold** Since $\text{CF} = 6.0 < 9$, RMS method is acceptable — but **VDV will also be computed** since the peak is significant. **Step 5 — Compute VDV** Assume fourth-power integral evaluates to: $$ \int_{0}^{30} a_w^4(t)\, dt = 50{,}000 \;\; (\text{m/s}^2)^4 \cdot \text{s} $$ Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VDV} &= \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_w^4(t)\, dt\right]^{1/4} \\ &= \left[50{,}000\right]^{1/4} \\ &= (5 \times 10^4)^{0.25} \end{aligned} $$ Computing step-by-step: $$ \begin{aligned} (5 \times 10^4)^{0.25} &= 5^{0.25} \times (10^4)^{0.25} \\ &= 1.495 \times 10 \\ &= 14.95 \;\; \text{m/s}^{1.75} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compare to ISO 2631 Acceptability Limits** $$ \begin{aligned} a_{w,rms} = 0.30 \;\; &\text{m/s}^2 \;\; < 0.315 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{COMFORT PASS} \\ \text{VDV} = 14.95 \;\; &\text{m/s}^{1.75} \;\; > 8.5 \;\; \text{m/s}^{1.75} \;\; \Rightarrow \text{COMFORT FAIL} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The transient shock (harshness) **violates the VDV limit** even though the steady RMS is acceptable. The harshness is therefore **NOT acceptable** — the VDV metric properly penalizes the sharp impact that RMS missed. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Harshness Severity Classification | Class | $a_{w,rms}$ (m/s²) | VDV (m/s$^{1.75}$) | CF | Subjective Verdict | Engineering Action | |-------|--------------------|-------------------|-----|-------------------|--------------------| | 1 — Excellent | $\le 0.10$ | $\le 4.0$ | $\le 4$ | "Refined, luxury feel" | Maintain | | 2 — Good | $0.10 - 0.22$ | $4.0 - 7.0$ | $4 - 6$ | "Comfortable" | Acceptable | | 3 — Acceptable | $0.22 - 0.32$ | $7.0 - 8.5$ | $6 - 9$ | "Acceptable, slightly firm" | Marginal — monitor | | 4 — Reduced Comfort | $0.32 - 0.50$ | $8.5 - 12.0$ | $9 - 12$ | "Harsh, tiring on long drives" | Retune suspension | | 5 — Unacceptable | $> 0.50$ | $> 12.0$ | $> 12$ | "Very harsh, objectionable" | Mandatory redesign | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A vehicle's seat-pan records $a_{w,rms} = 0.40$ m/s² during a 1-hour mixed road test. Compute (a) the equivalent 8-hour exposure value, (b) the Crest Factor given $a_{w,peak} = 4.2$ m/s², and (c) determine acceptability. **(a) Equivalent 8-hour acceleration (assuming energy-equivalent scaling):** $$ \begin{aligned} a_{w,eq(8h)} &= a_{w,rms(1h)} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_2}} \\ &= 0.40 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{8}} \\ &= 0.40 \cdot 0.3536 \\ &= 0.141 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Crest Factor:** $$ \text{CF} = \frac{4.2}{0.40} = 10.5 $$ **(c) Acceptability:** - $\text{CF} = 10.5 > 9$ → **VDV must be used** (transient/harshness case) - Without VDV value, $a_{w,eq(8h)} = 0.141$ m/s² is well below the health caution boundary (0.5 m/s²), but **transient harshness assessment is incomplete without VDV**. - **Verdict:** Further VDV measurement required; CF alone signals significant transient content. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Harshness Assessment Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Road Excitation Input
Pothole Bump Joint"] nodeB["Vehicle Suspension Filter
Springs Dampers Bushes"] nodeC["Tire Filter
Sidewall Compliance"] nodeD["Body Structure
Modal Transfer"] nodeE["Seat Mount System
Foam Rails Slides"] nodeF["Occupant Body
Biomechanical Response"] nodeG["Triaxial Accelerometer
ISO 8041 Seat Pan"] nodeH["Signal Conditioning
Anti Alias Filter"] nodeI["Wk Wd Wb Weighting
ISO 2631 Filters"] nodeJ["RMS Computation
Continuous Metric"] nodeK["VDV Computation
4th Root Transient"] nodeL["Crest Factor Check
CF vs 9 Threshold"] nodeM["Acceptability Decision
ISO 2631 Limits"] nodeN["Subjective Jury
SAE J1060 Rating"] nodeO["Engineering Action
Retune Redesign Approve"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM nodeK --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeO nodeN --> nodeO nodeM --> nodeN ``` ### 4.2 Harshness Acceptability Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Measured Seat Pan Signal"] nodeQ1["Compute CF"] nodeQ2{"CF greater than 9"} nodeR1["Use RMS only
ISO 2631 Table"] nodeR2["Use VDV
ISO 2631 Method"] nodeS1{"RMS less than 0.315"} nodeS2{"VDV less than 8.5"} nodeT1["Acceptable Comfort"] nodeT2["Reduced Comfort"] nodeT3["Unacceptable Harsh"] nodeStart --> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2 nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeR1 nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeR2 nodeR1 --> nodeS1 nodeR2 --> nodeS2 nodeS1 -->|Yes| nodeT1 nodeS1 -->|No| nodeT2 nodeS2 -->|Yes| nodeT1 nodeS2 -->|No| nodeT3 ``` ### 4.3 Frequency-Weighted Human Sensitivity Curves (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeInput["Raw Acceleration
0 to 80 Hz"] nodeWk["Wk Filter
Vertical Seat
Peak 4 to 8 Hz"] nodeWd["Wd Filter
Horizontal Seat
Peak 1 to 2 Hz"] nodeWb["Wb Filter
Whole Body Vertical
Peak 4 to 8 Hz"] nodeWc["Wc Filter
Motion Sickness
Peak 0.1 to 0.5 Hz"] nodeOutput["Weighted Accelerations
aw z x y"] nodeInput --> nodeWk nodeInput --> nodeWd nodeInput --> nodeWb nodeInput --> nodeWc nodeWk --> nodeOutput nodeWd --> nodeOutput nodeWb --> nodeOutput nodeWc --> nodeOutput ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **Define "Acceptable Degree of Harshness" as applied to automotive NVH. List any two ISO standards used to quantify it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Acceptable Degree of Harshness** is the quantified limit of mechanical shock and transient vibration inputs transmitted to vehicle occupants below which the ride experience is judged as **comfortable and not objectionable** by the majority of occupants (typically rated ≥ 6/10 on SAE scales). It is quantified using: 1. **ISO 2631-1** — Mechanical vibration and shock — Evaluation of human exposure to whole-body vibration (provides comfort/reduced-comfort/health limits via $a_{w,rms}$ and VDV). 2. **ISO 8041** — Human response to vibration — Instrumentation and methods for measurement (defines seat-pan accelerometer mounting and $W_k$, $W_d$, $W_b$ filter characteristics). *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — ISO 2631, 1 mark — ISO 8041)* --- #### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Differentiate between RMS acceleration and Vibration Dose Value (VDV). When is VDV preferred over RMS?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | RMS Acceleration $a_{w,rms}$ | Vibration Dose Value VDV | |-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------| | Mathematical form | $\left[\dfrac{1}{T}\int a_w^2\,dt\right]^{1/2}$ | $\left[\int a_w^4\,dt\right]^{1/4}$ | | Penalizes | Average energy | Peaks and transients (4th power) | | Applicable to | Steady-state vibration | Transient/shock/harshness | | Threshold signal | $\text{CF} < 9$ | $\text{CF} \geq 9$ | **VDV is preferred over RMS** when the signal contains **significant transient peaks** (shocks, bumps, harshness events), identified by Crest Factor ≥ 9, because the fourth-power integration amplifies peak contributions that RMS averages out. *(1.5 marks — distinction, 1.5 marks — applicability condition)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Instruction:** *"Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks."* --- #### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`** **(a) Explain in detail the ISO 2631-1 methodology for assessing the acceptable degree of whole-body vibration exposure in seated occupants. Include in your answer the role of frequency weighting ($W_k$, $W_d$, $W_b$), the RMS acceleration metric, and the comfort/health boundaries. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Scope and Purpose (1 mark):** ISO 2631-1 provides a standardized method for evaluating human exposure to whole-body vibration in the frequency range **0.5–80 Hz** for a seated occupant. It defines three exposure zones: - **Health guidance caution zone** (fatigue/decreased proficiency) - **Reduced comfort zone** (acceptable ride quality limit) - **Maximum exposure limit** (safety threshold) **Step 2 — Measurement Locations (1 mark):** Per ISO 2631-1 / ISO 8041, accelerometers are placed: - **Seat-pan** (interface between seat and occupant pelvis) — most significant - **Backrest** (supporting back) - **Feet** (vehicle floor reference) Tri-axial measurement captures vertical ($a_z$), fore-aft ($a_x$), and lateral ($a_y$) axes. **Step 3 — Frequency Weighting (2 marks):** The raw acceleration is filtered through weighting networks to reflect human sensitivity: - $W_k$ — applied to seat-pan **vertical ($z$)** vibration; peaks at **4–8 Hz** (human spine resonance) - $W_d$ — applied to seat-pan **horizontal ($x$, $y$)** vibration; peaks at **1–2 Hz** - $W_b$ — alternative whole-body vertical weighting - $W_c$ — low-frequency motion-sickness weighting (0.1–0.5 Hz) The weighted acceleration becomes $a_w(t)$. **Step 4 — RMS Acceleration Computation (1 mark):** $$ a_{w,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t)\, dt} $$ This represents the equivalent continuous vibration magnitude. **Step 5 — Comfort and Health Boundaries (2 marks):** | Boundary | $a_{w,rms}$ (m/s²) | Verdict | |----------|---------------------|---------| | Comfort preserved | $\le 0.315$ | Comfortable | | Reduced comfort | $0.315 - 0.63$ | Marginally acceptable | | Health caution (8-hr) | $\le 0.5$ | Safety limit | | Health risk | $> 1.0$ | Excessive | The **vector sum** of weighted RMS across axes may also be used: $$a_{w,total} = \sqrt{(1.4 a_{wx})^2 + (1.4 a_{wy})^2 + a_{wz}^2}$$ The factor 1.4 reflects higher human sensitivity to lateral/fore-aft motion. **Conclusion (Bonus):** ISO 2631-1 establishes a defensible engineering specification for acceptable harshness — the seat-pan weighted RMS must remain below **0.315 m/s²** for "comfortable" classification. --- **(b) Define Vibration Dose Value (VDV). A vehicle seat-pan records an acceleration signal over 25 seconds. The fourth-power integral evaluates to $\int_0^{25} a_w^4(t)\, dt = 1.2 \times 10^5 \; (\text{m/s}^2)^4 \cdot \text{s}$. Determine the VDV and decide whether the harshness is acceptable as per ISO 2631. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition of VDV (1 mark):** The Vibration Dose Value quantifies the cumulative exposure to vibration, particularly when transient shocks (harshness) dominate. It is defined as the **fourth root of the time integral of the fourth power of frequency-weighted acceleration**: $$ \text{VDV} = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_w^4(t)\, dt\right]^{1/4} \;\; [\text{m/s}^{1.75}] $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} T &= 25 \;\; \text{s} \\ \int_{0}^{25} a_w^4(t)\, dt &= 1.2 \times 10^5 \;\; (\text{m/s}^2)^4 \cdot \text{s} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VDV} &= \left[1.2 \times 10^5\right]^{1/4} \\ &= (1.2)^{0.25} \times (10^5)^{0.25} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} (1.2)^{0.25} &\approx 1.0466 \\ (10^5)^{0.25} &= 10^{5 \times 0.25} = 10^{1.25} \approx 17.783 \end{aligned} $$ Multiplying: $$ \text{VDV} \approx 1.0466 \times 17.783 \approx 18.61 \;\; \text{m/s}^{1.75} $$ **Step 4 — Apply ISO 2631 VDV Limits (1 mark):** | VDV Range (m/s$^{1.75}$) | Verdict | |--------------------------|---------| | $\le 8.5$ | Comfortable (acceptable) | | $8.5 - 12.0$ | Reduced comfort | | $> 12.0$ | Unacceptable harshness | **Step 5 — Final Verdict (1 mark):** Since $\text{VDV} = 18.61 \; \text{m/s}^{1.75} > 12.0 \; \text{m/s}^{1.75}$, the harshness is **UNACCEPTABLE**. The vehicle's suspension/seat system requires modification to reduce transient peak transmission. --- #### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` (Alternative Choice)** **(a) Discuss the subjective and objective methods used to assess the acceptable degree of harshness in automotive applications. Compare their merits and demerits. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1 — Subjective Methods (3 marks):** Subjective methods rely on **human jury ratings** under controlled conditions. **SAE J1060 / J1490 Procedure:** - Panel of **8–16 trained or untrained raters** (depending on study design) - Standardized road or rig test - Rating scale: typically **1 (worst) to 10 (best)** or bipolar scale (-3 to +3) - Statistical analysis: Mean Opinion Score (MOS), standard deviation - **Acceptability threshold:** Mean rating ≥ 6.0 typically denotes acceptable **Merits:** - Directly captures human perception (the ultimate customer) - Captures aspects physics alone misses (annoyance, fatigue expectation) - Required for final sign-off validation **Demerits:** - Time-consuming, expensive, requires panel coordination - High inter-rater variability (need statistical significance) - Influenced by ambient factors (temperature, noise, vehicle speed) - Cannot be used in early design phase --- **2 — Objective Methods (3 marks):** Objective methods use **instrumentation and standardized metrics**. **Standard Procedure (per ISO 2631 / ISO 8041):** - Tri-axial accelerometers at seat-pan, backrest, feet - Frequency weighting ($W_k$, $W_d$, $W_b$) - Compute $a_{w,rms}$ and/or VDV - Compare to ISO acceptability boundaries **Merits:** - Repeatable, reproducible - Cost-effective (one-time rig + sensors) - Enables early design evaluation (CAE correlation) - Quantitative — supports engineering trade-off studies **Demerits:** - May not capture all perceptual qualities (cultural variation, expectation) - Requires correlation with subjective data to be meaningful - Sensor placement errors invalidate results --- **3 — Comparative Summary (1 mark):** | Aspect | Subjective | Objective | |--------|-----------|-----------| | Directness of perception | High | Indirect | | Repeatability | Low | High | | Cost | High | Low | | Early-phase usability | Poor | Excellent | | Final sign-off | Mandatory | Supplementary | **Conclusion:** Modern NVH engineering uses **objective methods during development** and **subjective methods for final validation**. The two are correlated via regression models: $$\text{SR} = k_0 - k_1 \cdot a_{w,rms} - k_2 \cdot \text{VDV}$$ --- **(b) A car's front suspension is being designed. The target seat-pan weighted RMS acceleration is 0.28 m/s² on a 1-hour standard road profile. The measured peak acceleration is 3.36 m/s². Calculate the Crest Factor and decide whether RMS or VDV method should be used to assess acceptability. If VDV is required and the measured VDV is 9.2 m/s$^{1.75}$, comment on acceptability. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Crest Factor Calculation (2 marks):** $$ \text{CF} = \frac{a_{w,peak}}{a_{w,rms}} = \frac{3.36}{0.28} = 12.0 $$ **Step 2 — Method Selection (1 mark):** Since $\text{CF} = 12.0 > 9$, the **VDV method must be used** to assess acceptability (ISO 2631-1 requirement). **Step 3 — RMS Preliminary Check (1 mark):** Even though CF > 9, we can still note: $$ a_{w,rms} = 0.28 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 < 0.315 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{RMS alone: COMFORT PASS} $$ However, RMS is **not sufficient** when CF > 9; VDV is mandatory. **Step 4 — VDV Acceptability Check (2 marks):** ISO 2631 VDV comfort limit: $\text{VDV} \le 8.5 \; \text{m/s}^{1.75}$ Measured: $\text{VDV} = 9.2 \; \text{m/s}^{1.75}$ Since $9.2 > 8.5$, the **VDV slightly exceeds the comfort limit**. **Step 5 — Engineering Verdict and Recommendation (1 mark):** | Metric | Value | Limit | Verdict | |--------|-------|-------|---------| | $a_{w,rms}$ | 0.28 m/s² | 0.315 m/s² | PASS (but CF > 9, secondary) | | VDV | 9.2 m/s$^{1.75}$ | 8.5 m/s$^{1.75}$ | **MARGINAL FAIL** | | Crest Factor | 12.0 | > 9 | Transient regime | **Final Verdict:** The design is **marginally unacceptable** for harshness. The transient peaks are slightly excessive. **Recommendation:** - Increase damper rebound damping by ~10–15% to suppress peak overshoot - Re-tune tire section height (taller sidewall) to filter high-frequency shock - Re-evaluate and target VDV ≤ 8.0 m/s$^{1.75}$ for safety margin. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing VDV units:** VDV is in $\text{m/s}^{1.75}$, **not** $\text{m/s}^2$. Many students write the wrong unit and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Ignoring CF check:** If CF > 9, RMS-based verdict is **invalid** — examiners specifically look for VDV application. Failing to compute CF = guaranteed deduction. > 3. **Forgetting frequency weighting:** Reporting raw RMS (without $W_k$ weighting) is incorrect — always specify $a_{w,rms}$ (weighted), not $a_{rms}$. > 4. **Wrong VDV formula:** Students often confuse the exponent — VDV uses **4th root of 4th power integral**, not 2nd root. Verify: $(\text{units})^{4 \times 0.25} = \text{units}^1$ gives m/s$^{1.75}$. > 5. **Skipping vector sum:** In multi-axis seat measurement, ISO 2631 requires the weighted vector sum — forgetting lateral factors (1.4×) costs marks. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Harshness = transient/tactile discomfort**; distinct from noise (acoustic) and vibration (continuous). - ✅ **Acceptable Degree of Harshness** is quantified using **ISO 2631-1 / ISO 8041** standards. - ✅ **Two key metrics:** $a_{w,rms}$ (continuous) and **VDV** (transient, 4th-root, 4th-power integral). - ✅ **Crest Factor CF = $a_{peak}/a_{rms}$**; if **CF ≥ 9 → VDV is mandatory**. - ✅ **Comfort boundary:** $a_{w,rms} \le 0.315 \; \text{m/s}^2$; **VDV limit:** $\text{VDV} \le 8.5 \; \text{m/s}^{1.75}$. - ✅ **Health caution boundary:** $a_{w,rms} \le 0.5 \; \text{m/s}^2$ for 8-hour exposure. - ✅ **Frequency weightings:** $W_k$ (vertical seat), $W_d$ (horizontal seat), $W_b$ (whole-body), $W_c$ (motion sickness). - ✅ **Measurement points:** seat-pan (primary), backrest, feet — all tri-axial. - ✅ **Subjective rating scale:** SAE J1060 / J1490, 1–10 scale, ≥ 6 = acceptable. - ✅ **Regression model:** $\text{SR} = k_0 - k_1 a_{w,rms} - k_2 \text{VDV}$ links physics to perception. - ✅ **Vector sum formula:** $a_{total} = \sqrt{(1.4 a_x)^2 + (1.4 a_y)^2 + a_z^2}$. - ✅ **Seat Transmission Factor (STF)** = $a_{seat,rms}/a_{floor,rms}$ → typical target 0.6–0.8. - ✅ **Human resonance:** **4–8 Hz vertical**, **1–2 Hz horizontal** — design filters to avoid amplification. - ✅ **Engineering trade-off:** Stiffer suspension reduces harshness but increases road-noise transmission — quantified via acceptable harshness envelope. - ✅ **Always write units correctly:** VDV in m/s$^{1.75}$; RMS in m/s² (not g, unless explicitly converted).

topics.title: Sound Quality Indexes for High Frequency Tonal Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sound Quality Indexes for High Frequency Tonal Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Sound Quality (SQ)** is the field of psychoacoustics that objectively measures the subjective perception of sound beyond mere loudness. **Sound Quality Indexes (SQIs)** are mathematically defined, frequency-dependent metrics that correlate acoustic pressure signals with human auditory perception attributes such as sharpness, tonality, roughness, and articulation.

For **High Frequency Tonal Noise (HFTN)** — typically defined as acoustic energy concentrated in discrete spectral lines (tones) above **1 kHz** — the two dominant SQIs are:

- **Sharpness (S):** quantifies the "high-pitched, biting, piercing" character of a sound.
- **Tonality (K):** quantifies the extent to which a sound is perceived as containing distinct musical tones versus broadband noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** HFTN sources in an automobile include **gear whine** (1–8 kHz), **belt squeal** (2–10 kHz), **brake squeal** (1–15 kHz), **HVAC blower whistle** (2–6 kHz), and **tire cavity resonance** (200–300 Hz, but its harmonics dominate the high-frequency tonal field).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine standing next to two different engines:
- **Engine A:** emits a deep, low rumble (broadband, low-frequency heavy) — you would describe it as *powerful and smooth*.
- **Engine B:** emits a sharp, high-pitched whine (concentrated tones above 3 kHz) — you would describe it as *annoying, cheap, and piercing*, even if both engines measure the same **dB(A)**.

A sound-level meter treats them as equal loudness, but the human ear penalises Engine B. The difference is captured entirely by **sharpness** and **tonality**. They are the psychoacoustic "fine print" that explains why a quieter object is not necessarily a less annoying one.

### 1.3 Why High-Frequency Tonal Noise Demands Special Indexes

| Ear Property | Frequency Behavior | Consequence for HFTN |
|---|---|---|
| Equal-loudness contours (ISO 226) | Compress above 1–4 kHz | dB(A) under-penalises HFTN |
| Critical band rate (Bark) | Widen logarithmically | Resolves discrete tones → tonality perception rises |
| Outer/middle ear transfer (A-weighting) | Roll-off above 6 kHz | High-tone content escapes A-weighting penalty |

> [!NOTE]
> A pure **4 kHz tone at 60 dB** is perceived ~**5–8 times sharper** than a 500 Hz tone of the same A-weighted level. Standard SPL metrics cannot reflect this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sharpness (S) — DIN 45692:2009 Standard

Sharpness models the *centroid* of the perceived loudness distribution along the critical-band (Bark) axis. It is the **most cited high-frequency SQI** in KTU automotive modules.

**Conceptual Steps:**
1. Compute the time-varying specific loudness $N'(z, t)$ over the critical band rate $z$ (Bark).
2. Apply the **first-order moment** weighting: louder high-Bark bands contribute disproportionately.
3. Normalise by the total loudness to obtain a frequency-centroid metric in **acum** (acum = unit of sharpness).

**Primary Formulas:**

$$
S = 0.11 \, \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24\;\text{Bark}} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z \, dz}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24\;\text{Bark}} N'(z)\, dz} \quad \text{[acum]}
$$

$$
g(z) = 
\begin{cases}
1.0, & z \le 13.5 \text{ Bark} \\
0.066\,\exp(0.171\,z), & z > 13.5 \text{ Bark}
\end{cases}
$$

**Reference scaling (so 1 kHz pure tone @ 60 dB = 1 acum):**

$$
S_{\text{norm}} = 1.0 \, \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24\;\text{Bark}} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z \, dz}{N \cdot z_0}, \quad z_0 = 13.5 \text{ Bark}
$$

**Stationary narrow-band computation (Zwicker & Fastl, 1990):**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\sum_i N'_i \cdot z_i \cdot g(z_i)}{\sum_i N'_i} \quad [\text{acum}]
$$

**Subjective Interpretation Scale (Zwicker/Fastl):**

| Sharpness S (acum) | Subjective Descriptor |
|---|---|
| $< 0.7$ | Dull, mellow |
| $0.7 - 1.0$ | Neutral |
| $1.0 - 1.5$ | Sharp |
| $1.5 - 2.5$ | Very sharp, penetrating |
| $> 2.5$ | Extremely sharp, intolerable |

### 2.2 Tonality (K) — Aures Model & ECMA-74

Tonality quantifies the perceptual weight of tonal components in a sound. Two industry-standard variants exist.

#### (a) Aures Tonality (Zwicker/Fastl — psychoacoustic)

$$
K = \frac{c_1}{\sum N'_i \, dz}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta N_i \, s_i \quad \text{[tu]}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta N_i$ = excess loudness above the masked threshold in band $i$
- $s_i$ = specific loudness slope $\dfrac{dN'_i}{dz}$ (steep slopes signal tones)
- $c_1 = 1.0$ (calibration constant)
- **tu** = tonality unit

#### (b) Prominence Ratio (PR) — ECMA-74 / ISO 7779

The most widely used SQI for HFTN in OEM NVH teams.

$$
\text{PR}_i = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{10^{L_{p_i}/10}}{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{j} 10^{L_{p_j}/10}} \right) \quad \text{[dB]}
$$

Where:
- $L_{p_i}$ = SPL of the candidate tone in band $i$
- The summation runs over the critical band and its **two adjacent bands** ($n = 3$ typically) or ±5 % frequency neighbourhood.

**Tonal Acceptance Criterion (ECMA-74):**

| PR (dB) | Tonal Audibility |
|---|---|
| $< 4$ | Inaudible as tone |
| $4 - 8$ | Marginally perceptible |
| $> 8$ | Clearly tonal → fails acceptance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** A "tone" is not defined by amplitude alone. It is defined by **prominence above the local average critical-band level**. A 90 dB tone buried in 92 dB broadband noise has PR ≈ 0 dB → not tonal perceptually.

### 2.3 Articulation Index (AI) & Speech Intelligibility Index (SII)

For in-cabin NVH targeting voice-command systems, hands-free telephony, and ADAS auditory warnings, **SII** (ANSI S3.5-1997, replacing the older AI by French & Steinberg, 1947) is used.

$$
\text{AI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot T_i
$$

$$
\text{SII} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \cdot A_i \cdot B_i \cdot C_i
$$

Where:
- $W_i$ = band importance function (500 Hz – 4 kHz dominate, $W_i \in [0, 1]$)
- $T_i$ = transmission index of band $i$
- $I_i$ = band level distortion factor
- $A_i$ = audibility factor (SNR dependent)
- $B_i$ = band redundancy factor
- $C_i$ = upward-spread masking correction

**KTU SII Interpretation Scale (ANSI S3.5):**

| SII | Speech Intelligibility |
|---|---|
| $0.00 - 0.30$ | Poor (sub-word only) |
| $0.30 - 0.50$ | Fair (sentences possible) |
| $0.50 - 0.70$ | Good |
| $0.70 - 1.00$ | Excellent (normal communication) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet Table

| Index | Symbol | Domain / Unit | Formula (compact) | Key Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Loudness (Zwicker) | $N$ | sone | $N = 2^{0.1(L_N - 40)}$ | $L_N$ in phon |
| Specific Loudness | $N'$ | sone/Bark | from critical-band analysis | $0 \le z \le 24$ |
| Sharpness | $S$ | acum | $S = 0.11\,\dfrac{\sum N'_i z_i g(z_i)}{\sum N'_i}$ | $S > 1.5$ = sharp |
| Tonality (Aures) | $K$ | tu | $K = c_1 \sum \Delta N_i s_i / N$ | $K > 1$ = tonal |
| Prominence Ratio | $\text{PR}$ | dB | $\text{PR} = 10\log_{10}(L_{p_i}/\bar{L}_p)$ | PR $> 8$ dB = fail |
| Articulation Index | $\text{AI}$ | $0$ – $1$ | $\text{AI} = \sum W_i T_i$ | AI $\ge 0.5$ |
| Speech Intelligibility Index | $\text{SII}$ | $0$ – $1$ | $\text{SII} = \sum I_i A_i B_i C_i$ | SII $\ge 0.5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All SQIs are **dimensionless** or carry their own named unit (acum, tu, sone, phon). They are never expressed in dB directly — the **dB scale is reserved for physical SPL** inputs to the SQI computation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NVH Target Setting:** OEMs set $S \le 1.4$ acum at idle and $S \le 1.8$ acum at WOT (wide-open throttle) for premium cabins.
- **Gear Whine Diagnosis:** PR at 1×, 2×, 3× meshing frequency in the 2–8 kHz band flags HFTN issues months before subjective evaluations.
- **ADAS & Voice Control Certification:** SII $\ge 0.5$ is a homologation requirement for hands-free emergency call (eCall/HFC) in EU vehicles.
- **Legal/Workplace Safety:** ECMA-74 PR is the **EN ISO 7779** machinery noise compliance metric for information-technology equipment.
