## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Sharpness of a 4 kHz Pure Tone at 60 dB

**Given:** Plane-wave pure tone, $f = 4000\;\text{Hz}$, $L_p = 60\;\text{dB SPL}$, free-field frontal incidence.

**Step 1 — Map frequency to critical-band rate (Bark) using the Traunmüller (1990) approximation:**

$$
z = 26.81\,\frac{f/1000}{1960 + f/1000} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = 26.81 \cdot \frac{4}{1960 + 4} - 0.53 = 26.81 \cdot \frac{4}{1964} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = 26.81 \cdot 0.002036 - 0.53 = 0.05460 - 0.53
$$

$$
z = -0.475 \text{ Bark}
$$

**Correction for $z < 0$:** the 4 kHz tone lies below the lowest critical band in the Zwicker model, so we set the active critical-band rate as the lower-edge Bark of band 8, $z_8 \approx 7.8$ Bark (a common textbook approximation for the 4 kHz centre).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations frequently test the **mapping inversion**: given $z$ (Bark), recover $f$ via $f = 1960\,(z + 0.53)/(26.81 - (z + 0.53))$.

**Step 2 — Compute loudness level $L_N$ from SPL using ISO 226 equal-loudness contour.**

At 4 kHz, the 60-phon contour is approximately at 60 dB SPL (this is the reference point of ISO 226 at 1 kHz and rolls off). Using the Zwicker free-field lookup table for 4 kHz:

$$
L_N(4\;\text{kHz}, 60\;\text{dB}) \approx 59\;\text{phon}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert phon to sone:**

$$
N = 2^{0.1(L_N - 40)} = 2^{0.1 \times 19} = 2^{1.9} = 3.732 \;\text{sone}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert sone to specific loudness $N'$.** For a pure tone fully inside a single critical band:

$$
N'_i = \frac{N}{\Delta z_i}
$$

Critical-band width at $z \approx 7.8$ is $\Delta z \approx 1.0$ Bark (Zwicker). So:

$$
N'_i = \frac{3.732}{1.0} = 3.732 \;\text{sone/Bark}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the sharpness weight $g(z)$.** Since $z = 7.8 < 13.5$:

$$
g(z) = 1.0
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the sharpness formula:**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{N'_i \cdot z_i \cdot g(z_i)}{N'_i} = 0.11 \cdot z_i \cdot g(z_i)
$$

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot 7.8 \cdot 1.0 = 0.858 \;\text{acum}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the standard Zwicker/Fastl normalisation constant (s = 0.11 was selected so a 1 kHz / 60 dB tone = 1 acum). For a 4 kHz tone:**

$$
S_{4\;\text{kHz}} \approx 0.86 \;\text{acum}
$$

This is mildly sharp — far below the 2.0+ acum that brake squeal reaches.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Prominence Ratio of a 3.2 kHz Gear-Whine Tone

**Given (NVH test, WOT, 2nd gear, vehicle cabin microphone):**
- Tone at $f_t = 3200\;\text{Hz}$, $L_{p_t} = 52\;\text{dB SPL}$
- Critical-band centred at 3.2 kHz has $L_{p_{\text{centre}}} = 52\;\text{dB}$, two adjacent bands at $L_{p_{\text{low}}} = 41\;\text{dB}$, $L_{p_{\text{high}}} = 43\;\text{dB}$.

**Step 1 — Convert each band SPL to linear intensity:**

$$
I_t = 10^{52/10} = 10^{5.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{5} \;\text{(a.u.)}
$$

$$
I_{\text{low}} = 10^{41/10} = 1.259 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
I_{\text{high}} = 10^{43/10} = 1.995 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean intensity of the three bands:**

$$
\bar{I} = \frac{1.585 \times 10^{5} + 1.259 \times 10^{4} + 1.995 \times 10^{4}}{3}
$$

$$
\bar{I} = \frac{1.585 \times 10^{5} + 0.3195 \times 10^{5}}{3} = \frac{1.9045 \times 10^{5}}{3}
$$

$$
\bar{I} = 6.348 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Prominence Ratio:**

$$
\text{PR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_t}{\bar{I}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.585 \times 10^{5}}{6.348 \times 10^{4}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{PR} = 10 \log_{10}(2.497) = 10 \times 0.3974
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PR} = 3.97 \;\text{dB} \;\;(\text{marginal — engineering team will flag for retest})}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Aures Tonality of a Pure Tone in Broadband Noise

**Given:** A 2 kHz pure tone at $L_p = 55$ dB SPL, masked by broadband pink noise of $L_{p,\text{noise}} = 45$ dB SPL within the surrounding critical band.

**Step 1 — Compute specific loudness of the masked excitation.** Above masked threshold, the excess specific loudness is approximately:

$$
\Delta N' = N'_{\text{total}} - N'_{\text{mask}}
$$

Using the Zwicker approximation (loudness-additive for uncorrelated signals):

$$
N'_{\text{total}} \approx 1.5\;\text{sone/Bark},\quad N'_{\text{mask}} \approx 0.4\;\text{sone/Bark}
$$

$$
\Delta N' = 1.1 \;\text{sone/Bark}
$$

**Step 2 — Loudness slope $s_i$:** for a pure tone in critical band $z_5 \approx 4.5$ Bark, the slope $s_i$ from Zwicker tables:

$$
s_i \approx 0.35 \;\text{(normalised, dimensionless)}
$$

**Step 3 — Total loudness $N$ (sone).** For a 2 kHz tone at 55 dB, $L_N \approx 56$ phon:

$$
N = 2^{0.1(56 - 40)} = 2^{1.6} = 3.03\;\text{sone}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Aures formula (single band):**

$$
K = \frac{c_1 \cdot \Delta N' \cdot s_i}{N} = \frac{1.0 \cdot 1.1 \cdot 0.35}{3.03}
$$

$$
\boxed{K = 0.127 \;\text{tu}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $K \approx 0.13$ tu is **sub-threshold** (KTU rule of thumb: $K \ge 1$ tu is clearly tonal; $0.3 \le K < 1$ is borderline; $K < 0.3$ is non-tonal). The tone is acoustically present but **perceptually masked** by the broadband noise.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which SQI to Use?

| Application | Recommended SQI | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Brake squeal (2–15 kHz pure tones) | **Sharpness + PR** | Pure tones embedded in low background |
| HVAC blower whistle (2–6 kHz) | **Sharpness** | Multi-tone, perceived as "hissy" |
| Gear whine (1–8 kHz harmonics) | **PR + Tonality** | Discrete harmonics, audibility-driven |
| Powertrain boom (low frequency) | **Roughness** (Module 3) | Modulation dominated |
| In-cabin speech intelligibility | **SII / AI** | Communication metric |
| EV warning sound design | **Sharpness + Loudness** | Pedestrian-safety band (1–4 kHz) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define "Sharpness" of a sound as per DIN 45692. Mention the threshold value above which a sound is generally classified as "very sharp" and list the two physical properties of the human ear that cause A-weighted SPL to underestimate sharpness.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Sharpness $S$ is a psychoacoustic SQI defined as the **first-order moment of specific loudness $N'(z)$ about the critical-band rate (Bark) axis, weighted by the high-frequency emphasising function $g(z)$**, expressed in **acum**.

  $$
  S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\sum N'_i \cdot z_i \cdot g(z_i)}{\sum N'_i}
  $$

- **Threshold (1 mark):** $S > 1.5$ acum → "very sharp / penetrating"; $S > 2.5$ acum → "extremely sharp / intolerable".

- **Ear properties (1 mark):**
  1. **Equal-loudness contour compression above 3–4 kHz** — perceived loudness rises faster than A-weighting implies.
  2. **A-weighting roll-off above 6 kHz** (−∞ asymptote at 20 kHz) actively *attenuates* the very bands that drive sharpness perception.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is Prominence Ratio (PR) as defined in ECMA-74? State the PR threshold above which a tone is considered audibly tonal and a cause for NVH rejection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** PR is the logarithmic ratio, in dB, of the **sound-pressure intensity of a candidate tonal component to the mean intensity of the surrounding critical-band context** (typically ±1 critical band, or ±5 % of frequency).

  $$
  \text{PR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_t}{\bar{I}_{\text{context}}}\right) \;\text{dB}
  $$

- **Threshold (1 mark):** **PR $\ge$ 8 dB** → tone is clearly audible and constitutes an **NVH acceptance failure** under ECMA-74 / ISO 7779.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive, with a sketch of the function $g(z)$ versus critical-band rate $z$, the **DIN 45692 Sharpness formula** for a stationary narrow-band sound. Clearly state the units and the normalisation reference. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cabin NVH test produces the following specific-loudness spectrum (stationary, free field). Compute the **sharpness** and state the subjective rating.

| Band $i$ | $z_i$ (Bark) | $N'_i$ (sone/Bark) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 5.0 | 1.2 |
| 3 | 9.0 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 12.0 | 1.8 |
| 5 | 15.0 | 1.0 |
| 6 | 18.0 | 0.6 |
| 7 | 21.0 | 0.3 |

(7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Loudness framework (2 marks):* Specific loudness $N'(z)$ is the **loudness density per unit critical band**; integrating over the full 24 Bark range gives total loudness $N = \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz$ in **sone**.

*Step 2 — Definition of sharpness as a moment (2 marks):* Sharpness is the *centroid* of the loudness distribution along the Bark axis, biased towards higher $z$ by the weighting $g(z)$:

$$
S = c_0 \cdot \frac{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z \, dz}{\int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz}, \quad c_0 = 0.11
$$

*Step 3 — Sketch of $g(z)$ (2 marks):*

```
g(z)
  |
1 |---------___________________________
  |                                  ``---.._
  |                                          ``--.._
  |                                                  ``---.._
0 +-------------------------------------------------------- z (Bark)
  0     5    10   13.5                              24
```

For $z \le 13.5$ Bark, $g(z) = 1$. For $z > 13.5$ Bark, $g(z) = 0.066\,e^{0.171\,z}$ (rapidly rising exponential, e.g., $g(20) \approx 0.79$, $g(24) \approx 1.55$).

*Step 4 — Normalisation reference (1 mark):* The constant $c_0 = 0.11$ and reference $z_0 = 13.5$ Bark are chosen so that a **1 kHz pure tone at 60 dB SPL** (free-field frontal) yields exactly **$S = 1.0$ acum**.

**(b) Numerical evaluation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Compute $g(z_i)$:*

| $z_i$ | $g(z_i)$ |
|---|---|
| 2.0 | 1.0 |
| 5.0 | 1.0 |
| 9.0 | 1.0 |
| 12.0 | 1.0 |
| 15.0 | $0.066\,e^{0.171\times 15} = 0.066\,e^{2.565} = 0.066 \times 13.00 = 0.858$ |
| 18.0 | $0.066\,e^{0.171\times 18} = 0.066\,e^{3.078} = 0.066 \times 21.71 = 1.433$ |
| 21.0 | $0.066\,e^{0.171\times 21} = 0.066\,e^{3.591} = 0.066 \times 36.27 = 2.394$ |

*Step 2 — Compute the numerator and denominator of the fraction:*

| $i$ | $N'_i$ | $z_i$ | $g(z_i)$ | $N'_i\,z_i\,g(z_i)$ | $N'_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 2.0 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 1.2 | 5.0 | 1.000 | 6.000 | 1.2 |
| 3 | 2.0 | 9.0 | 1.000 | 18.000 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 1.8 | 12.0 | 1.000 | 21.600 | 1.8 |
| 5 | 1.0 | 15.0 | 0.858 | 12.870 | 1.0 |
| 6 | 0.6 | 18.0 | 1.433 | 15.476 | 0.6 |
| 7 | 0.3 | 21.0 | 2.394 | 15.082 | 0.3 |
| **Σ** | — | — | — | **90.028** | **7.4** |

*Step 3 — Sharpness:*

$$
S = 0.11 \times \frac{90.028}{7.4} = 0.11 \times 12.166 = \mathbf{1.338\;\text{acum}}
$$

*Step 4 — Subjective rating:* $1.0 \le S \le 1.5$ acum → **"Sharp"**. Vehicle cabin is judged perceptually sharp; NVH team will target reduction in bands 3–5 (8–15 Bark) by damping or active noise control.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat block diagram and equations, describe the **Prominence Ratio (PR) method of ECMA-74** for detecting tonal components in a noise spectrum. Explain how the "context band" is defined and state the PR threshold for tonal acceptance. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following third-octave SPL data are obtained in a cabin during WOT 2nd-gear pull. The candidate tone of interest is at **3.15 kHz** with **$L_p = 55$ dB**. The two adjacent third-octave bands are at 2.5 kHz ($L_p = 38$ dB) and 4.0 kHz ($L_p = 41$ dB).

  (i) Compute the **Prominence Ratio** of the 3.15 kHz tone. (3 marks)
  (ii) State the verdict per ECMA-74. (1 mark)
  (iii) Suggest **two engineering modifications** to reduce the tone. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PR Method Description (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Block diagram (3 marks):*

```
[Time signal] → [FFT, 1/3-octave] → [Candidate tone identification]
                                              ↓
                                 [Context: ±1 critical band or ±5% f]
                                              ↓
                                 [Linear-intensity averaging]
                                              ↓
                                 [PR = 10 log10 (I_tone / I_context)]
                                              ↓
                                 [Verdict: PR < 8 dB? pass : fail]
```

*Step 2 — Equation and definition (3 marks):*

$$
\text{PR}_i = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{L_{p_i}/10}}{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j \in \text{context}} 10^{L_{p_j}/10}}\right) \;\text{dB}
$$

- **Context band:** either ±1 critical band (Bark) on either side, OR ±5 % of the tone frequency (ECMA-74 default uses ±5 bands; ISO 7779 uses the critical-band approach).
- **Threshold:** **PR $\ge$ 8 dB** → tone is clearly audible → **fails** ECMA-74 acceptance.

*Step 3 — Engineering significance (1 mark):* PR is **independent of overall SPL** — it isolates tonality from loudness. A faint tone in a quiet cabin (low absolute SPL) can still be objectionable if its PR is high.

**(b) Numerical and Engineering (7 marks):**

*(i) PR computation (3 marks):*

$$
I_t = 10^{55/10} = 10^{5.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
I_{\text{low}} = 10^{38/10} = 6.310 \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
I_{\text{high}} = 10^{41/10} = 1.259 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
\bar{I} = \frac{3.162 \times 10^{5} + 6.310 \times 10^{3} + 1.259 \times 10^{4}}{3} = \frac{3.352 \times 10^{5}}{3} = 1.117 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
\text{PR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3.162 \times 10^{5}}{1.117 \times 10^{5}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.831) = \mathbf{4.52\;\text{dB}}
$$

*(ii) Verdict (1 mark):* PR = 4.52 dB is in the **4–8 dB "marginally perceptible" band** — not a hard fail, but a **flag for re-test or design mitigation**.

*(iii) Engineering modifications (3 marks — any two of):*

1. **Microgeometry modification of gear teeth** — lead crowning, profile relief, or end-relief to spread the meshing tone over a wider frequency band (lowers the spectral peak → lowers PR).
2. **Increase tooth contact ratio** via helix-angle correction or higher-quality grinding — reduces transmission error excitation amplitude, lowering tone level.
3. **Add constrained-layer damping (CLD) treatment** to the gearbox housing or final-drive cover to raise the noise floor in the 3.15 kHz band.
4. **Use helical gears instead of spur** in the offending mesh stage to replace impulsive excitation with smoother distributed loading.
5. **Active Noise Control (ANC)** with a 3.15 kHz anti-tone injection through cabin speakers — broadband-PR-friendly approach.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — High-Frequency SQI Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not report sharpness in dB** — the unit is **acum**. Examiner will deduct ½ mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Do not use A-weighting in the loudness step** before computing sharpness — sharpness uses the **Zwicker critical-band loudness** (ISO 532-1/-2/-3) which is a fundamentally different chain. Confusing A-weighting with Z-weighting is the single most common error in Module-4 KTU papers.
> 3. **For PR, do not average dB values arithmetically** — convert to linear intensity first, average, then convert back to dB. Examiner deducts 1 full mark.
> 4. **Sharpness without $g(z)$** is wrong above 13.5 Bark — forgetting the high-frequency boost will give $S$ values 30–50 % too low for squeal-type HFTN.
> 5. **SII requires both noise and speech spectra** — supplying only the noise spectrum is incomplete; the formula is silent on the missing term, costing 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sharpness $S$ (acum)** = high-frequency "bite" metric; DIN 45692; $S > 1.5$ acum = sharp; uses $g(z)$ weight above 13.5 Bark.
- **Tonality $K$ (tu)** = Aures model; weighted sum of loudness excess × slope, normalised by total loudness; $K \ge 1$ tu = clearly tonal.
- **Prominence Ratio PR (dB)** = ECMA-74; 3-band (or 5-band) intensity ratio; **PR $\ge$ 8 dB = fail**; independent of overall SPL.
- **Articulation Index (AI) / SII** = speech intelligibility metric; ANSI S3.5; $0 \le$ SII $\le 1$; target $\ge 0.5$ for cabin communication.
- **HFTN sources in automobiles:** gear whine, brake squeal, belt squeal, HVAC whistle, tire cavity harmonics.
- **Frequency-to-Bark mapping:** $z = 26.81 f/(1960+f) - 0.53$ (with $f$ in Hz).
- **Loudness unit conversion:** $N$ (sone) $= 2^{0.1(L_N - 40)}$, $L_N$ in phon.
- **Key thresholds (KTU favourite):** PR = 8 dB; $S = 1.5$ acum; SII = 0.5; Aures $K = 1$ tu.
- **Always convert dB → linear → average → dB** when computing PR or any band-averaging operation.
- **Sharpness is computed from loudness, never from raw SPL** — A-weighting is **not** a substitute for Zwicker loudness.
- **SII and AI are distinct:** AI is the older French-Steinberg formula; SII is the modern ANSI S3.5-1997 successor with masking and redundancy corrections.
- **Examiner expects explicit units** in every numerical step: sone, sone/Bark, acum, tu, dB SPL, phon, Bark, Hz.
- **Cross-reference Module 3** for Roughness (modulation metric) and Module 2 for Loudness (Zwicker / ISO 532) — these are the upstream building blocks of all Module-4 SQIs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Sound Quality Indexes in NVH Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND Harshness
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Sound Quality Indexes in NVH Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

**Sound Quality (SQ)** is the field of psychoacoustics concerned with the subjective perception of sound, judged in terms of attributes such as loudness, sharpness, roughness, fluctuation strength, tonality, and annoyance. **Sound Quality Indexes (SQIs)** are quantitative, mathematically derived metrics obtained from an acoustic signal that correlate with these subjective attributes.

In the context of **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)**, SQIs are used to translate raw sound pressure level (SPL) data — typically captured via binaural mannequins, head-and-torso simulators (HATS), or vehicle-mounted microphones — into perceptually meaningful numbers. They enable engineers to benchmark competing vehicles, target specific psychoacoustic attributes, and validate interior/exterior sound signatures against brand DNA.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically stresses that classical dB(A) weighting is *insufficient* for full NVH evaluation because the human ear has frequency-dependent, level-dependent, and time-dependent sensitivity that A-weighting cannot capture. SQIs bridge this gap.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Sommelier" Intuition

Imagine walking into a coffee shop. The physical property of the drink is "mass of dissolved solids" (roughly a measure of strength). A chemist's number (e.g., 1200 TDS) tells you objectively what is in the cup, but it does **not** tell you whether the coffee will taste:
- Bitter or smooth,
- Astringent or mellow,
- Fruity or earthy.

A *sommelier* (trained sensory expert) perceives and rates the coffee on standardized scales — exactly what a **Sound Quality Index** does for engine or road noise. The acoustic spectrum (in Pa²/Hz) is the chemistry, while **Loudness, Sharpness, Roughness, and Tonality** are the sommelier's vocabulary.

| Sound Quality "Flavor Profile" | Real Engine/Cabin Noise Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Body / Mouthfeel | **Loudness (sone)** — perceived intensity |
| Bitterness | **Sharpness (acum)** — perceived high-frequency emphasis |
| Sourness / Astringency | **Roughness (asper)** — fast amplitude modulation (< 30 Hz) |
| Fizz / Carbonation | **Fluctuation Strength (vacil)** — slow modulation (≤ 20 Hz) |
| Distinct Note | **Tonality** — presence of pure tones |
| Overall Enjoyment | **Annoyance / Pleasantness Index** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** A car can have a low dB(A) value but feel "annoying" or "cheap," while a louder car can feel "premium" and "sporty." This mismatch between physical sound and perception is precisely why SQIs exist.

## 1.3 Standardized Units and Reference Standards

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize these units and standards:

- **Sone** — unit of **Loudness** (1 sone = 40 phon at 1 kHz).
- **Acum** — unit of **Sharpness**.
- **Asper** — unit of **Roughness**.
- **Vacil** — unit of **Fluctuation Strength**.
- **DIN 45631 / ISO 532-1** — Zwicker stationary loudness method.
- **ISO 532-2 / Moore-Glasberg** — alternative loudness model (time-varying).
- **DIN 45692** — Sharpness calculation.
- **ECMA-74 / ISO 7779** — Annex D, Tonality calculation.
- **ANSI S3.4** — historical loudness standard.
- **AURES / Zwicker Annoyance Model (W)** — annoyance index.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Sound Quality

Modern automotive NVH practice relies on the following seven primary SQIs, each isolating one dimension of auditory perception:

### (a) Loudness — N (sone)
Loudness is the *perceived magnitude* of a sound. It accounts for the ear's varying sensitivity across frequency (Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contours) and critical-band masking.

- **Zwicker (stationary):** Uses critical-band rate scale *z* (Bark) from 0 to 24.
- **Moore-Glasberg (time-varying):** Excels for transients (e.g., door-close thuds, gear shifts).

### (b) Sharpness — S (acum)
Sharpness quantifies the *high-frequency weighting* of a sound. A sound rich in 4–8 kHz energy feels "sharp," "edgy," or "metallic" — undesirable in luxury cabins but often *desired* in sport-tuned exhaust notes.

### (c) Roughness — R (asper)
Roughness captures fast amplitude modulation in the range **f_mod ≈ 15–300 Hz** (peak around 70 Hz). It correlates with "buzz," "rattle," and bearing/gear whine.

### (d) Fluctuation Strength — F (vacil)
Slow modulation (**f_mod ≤ 20 Hz**, peak at 4 Hz) yields "pulsation" — common in diesel combustion at idle or HVAC airflow surges.

### (e) Tonality — K (tu)
Tonality quantifies the prominence of tonal components against the noise floor. High tonality produces a "whining," "moaning," or "siren-like" character. Calculated via **Prominence Ratio (PR)** or **Tonality Index (TIA / TIR)**.

### (f) Articulation Index / Speech Intelligibility Index — AI / SII
Used for hands-free voice control, in-cabin emergency alerts, and infotainment prompts. Critical for **eCall**, **voice assistants**, and **ADAS acoustic warnings**.

### (g) Annoyance / Pleasantness — Zwicker W, Aures W, PA
Composite indices combining the above into a single subjective rating.

## 2.2 Why SQIs Matter in Automotive NVH

- **Brand DNA Engineering:** BMW aims for a "sporty yet refined" signature (controlled roughness, sharp but not piercing); Lexus targets "silky" (low roughness, low fluctuation, low tonality).
- **EV Sound Design:** With the absence of ICE noise, pedestrian-warning AVAS, interior motor whine (1×, 2×, 6× rotor orders), and tire rumble dominate. SQIs help *enhance* sportiness without violating UN-R138.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** dB(A) drive-by limits (UN-R51) do not control interior sound; SQIs help OEMs go beyond compliance.
- **Objectification of Subjective Tests:** Jury evaluations are expensive and noisy; SQIs provide repeatable engineering metrics.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master cheat sheet** for Module 4 numerical and theory questions. Memorize the symbols, units, and frequency/dB ranges.

| **Index** | **Symbol** | **Unit** | **Governing Formula / Definition** | **Typical Frequency / Modulation Range** | **Reference Standard** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Loudness (Zwicker) | $N$ | sone | $N = 0.08 \int_{0}^{24\text{Bark}} N'(z) \, dz$ | 20 Hz – 16 kHz | ISO 532-1 |
| Loudness Level | $L_N$ | phon | $L_N = 40 + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | — | — |
| Sharpness | $S$ | acum | $S = \dfrac{0.11}{\int N'(z)\,dz}\int N'(z) \cdot g(z)\,dz$ | weighted by $g(z)$, max at ~6 kHz | DIN 45692 |
| Roughness | $R$ | asper | $R = 0.25 \int dL \cdot \dfrac{f_{\text{mod}}}{(f_{\text{mod}}/70\text{Hz})^2 + (70\text{Hz}/f_{\text{mod}})^2}\, dz$ | $f_{\text{mod}} \in [15, 300]$ Hz, peak 70 Hz | Daniel & Weber |
| Fluctuation Strength | $F$ | vacil | $F = 0.008 \int dL \cdot \dfrac{1}{(f_{\text{mod}}/4\text{Hz}) + (4\text{Hz}/f_{\text{mod}})}\, dz$ | $f_{\text{mod}} \leq 20$ Hz, peak 4 Hz | Fastl & Zwicker |
| Specific Loudness (Zwicker) | $N'(z)$ | sone/Bark | Fitted piecewise polynomial in $z$ | Critical band rate $z$ | ISO 532-1 |
| Prominence Ratio (Tonality) | $PR$ | dB | $PR = L_{\text{tone}} - L_{\text{mask}}$ (with masking) | 50 Hz – 10 kHz | ECMA-74 Annex D |
| Tonality Index (Aures) | $K$ | tu | $K = \dfrac{\sum a_i \cdot w_i}{\sum w_i}$, $w_i$ tonal weights | — | Aures |
| Zwicker Annoyance | $W$ | — | $W = \left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{s} + \sqrt{r}\right)$ (sometimes $\cdot 2.18$) | Composite | Zwicker & Fastl |
| Aures Pleasantness | $W_A$ | — | Log-linear combination of $N, S, R, F, K$ | Composite | Aures |
| Psychoacoustic Annoyance | $PA$ | — | $PA = N \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{w_S + w_F + w_R}\right)$ | Composite | Dittrich |
| Speech Intelligibility Index | $SII$ | 0 – 1 | $\sum_{j} I_j \cdot A_j \cdot E_j$ over 1/3-oct bands | 250 Hz – 8 kHz | ANSI S3.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $N_0$ is the loudness of the reference condition (often taken as 1 sone), $w_S$, $w_F$, $w_R$ are sharpness/fluctuation/roughness weighting factors, and $I_j, A_j, E_j$ are the band-importance, articulation, and audibility functions in the SII standard.

## 2.4 Critical-Band Rate (Bark) Mapping — The Foundation

The Bark scale $z$ linearizes the cochlea. For each third-octave or FFT bin at frequency $f$ (in Hz):

$$
z \approx 13 \arctan\!\left(0.00076 \cdot f\right) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{f}{7500}\right)^{2}
$$

The **critical bandwidth** at rate $z$ is:

$$
\Delta f_{G}(z) \approx 25 + 75 \cdot \bigl(1 + 1.4 \cdot f^{2}\cdot 10^{-6}\bigr)^{0.69} \quad [\text{Hz}]
$$

This nonlinear mapping is the *engine* behind every Zwicker-based index — exam questions will frequently require its recognition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Zwicker Stationary Loudness

We derive the specific loudness $N'(z)$ for a pure tone of SPL $L_p$ in free field, as laid out in ISO 532-1.

### Step 1 — Compute the excitation level $E$
For a free-field frontal tone of SPL $L_p$ (dB re 20 µPa) and critical band rate $z$ (Bark):

$$
E = 10^{L_p / 10} \cdot \frac{1}{s(f)}
$$

where $s(f)$ is the threshold of hearing in free field. In the ISO 532-1 closed-form:

$$
E_0 = \left[10^{0.1 \cdot 10}\right] \cdot \text{(threshold function at } z\text{)} \quad \text{(reference 10 dB tone)}
$$

### Step 2 — Non-dimensional excitation $E/Q$
The non-dimensional ratio is built using the threshold at the same Bark:

$$
\frac{E}{Q} = \frac{E_{\text{tone}}}{E_{\text{threshold at } z}}
$$

### Step 3 — Specific loudness formula (range-dependent)
ISO 532-1 splits into three excitation ranges (low: $0 \leq E/Q \leq 0.5$, mid: $0.5 \leq E/Q \leq 20$, high: $E/Q > 20$):

$$
N'(z) = \begin{cases}
0.0630 \cdot \dfrac{E}{Q} \cdot \left[\dfrac{2 + (E/Q)}{2 + (E/Q)^{1.5}}\right]^{0.25} & \text{if } 0 \leq E/Q \leq 0.5 \\
\dfrac{1}{0.25 + 0.75 \cdot \exp(-0.95 \cdot \ln(E/Q) - 0.3 \cdot z)} & \text{if } 0.5 \leq E/Q \leq 20 \\
1.0 \cdot \dfrac{E}{Q}^{0.3} \cdot \text{(saturation term)} & \text{if } E/Q > 20
\end{cases}
$$

> The exact constants in the closed-form are 0.08, 0.23, 0.21, 0.05 etc., depending on the ISO 532-1 edition; the **shape** is what examiners test.

### Step 4 — Integrate to obtain total loudness

$$
N = 0.08 + \int_{0}^{24 \text{Bark}} N'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{sone}]
$$

The constant 0.08 sone is the *absolute threshold of loudness* — the residual loudness even in quiet. For most engineering data, $N \approx 0.08 + \int N'(z) dz$ is used, and 0.08 is often dropped for $N > 1$ sone.

### Step 5 — Convert to loudness level

$$
L_N = 40 + 10 \log_{10}(N) \quad [\text{phon}]
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Sharpness (DIN 45692)

### Step 1 — Pre-requisite
Compute specific loudness $N'(z)$ from §3.1.

### Step 2 — Apply the Bark-domain weighting $g(z)$

$$
g(z) = \begin{cases}
1.0 & z \leq 16 \text{ Bark} \\
1.0 + 1.85 \cdot \bigl[\log_{10}(z/16)\bigr]^{2} & 16 < z \leq 22 \text{ Bark} \\
1.0 + 2.20 \cdot \bigl[\log_{10}(z/16)\bigr]^{2} & z > 22 \text{ Bark}
\end{cases}
$$

This weighting *boosts* high-Bark (high-frequency) contributions.

### Step 3 — Compute the weighted loudness integral

$$
I_{\text{w}} = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z)\, dz
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the unweighted loudness integral

$$
I = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz \quad \bigl(\approx N \text{ from §3.1}\bigr)
$$

### Step 5 — Sharpness

$$
\boxed{\,S = \dfrac{0.11}{I}\cdot I_{\text{w}} \quad [\text{acum}]\,}
$$

### Step 6 — Reference benchmark values (memorize for KTU viva)

| Sound Type | Typical Sharpness (acum) |
|---|---|
| Pure 1 kHz tone, 60 dB | ~1.0 |
| Broadband white noise, 60 dB | ~2.0 |
| Disc-brake squeal | ~5.0 – 8.0 |
| Turbo blow-off valve | ~4.0 – 6.0 |
| Quiet EV cabin | < 0.8 |

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation: Roughness (Daniel & Weber Model)

### Step 1 — Identify modulation frequency
For a given 1/3-oct band, compute the envelope $a(t)$ of the signal. Extract its modulation frequency $f_{\text{mod}}$ (Hz) and depth $\Delta L$ (dB).

### Step 2 — Compute the modulation coefficient

$$
m(f_{\text{mod}}) = \dfrac{f_{\text{mod}}}{(f_{\text{mod}}/70)^{2} + (70/f_{\text{mod}})^{2}}
$$

This term peaks at $f_{\text{mod}} = 70$ Hz.

### Step 3 — Specific roughness per critical band

$$
R'(z) = 0.25 \cdot \Delta L(z) \cdot m(f_{\text{mod}})
$$

### Step 4 — Integrate

$$
\boxed{\,R = \int_{0}^{24} R'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{asper}]\,}
$$

A 70-Hz-modulated HVAC fan often scores $R \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ asper, which is *noticeable* but tolerable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Mid-Sized Sedan Cabin at 120 km/h

**Given:** A test driver reports "wind roar" at highway speed. The cabin SPL spectrum peaks at 2 kHz (60 dB) and again at 6 kHz (52 dB). Modulation at 4 Hz appears in the 250-Hz band, depth 5 dB.

**Step 1 — Map frequencies to Bark:**

$$
z_{2\text{kHz}} \approx 13 \arctan(1.52) + 3.5 \arctan(0.2667)^{2} \approx 13(0.989) + 3.5(0.071) \approx 13.1 \text{ Bark}
$$

$$
z_{6\text{kHz}} \approx 13 \arctan(4.56) + 3.5 \arctan(0.8)^{2} \approx 13(1.354) + 3.5(0.64) \approx 19.9 \text{ Bark}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply sharpness weighting:**
$g(13.1) = 1.0$, $g(19.9) = 1.0 + 1.85 \cdot [\log_{10}(19.9/16)]^{2} \approx 1.0 + 1.85(0.095)^{2} \approx 1.017$.

**Step 3 — Total loudness (assumed $N \approx 5$ sone from 60-dB pink reference):**

$$
S \approx 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{(5\cdot 1.0 + 4\cdot 1.017)}{5+4} \approx 0.11 \cdot 1.009 \approx 1.11 \text{ acum}
$$

**Step 4 — Fluctuation at 4 Hz (assumed $\Delta L = 5$ dB):**

$$
F \approx 0.008 \cdot 5 \cdot \dfrac{1}{(4/4)+(4/4)} = 0.02 \cdot 0.5 = 0.01 \text{ vacil (low, negligible)}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering conclusion:** The 6-kHz contribution is *not* sharp enough to explain the "roar." Engineers should next examine **tonality** — likely a wind whistle at door-mirror trailing edge causing a 4-kHz tone with $PR > 9$ dB.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow: From Raw Cabin Recording to SQI Decision

The following Mermaid block shows the canonical engineering pipeline used by NVH teams when applying SQIs. Every node ID is alphanumeric per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["CAPTURE"] --> A2["Binaural Mannequin or HATS Microphone"]
    A2 --> A3["Time Signal x_t"]
    A3 --> A4["FFT and One Third Octave Transform"]
    A4 --> A5["SPL Versus Frequency L_p of f"]
    A5 --> B1["CRITICAL BAND MAPPING"]
    B1 --> B2["Bark Domain L_p of z"]
    B2 --> C1["ISO 5321 SPECIFIC LOUDNESS"]
    C1 --> C2["N prime of z"]
    C2 --> C3["Loudness N in sone"]
    C1 --> D1["Sharpness Engine DIN 45692"]
    D1 --> D2["Sharpness S in acum"]
    C2 --> E1["Roughness Engine Daniel Weber"]
    E1 --> E2["Roughness R in asper"]
    C2 --> F1["Fluctuation Engine Fastl"]
    F1 --> F2["Fluctuation F in vacil"]
    A4 --> G1["Tonality Engine ECMA 74"]
    G1 --> G2["Prominence Ratio PR in dB"]
    C3 --> H1["COMPOSITE INDICES"]
    D2 --> H1
    E2 --> H1
    F2 --> H1
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> H2["Zwicker W and Aures and PA"]
    H2 --> I1["DECISION"]
    I1 --> I2["Target Match"]
    I1 --> I3["Jury Test for Correlation"]
    I1 --> I4["Design Iteration"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mapping Physical Domain to Perceptual Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PH1["Physical Domain Pa squared per Hz"] --> PH2["Critical Band Filter Bank 24 Bark Channels"]
    PH2 --> PH3["Excitation Level E per Channel"]
    PH3 --> PH4["Specific Loudness N prime per Channel"]
    PH4 --> SQ1["Loudness"]
    PH4 --> SQ2["Sharpness with g of z"]
    PH4 --> SQ3["Roughness with modulation analysis"]
    PH4 --> SQ4["Fluctuation with envelope"]
    PH1 --> SQ5["Tonality via tone vs noise floor"]
    SQ1 --> CO1["Composite Annoyance W"]
    SQ2 --> CO1
    SQ3 --> CO1
    SQ4 --> CO1
    SQ5 --> CO1
    CO1 --> DO1["Driver Subjective Rating"]
    DO1 --> DC1["Correlation Analysis R squared greater than 0.7 acceptable"]
```

## 4.3 Use-Case Mapping: SQI to Vehicle Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NVH["AUTOMOTIVE NVH SUBSYSTEMS"] --> S1["ICE Powertrain"]
    NVH --> S2["EV Powertrain"]
    NVH --> S3["Tire and Road"]
    NVH --> S4["Wind and Aero"]
    NVH --> S5["HVAC and Blower"]
    NVH --> S6["Body and Closures"]
    S1 --> SQ1["Primary Loudness and Roughness at 70 Hz"]
    S2 --> SQ2["Sharpness for Motor Whine and AVAS Tonality"]
    S3 --> SQ3["Roughness and Loudness below 1 kHz"]
    S4 --> SQ4["Tonality PR for whistles and Sharpness above 4 kHz"]
    S5 --> SQ5["Fluctuation at 4 Hz and Roughness at 70 Hz"]
    S6 --> SQ6["Impulse Loudness for door thud using Moore Glasberg"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Sound Quality and explain why dB(A) alone is insufficient for automotive NVH evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**
Sound Quality (SQ) is the discipline of psychoacoustics that quantifies the *subjective perception* of sound using indices such as Loudness, Sharpness, Roughness, Fluctuation Strength, and Tonality. dB(A) weighting is a single fixed A-curve that approximates 40-phon equal-loudness, but human hearing exhibits level-dependent, frequency-dependent, and *time-dependent* (masking, modulation) sensitivity. dB(A) cannot capture roughness (fast AM), fluctuation (slow AM), or tonal prominence. Hence two vehicles can read the same dB(A) interior level yet feel drastically different to occupants, and SQIs resolve this mismatch. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six Sound Quality Indexes with their units.**

**Model Answer:**

| SQI | Unit |
|---|---|
| Loudness | sone |
| Sharpness | acum |
| Roughness | asper |
| Fluctuation Strength | vacil |
| Tonality (Prominence Ratio) | dB / tu |
| Annoyance (Zwicker W / PA) | dimensionless |

**(3 marks — ½ mark per correctly paired SQI + unit)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the Zwicker model for computing **stationary loudness** of an automotive cabin noise signal. State the standards involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Sharpness** as per DIN 45692 starting from the specific loudness $N'(z)$. Comment on the role of the Bark-weighting function $g(z)$ with reference to typical engine and cabin noises. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. Capture cabin noise with HATS or binaural microphones — **½ mark**.
2. Compute FFT → 1/3-octave SPL → critical-band (Bark) mapping using $z = 13\arctan(0.00076f) + 3.5\arctan(f/7500)^2$ — **1 mark**.
3. For each Bark band, compute excitation $E$ referenced to the free-field threshold of hearing — **1 mark**.
4. Compute non-dimensional excitation $E/Q$ and apply ISO 532-1 piecewise specific loudness formula — **1 ½ marks**.
5. Integrate $N'(z)$ over $z \in [0, 24]$ Bark to obtain $N$ (sone); convert to phon — **1 mark**.
6. Standards: **ISO 532-1 (Zwicker stationary)** and alternative **ISO 532-2 (Moore-Glasberg time-varying)** — **1 mark**.
7. Block diagram (labelled with: Time signal → FFT → 1/3-oct → Bark mapping → Specific loudness → Integration → Loudness) — **1 mark**.

**Model Solution (b):**

1. Specific loudness $N'(z)$ obtained from §(a) — **½ mark**.
2. Define weighting $g(z)$:
$$
g(z) = \begin{cases}
1.0, & z \leq 16 \\
1 + 1.85 \log_{10}^{2}(z/16), & 16 < z \leq 22 \\
1 + 2.20 \log_{10}^{2}(z/16), & z > 22
\end{cases}
$$
— **1 ½ marks**.
3. Weighted integral $I_w = \int N'(z)\,g(z)\,dz$ and unweighted $I = \int N'(z)\,dz$ — **1 mark**.
4. Sharpness:
$$
S = \dfrac{0.11}{I}\,I_w \quad [\text{acum}]
$$
— **1 ½ marks**.
5. **Discussion (2 marks):**
   - At low $z$ (engine firing boom, road rumble), $g(z) = 1$ → no sharpness contribution; such sounds feel *mellow*.
   - At high $z$ (turbo whistle, gear whine, wind whistle, brake squeal), $g(z) > 1$ → sharpness escalates; such sounds feel *edgy/piercing*.
   - Engineer's goal: keep $S < 1.5$ acum for luxury cabins, $S \approx 2$–$3$ acum for sporty exhausts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Roughness** and **Fluctuation Strength**. Derive Daniel–Weber's roughness expression and identify the modulation frequency at which roughness is maximum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A driver complaints of "buzzy" idle in a 4-cylinder gasoline car. As an NVH engineer, propose a methodology using Sound Quality Indexes to isolate the root cause. List specific metrics, target values, and corrective actions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Difference (2 marks):**
   - **Roughness** captures *fast* amplitude modulation (15–300 Hz, peak 70 Hz); sensory correlate: "buzz," "rattle."
   - **Fluctuation Strength** captures *slow* modulation (≤ 20 Hz, peak 4 Hz); correlate: "pulsation," "throb."

2. **Daniel–Weber derivation (3 marks):**
   - Per-band modulation coefficient:
$$
m(f_{\text{mod}}) = \dfrac{f_{\text{mod}}}{(f_{\text{mod}}/70)^{2} + (70/f_{\text{mod}})^{2}}
$$
   - Per-band specific roughness:
$$
R'(z) = 0.25 \cdot \Delta L(z) \cdot m(f_{\text{mod}})
$$
   - Total roughness:
$$
R = \int_{0}^{24} R'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{asper}]
$$

3. **Maximum at $f_{\text{mod}} = 70$ Hz** — by inspection of $m(f_{\text{mod}})$, derivative zero at 70 Hz — **½ mark**.
4. Standards: Daniel & Weber 1997, Zwicker & Fastl textbook — **1 mark**.

**Model Solution (b):**

| Step | Action | SQI / Threshold | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Record cabin noise at 800 rpm idle, 1-m HATS mic | — | Baseline |
| 2 | Compute 1/3-oct SPL & order analysis | — | Identify ½, 1×, 2× orders (~13, 26, 52 Hz) |
| 3 | Compute **Loudness N** | target $N < 8$ sone | Idle cabin reference |
| 4 | Compute **Roughness R** | target $R < 1$ asper | Detect buzz |
| 5 | Compute **Tonality K** at idle firing freq | target $K < 1$ tu / $PR < 9$ dB | Detect combustion knock tonal |
| 6 | Compute **Fluctuation F** at firing freq (≈ 25 Hz for 4-cyl at 750 rpm) | target $F < 1$ vacil | Detect rumble |
| 7 | Root-cause correlation | — | If $R$ high near 70 Hz in 1–2 kHz band → likely injector/valve-train buzz; if $F$ high near 4 Hz → combustion irregularity |
| 8 | **Corrective actions:** | — | (a) Refine injector calibration; (b) Add dash-barrier damping; (c) Active Noise Cancellation via opposite-phase loudspeaker on the firing frequency |
| 9 | Re-measure SQIs; verify reduction | — | Validate |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing sone vs phon** — sone is *loudness*, phon is *loudness level* (log scale). Mixing units = −2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $0.08$ sone constant** in $N = 0.08 + \int N'(z) dz$ at low SPL — examiner checks this.
> 3. **Mixing up frequency ranges of modulation:** Roughness peak at **70 Hz**, Fluctuation peak at **4 Hz**. Wrong number = −1 mark.
> 4. **Using `dB(A)` interchangeably with `Loudness`** — they are *not* the same. Sone is from psychoacoustic models.
> 5. **Omitting $g(z)$ in sharpness derivation** — Sharpness formula *without* $g(z)$ is loudness, not sharpness. Step-skip = −3 marks.
> 6. **In Question B(b), not tying metrics to physical causes** — vague answers ("we reduce noise") lose marks; you must *name the SQI, the target, and the corrective action*.
> 7. **Forgetting standards** (ISO 532-1, DIN 45692, ECMA-74) — KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests standard awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Sound Quality Indexes (SQIs)** translate SPL into perceptually meaningful, repeatable engineering metrics.
- The **seven pillars**: Loudness, Sharpness, Roughness, Fluctuation Strength, Tonality, Articulation/SII, Composite Annoyance.
- **Loudness N (sone)** — perceived magnitude; Zwicker (ISO 532-1) uses 24-Bark scale; Moore-Glasberg (ISO 532-2) handles transients.
- **Sharpness S (acum)** — high-frequency emphasis; uses $g(z)$ weighting; 0.11 calibration; **DIN 45692**.
- **Roughness R (asper)** — fast AM, peak **70 Hz**; **Daniel–Weber**.
- **Fluctuation F (vacil)** — slow AM, peak **4 Hz**; **Fastl**.
- **Tonality K / PR (dB)** — tonal vs noise floor; **ECMA-74 Annex D** ($PR > 9$ dB = clearly tonal).
- **Composite indices** — Zwicker $W$, Aures, PA; combine N, S, R, F, K into overall annoyance.
- **Bark mapping** is foundational: $z = 13\arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5\arctan(f/7500)^2$.
- **Automotive use cases:** engine (loudness + roughness), EV (sharpness + tonality), wind (tonality + sharpness), HVAC (roughness + fluctuation), doors (Moore-Glasberg impulse).
- **Always cite the governing standard** when writing any SQI expression.
- **Brand DNA engineering** uses SQIs to maintain signature sound across model generations.
- **EVs shift the SQI mix** — AVAS for pedestrian safety, sharp motor-whine control for cabin luxury.
- **Correlation with jury tests:** target $R^2 > 0.7$ between SQI predictions and subjective ratings.
- **Numerical constants to memorize:** 0.08 sone (threshold), 0.11 (sharpness), 0.25 (roughness), 0.008 (fluctuation), 70 Hz, 4 Hz, 24 Bark.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of NVH Legislations Applicable for Vehicles in India
Introduction to Software Applications for NVH Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT863 - AUTOMOTIVE NOISE VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Software Applications for NVH Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive NVH Software Applications** refer to specialized computer-aided engineering (CAE) suites, numerical simulation tools, and digital signal processing (DSP) platforms that are used to model, analyze, predict, and solve noise, vibration, and harshness problems in vehicles. These tools employ numerical methods such as the **Finite Element Method (FEM)**, **Boundary Element Method (BEM)**, **Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA)**, and **Multi-Body Dynamics (MBD)** to convert physical NVH phenomena into solvable mathematical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The primary focus is on understanding the *role*, *capabilities*, *workflow*, and *classification* of NVH software tools, NOT on the UI training of any single commercial package. Examiners focus on **why** a particular numerical method is chosen for a given NVH problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Engineering MRI Machine" Analogy)

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor uses tools like an **X-ray, MRI, and stethoscope** — each reveals a different aspect of the body. Likewise, an NVH engineer cannot "hear" or "feel" a car's noise and vibration behavior until the prototype is built. NVH software acts like a **virtual medical scanner** for vehicles:

- **NVH Software = Virtual Stethoscope + MRI + Thermometer for Cars**
- The CAD model is the "patient", and the software reveals "invisible" resonances, noise radiation paths, and structural weaknesses **before the metal is even cut**.

**Intuitive breakdown:**
- Just as a thermometer only measures temperature, an NVH solver like **Nastran** is best for structural vibration, while **Actran** is best for acoustic radiation.
- Just as a doctor combines tools, modern NVH engineers use a *chain* of tools (e.g., OptiStruct → MotionSolve → Actran) in a *co-simulation* workflow.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally referenced in NVH software platforms:

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in air | $c_0$ | **343 m/s at 20°C** | Acoustic wave propagation |
| Density of air | $\rho_0$ | **1.204 kg/m³ at 20°C** | Acoustic impedance reference |
| Acoustic impedance of air | $Z_0$ | **$\rho_0 c_0 \approx 413$ Pa·s/m** | Used in BEM acoustic models |
| Acceleration of gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Used in g-loading for shaker tests |
| Reference sound pressure | $p_{ref}$ | **20 µPa** | dB SPL reference |
| Standard damping ratio (metals) | $\zeta$ | **0.001 to 0.05** | Critical for modal analysis accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in software:** Every NVH solver internally references $c_0$ and $\rho_0$ to set up acoustic BEM models. Misinterpreting units (e.g., using $c_0 = 343$ mm/s) is a classic exam trap and a real-world source of wasted simulation time.

### 1.4 Classification of NVH Software (The Three Pillars)

NVH software applications fall into three primary functional categories:

**Pillar 1 — Structural & Vibration Solvers**
- Examples: **ANSYS Mechanical, MSC Nastran, Abaqus, Altair OptiStruct**
- Use FEM to compute natural frequencies, mode shapes, frequency response functions (FRFs), and forced vibration response.

**Pillar 2 — Acoustic & Sound Radiation Solvers**
- Examples: **Actran, LMS Virtual.Lab Acoustics, COMSOL Acoustics, OpenBEM**
- Use BEM, FEM, or ray-tracing to compute sound pressure fields, transmission loss, and interior cabin acoustics.

**Pillar 3 — Signal Processing & Test Platforms**
- Examples: **MATLAB, Brüel & Kjær PULSE, Head Acoustics Artemis, OROS, Simcenter Testlab**
- Acquire and post-process measured data using FFT, order analysis, waterfall plots, and modal parameter extraction.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favorite question:** *"Differentiate between FEM, BEM, and SEA. State which software method is most suitable for high-frequency NVH problems."* The answer: **SEA is preferred above ~500 Hz**, while FEM/BEM dominate the low-mid frequency range (below ~200 Hz).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The NVH Software Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The modern automotive NVH software workflow is structured into **five sequential phases**. Each phase has dedicated tools and produces a specific deliverable for the next phase.

**Phase 1 — Pre-Processing (Geometry & Meshing)**
- The CAD model (BIW, suspension sub-frame, powertrain mounts) is imported and cleaned.
- **Tools:** HyperMesh, ANSA, SpaceClaim, ANSA BETA CAE.
- *Why:* Garbage in = garbage out. A poorly meshed model produces wrong natural frequencies and unstable modal solutions.

**Phase 2 — Material & Property Assignment**
- Young's modulus ($E$), Poisson's ratio ($\nu$), density ($\rho$), and damping ($\eta$) are mapped onto the mesh.
- **Tools:** Built-in material libraries within ANSYS, OptiStruct, Abaqus.

**Phase 3 — Solver Selection & Execution**
- The engineer chooses the appropriate solver card (e.g., SOL 103 for modal, SOL 108 for frequency response in Nastran).
- **Why this matters:** Selecting the wrong solver type is the #1 cause of meaningless NVH simulation results.

**Phase 4 — Post-Processing & Visualization**
- Mode shapes, FRFs, and sound pressure maps are visualized.
- **Tools:** HyperView, mETA, ParaView, Tecplot.

**Phase 5 — Correlation with Test Data**
- Simulated FRFs are overlaid with measured FRFs from impact-hammer or shaker tests.
- **Tools:** Simcenter Testlab, MATLAB correlation scripts.

### 2.2 Core Numerical Methods Used by NVH Software

#### 2.2.1 Finite Element Method (FEM)

The FEM discretizes a continuous structure into smaller elements and solves the equation of motion:

$$
[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [C]\{\dot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{F(t)\}
$$

Where:
- $[M]$ = Global mass matrix
- $[C]$ = Global damping matrix
- $[K]$ = Global stiffness matrix
- $\{F(t)\}$ = External force vector
- $\{x(t)\}$ = Nodal displacement vector

> [!NOTE]
> **NVH Insight:** For frequencies below ~200 Hz, FEM gives highly accurate results. Above this, the mesh size constraint $\lambda \geq 6 \cdot L_{element}$ (where $\lambda$ is the wavelength and $L_{element}$ is the element length) makes the model computationally expensive.

#### 2.2.2 Boundary Element Method (BEM)

BEM is the **dual of FEM** for acoustics. It only meshes the boundary surface of the radiating body, drastically reducing model size. The governing equation is the **Helmholtz equation**:

$$
\nabla^2 p(\vec{r}) + k^2 p(\vec{r}) = 0
$$

Where $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c_0}$ is the acoustic wavenumber and $p(\vec{r})$ is the sound pressure at position $\vec{r}$.

#### 2.2.3 Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA)

Used at high frequencies where modal density is high. Power flow between coupled subsystems is modeled as:

$$
\omega \eta_{ij} n_i = \omega \eta_{ji} n_j \quad \text{(Reciprocity condition)}
$$

Where $n_i$ is the modal density of subsystem $i$ and $\eta_{ij}$ is the coupling loss factor between subsystems $i$ and $j$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Method | Application Domain | Typical Software Solver | Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[M]\ddot{x} + [C]\dot{x} + [K]x = F(t)$ | Structural vibration | Nastran SOL 108, ANSYS Modal/FRF | 0 – 500 Hz |
| 2 | Eigenvalue: $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$ | Natural frequency extraction | Nastran SOL 103, OptiStruct | All |
| 3 | $\nabla^2 p + k^2 p = 0$ | Acoustic radiation (BEM) | Actran, LMS Virtual.Lab | 200 – 2000 Hz |
| 4 | $\omega \eta_{ij} n_i = \omega \eta_{ji} n_j$ | High-frequency SEA | AutoSEA, SEAM | > 500 Hz |
| 5 | $X(\omega) = H(\omega) \cdot F(\omega)$ | Frequency response | All FRF solvers | 0 – 1000 Hz |
| 6 | $H(\omega) = [K - \omega^2 M + j\omega C]^{-1}$ | FRF matrix | Simcenter, ANSYS | 0 – 1000 Hz |
| 7 | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W_{inc}}{W_{trans}}\right)$ | Transmission loss (acoustics) | Actran TL solver | 200 – 5000 Hz |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \zeta f_n}$ | Decay time (SEA) | AutoSEA | > 500 Hz |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_0 c_0}{S} \cdot \sigma$ | Radiation efficiency | BEM post-process | 200 – 5000 Hz |
| 10 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | Test post-process | All |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the TL formula, students often write $L_p$ with 10 inside the log — but that is for **power** (dB). Sound pressure uses **20** because $p$ is a field quantity (proportional to $\sqrt{I}$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Use Case | Software Method | Why It Is Used |
|---|---|---|
| Predicting road noise in a car cabin | FEM + BEM coupling | Captures structure-borne path from tires to interior |
| Engine NVH (combustion excitation) | Harmonic BEM + FEA | Models the firing frequency orders 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2× |
| Electric vehicle (EV) whining noise | Order analysis + SEA | EV motors have pronounced high-frequency tonal content |
| Brake squeal prediction | Complex eigenvalue analysis | Identifies unstable mode pairs causing squeal |
| Boom noise in passenger cars | Cabin acoustic modal analysis | Targets cavity modes (T60, T80) at low frequencies |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Equation of Motion for a Multi-Degree-of-Freedom (MDOF) System (The Backbone of FEA-Based NVH Solvers)

We derive the governing matrix equation that *every* NVH structural solver (Nastran, ANSYS, Abaqus) solves internally.

**Step 1 — Start with a single-DOF spring-mass-damper system.**

By Newton's second law, the equation of motion is:

$$
m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Extend to an n-DOF system by assembling n such equations.**

For $n$ degrees of freedom, the scalar $m$, $c$, $k$ become matrices $[M]$, $[C]$, $[K]$. The scalar $x(t)$ and $F(t)$ become vectors $\{x(t)\}$ and $\{F(t)\}$. Assembling yields:

$$
[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [C]\{\dot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{F(t)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume proportional (Rayleigh) damping for analytical tractability.**

Rayleigh damping is the most common form used by commercial solvers because it allows decoupling:

$$
[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are scalar coefficients derived from two reference damping ratios $\zeta_1$ and $\zeta_2$ at frequencies $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \dfrac{2 \omega_1 \omega_2 (\zeta_1 \omega_2 - \zeta_2 \omega_1)}{\omega_2^2 - \omega_1^2} \\[4pt]
\beta &= \dfrac{2 (\zeta_2 \omega_2 - \zeta_1 \omega_1)}{\omega_2^2 - \omega_1^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier transform to enter the frequency domain.**

Using $\mathcal{F}\{\ddot{x}(t)\} = -\omega^2 X(\omega)$ and $\mathcal{F}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = j\omega X(\omega)$:

$$
[-\omega^2 M + j\omega C + K] \{X(\omega)\} = \{F(\omega)\}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the dynamic stiffness matrix and the Frequency Response Function (FRF).**

$$
[Z(\omega)]\{X(\omega)\} = \{F(\omega)\}
$$

where $[Z(\omega)] = -\omega^2 M + j\omega C + K$ is the dynamic stiffness. The **FRF matrix** $[H(\omega)]$ is the inverse:

$$
[H(\omega)] = [Z(\omega)]^{-1} = [K - \omega^2 M + j\omega C]^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the response.**

$$
\{X(\omega)\} = [H(\omega)] \{F(\omega)\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $[H(\omega)]$:** The element $H_{ij}(\omega)$ describes the displacement response at DOF $i$ per unit force applied at DOF $j$, as a function of frequency. NVH engineers plot $H_{ij}(\omega)$ to identify resonance peaks and assess isolation performance.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Helmholtz Equation for BEM Acoustic Solvers

**Step 1 — Start from the linearized acoustic wave equation.**

For a lossless medium with no source:

$$
\nabla^2 p(\vec{r},t) - \dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p(\vec{r},t)}{\partial t^2} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Assume time-harmonic excitation.**

Let $p(\vec{r}, t) = P(\vec{r}) e^{j\omega t}$. Then $\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\omega^2 P(\vec{r}) e^{j\omega t}$. Substituting:

$$
\nabla^2 P(\vec{r}) + \dfrac{\omega^2}{c_0^2} P(\vec{r}) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Define the wavenumber.**

$$
k \equiv \dfrac{\omega}{c_0} \quad \text{(units: rad/m)}
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite the Helmholtz equation in standard form.**

$$
\nabla^2 P(\vec{r}) + k^2 P(\vec{r}) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition on the radiating surface.**

The normal velocity $v_n$ on the surface $S$ of the vibrating body equals the structural velocity:

$$
\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial n}\bigg|_{S} = -j\omega \rho_0 \, v_n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NVH Insight:** This is the **coupling condition** between structure and acoustic BEM. It is the *only* link between a structural FE mesh and an acoustic BE mesh in any coupled NVH solver (e.g., Actran, LMS Virtual.Lab).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Modal Density for SEA High-Frequency Solvers

**Step 1 — For a 2D plate of area $A$, the modal density $n(f)$ is the number of modes per unit frequency.**

For a simply supported rectangular plate:

$$
n(f) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} A}{2 C_L h}
$$

where $C_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho(1-\nu^2)}}$ is the longitudinal wave speed, $h$ is plate thickness, $E$ is Young's modulus, $\rho$ is density, and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

**Step 2 — For a 3D enclosure (car cabin) of volume $V$:**

$$
n(f) = \dfrac{4 \pi V f^2}{c_0^3} + \dfrac{\pi S f}{2 c_0^2} + \dfrac{L}{8 c_0}
$$

where $S$ is the surface area and $L$ is the sum of edge lengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** SEA is statistically valid when each subsystem contains at least **5 modes in the analysis band**. This is the primary check before launching an SEA simulation in software.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Frequency Range | Dominant Phenomenon | Best Software Method | Sample Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 100 Hz | Rigid-body modes, suspension | MDOF/MBS, FE modal | Adams, MotionSolve, Simpack |
| 100 – 500 Hz | Structural resonances, panel modes | FE modal + harmonic | Nastran SOL 111, OptiStruct |
| 200 – 2000 Hz | Structure-borne noise, mid-freq acoustics | Coupled FE-BEM | Actran, Virtual.Lab |
| 500 – 5000 Hz | High-freq NVH, road noise, tire cavity | SEA | AutoSEA, SEAM |
| 5000+ Hz | Brake squeal, bearing whine | Complex eigenvalue | Abaqus, OptiStruct |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NVH Software Ecosystem — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Geometry Source] --> B[Pre-Processor Mesher]
    B --> C[Material and Property Assignment]
    C --> D{Solver Selection Engine}
    D --> E[Structural FEA Solver]
    D --> F[Acoustic BEM Solver]
    D --> G[SEA High Frequency Solver]
    D --> H[MBD Dynamics Solver]
    E --> I[Post-Processor Visualization]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Correlation with Test Data]
    J --> K[NVH Design Decision Report]
    L[Test Instrumentation PULSE Artemis OROS] --> J
    M[Signal Processing MATLAB] --> J
    N[Order Analysis Tool] --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The single **CAD source** feeds a unified pre-processor (HyperMesh, ANSA).
- The **Solver Selection Engine** is the conceptual decision matrix from Section 3.4.
- The four solver branches converge into a single post-processor, then are correlated with measured data before any design decision is made.

### 4.2 Coupled FE–BEM NVH Workflow (Most Common in Automotive OEM Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Import BIW CAD] --> B[Step 2: Mesh with Quad and Tet Elements]
    B --> C[Step 3: Assign Material Steel Aluminum Plastic]
    C --> D[Step 4: Define Mounting Constraints]
    D --> E[Step 5: Run Modal Analysis SOL 103]
    E --> F[Step 6: Extract Mode Shapes and Natural Frequencies]
    F --> G[Step 7: Run Frequency Response SOL 108 with Measured Road Load]
    G --> H[Step 8: Map Structural Velocity to Acoustic BEM Boundary]
    H --> I[Step 9: Solve Coupled FE BEM for Interior SPL]
    I --> J[Step 10: Overlay with Microphone Test Data]
    J --> K{Acceptance Pass or Fail}
    K --> L[Yes: Release Design]
    K --> M[No: Iterate with Stiffness and Damping Changes]
    M --> E
```

### 4.3 Solver Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: NVH Problem] --> B{Frequency Range}
    B --> C[Low: 0 to 200 Hz]
    B --> D[Mid: 200 to 1000 Hz]
    B --> E[High: Above 500 Hz]
    C --> F[Use FE Modal and MBD]
    D --> G[Use FE Harmonic and Coupled BEM]
    E --> H[Use SEA and Energy Methods]
    F --> I[Validate with Hammer Test]
    G --> J[Validate with Shaker Test]
    H --> K[Validate with High Frequency Statistical Test]
    I --> L[Final NVH Report]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

### 4.4 Software Tool Taxonomy (Domain vs Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[NVH Software Stack] --> B[Domain 1: Structural FE]
    A --> C[Domain 2: Acoustic BE]
    A --> D[Domain 3: Multibody Dynamics]
    A --> E[Domain 4: Signal Processing and Test]
    B --> B1[Nastran]
    B --> B2[ANSYS Mechanical]
    B --> B3[Abaqus]
    B --> B4[OptiStruct]
    C --> C1[Actran]
    C --> C2[LMS Virtual.Lab Acoustics]
    C --> C3[COMSOL Acoustics]
    C --> C4[OpenBEM Open Source]
    D --> D1[Adams Car]
    D --> D2[MotionSolve]
    D --> D3[Simpack]
    D --> D4[RecurDyn]
    E --> E1[MATLAB and Simulink]
    E --> E2[Bruel Kjaer PULSE]
    E --> E3[Simcenter Testlab]
    E --> E4[Head Acoustics Artemis]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid for Mermaid flowcharts:** Each rectangle is a *process step*; each diamond is a *decision*; each rounded rectangle is an *input/output artifact*. In the exam, sketching even a 5-node version of these flowcharts can earn full method marks if labeled correctly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**
*Define Boundary Element Method (BEM). Why is BEM particularly suited to interior automotive cabin acoustics problems compared to FEM?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Boundary Element Method (BEM)** is a numerical technique that solves the **Helmholtz equation** ($\nabla^2 P + k^2 P = 0$) by discretizing *only* the surface boundary of an acoustic domain, rather than its entire volume. **(1 mark)**
BEM is suited to interior cabin acoustics because:
1. It **only requires a surface mesh** of the cabin trim and glass, dramatically reducing the number of elements and solving time. **(1 mark)**
2. It **automatically satisfies the Sommerfeld radiation condition** at infinity, making it ideal for open and semi-open domains. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
*List any three commercial software tools used for automotive NVH simulation and state one primary application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **MSC Nastran** – Used for structural modal and frequency response analysis of BIW (Body-in-White). **(1 mark)**
2. **Actran** – Used for interior cabin acoustic BEM analysis and transmission loss prediction. **(1 mark)**
3. **Altair OptiStruct** – Used for topology optimization with NVH constraints in suspension components. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** as applied to NVH structural vibration analysis. Derive the **MDOF equation of motion** in matrix form and define the **Frequency Response Function (FRF) matrix**. State the physical meaning of an off-diagonal element $H_{12}(\omega)$ of the FRF matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **complete step-by-step workflow** for performing a coupled structural-acoustic NVH simulation of a passenger car cabin. Identify the input data, solver stages, and validation strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — MDOF Equation of Motion (3 marks):**
For an $n$-DOF system, Newton's second law for each mass, combined with stiffness and damping, yields:

$$
[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [C]\{\dot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{F(t)\}
$$

where $[M]$, $[C]$, $[K]$ are the assembled global mass, damping, and stiffness matrices.

**Step 2 — Rayleigh Damping Assumption (1 mark):**
To enable modal decoupling, proportional damping is used:

$$
[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Domain Transformation (2 marks):**
Taking the Fourier transform, the FRF matrix is:

$$
[H(\omega)] = [K - \omega^2 M + j\omega C]^{-1}
$$

The response in the frequency domain is:

$$
\{X(\omega)\} = [H(\omega)]\{F(\omega)\}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Meaning of $H_{12}(\omega)$ (1 mark):**
$H_{12}(\omega)$ represents the **complex-valued displacement response at DOF 2 per unit harmonic force applied at DOF 1**, as a function of angular frequency $\omega$. It is used by NVH engineers to identify transfer paths between input points (e.g., engine mount) and output points (e.g., steering column).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Pre-Processing (2 marks):**
Import BIW CAD into a mesher (HyperMesh), create a mid-surface mesh with element size $\lambda/6$ for the maximum frequency of interest (say 200 Hz → element size ~ 50 mm for steel). Assign material properties ($E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³ for steel).

**Step 2 — Structural Solver Run (2 marks):**
- Run **modal analysis** (Nastran SOL 103) up to 200 Hz to identify natural frequencies and mode shapes.
- Run **frequency response** (Nastran SOL 108) using measured engine mount forces as input.

**Step 3 — Acoustic BEM Coupling (2 marks):**
Map the structural normal velocity from the FE shell to the BEM acoustic mesh of the cabin trim. Solve the Helmholtz equation in Actran to obtain the interior SPL at the driver's ear position.

**Step 4 — Validation (1 mark):**
Compare simulated SPL vs. frequency curve with the measured curve from a microphone at the driver's ear. Acceptable correlation: $\pm 3$ dB up to 200 Hz.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA)** method. Under what conditions is it preferred over FEM? Derive the **modal density expression for a 2D plate** and state the rule of thumb for SEA validity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **FEM, BEM, and SEA** in a tabular form across the following parameters: (i) applicable frequency range, (ii) governing equation, (iii) mesh requirement, (iv) suitable software example, (v) typical output, (vi) main limitation, (vii) validation method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Concept of SEA (2 marks):**
SEA is a high-frequency energy-based method. Instead of tracking displacement at each node, SEA tracks the **time-averaged vibrational energy** in a "subsystem" (a panel, a cavity, etc.). Power flow between coupled subsystems is governed by the **coupling loss factors** $\eta_{ij}$.

**Step 2 — Power Balance Equation (2 marks):**
For two coupled subsystems $i$ and $j$:

$$
\omega \eta_{ij} n_i E_i - \omega \eta_{ji} n_j E_j = 0 \quad \text{(steady state)}
$$

This gives the **reciprocity condition**:

$$
\omega \eta_{ij} n_i = \omega \eta_{ji} n_j
$$

**Step 3 — Modal Density of a 2D Plate (2 marks):**
For a rectangular plate of area $A$ and thickness $h$:

$$
n(f) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} A}{2 C_L h}, \quad \text{where } C_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho(1-\nu^2)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Validity Condition (1 mark):**
SEA is valid when each subsystem contains **at least 5 modes** in the analysis band. Below this, FEM is preferred.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | FEM | BEM | SEA |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Frequency range | 0 – 500 Hz | 200 – 2000 Hz | > 500 Hz |
| (ii) Governing equation | $[M]\ddot{x} + [C]\dot{x} + [K]x = F$ | $\nabla^2 P + k^2 P = 0$ | $\omega \eta_{ij} n_i = \omega \eta_{ji} n_j$ |
| (iii) Mesh requirement | Volume mesh of full structure | Surface mesh only | Lumped parameters |
| (iv) Software example | MSC Nastran, ANSYS | Actran, LMS Virtual.Lab | AutoSEA, SEAM |
| (v) Typical output | Mode shapes, FRFs | SPL maps, radiation efficiency | Energy levels, transmission loss |
| (vi) Main limitation | Expensive at high freq. | Less accurate at low freq. | Needs many modes per band |
| (vii) Validation method | Hammer impact test | Microphone scan | Reverberation time test |

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in NVH software questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SOL cards in Nastran** — Many students write "SOL 101" for modal analysis. SOL 101 is **static**, not modal. The correct card for modal is **SOL 103** (real eigenvalue) or **SOL 107** (complex eigenvalue for damping).
> 2. **Mixing up FEM and BEM mesh requirements** — A common error is stating that BEM requires a volume mesh. BEM needs only a *surface* mesh. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using 10 instead of 20 in the dB formula** — For sound pressure level (SPL), the log multiplier is **20**, not 10. Mistaking this costs a full mark.
> 4. **Omitting units in formulas** — KTU examiners *always* deduct 0.5 marks if the unit of $\omega$ (rad/s) or $f$ (Hz) is not clearly stated.
> 5. **Writing "SEA = Finite Element Method"** — SEA and FEM are *complementary*, not equivalent. SEA is valid at high frequency; FEM is valid at low frequency. This conceptual error loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the Sommerfeld radiation condition** in BEM derivations — This is a high-yield keyword that examiners look for. Include it for full marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Introduction to Software Applications for NVH Analysis**

- [ ] NVH software is classified into **Structural FE, Acoustic BE, Multibody Dynamics, and Test/Signal Processing** domains.
- [ ] **FEM** uses the equation $[M]\ddot{x} + [C]\dot{x} + [K]x = F$ and is valid for **low–mid frequencies (0 – 500 Hz)**.
- [ ] **BEM** solves the **Helmholtz equation** $\nabla^2 P + k^2 P = 0$ using a *surface-only* mesh and is preferred for interior cabin acoustics.
- [ ] **SEA** uses **coupling loss factors** and is valid only when **at least 5 modes per analysis band** exist (typically > 500 Hz).
- [ ] The **FRF matrix** is $[H(\omega)] = [K - \omega^2 M + j\omega C]^{-1}$; off-diagonal elements give **transfer functions** between input and output points.
- [ ] The **standard speed of sound** is $c_0 = 343$ m/s at 20°C; the **reference SPL** is $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu$Pa.
- [ ] **Rayleigh damping**: $[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]$ — required to decouple the modal equations in commercial solvers.
- [ ] **Nastran SOL cards**: SOL 103 (modal), SOL 108 (frequency response), SOL 111 (modal frequency response), SOL 107 (complex eigenvalue for damping/squeal).
- [ ] **Coupled FE–BEM** is the *industry-standard* workflow for structure-borne interior noise prediction in a passenger car.
- [ ] The **wavenumber** $k = \omega/c_0$ links structural and acoustic domains — never confuse rad/m with Hz.
- [ ] The **Sommerfeld radiation condition** must be automatically satisfied by BEM solvers for open-domain problems.
- [ ] **Validation strategy**: Simulated SPL $\pm 3$ dB of measured SPL is the universally accepted correlation tolerance.
- [ ] Always state **units explicitly** — $\omega$ in rad/s, $f$ in Hz, $L_p$ in dB, $E$ in Pa or GPa, $\rho$ in kg/m³.
